topics.title: Earth Pressure - At Rest
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Earth Pressure - At Rest

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Earth Pressure at Rest (K₀ Condition):** It is the lateral (horizontal) earth pressure exerted by a soil mass when it is in its natural, undisturbed state, with **no lateral deformation or yield** of the soil or the retaining structure. The ratio of effective horizontal stress to effective vertical stress at this state is termed the **Coefficient of Earth Pressure at Rest**, denoted as **K₀**.

Mathematically, for a cohesionless soil deposit at rest:

$$K_0 = \frac{\sigma'_h}{\sigma'_v}$$

Where:
- $\sigma'_h$ = Effective horizontal stress
- $\sigma'_v$ = Effective vertical stress

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike active (Kₐ) or passive (Kₚ) states, the at-rest condition assumes the retaining wall is **infinitely rigid** and undergoes **zero lateral strain** ($\epsilon_h = 0$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine pushing a thick hardcover book sideways against a wall. If the book is tightly sandwiched and **cannot move or deform**, the sideways pressure you feel on your hand represents the **at-rest earth pressure**. Now, if you slowly pull the book away from the wall (allowing the soil to expand slightly), the pressure drops — this becomes the **active state**. Conversely, if you push the book hard into the wall (compressing the soil), the resistance you feel increases dramatically — this is the **passive state**.

**Another analogy:** Think of soil grains in a deep, undisturbed natural deposit. The weight of overlying layers squeezes them vertically, and they push sideways against imaginary "walls" formed by adjacent soil — but since nothing is moving, the soil simply rests in equilibrium.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unit weight of water | $\gamma_w$ | **9.81** (often taken as **10**) | kN/m³ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | g | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Atmospheric pressure (reference) | Pₐₜₘ | **101.325** | kPa |
| Poisson's ratio (for soil) | $\mu$ or $\nu$ | **0.25 to 0.50** | dimensionless |
| K₀ (sands) | K₀ | **0.4 to 0.6** | dimensionless |
| K₀ (normally consolidated clays) | K₀ | **0.5 to 0.7** | dimensionless |
| K₀ (overconsolidated clays) | K₀(OC) | **0.8 to 1.5+** | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Board Exam Tip: Always state that **K₀ < 1** for normally consolidated soils, since lateral stress is always less than vertical stress in a free-field deposit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The 'Why' and 'How'

The **at-rest** condition represents the **in-situ stress state** of a soil deposit that has not experienced any lateral yielding. This is the starting reference state for all earth pressure problems in Foundation Engineering.

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown:

1. **In-situ vertical stress** develops due to the self-weight of soil: $\sigma'_v = \gamma' \cdot z$
2. **Lateral stress** arises as a natural Poisson's effect — soil grains cannot be compressed vertically without pushing sideways.
3. If the lateral strain is constrained ($\epsilon_h = 0$), the horizontal stress becomes a fixed fraction of the vertical stress.
4. This fixed fraction is the **coefficient of earth pressure at rest, K₀**.
5. K₀ depends on the soil's stress history (OCR), mineralogy, and depositional process.

### Conditions Defining the At-Rest State:
- ✅ **Zero lateral strain** ($\epsilon_h = 0$)
- ✅ **No shear failure** in the soil mass
- ✅ **Elastic equilibrium** under self-weight stresses
- ✅ **Stress history preserved** (K₀-consolidated deposit)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 80%+ of KTU University Exam questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Application / Condition | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_0 = \dfrac{\sigma'_h}{\sigma'_v}$ | General definition | $\sigma'_h$, $\sigma'_v$ |
| 2 | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi'$ | **Jaky's formula** — Normally consolidated (NC) soils | $\phi'$ = effective friction angle |
| 3 | $K_{0(OC)} = (1 - \sin\phi') \cdot OCR^{\sin\phi'}$ | **Overconsolidated (OC) soils** | OCR = Over Consolidation Ratio |
| 4 | $K_0 = \dfrac{\mu}{1 - \mu}$ | Elastic theory (linear, isotropic) | $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| 5 | $K_0 = (1 - \sin\phi') \cdot OCR^{\sin\phi'}$ | Modified Mayne & Kulhawy (general) | Combines NC + OC effects |
| 6 | $\sigma'_h = K_0 \cdot \sigma'_v$ | Horizontal effective stress at depth z | — |
| 7 | $p_0 = K_0 \cdot \gamma' \cdot z$ | Lateral pressure at depth z (cohesionless) | $\gamma'$ = effective unit weight |
| 8 | $K_{0(NC)} = \dfrac{\sigma'_{p}}{\sigma'_{v0}}$ | From oedometer test (where $\sigma'_p$ = preconsolidation pressure) | — |
| 9 | $K_0 \cdot K_a = 1$ (only if $\mu \cdot \mu_p$ relation holds) | Theoretical limit check | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside LaTeX expressions** when writing on the answer sheet. Use `\vert` or `\mid` to express absolute value or "given that" notation. (KTU 2024 Scheme: Smart answer scripts use clean LaTeX.)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of rigid basement walls** and **shear walls** where yielding is prevented.
- **Analysis of braced excavations** during construction.
- **Tunnel lining design** (initial stress estimation).
- **Pile foundation analysis** under lateral loading.
- **Earth pressure calculations for anchored bulkheads** (initial state).
- **Geostatic stress analysis** for slope stability and settlement problems.
- **Calibration of constitutive models** in finite element geotechnical software.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: K₀ from Elastic Theory (Boussinesq-Type Approach)

Consider an element of soil at depth z, subjected to:
- Vertical stress: $\sigma'_v = \gamma' z$
- Horizontal stress: $\sigma'_h = K_0 \cdot \sigma'_v$
- Zero lateral strain: $\epsilon_h = 0$

Using the **generalized Hooke's law** for a linear, isotropic, elastic material:

$$\epsilon_h = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma'_h - \mu(\sigma'_v + \sigma'_{h2}) \right]$$

For axisymmetric conditions where $\sigma'_{h1} = \sigma'_{h2} = \sigma'_h$:

$$\epsilon_h = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma'_h - \mu \sigma'_v - \mu \sigma'_h \right]$$

$$\epsilon_h = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma'_h(1 - \mu) - \mu \sigma'_v \right]$$

**Apply the at-rest condition** $\epsilon_h = 0$:

$$0 = \sigma'_h(1 - \mu) - \mu \sigma'_v$$

$$\sigma'_h(1 - \mu) = \mu \sigma'_v$$

$$\sigma'_h = \frac{\mu}{1 - \mu} \cdot \sigma'_v$$

Therefore, by definition:

$$K_0 = \frac{\sigma'_h}{\sigma'_v} = \frac{\mu}{1 - \mu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** This is valid only for **linear elastic, isotropic** soils. Real soils are non-linear, anisotropic, and stress-history dependent — hence the empirical Jaky's formula is preferred in practice.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Jaky's Formula (Empirical, Most Used)

**Jaky (1944)** proposed, based on experimental observations of granular soil deposits:

$$K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi'$$

### Derivation Logic (Physical Reasoning):

Consider a soil element with vertical effective stress $\sigma'_v$ and horizontal effective stress $\sigma'_h = K_0 \sigma'_v$.

For a **wedge of soil** at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal, using the **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion** as a limiting reference:

The stresses on the inclined plane must satisfy the **Mohr circle** tangency condition for the soil in a **K₀-consolidated state** (no failure, but at the limit of elastic equilibrium).

After mathematical reduction (using stress transformation and the Mohr-Coulomb envelope $\tau = \sigma' \tan\phi'$):

$$\sigma'_h = \sigma'_v \left( 1 - \sin\phi' \right)$$

This simplifies to:

$$K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi'$$

### Numerical Validation:

| Soil Type | $\phi'$ (degrees) | $\sin\phi'$ | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi'$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Loose sand | 30° | 0.500 | 0.500 |
| Medium sand | 33° | 0.545 | 0.455 |
| Dense sand | 38° | 0.616 | 0.384 |
| NC Clay (soft) | 25° | 0.423 | 0.577 |
| OC Clay (stiff) | 30° | 0.500 | 0.500 (× OCR⁰·⁵ factor) |

## 3.3 Derivation 3: K₀ for Overconsolidated Soils (Mayne & Kulhawy, 1982)

For OC soils, K₀ is **higher** than for NC soils. The relationship is:

$$K_{0(OC)} = (1 - \sin\phi') \cdot OCR^{\sin\phi'}$$

### Step-by-Step Logical Build:

1. For NC soil: $K_{0(NC)} = 1 - \sin\phi'$
2. For OC soil, define OCR = $\dfrac{\sigma'_p}{\sigma'_{v0}}$ (preconsolidation pressure ÷ current vertical stress)
3. Empirical observation: K₀ increases with OCR following a power law.
4. Combine NC baseline with OCR factor:

$$K_{0(OC)} = K_{0(NC)} \cdot OCR^{\sin\phi'}$$

$$K_{0(OC)} = (1 - \sin\phi') \cdot OCR^{\sin\phi'}$$

### Worked Numerical Example:

**Given:** $\phi' = 30°$, OCR = 4

**Step 1:** Compute $K_{0(NC)}$

$$K_{0(NC)} = 1 - \sin 30° = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$

**Step 2:** Compute OCR exponent

$$\sin\phi' = 0.5$$

**Step 3:** Apply OC formula

$$K_{0(OC)} = 0.5 \times 4^{0.5} = 0.5 \times 2.0 = 1.0$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $K_0 > 1.0$, the horizontal stress exceeds the vertical stress — a hallmark of heavily overconsolidated deposits (e.g., stiff fissured clays, glacial tills).

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Formula to Use?

| Soil State | OCR | Recommended Formula | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normally Consolidated | OCR = 1 | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi'$ (Jaky) | Empirical, well-validated |
| Lightly Overconsolidated | 1 < OCR < 3 | $K_{0(OC)} = (1-\sin\phi') OCR^{\sin\phi'}$ | Mayne-Kulhawy recommended |
| Heavily Overconsolidated | OCR > 3 | $K_{0(OC)} = (1-\sin\phi') OCR^{\sin\phi'}$ | Same formula; verify with tests |
| Elastic, isotropic assumption | — | $K_0 = \mu/(1-\mu)$ | Only for theoretical/initial estimates |
| From oedometer test | — | $K_0 = \sigma'_p / \sigma'_{v0}$ | Back-calculated from lab data |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Earth Pressure State Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Soil Mass in Natural State] --> B0[Geostatic Equilibrium]
    B0 --> C0{Lateral Strain Condition?}
    C0 -->|eps_h = 0| D0[AT REST State]
    C0 -->|eps_h greater than 0, expansion| E0[ACTIVE State]
    C0 -->|eps_h less than 0, compression| F0[PASSIVE State]
    D0 --> D1[K0 = 1 minus sin phi prime]
    D0 --> D2[Sigma prime h = K0 times Sigma prime v]
    D0 --> D3[Wall does NOT yield]
    E0 --> E1[Ka less than K0]
    E0 --> E2[Wall moves AWAY from soil]
    E0 --> E3[Minimum lateral pressure]
    F0 --> F1[Kp greater than K0]
    F0 --> F2[Wall moves TOWARDS soil]
    F0 --> F3[Maximum lateral pressure]
    D0 --> G0[KTU Reference State]
    G0 --> H0[Used for rigid retaining structures]
    G0 --> I0[Initial stress for numerical modelling]
    G0 --> J0[Braced cut and basement wall design]
    style A0 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style D0 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style E0 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#ef6c00
    style F0 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style G0 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
```

## 4.2 Stress Element Diagram: At-Rest Condition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Soil Element at Depth z]
        V0[Vertical Stress sigma prime v = gamma prime z]
        H0[Horizontal Stress sigma prime h = K0 sigma prime v]
    end
    V0 --> V1[Acts on TOP and BOTTOM faces]
    H0 --> H1[Acts on LEFT and RIGHT faces]
    V1 --> R0[At Rest: No lateral yield]
    H1 --> R0
    R0 --> R1[Sigma prime h divided by sigma prime v = K0]
    R0 --> R2[Effective stress principle: Use gamma prime below water table]
    style A fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style R0 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: K₀ Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Need K0 Value] --> S1{Available Data?}
    S1 -->|Friction angle phi prime| S2[Use Jaky Formula]
    S1 -->|OCR and phi prime| S3[Use Mayne Kulhawy Formula]
    S1 -->|Poisson ratio mu| S4[Use Elastic Formula]
    S1 -->|Oedometer Test Data| S5[Back Calculate K0]
    S2 --> S6[K0 = 1 minus sin phi prime]
    S3 --> S7[K0 OC = 1 minus sin phi prime times OCR to power sin phi prime]
    S4 --> S8[K0 = mu divided by 1 minus mu]
    S5 --> S9[K0 = sigma prime p divided by sigma prime v0]
    S6 --> S10[Apply to design]
    S7 --> S10
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[End: K0 Value for At Rest Analysis]
    style S0 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style S10 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style S11 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the coefficient of earth pressure at rest. How is it different from the active earth pressure coefficient?**

**Model Answer:**

The **coefficient of earth pressure at rest (K₀)** is defined as the ratio of effective horizontal stress to effective vertical stress in a soil mass that has **not undergone any lateral strain or deformation**.

$$K_0 = \frac{\sigma'_h}{\sigma'_v}$$

**Key Differences:**

| Parameter | At-Rest (K₀) | Active (Kₐ) |
|---|---|---|
| Lateral strain | $\epsilon_h = 0$ | $\epsilon_h > 0$ (soil expands) |
| Wall movement | No yield | Wall moves away from soil |
| Magnitude | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi'$ | $K_a = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi'}{1 + \sin\phi'}$ |
| Stress state | $K_0 < 1$ (usually) | $K_a < K_0$ (minimum) |
| Failure condition | No failure | Shear failure (limit state) |

> [!NOTE]
> K₀ is a **service state** condition, whereas Kₐ is a **limit state (failure)** condition.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State Jaky's empirical formula for K₀ and mention its applicability.**

**Model Answer:**

**Jaky's Formula (1944):**

$$K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi'$$

where $\phi'$ is the **effective angle of internal friction** of the soil.

**Applicability:**
- ✅ **Normally consolidated (NC)** cohesionless and cohesive soils
- ✅ Granular deposits formed under **one-dimensional compression** (oedometric conditions)
- ✅ **K₀-consolidated** laboratory and field specimens
- ❌ Not directly applicable to **heavily overconsolidated (OC)** soils without OCR correction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Jaky's formula is the **single most tested formula** in KTU exams for this topic. Always quote it with the year and the term "normally consolidated."

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: A 10 m deep sand deposit has a unit weight of 18 kN/m³. The water table is at 2 m below the ground surface. The effective friction angle is 35°. Estimate the at-rest earth pressure at the base of the deposit. Also, compute the lateral thrust per metre run on a rigid basement wall.**

#### Part (a) — Compute K₀ and Lateral Stress Distribution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify soil parameters**

$$\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3, \quad \gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3, \quad \phi' = 35°$$

**Step 2: Compute effective unit weights**

Above water table (0 to 2 m):

$$\gamma_{above} = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3 \quad (\text{total} = \text{effective above WT})$$

Below water table (2 m to 10 m):

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$$

Assuming saturated unit weight $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³ (typical for sand):

$$\gamma' = 20 - 9.81 = 10.19 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Step 3: Apply Jaky's formula for K₀**

$$K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi' = 1 - \sin 35° = 1 - 0.5736 = 0.4264$$

**Step 4: Compute effective vertical stress at base (z = 10 m)**

$$\sigma'_v = \gamma_{above} \cdot 2 + \gamma' \cdot 8$$

$$\sigma'_v = (18 \times 2) + (10.19 \times 8)$$

$$\sigma'_v = 36 + 81.52 = 117.52 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5: Compute horizontal effective stress at base**

$$\sigma'_h = K_0 \cdot \sigma'_v = 0.4264 \times 117.52$$

$$\sigma'_h = 50.11 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

#### Part (b) — Compute Lateral Thrust per Metre Run (7 Marks)

**Step 6: Construct stress distribution diagram**

| Depth (m) | $\sigma'_v$ (kN/m²) | $\sigma'_h = K_0 \sigma'_v$ (kN/m²) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 36.00 | 15.35 |
| 10 | 117.52 | 50.11 |

**Step 7: Compute lateral thrust (area of stress diagram)**

**Region 1 (0 to 2 m) — Triangular:**

$$P_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 15.35 = 15.35 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Region 2 (2 to 10 m) — Trapezoidal:**

$$P_2 = \frac{1}{2} \times (15.35 + 50.11) \times 8 = \frac{1}{2} \times 65.46 \times 8 = 261.84 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Total Lateral Thrust:**

$$P_{total} = P_1 + P_2 = 15.35 + 261.84 = 277.19 \text{ kN/m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** K₀ = 0.426, Lateral thrust ≈ 277.2 kN per metre run of wall.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: A soft clay deposit (NC) has a saturated unit weight of 19 kN/m³, $\phi' = 25°$, and OCR = 1. After consolidation under an old building, the OCR has increased to 3. Compute the at-rest lateral earth pressure at the mid-depth of a 8 m thick layer, and compare the K₀ values for NC and OC states. Water table is at the ground surface.**

#### Part (a) — K₀ for Normally Consolidated State (7 Marks)

**Step 1: NC parameters**

$$\phi' = 25°, \quad OCR = 1, \quad \gamma_{sat} = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Effective unit weight (since water table at surface)**

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 19 - 9.81 = 9.19 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Step 3: K₀ using Jaky's formula**

$$K_{0(NC)} = 1 - \sin 25° = 1 - 0.4226 = 0.5774$$

**Step 4: Effective vertical stress at mid-depth (z = 4 m)**

$$\sigma'_v = \gamma' \cdot z = 9.19 \times 4 = 36.76 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5: NC horizontal stress**

$$\sigma'_{h(NC)} = K_{0(NC)} \cdot \sigma'_v = 0.5774 \times 36.76 = 21.22 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

#### Part (b) — K₀ for Overconsolidated State (7 Marks)

**Step 6: Apply Mayne-Kulhawy formula for OC state**

$$K_{0(OC)} = (1 - \sin\phi') \cdot OCR^{\sin\phi'}$$

$$K_{0(OC)} = 0.5774 \times 3^{0.4226}$$

**Step 7: Compute $3^{0.4226}$**

$$3^{0.4226} = e^{0.4226 \cdot \ln 3} = e^{0.4226 \times 1.0986} = e^{0.4643} = 1.591$$

**Step 8: Final K₀ for OC**

$$K_{0(OC)} = 0.5774 \times 1.591 = 0.9186$$

**Step 9: OC horizontal stress at mid-depth**

$$\sigma'_{h(OC)} = K_{0(OC)} \cdot \sigma'_v = 0.9186 \times 36.76 = 33.77 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 10: Comparison**

| Parameter | NC State | OC State (OCR = 3) |
|---|---|---|
| $K_0$ | 0.5774 | 0.9186 |
| $\sigma'_h$ at 4 m (kN/m²) | 21.22 | 33.77 |
| % increase | — | **+59.1%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Overconsolidation increases K₀ by ~59% in this case, demonstrating why OC clays exert much higher lateral pressures on rigid structures.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use $\gamma'$ below water table** — Using $\gamma_{sat}$ instead of $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ is the **#1 reason for losing 2-3 marks** in earth pressure problems.
>
> 2. **Confusing OCR conditions** — Applying Jaky's formula directly to overconsolidated soils gives **erroneously low K₀**. Always check OCR and apply Mayne-Kulhawy if OCR > 1.
>
> 3. **Mixing total and effective stress** — Below the water table, $\sigma'_h = K_0 \sigma'_v$, **NOT** $K_0 (\sigma_v - u)$ applied incorrectly. State the effective stress principle clearly.
>
> 4. **Unit errors** — Depth in metres, stress in kN/m², thrust in kN/m. Watch unit consistency.
>
> 5. **Skipping the stress distribution diagram** — Always draw a labelled pressure diagram in the answer. KTU examiners award 1-2 marks for proper diagrams.
>
> 6. **Stating "at-rest = active"** — These are **fundamentally different** conditions. At-rest has $\epsilon_h = 0$; active has failure-level yielding.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning "Normally Consolidated"** with Jaky's formula — Always state the soil condition for full credit.
>
> 8. **Calculation slip in exponent** — For $OCR^{\sin\phi'}$, mistakes in $\ln$ or $e^x$ evaluation lead to wrong K₀. Show intermediate steps.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme**

- ✅ K₀ = $\sigma'_h / \sigma'_v$ — ratio of horizontal to vertical effective stress in undeformed soil
- ✅ At-rest condition = **zero lateral strain** ($\epsilon_h = 0$)
- ✅ **Jaky's formula**: $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi'$ — for **normally consolidated** soils
- ✅ **Mayne-Kulhawy**: $K_{0(OC)} = (1 - \sin\phi') \cdot OCR^{\sin\phi'}$ — for **overconsolidated** soils
- ✅ **Elastic theory**: $K_0 = \mu / (1 - \mu)$ — for theoretical, isotropic, linear-elastic analysis
- ✅ Typical K₀ range: **0.4–0.6** (sands), **0.5–0.7** (NC clays), **0.8–1.5+** (OC clays)
- ✅ **Overconsolidation increases K₀** (K₀(OC) > K₀(NC))
- ✅ Use **effective unit weight** $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ below water table
- ✅ K₀ is a **service state** (not a failure state like Kₐ and Kₚ)
- ✅ **Order**: $K_a < K_0 < K_p$ (for cohesionless soils)
- ✅ K₀ used in design of: **rigid basement walls, braced cuts, pile lateral loads, tunnel linings, anchored bulkheads**
- ✅ Always draw **stress distribution diagram** (triangular/trapezoidal) for full marks
- ✅ Common test: **oedometer back-calculation** — $K_0 = \sigma'_p / \sigma'_{v0}$
- ✅ Poisson's ratio for soils typically: **0.25 to 0.50** (dimensionless)
- ✅ Critical KTU 2024 takeaway: K₀ is the **reference state** for all earth pressure problems in Foundation Engineering
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active and Passive Earth Pressures - Rankine's Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Active and Passive Earth Pressures - Rankine's Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Earth Pressure

Whenever a structure (such as a retaining wall, basement, or sheet pile) is built to retain a mass of soil, the soil exerts a lateral pressure on the structure. This lateral force is called **Earth Pressure**, and its magnitude depends on the displacement of the structure relative to the soil mass. The study of earth pressure is one of the most classical and fundamental problems in Geotechnical Engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Earth Pressure Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
> Earth pressure is the lateral pressure exerted by soil mass on a retaining structure. It is a function of the unit weight, shear strength parameters (cohesion *c* and angle of internal friction *φ*), depth of backfill, drainage conditions, and most importantly, the lateral strain permitted in the soil mass.

## 1.2 The Three States of Lateral Earth Pressure

Depending on the relative movement between the wall and the backfill, soil can exist in three distinct limiting stress states:

| State | Trigger Condition | Lateral Stress | Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **At-Rest State ($K_0$)** | Wall is perfectly stationary (no lateral strain, $\varepsilon_h = 0$) | $\sigma_h = K_0 \sigma_v$ | Intermediate |
| **Active State ($K_a$)** | Wall moves **away** from the backfill (soil expands laterally) | $\sigma_h = K_a \sigma_v$ | **Minimum** |
| **Passive State ($K_p$)** | Wall moves **towards** the backfill (soil is compressed laterally) | $\sigma_h = K_p \sigma_v$ | **Maximum** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Range of $K$ values for cohesionless soil:**
> $K_a < K_0 < K_p$
> Numerically, for sand with $\phi = 30°$: $K_a = 0.333$, $K_0 \approx 0.5$, $K_p = 3.0$
> Notice the dramatic asymmetry — passive pressure is roughly **9 times** active pressure for the same soil!

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Squeezing Towel Rack"**
> Imagine a tall towel rack firmly holding a thick stack of folded towels (this is your retaining wall and soil mass).
> 1. **At-Rest**: The rack is locked. The towels simply stand there pushing horizontally with some natural, undisturbed force. This is $K_0$.
> 2. **Active State**: If you slowly tilt the rack backward, the towels relax and slide apart slightly. They "let go" of the rack. The sideways push on the rack drops to its lowest value. This is $K_a$ — the soil has expanded.
> 3. **Passive State**: Now if you instead push the rack *into* the stack of towels (compressing them), they jam together and bulge sideways with a massive, resisting force. The sideways push soars to its maximum. This is $K_p$ — the soil has been compressed.
> 
> **Key Engineering Takeaway:** Designers always favor the *active* condition when sizing retaining walls because nature does most of the destructive work for free. A wall that resists active pressure will *automatically* fail long before passive pressure is ever reached.

## 1.4 Overview of Rankine's Theory (1857)

**William John Macquorn Rankine** (Scottish physicist, 1857) developed the first rigorous analytical theory for earth pressure, based on the concept of **plastic equilibrium** in a semi-infinite soil mass. Unlike Coulomb's theory (which considers wall friction), Rankine's theory assumes a **smooth, vertical wall** and a **homogeneous, dry, cohesionless backfill with a horizontal surface**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Rankine's Active State:**
> "A state of plastic equilibrium in which the major principal stress is vertical and the minor principal stress is horizontal, achieved when the soil mass is allowed to expand laterally without any restraint."
> 
> **Core Definition — Rankine's Passive State:**
> "A state of plastic equilibrium in which the major principal stress is horizontal and the minor principal stress is vertical, achieved when the soil mass is laterally compressed."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rankine's Theory — Fundamental Assumptions

For the theory to hold mathematically, Rankine made the following idealized assumptions:

1. The soil mass is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and **semi-infinite** in extent.
2. The ground surface is a **horizontal plane** (no slope).
3. The retaining wall is **vertical** and its back face is **smooth** (no wall friction, $\delta = 0$).
4. The soil is **dry** or the water table effects can be handled separately as pore-water pressure.
5. The soil is in a state of **plastic equilibrium** (every point is on the verge of failure).
6. The failure surface is a **plane** in Rankine's case (unlike the curved log-spiral surface of true passive failure).
7. The soil shear strength obeys the **Mohr-Coulomb** failure criterion: $\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi$.

## 2.2 The Three Principal Stress States — Conceptual Logic

Consider an elementary soil cube deep inside a semi-infinite horizontal soil mass:

**Step 1 — Initial State (No Lateral Strain):**
Vertical stress = $\sigma_v = \gamma z$ (overburden weight).
Horizontal stress = $\sigma_h = K_0 \sigma_v$.
This is the *in-situ* condition *before* any wall is built.

**Step 2 — Wall Construction & Active Mobilization:**
- Once the wall is built and slowly *yields away* from the soil, the soil expands laterally.
- The soil grains rearrange; the structure loosens.
- The **horizontal stress drops** (becomes the minor principal stress).
- Vertical stress stays essentially equal to the overburden: $\sigma_v = \gamma z$.
- So: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_v$ and $\sigma_3 = \sigma_h = \sigma_a$.
- The Mohr circle expands until it **just touches** the Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope.

**Step 3 — Wall Compression & Passive Mobilization:**
- If the wall is instead *pushed* into the soil mass, the soil is compressed laterally.
- The **horizontal stress rises** (becomes the major principal stress).
- $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h = \sigma_p$ and $\sigma_3 = \sigma_v$.
- Again, the Mohr circle expands until it is tangent to the failure envelope, but on the opposite side.

## 2.3 Failure Plane Orientations (Rankine's Sliding Wedges)

A very common KTU sub-question: *"At what angle does the failure plane develop?"*

In the active state, the failure plane makes an angle of $\left(45 + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ with the **horizontal** (i.e., the wedge slides downward and outward).

In the passive state, the failure plane makes an angle of $\left(45 - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ with the **horizontal** (i.e., the wedge is pushed upward and inward).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:**
> Active = "Relaxed" → Failure plane is **steeper** (closer to vertical).
> Passive = "Pressed" → Failure plane is **flatter** (closer to horizontal).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Validity / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Coefficient of Active Earth Pressure** (Cohesionless) | $K_a = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | $\phi$ = angle of internal friction (degrees) | Dry, horizontal backfill |
| **Coefficient of Passive Earth Pressure** (Cohesionless) | $K_p = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | $\phi$ = angle of internal friction (degrees) | Dry, horizontal backfill |
| **Coefficient of Earth Pressure at Rest** (Jaky, 1944) | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ | $\phi$ = angle of internal friction (degrees) | Normally consolidated soils |
| **Active Pressure Intensity** (Cohesionless) | $p_a = K_a \,\gamma\, z$ | $\gamma$ = unit weight ($\text{kN/m}^3$), $z$ = depth (m), $p_a$ in $\text{kN/m}^2$ | Increases linearly with depth |
| **Passive Pressure Intensity** (Cohesionless) | $p_p = K_p \,\gamma\, z$ | Same as above | Increases linearly with depth |
| **Total Active Thrust per unit length** (Cohesionless) | $P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \,\gamma\, H^2$ | $H$ = height of wall (m), $P_a$ in $\text{kN/m}$ | Acts at $H/3$ from base |
| **Total Passive Thrust per unit length** (Cohesionless) | $P_p = \dfrac{1}{2} K_p \,\gamma\, H^2$ | Same as above | Acts at $H/3$ from base |
| **Active Pressure** (Cohesive $c$-$\phi$ soil) | $p_a = K_a \,\sigma_v - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$ | $c$ = cohesion ($\text{kN/m}^2$) | May be negative near top → tension zone |
| **Passive Pressure** (Cohesive $c$-$\phi$ soil) | $p_p = K_p \,\sigma_v + 2c\sqrt{K_p}$ | $c$ = cohesion ($\text{kN/m}^2$) | Always positive |
| **Depth of Tension Crack** | $z_c = \dfrac{2c}{\gamma\sqrt{K_a}}$ | $z_c$ in meters | Critical for design — soil pulls away from wall |
| **Rankine Active with Surcharge** $q$ | $p_a = K_a(\gamma z + q)$ | $q$ = surcharge ($\text{kN/m}^2$) | Adds uniform pressure $K_a q$ over full depth |
| **Rankine Active with Submerged Backfill** | $p_a = K_a \,\gamma' z + \gamma_w z$ | $\gamma'$ = submerged unit weight, $\gamma_w = 9.81\,\text{kN/m}^3$ | Below water table |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this important in practice?**
> 1. **Design of Retaining Walls** — gravity walls, cantilever walls, counterfort walls, basement walls.
> 2. **Sheet Pile Design** — for braced cuts, cofferdams, and waterfront structures.
> 3. **Tunnel & Shaft Linings** — to estimate radial stresses on circular excavations.
> 4. **Stability of Slopes & Embankments** — Rankine's wedge method for slip surface analysis.
> 5. **Design of Bridge Abutments** — to compute lateral load transfer to piles.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of $K_a$ and $K_p$ (Cohesionless Soil — Mohr-Coulomb Approach)

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Mohr Circle

Consider a soil element at depth $z$ in a semi-infinite mass.

Vertical effective stress:
$$\sigma_v = \gamma z$$

Let the unknown horizontal effective stress be $\sigma_h$.

The Mohr circle for the soil element is drawn in the $\sigma$-$\tau$ plane with:
- Center: $C = \dfrac{\sigma_v + \sigma_h}{2}$
- Radius: $R = \dfrac{\sigma_v - \sigma_h}{2}$

### 3.1.2 Tangency Condition (Failure Criterion)

For the soil to be in a state of plastic (limiting) equilibrium, the Mohr circle must be **tangent** to the Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope.

For cohesionless soil ($c = 0$), the failure envelope passes through the origin with slope $\tan\phi$.

**Geometric condition for tangency:**

In the Mohr circle, the radius $R$ and center $C$ are related to the friction angle $\phi$ by:
$$\sin\phi = \dfrac{R}{C}$$

This is the standard trigonometric identity derived from the right-angled triangle formed by the center, the origin, and the point of tangency.

### 3.1.3 Derivation for the Active Case

In the **active state**, the soil expands, so the **horizontal stress is the minor principal stress**:
$$\sigma_3 = \sigma_h, \quad \sigma_1 = \sigma_v$$

Substituting:
$$R = \dfrac{\sigma_v - \sigma_h}{2}, \quad C = \dfrac{\sigma_v + \sigma_h}{2}$$

Tangency condition:
$$\sin\phi = \dfrac{\sigma_v - \sigma_h}{\sigma_v + \sigma_h}$$

Cross-multiplying:
$$(\sigma_v + \sigma_h)\sin\phi = \sigma_v - \sigma_h$$

Expanding and collecting $\sigma_h$ terms on one side:
$$\sigma_v \sin\phi + \sigma_h \sin\phi = \sigma_v - \sigma_h$$
$$\sigma_h + \sigma_h \sin\phi = \sigma_v - \sigma_v \sin\phi$$
$$\sigma_h(1 + \sin\phi) = \sigma_v(1 - \sin\phi)$$

Therefore:
$$\sigma_h = \sigma_v \cdot \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = K_a \, \sigma_v$$

Hence proved:
$$K_a = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$$

### 3.1.4 Trigonometric Identity for $K_a$

Using the half-angle identity:
$$\dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \dfrac{\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) - \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)}{\left(\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) + \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)\right)^2} = \left(\dfrac{\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) - \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)}{\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) + \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)}\right)^2$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\cos(\phi/2)$:
$$K_a = \left(\dfrac{1 - \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)}{1 + \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)}\right)^2 = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

### 3.1.5 Derivation for the Passive Case

In the **passive state**, the wall compresses the soil, so the **horizontal stress is the major principal stress**:
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_h, \quad \sigma_3 = \sigma_v$$

Tangency condition now becomes:
$$\sin\phi = \dfrac{\sigma_h - \sigma_v}{\sigma_h + \sigma_v}$$

Cross-multiplying:
$$(\sigma_h + \sigma_v)\sin\phi = \sigma_h - \sigma_v$$
$$\sigma_h \sin\phi + \sigma_v \sin\phi = \sigma_h - \sigma_v$$
$$\sigma_h - \sigma_h \sin\phi = \sigma_v + \sigma_v \sin\phi$$
$$\sigma_h(1 - \sin\phi) = \sigma_v(1 + \sin\phi)$$

Therefore:
$$\sigma_h = \sigma_v \cdot \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = K_p \, \sigma_v$$

Hence proved:
$$K_p = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

### 3.1.6 Quick Sanity Check

For $\phi = 30°$:
- $\sin 30° = 0.5$
- $K_a = \dfrac{0.5}{1.5} = 0.333$ ✓
- $K_p = \dfrac{1.5}{0.5} = 3.0$ ✓
- Product: $K_a \cdot K_p = 1$ (a beautiful general identity: $K_a \cdot K_p = 1$)

---

## 3.2 Derivation for Cohesive Soil ($c$-$\phi$ Soil)

### 3.2.1 Setup with Cohesion

For a $c$-$\phi$ soil, the Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope has a $c$-intercept:
$$\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi$$

The Mohr circle tangency condition in the **active state** gives the geometric relation:
$$\sin\phi = \dfrac{\sigma_v - \sigma_h}{\sigma_v + \sigma_h + 2c\cot\phi}$$

### 3.2.2 Solving for $\sigma_h$ in Active State

Cross-multiplying:
$$(\sigma_v + \sigma_h + 2c\cot\phi)\sin\phi = \sigma_v - \sigma_h$$

Expanding:
$$\sigma_v \sin\phi + \sigma_h \sin\phi + 2c\cot\phi \sin\phi = \sigma_v - \sigma_h$$

Rearranging for $\sigma_h$:
$$\sigma_h(1 + \sin\phi) = \sigma_v(1 - \sin\phi) - 2c\cot\phi \sin\phi$$

Using the identity $\cot\phi \cdot \sin\phi = \cos\phi$ and noting that $\sqrt{K_a} = \dfrac{\cos\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$:

$$\sigma_h = K_a\,\sigma_v - 2c \cdot \dfrac{\cos\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = K_a\,\sigma_v - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$$

Therefore:
$$p_a = K_a\,\sigma_v - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$$

### 3.2.3 Derivation of Tension Crack Depth $z_c$

At the top of the wall ($z = 0$), $\sigma_v = 0$. Therefore:
$$p_a = -2c\sqrt{K_a}$$

The negative sign means the soil is in **tension** (it tries to pull away from the wall). The depth at which $p_a = 0$ defines the tension crack:

$$0 = K_a\,\gamma z_c - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$$
$$z_c = \dfrac{2c}{\gamma\sqrt{K_a}}$$

### 3.2.4 Passive Pressure for $c$-$\phi$ Soil (Statement Only)

By the symmetric derivation (with $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h$):
$$p_p = K_p\,\sigma_v + 2c\sqrt{K_p}$$

The cohesion **adds** to the passive resistance, making it even more robust.

---

## 3.3 Effect of Surcharge on Active Thrust

If a uniform surcharge $q$ ($\text{kN/m}^2$) acts on the ground surface behind the wall, it is equivalent to an additional layer of soil of height $h_s = q/\gamma$.

The total vertical stress at depth $z$ becomes:
$$\sigma_v = \gamma z + q$$

Hence the active pressure at depth $z$:
$$p_a = K_a(\gamma z + q) = K_a\,\gamma z + K_a q$$

The total active thrust is the sum of the triangular and rectangular components:
$$P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 + K_a q H$$

---

## 3.4 Effect of Water Table

If the water table is at depth $H_1$ below the ground surface:

**For $0 \le z \le H_1$ (Above WT):**
$$p_a = K_a \gamma_t z$$

**For $z > H_1$ (Below WT):**
$$p_a = K_a \gamma_t H_1 + K_a \gamma' (z - H_1) + \gamma_w (z - H_1)$$

The last term is the **hydrostatic pore-water pressure** that must be added separately and resisted by the wall.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 State Transition Diagram (Wall Movement)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Natural Soil Mass at Rest"] --> B["Retaining Wall Constructed"]
    B --> C{"Wall Movement Direction?"}
    C -- "No Movement" --> D["At-Rest State Ko"]
    C -- "Moves AWAY from Soil" --> E["Active State Ka"]
    C -- "Moves TOWARDS Soil" --> F["Passive State Kp"]
    D -- "Stress Magnitude" --> G["sigma_h = Ko sigma_v"]
    E -- "Stress Magnitude" --> H["sigma_h = Ka sigma_v MINIMUM"]
    F -- "Stress Magnitude" --> I["sigma_h = Kp sigma_v MAXIMUM"]
    E -- "Failure Plane Angle" --> J["45 + phi/2 from Horizontal"]
    F -- "Failure Plane Angle" --> K["45 - phi/2 from Horizontal"]
```

## 4.2 Mohr Circle — Active vs. Passive States

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["Mohr Circle Construction"] --> N1["Cohesionless Soil c=0"]
    M1 --> N2["Cohesive Soil c greater than 0"]
    N1 --> O1["Active State"]
    N1 --> O2["Passive State"]
    O1 --> P1["sigma_1 = sigma_v sigma_3 = sigma_a"]
    O2 --> P2["sigma_1 = sigma_p sigma_3 = sigma_v"]
    P1 --> Q1["Failure Envelope Tangent Above sigma_a"]
    P2 --> Q2["Failure Envelope Tangent Below sigma_p"]
    Q1 --> R1["Ka = 1 minus sin phi over 1 plus sin phi"]
    Q2 --> R2["Kp = 1 plus sin phi over 1 minus sin phi"]
```

## 4.3 Pressure Distribution Block Diagram (Cohesionless Backfill, Height H)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Top of Wall z equals 0"] --> S2["pa equals 0 at top"]
    S2 --> S3["Linear Increase with Depth"]
    S3 --> S4["pa equals Ka gamma H at base z equals H"]
    S4 --> S5["Total Active Thrust Pa equals 0.5 Ka gamma H squared"]
    S5 --> S6["Acts at H/3 from Base"]
    T1["Passive side Mirror Image"] --> T2["pp equals 0 at top"]
    T2 --> T3["pp equals Kp gamma H at base"]
    T3 --> T4["Pp equals 0.5 Kp gamma H squared at H/3 from Base"]
```

## 4.4 Cohesive Soil — Negative Pressure Zone

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U1["Top of Wall z equals 0"] --> U2["pa equals minus 2c sqrt Ka NEGATIVE Tension"]
    U2 --> U3["Tension Zone Depth z_c"]
    U3 --> U4["pa equals 0 at z equals z_c"]
    U4 --> U5["pa increases linearly below z_c"]
    U5 --> U6["At z equals H pa equals Ka gamma H minus 2c sqrt Ka"]
    V1["Design Implication"] --> V2["Ignore Tension Zone"]
    V2 --> V3["Use Effective Height H minus z_c for Net Active Thrust"]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between active and passive earth pressure. When does each develop in the field?**

**Model Answer:**

Active earth pressure is the **minimum** lateral pressure exerted by the soil when the retaining wall yields **away** from the backfill, allowing the soil mass to expand laterally and mobilize its shear strength along a failure plane. It is the **minor principal stress** condition: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_v$, $\sigma_3 = \sigma_h = K_a \sigma_v$.

Passive earth pressure is the **maximum** lateral pressure exerted by the soil when the wall is pushed **into** the backfill, compressing the soil mass. It is the **major principal stress** condition: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h = K_p \sigma_v$, $\sigma_3 = \sigma_v$.

**Field occurrences:**
- Active: gravity retaining walls, cantilever walls tilting slightly outward.
- Passive: the *front* face of bridge abutments, anchored bulkheads, deep foundations resisting lateral loads.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "active = loose soil" and "passive = dense soil." This is **wrong**. Both states occur in *any* soil, depending on wall movement, *not* on relative density. Density only affects the *magnitude* of $K_a$ and $K_p$.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: State the assumptions of Rankine's Earth Pressure Theory.**

**Model Answer:**

1. The soil mass is **homogeneous, isotropic, and semi-infinite**.
2. The ground surface is a **plane** (horizontal or inclined at a known angle).
3. The retaining wall is **vertical** and **smooth** (wall friction $\delta = 0$).
4. The soil is in a state of **plastic equilibrium** (every point at failure).
5. Failure occurs along a **plane surface** (Rankine's planar assumption).
6. Soil obeys the **Mohr-Coulomb** failure criterion $\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi$.
7. Soil is **dry** (or pore-water pressure is treated separately).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**A smooth vertical retaining wall, 8 m high, retains a dry cohesionless backfill with $\phi = 30°$ and $\gamma = 18\,\text{kN/m}^3$. The backfill is horizontal.**

**(a) [7 Marks] Compute the magnitude, direction, and point of application of the active earth pressure on the wall. Also determine the orientation of the failure plane.**

**(b) [7 Marks] If the backfill becomes submerged (water table rises to the top), determine the new active thrust and the additional hydrostatic thrust on the wall. Take $\gamma_{sat} = 20\,\text{kN/m}^3$.**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Compute $K_a$:**

$$K_a = \dfrac{1 - \sin 30°}{1 + \sin 30°} = \dfrac{0.5}{1.5} = \dfrac{1}{3} = 0.333$$

**Step 2 — Active pressure intensity at base ($z = H = 8$ m):**

$$p_a = K_a \gamma H = 0.333 \times 18 \times 8 = 48\,\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total Active Thrust (area of triangular distribution):**

$$P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.333 \times 18 \times 8^2 = 192\,\text{kN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Point of Application:**

The triangular distribution has its centroid at $H/3$ from the base:
$$z_{act} = \dfrac{H}{3} = \dfrac{8}{3} = 2.67\,\text{m above the base}$$

**Step 5 — Failure Plane Orientation:**

The Rankine active wedge slides along a plane inclined at:
$$\alpha = 45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2} = 45° + 15° = 60° \text{ with the horizontal}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Compute submerged unit weight:**

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 20 - 9.81 = 10.19\,\text{kN/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Effective active thrust from submerged soil:**

$$P_a' = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma' H^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.333 \times 10.19 \times 8^2 = 108.6\,\text{kN/m}$$

**Step 3 — Hydrostatic thrust (water acts on the back of wall from top to 8 m):**

$$P_w = \dfrac{1}{2} \gamma_w H^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 9.81 \times 8^2 = 313.92\,\text{kN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Total Lateral Thrust on Wall:**

$$P_{total} = P_a' + P_w = 108.6 + 313.92 = 422.52\,\text{kN/m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently:
> 1. Forget to use **submerged** unit weight $\gamma'$ below the water table and use $\gamma_{sat}$ instead — **deduct 2 marks**.
> 2. Compute $K_a$ with $\phi$ in **radians** instead of degrees — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 3. Forget to add the hydrostatic water thrust in submerged condition — **deduct 3 marks** (this is a major conceptual error).
> 4. State the point of application as $H/2$ instead of $H/3$ — **deduct 1 mark**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**A 6 m high smooth vertical wall retains a saturated cohesive backfill with $c = 20\,\text{kN/m}^2$, $\phi = 20°$, and $\gamma = 19\,\text{kN/m}^3$.**

**(a) [7 Marks] Determine the depth of the tension crack and the net active earth pressure on the wall, ignoring tension zone.**

**(b) [7 Marks] If a uniform surcharge of $30\,\text{kN/m}^2$ is applied on the backfill surface, find the new active thrust and check whether the tension crack depth changes.**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Compute $K_a$:**

$$K_a = \dfrac{1 - \sin 20°}{1 + \sin 20°} = \dfrac{1 - 0.342}{1 + 0.342} = \dfrac{0.658}{1.342} = 0.490$$

$$\sqrt{K_a} = 0.700$$

**Step 2 — Depth of Tension Crack:**

$$z_c = \dfrac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}} = \dfrac{2 \times 20}{19 \times 0.700} = \dfrac{40}{13.3} = 3.01\,\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Active pressure at base ($z = 6$ m):**

$$p_a = K_a \gamma H - 2c\sqrt{K_a} = 0.490 \times 19 \times 6 - 2 \times 20 \times 0.700$$
$$p_a = 55.86 - 28.0 = 27.86\,\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Effective height for net active thrust (ignoring tension zone):**

$$H_{eff} = H - z_c = 6 - 3.01 = 2.99\,\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Net Active Thrust:**

$$P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_{eff}^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.490 \times 19 \times (2.99)^2$$
$$P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.490 \times 19 \times 8.94 = 41.6\,\text{kN/m}$$

This thrust acts at $H_{eff}/3$ from the base, i.e., $2.99/3 = 1.0\,\text{m}$ from the base.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Equivalent surcharge soil height:**

$$h_s = \dfrac{q}{\gamma} = \dfrac{30}{19} = 1.58\,\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Modified pressure at base:**

$$p_a' = K_a (\gamma H + q) - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$$
$$p_a' = 0.490(19 \times 6 + 30) - 28.0$$
$$p_a' = 0.490(114 + 30) - 28.0 = 0.490 \times 144 - 28.0 = 70.56 - 28.0 = 42.56\,\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Check tension crack depth with surcharge:**

The tension crack depth depends on cohesion, $\gamma$, and $K_a$ — **none of which change with surcharge**. The tension zone is a property of the soil, not the loading.

$$z_c = 3.01\,\text{m} \text{ (unchanged)}$$

**Step 4 — Total Active Thrust (Triangular + Rectangular components):**

Triangular (from soil weight):
$$P_{a,1} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.490 \times 19 \times (6 - 3.01)^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.490 \times 19 \times 8.94 = 41.6\,\text{kN/m}$$

Rectangular (from surcharge, acts over full effective height):
$$P_{a,2} = K_a \cdot q \cdot H_{eff} = 0.490 \times 30 \times 2.99 = 43.96\,\text{kN/m}$$

**Total:**
$$P_a^{total} = 41.6 + 43.96 = 85.56\,\text{kN/m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Sub-question (b):**
> 1. Forgetting to compute $H_{eff}$ separately because the surcharge is **uniform** and acts over the full height above the tension crack — partial marking.
> 2. Students often *recalculate* $z_c$ with the surcharge in the formula — this is **conceptually wrong**. The tension crack is a function of $c$, $\phi$, and $\gamma$ only. The surcharge modifies the *active thrust magnitude* but not the tension zone depth.
> 3. Not showing the rectangular pressure distribution component ($K_a q$) acting uniformly above the tension crack zone — **deduct 2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Master Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSING ERRORS IN KTU EXAMS:**
> 
> | Mistake | Marks Lost | Fix |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Using $\phi$ in radians | 1 per occurrence | Always default to **degrees** in Rankine calculations |
> | Forgetting $\gamma_w$ thrust in submerged case | 2-3 | Submerged = $\gamma' \cdot$ for soil + separate water pressure |
> | Using $H$ instead of $H_{eff}$ for cohesive soil net thrust | 2 | Always subtract tension zone $z_c$ |
> | Plotting $p_a$ as zero at base | 2 | It is **maximum** at the base, zero at top |
> | Stating $H/2$ as point of application | 1 | For triangular distribution, it is $H/3$ from base |
> | Confusing principal stresses (active vs. passive) | 2-3 | Active: $\sigma_1$ is vertical; Passive: $\sigma_1$ is horizontal |

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Rankine's Earth Pressure Theory**
> 
> - **Three States:** At-Rest ($K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$), Active ($K_a$, minimum), Passive ($K_p$, maximum).
> - **Key Identity:** $K_a \cdot K_p = 1$ (for cohesionless soil).
> - **Critical Angles:**
>   - Active failure plane: $\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ with horizontal
>   - Passive failure plane: $\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ with horizontal
> - **For Cohesionless Soil:**
>   - $p_a = K_a \gamma z$ (linear, zero at top)
>   - $P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2$ (acts at $H/3$ from base)
> - **For Cohesive ($c$-$\phi$) Soil:**
>   - $p_a = K_a \gamma z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$ (may be negative near surface!)
>   - Tension crack depth: $z_c = \dfrac{2c}{\gamma\sqrt{K_a}}$
>   - **Net thrust** uses $H_{eff} = H - z_c$ (ignore tension zone)
> - **Surcharge Effect:** Equivalent to extra soil layer of height $h_s = q/\gamma$. Adds **rectangular** pressure $K_a q$ over full height.
> - **Water Table Effect:** Use $\gamma'$ (submerged) for effective stress below WT. Add separate hydrostatic thrust $\dfrac{1}{2} \gamma_w z^2$.
> - **Rankine's Theory is for:** Smooth vertical wall + horizontal backfill + dry soil (water treated separately). For rough walls or inclined backfill, use **Coulomb's Theory**.
> - **Order of Magnitude:** $K_p \gg K_a$ — passive resistance is always much greater than active thrust.
> - **Wall Movement Needed:** About $0.1\%$ to $0.5\%$ of $H$ for active state; $1\%$ to $5\%$ of $H$ for passive state (passive needs much more movement to mobilize).
> - **Units Convention:** $\gamma$ in $\text{kN/m}^3$, depth in m → pressure in $\text{kN/m}^2$, thrust in $\text{kN/m}$ (per meter run of wall).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earth Pressure and Point of Application for Cohesionless and Cohesive Soils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Earth Pressure and Point of Application for Cohesionless and Cohesive Soils

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Earth Pressure?

**Earth pressure** is the lateral (horizontal) pressure exerted by soil mass on a retaining structure such as a retaining wall, sheet pile, basement wall, or abutment. It is one of the most fundamental concepts in geotechnical engineering and forms the basis for the design of all earth-retaining structures.

In the vertical direction, soil is in equilibrium under its own weight (governed by unit weight **γ** in **kN/m³**). However, when a vertical cut is made (or a wall is constructed), the soil can no longer sustain its vertical equilibrium alone — a portion of the vertical stress is transferred into **lateral stress**, which acts on the wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBCET504 – Module 1):**
> *Earth pressure is the lateral pressure exerted by soil on a retaining structure. It depends on the type of soil (cohesionless or cohesive), the wall movement, the backfill conditions, and the shear strength parameters of the soil (c and φ).*

## 1.2 The Three Limiting States of Earth Pressure

The magnitude of earth pressure depends almost entirely on **how much the wall moves**. The wall movement governs whether the soil behind it mobilizes its full shear strength or only a part of it. There are three classical limiting states:

| State | Wall Movement | Pressure Magnitude | Soil Shear Mobilization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **At-Rest (K₀)** | No movement | Intermediate | 0 % |
| **Active (Kₐ)** | Wall moves *away* from backfill | **Minimum** | Fully mobilized (downward slip) |
| **Passive (Kₚ)** | Wall moves *towards* backfill | **Maximum** | Fully mobilized (upward slip) |

Where **K** is the **coefficient of earth pressure** (dimensionless).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bookshelf Push" Intuition

Imagine a row of books standing upright on a shelf, packed tightly together. If you do **nothing**, each book pushes its neighbor sideways with a moderate force — this is the **at-rest condition**.

Now, if you **slowly pull the front book outward** (away from the pack), the books behind it expand slightly and "relax" their push. The lateral force on the front book **drops** — this is the **active state** (the soil is actively pushing the wall outward with minimum force).

Conversely, if you **push the front book hard into the pack**, the books jam together and resist fiercely. The lateral force **rises dramatically** — this is the **passive state** (the wall is being pushed into the soil mass, and the soil resists with maximum force).

> [!NOTE]
> The same physics governs soil behavior. Active pressure requires only a tiny wall movement (about **0.1 % to 0.5 % of wall height H**), while passive pressure requires a much larger movement (**2 % to 5 % of H**) to fully mobilize.

## 1.4 Types of Soils Considered

- **Cohesionless Soils** (sands, gravels): c = 0, governed only by **internal friction angle φ**.
- **Cohesive Soils** (clays): c ≠ 0, governed by both **cohesion c (kPa)** and **friction angle φ**.

The mathematical treatment of earth pressure differs significantly for these two categories, which is the precise focus of this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rankine's Earth Pressure Theory (1857)

**William John Macquorn Rankine**, a Scottish civil engineer, proposed the simplest and most widely used earth pressure theory. It assumes:

1. The soil is **homogeneous, isotropic, and semi-infinite**.
2. The ground surface is a **plane** (horizontal or inclined).
3. The wall is **smooth, vertical, and frictionless** (no wall friction).
4. Failure occurs as the soil mass reaches a **plastic equilibrium** state.
5. The wall is sufficiently **rigid** but yields just enough to mobilize active/passive states.

### 2.1.1 Coefficient of Earth Pressure at Rest (K₀)

When the wall does not move at all (zero lateral strain), the ratio of horizontal to vertical effective stress is denoted **K₀**.

$$K_0 = \frac{\sigma'_h}{\sigma'_v} = 1 - \sin\phi$$

This empirical formula is known as **Jaky's Equation (1944)** and is the standard for normally consolidated cohesionless soils.

For overconsolidated soils:
$$K_0 = (1 - \sin\phi) \cdot OCR^{\sin\phi}$$

### 2.1.2 Active Earth Pressure Coefficient (Kₐ)

For a cohesionless soil, Rankine derived:

$$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

### 2.1.3 Passive Earth Pressure Coefficient (Kₚ)

$$K_p = \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reciprocal Property:** $K_a \cdot K_p = 1$. This is a fundamental check used in KTU numerical problems to verify your calculated coefficients.

## 2.2 Earth Pressure for Cohesionless Soils (c = 0)

### Vertical and Horizontal Effective Stresses at Depth z

$$\sigma_v = \gamma \cdot z$$

$$\sigma_h = K \cdot \gamma \cdot z$$

### Active Pressure at Depth z

$$\sigma_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z$$

This is **linear with depth** — zero at the surface, maximum at the base.

### Total Active Thrust (Force per unit length of wall)

$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H^2$$

Where:
- **H** = height of the wall (in **m**)
- **γ** = unit weight of soil (in **kN/m³**)
- **Kₐ** = active earth pressure coefficient (dimensionless)

### Passive Thrust

$$P_p = \frac{1}{2} \cdot K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot H^2$$

## 2.3 Earth Pressure for Cohesive Soils (c ≠ 0, φ ≠ 0)

When cohesion is present, the Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope no longer passes through the origin, and the active pressure at the top of the wall becomes **negative** (i.e., tension).

### Active Pressure at Depth z

$$\sigma_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$$

### Passive Pressure at Depth z

$$\sigma_p = K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot z + 2c\sqrt{K_p}$$

### Total Active Thrust

$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H^2 - 2c \cdot H \cdot \sqrt{K_a}$$

### Total Passive Thrust

$$P_p = \frac{1}{2} \cdot K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot H^2 + 2c \cdot H \cdot \sqrt{K_p}$$

### Depth of Tension Crack (z_t)

The negative active pressure near the surface implies a **tension zone** in the soil, which cannot actually exist. A tension crack of depth z_t develops:

$$z_t = \frac{2c}{\gamma \cdot \sqrt{K_a}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The tension crack is assumed to be **air-filled** (zero lateral pressure) for active analysis. For passive analysis, the tension crack is normally **filled with water**, increasing wall loading.

## 2.4 Point of Application of Earth Pressure (Critical for KTU!)

The **point of application** of the resultant thrust is the centroid of the pressure diagram.

| Soil Type | Pressure Diagram Shape | Point of Application (from base) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cohesionless** (active) | Triangle | **H/3** |
| **Cohesionless** (passive) | Triangle | **H/3** |
| **Cohesive** (active, full depth) | Trapezoid | $\dfrac{\sum P_i \cdot \bar{y}_i}{\sum P_i}$ (centroid) |
| **Cohesive** (with tension crack) | Triangle only (from z_t to H) | $\dfrac{H - z_t}{3}$ from base |

For a **cohesive soil with tension crack**, the active thrust acts at a height of:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{H - z_t}{3} \quad \text{from the base}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU mistake is to assume the thrust acts at H/3 for cohesive soils **without** considering the tension crack. Always use the depth of tension crack when computing the actual lever arm for **overturning and sliding stability checks**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the most important reference for KTU exams. Memorize every entry.**

| # | Parameter | Cohesionless Soil (c = 0) | Cohesive Soil (c, φ) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | At-rest coefficient | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ (approx) | – |
| 2 | Active coefficient | $K_a = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$ | Same expression | – |
| 3 | Passive coefficient | $K_p = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}$ | Same expression | – |
| 4 | Pressure at depth z (active) | $\sigma_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z$ | $\sigma_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$ | kPa |
| 5 | Pressure at depth z (passive) | $\sigma_p = K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot z$ | $\sigma_p = K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot z + 2c\sqrt{K_p}$ | kPa |
| 6 | Total active thrust | $P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2$ | $P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 - 2cH\sqrt{K_a}$ | kN/m |
| 7 | Total passive thrust | $P_p = \frac{1}{2} K_p \gamma H^2$ | $P_p = \frac{1}{2} K_p \gamma H^2 + 2cH\sqrt{K_p}$ | kN/m |
| 8 | Tension crack depth | Not applicable | $z_t = \dfrac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}}$ | m |
| 9 | Point of application (active) | $H/3$ from base | $(H - z_t)/3$ from base (with crack) | m |
| 10 | Point of application (passive) | $H/3$ from base | Centroid of pressure polygon | m |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

The theory of earth pressure is essential in the design of:

- **Retaining walls** (gravity, cantilever, counterfort)
- **Sheet pile bulkheads** in marine construction
- **Bridge abutments**
- **Basement walls** of high-rise buildings
- **Braced excavations** and deep cuts
- **Underground tunnels and culverts**

A conservative assumption of active pressure is used in **design of retaining walls**, while passive pressure is the **resisting force** in sliding and bearing capacity checks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Rankine's Active Earth Pressure Coefficient (Kₐ)

### Setup
Consider an element of soil at depth **z** below a horizontal ground surface, with vertical effective stress $\sigma'_v = \gamma z$.

The horizontal stress $\sigma'_h$ is the unknown. The element is in **active failure** when the wall has moved away sufficiently for the major principal stress to rotate such that the vertical stress is now the **major principal stress ($\sigma_1$)** and horizontal stress becomes the **minor principal stress ($\sigma_3$)**.

### Mohr Circle Construction

The Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion states that at failure:

$$\sin\phi = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3} \quad \text{(for c = 0 case)}$$

Rearranging for $\sigma_3$ in terms of $\sigma_1$:

$$\sigma_1 \cdot \sin\phi + \sigma_3 \cdot \sin\phi = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$$

$$\sigma_1 (1 - \sin\phi) = \sigma_3 (1 + \sin\phi)$$

$$\frac{\sigma_3}{\sigma_1} = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$$

Since in the active state, $\sigma_1 = \sigma_v = \gamma z$ and $\sigma_3 = \sigma_h = \sigma_a$:

$$K_a = \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_v} = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Step 1 establishes the Mohr-Coulomb failure line; Step 2 solves for the stress ratio at failure; Step 3 identifies which stress is major vs minor in the active state.

### 3.1.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Algebraic Expansion

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 \cdot \sin\phi + \sigma_3 \cdot \sin\phi &= \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \\
\sigma_1 - \sigma_1 \cdot \sin\phi &= \sigma_3 + \sigma_3 \cdot \sin\phi \\
\sigma_1 (1 - \sin\phi) &= \sigma_3 (1 + \sin\phi) \\
\frac{\sigma_3}{\sigma_1} &= \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The first line groups the sine terms; the second line isolates $\sigma_1$ on the left and $\sigma_3$ on the right; the third line factors out the common terms; the fourth line forms the ratio which defines $K_a$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Rankine's Passive Earth Pressure Coefficient (Kₚ)

In the passive state, the wall moves *into* the soil, so the **horizontal stress becomes the major principal stress** and the vertical stress is the minor principal stress.

That is: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h = \sigma_p$ and $\sigma_3 = \sigma_v = \gamma z$.

Applying the same Mohr-Coulomb equation:

$$\frac{\sigma_3}{\sigma_1} = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = K_a$$

Inverting to get the ratio of $\sigma_1$ to $\sigma_3$:

$$\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_3} = \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \frac{1}{K_a} = K_p$$

Therefore:

$$K_p = \tan^2\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

> **Conversion Logic:** In active state, we computed $\sigma_3/\sigma_1$. In passive state, the roles of major and minor principal stresses swap, so we invert the ratio. This inversion is why $K_p = 1/K_a$ — a key result for KTU problems.

## 3.3 Derivation of Total Active Thrust for Cohesionless Soil

The pressure varies linearly from 0 at the top to $K_a \cdot \gamma H$ at the base. The total force per unit length of wall is the **area of this triangular pressure diagram**.

$$P_a = \int_0^H \sigma_a(z) \, dz = \int_0^H K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z \, dz$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_a &= K_a \cdot \gamma \int_0^H z \, dz \\
&= K_a \cdot \gamma \left[\frac{z^2}{2}\right]_0^H \\
&= \frac{1}{2} K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H^2
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The integral of z from 0 to H equals H²/2 (standard calculus). The factor 1/2 is geometric — it is simply the area of a triangle with base H and height $K_a \gamma H$.

## 3.4 Derivation of Total Active Thrust for Cohesive Soil

The pressure at depth z is $\sigma_a(z) = K_a \gamma z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$.

$$P_a = \int_0^H \left(K_a \gamma z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}\right) dz$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_a &= K_a \gamma \int_0^H z \, dz - 2c\sqrt{K_a} \int_0^H dz \\
&= K_a \gamma \cdot \frac{H^2}{2} - 2c\sqrt{K_a} \cdot H \\
&= \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 - 2cH\sqrt{K_a}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The first term is identical to the cohesionless case. The second term subtracts a rectangular area of height $2c\sqrt{K_a}$ (negative pressure) over the depth H.

## 3.5 Derivation of Depth of Tension Crack (z_t)

Setting $\sigma_a = 0$ at the top of the active zone:

$$K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z_t - 2c\sqrt{K_a} = 0$$

$$K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z_t = 2c\sqrt{K_a}$$

$$z_t = \frac{2c\sqrt{K_a}}{K_a \cdot \gamma} = \frac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The negative pressure at small depths is physically impossible (soil cannot sustain tension over long periods). We assume the soil in this zone cracks open, and the depth where active pressure becomes zero defines the bottom of the tension crack.

## 3.6 Point of Application of Active Thrust for Cohesive Soil (with tension crack)

Below the tension crack (z > z_t), the pressure is **purely positive** and varies linearly from 0 (at z = z_t) to $K_a \gamma H - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$ (at z = H).

The pressure diagram is a **triangle** with:
- Base height = $H - z_t$
- Peak pressure at base = $\sigma_a(H) = K_a \gamma H - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$

The centroid of this triangle (the point of application of the resultant thrust) lies at one-third of the base from the **larger end** (the bottom of the wall):

$$\bar{y} = \frac{H - z_t}{3} \quad \text{(measured upward from the base)}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The centroid of a triangle from its base is H/3. Here, the "base" of the pressure triangle is the wall height (H − z_t), and the centroid lies one-third up from the wall base.

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A 6 m high retaining wall retains a cohesionless backfill with $\phi = 30°$ and $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. Determine the active earth pressure, total thrust, and point of application.

### Step 1: Calculate $K_a$

$$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin 30°}{1 + \sin 30°} = \frac{1 - 0.5}{1 + 0.5} = \frac{0.5}{1.5} = \frac{1}{3}$$

### Step 2: Active Pressure at Base (z = H = 6 m)

$$\sigma_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H = \frac{1}{3} \times 18 \times 6 = 36 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 3: Total Active Thrust

$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3} \times 18 \times 6^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3} \times 18 \times 36$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_a &= \frac{18 \times 36}{6} = \frac{648}{6} = 108 \text{ kN/m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Point of Application (from base)

$$\bar{y} = \frac{H}{3} = \frac{6}{3} = 2 \text{ m from the base}$$

**Result:** $P_a = 108$ kN/m, acting at 2 m above the base, horizontally.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Active Pressure Distribution Diagram (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **flow architecture of earth pressure development** along a retaining wall, from the at-rest state to the active and passive states. This is a high-fidelity block-level functional schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Soil Mass at Depth z] --> B{Determine Soil Type}
    B -->|c = 0| C[Cohesionless Soil Path]
    B -->|c not equal 0| D[Cohesive Soil Path]
    C --> E[Apply Rankine Theory]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Wall Movement Direction}
    F -->|No Movement| G[At-Rest State]
    F -->|Wall Moves Away| H[Active State]
    F -->|Wall Moves Towards| I[Passive State]
    G --> J[K-zero = 1 minus sin phi]
    H --> K[Ka from Rankine]
    I --> L[Kp = 1 divided by Ka]
    K --> M[Linear Pressure Diagram]
    L --> N[Linear Pressure Diagram]
    J --> O[Total Thrust Calculation]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    D --> P{Check Negative Pressure}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Develop Tension Crack]
    Q --> R[z-t = 2c divided by gamma sqrt Ka]
    R --> S[Modified Thrust from z-t to H]
    S --> T[Point of Application = H minus z-t divided by 3]
    O --> U[Design Check for Retaining Wall]
    T --> U
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Follow the path from soil mass to soil-type branching, then to wall-movement states, finally arriving at thrust calculation and design check. The cohesive-soil branch includes the special tension-crack sub-routine that modifies both the thrust magnitude and its point of application.

## 4.2 Pressure Distribution Profiles (Conceptual Schematics)

### Cohesionless Soil — Active State

The pressure distribution is a perfect **right triangle** with zero at the top and maximum at the base.

- Top of wall (z = 0): $\sigma_a = 0$ kPa
- Base of wall (z = H): $\sigma_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H$ kPa
- Resultant force: $P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2$ kN/m
- Point of application: H/3 from base

### Cohesive Soil — Active State (with tension crack)

The pressure distribution has **three distinct zones**:

- **Zone 1 (0 to z_t):** Theoretical negative pressure (tension) — replaced by tension crack with zero pressure.
- **Zone 2 (z_t to H):** Positive linear pressure from 0 at z_t to maximum at H.
- **Resultant force:** $P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 - 2cH\sqrt{K_a}$ (computed only over the active region, i.e., from z_t to H).
- **Point of application:** $\bar{y} = (H - z_t)/3$ from base.

### Cohesive Soil — Passive State

The pressure distribution is a **trapezoid** with a positive offset $2c\sqrt{K_p}$ at the top.

- Top of wall (z = 0): $\sigma_p = 2c\sqrt{K_p}$ kPa
- Base of wall (z = H): $\sigma_p = K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot H + 2c\sqrt{K_p}$ kPa
- Resultant force: $P_p = \frac{1}{2} K_p \gamma H^2 + 2cH\sqrt{K_p}$ kN/m
- Point of application: Centroid of the trapezoid, computed as $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum P_i \bar{y}_i}{\sum P_i}$.

## 4.3 Wall Movement vs Earth Pressure State — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wall Constructed] --> B[Wall at Rest]
    B --> C{Small Wall Movement}
    C -->|Away from Soil 0.1 percent H| D[Active Failure Wedge]
    C -->|Towards Soil 2 to 5 percent H| E[Passive Failure Wedge]
    D --> F[Active Thrust Pa minimum]
    E --> G[Passive Resistance Pp maximum]
    F --> H[Design Action: Use Pa for Wall Stability]
    G --> I[Design Action: Use Pp as Resisting Force]
```

> **Interpretation:** This schematic illustrates the **direction-dependent mobilization** of soil shear strength. Designers typically rely on active thrust (smaller, more economical) for sizing the wall and use passive resistance (larger) for the resisting side in stability checks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the terms "Active Earth Pressure" and "Passive Earth Pressure". How are they related to wall movement?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Active Earth Pressure (1 mark):** Active earth pressure is the *minimum* lateral pressure exerted by soil on a retaining wall when the wall moves *away* from the backfill, allowing the soil to expand laterally and mobilize its full shear strength.
- **Passive Earth Pressure (1 mark):** Passive earth pressure is the *maximum* lateral pressure exerted by soil on a retaining wall when the wall is pushed *into* the backfill, compressing the soil and mobilizing its full shear strength in the opposite direction.
- **Wall Movement Relation (1 mark):** Active state requires only about 0.1 %–0.5 % movement of wall height H; passive state requires about 2 %–5 % movement. The relationship $K_p = 1/K_a$ holds for cohesionless soils.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Write a short note on the "Depth of Tension Crack" in cohesive soils. Give the formula.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In cohesive soils, the active pressure equation $\sigma_a = K_a \gamma z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$ yields **negative** values near the ground surface. Since soil cannot sustain sustained tension, a **tension crack** develops in this region. The depth of this crack is given by:

$$z_t = \frac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}}$$

The crack is normally **air-filled** in active analysis (zero pressure assumed inside) and is located at the back of the wall. The presence of the tension crack **reduces the active thrust** and **raises the point of application** above H/3, increasing the overturning moment.

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. They are split into sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** (analysis/derivation) and **(b) 7 marks** (numerical/design).

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question based on KTU 2024 pattern]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the Rankine's active earth pressure coefficient $K_a$ for a cohesionless soil with horizontal backfill. State the assumptions clearly.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 7 m high retaining wall retains a **cohesive** backfill with the following properties:
- Unit weight $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³
- Cohesion $c = 18$ kPa
- Angle of internal friction $\phi = 20°$

Compute the **active earth pressure at the base**, **total active thrust per metre run**, **depth of tension crack**, and the **point of application** of the resultant thrust from the base.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of $K_a$ (7 marks)

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Soil is homogeneous, isotropic, and semi-infinite.
2. The wall is smooth, vertical, and the backfill surface is horizontal.
3. Soil is in a state of plastic equilibrium at failure.
4. Shear strength is governed by Mohr-Coulomb criterion.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a soil element at depth z with vertical stress $\sigma_v = \gamma z$. In the **active state**, the wall moves away from the soil, so vertical stress is the major principal stress and horizontal stress is the minor principal stress.

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_v = \gamma z, \quad \sigma_3 = \sigma_a$$

At failure (Mohr-Coulomb, c = 0):

$$\sin\phi = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin\phi &= \frac{\gamma z - \sigma_a}{\gamma z + \sigma_a} \\
\sin\phi \cdot (\gamma z + \sigma_a) &= \gamma z - \sigma_a \\
\gamma z \sin\phi + \sigma_a \sin\phi &= \gamma z - \sigma_a \\
\sigma_a (1 + \sin\phi) &= \gamma z (1 - \sin\phi) \\
\sigma_a &= \gamma z \cdot \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} \\
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_v} &= \tan^2\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = K_a
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $K_a = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$. (1 mark)

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute $K_a$ (1 mark)**

$$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin 20°}{1 + \sin 20°} = \frac{1 - 0.342}{1 + 0.342} = \frac{0.658}{1.342} = 0.490$$

**Step 2: Active Pressure at Base (z = 7 m) (2 marks)**

$$\sigma_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$$

$$\sigma_a = 0.490 \times 19 \times 7 - 2 \times 18 \times \sqrt{0.490}$$

$$\sigma_a = 65.17 - 2 \times 18 \times 0.7 = 65.17 - 25.20 = 39.97 \approx 40 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Total Active Thrust (2 marks)**

$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 - 2cH\sqrt{K_a}$$

$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.490 \times 19 \times 7^2 - 2 \times 18 \times 7 \times 0.7$$

$$P_a = 228.10 - 176.4 = 51.70 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 4: Depth of Tension Crack (1 mark)**

$$z_t = \frac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}} = \frac{2 \times 18}{19 \times 0.7} = \frac{36}{13.3} = 2.706 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Point of Application from Base (1 mark)**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{H - z_t}{3} = \frac{7 - 2.706}{3} = \frac{4.294}{3} = 1.43 \text{ m from the base}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question based on KTU 2024 pattern]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain Rankine's theory of earth pressure. Clearly state the assumptions and the differences between active and passive states.

**(b) [7 marks]** A retaining wall of height 8 m retains a cohesionless backfill with $\phi = 30°$ and $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³. If the water table rises to a depth of 3 m from the top, compute:
- Active and passive earth pressure coefficients.
- Total active thrust (considering submerged condition below water table with $\gamma_{sub} = 10$ kN/m³).
- Point of application of the resultant active thrust.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Rankine's theory (1857) gives the lateral earth pressure on a retaining wall assuming the soil is in a state of plastic equilibrium. The pressure depends on whether the wall yields (active) or is pushed into the soil (passive).

**Assumptions (3 marks):**
1. Soil is homogeneous, isotropic, and the backfill surface is plane.
2. The wall is smooth and vertical (no wall friction).
3. Failure surfaces are planar and soil mass behaves as a continuum.
4. Soil shear strength follows the Mohr-Coulomb criterion.
5. Wall yields sufficiently to mobilize either active or passive state.

**Active vs Passive Difference (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Active State | Passive State |
|---|---|---|
| Wall movement | Away from backfill | Towards backfill |
| Magnitude of pressure | **Minimum** | **Maximum** |
| Principal stresses | $\sigma_v = \sigma_1$, $\sigma_h = \sigma_3$ | $\sigma_h = \sigma_1$, $\sigma_v = \sigma_3$ |
| Coefficient | $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$ | $K_p = \tan^2(45° + \phi/2)$ |
| Typical movement | 0.1 %–0.5 % of H | 2 %–5 % of H |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute $K_a$ and $K_p$ (1 mark)**

$$K_a = \tan^2(45° - 15°) = \tan^2 30° = \frac{1}{3} = 0.333$$

$$K_p = \tan^2(45° + 15°) = \tan^2 60° = 3.0$$

**Step 2: Compute Pressures at Key Depths (3 marks)**

At z = 0 (top): $\sigma_a = 0$

At z = 3 m (water table, just above WT):
$$\sigma_a = K_a \cdot \gamma_{dry} \cdot 3 = 0.333 \times 17 \times 3 = 17.0 \text{ kPa}$$

At z = 3 m (just below WT, using submerged unit weight and including water pressure):
$$\sigma_a = K_a \cdot \gamma_{dry} \cdot 3 + K_a \cdot \gamma_{sub} \cdot 0 + 0 \text{ (water contributes separately)}$$

At z = 8 m (base):
$$\sigma_a = 0.333 \times 17 \times 3 + 0.333 \times 10 \times 5 = 17.0 + 16.65 = 33.65 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Total Active Thrust (2 marks)**

$$P_a = P_{a1} + P_{a2}$$

$$P_{a1} = \frac{1}{2} \times 17.0 \times 3 = 25.5 \text{ kN/m (upper triangle, dry zone)}$$

$$P_{a2} = \frac{1}{2} \times (17.0 + 33.65) \times 5 = \frac{1}{2} \times 50.65 \times 5 = 126.625 \text{ kN/m (trapezoid, submerged zone)}$$

$$\boxed{P_a = 25.5 + 126.625 = 152.125 \text{ kN/m}}$$

**Step 4: Point of Application (1 mark)**

For the upper triangle: $\bar{y}_1 = (8 - 3/3) = 8 - 1 = 7$ m from base (i.e., the centroid is 1 m below the top, which is 7 m above the base).

For the lower trapezoid: $\bar{y}_2$ = centroid of a trapezoid with parallel sides 17.0 and 33.65, height 5 m.

$$\bar{y}_2 = \frac{5}{3} \cdot \frac{2 \times 33.65 + 17.0}{33.65 + 17.0} = \frac{5}{3} \cdot \frac{84.3}{50.65} = \frac{5}{3} \cdot 1.664 = 2.77 \text{ m from base}$$

Combined point of application:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{P_{a1} \cdot \bar{y}_1 + P_{a2} \cdot \bar{y}_2}{P_{a1} + P_{a2}} = \frac{25.5 \times 7 + 126.625 \times 2.77}{152.125} = \frac{178.5 + 350.75}{152.125} = 3.48 \text{ m from base}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Earth Pressure Problems (KTU 2024):**
>
