- **Definition:** Layered backfill = stratified soil mass with two or more distinct layers (different γ, φ, c) behind a retaining wall.
- **Vertical stress** at depth z is the **cumulative** sum of layer weights: $\sigma_v(z) = \sum \gamma_i h_i$. It is **continuous** at interfaces.
- **Horizontal stress** at depth z: $\sigma_h(z) = K_{a,i} \cdot \sigma_v(z)$, where $K_{a,i}$ belongs to the **layer containing z**. Hence, $\sigma_h$ is **discontinuous** at interfaces.
- **Direction of jump:** If the lower layer has **higher φ** (denser, stiffer), $K_a$ drops, so $\sigma_h$ **drops** (jump is downward). If lower layer has **lower φ** (looser, weaker), $\sigma_h$ **rises** (jump is upward).
- **Active coefficient formula:** $K_a = (1 - \sin\phi)/(1 + \sin\phi) = \tan^2(45 - \phi/2)$. Always dimensionless.
- **Total active thrust:** Sum the areas of the polygonal pressure distribution. **Never** use $P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2$ for layered backfill.
- **Point of application:** Take moments about a convenient reference (typically the base) — not simply H/3.
- **Trapezoid area formula:** $A = \frac{1}{2}(\sigma_{top} + \sigma_{bot}) \times h$.
- **Trapezoid centroid from its top:** $\bar{z} = \frac{h}{3} \cdot \dfrac{2\sigma_{bot} + \sigma_{top}}{\sigma_{top} + \sigma_{bot}}$.
- **Units to remember:** γ in kN/m³, σ in kN/m², thrust per m run in kN/m, depth in m, angles in degrees.
- **Layer-wise thrust formula (general):** $P_{a,i} = K_{a,i} \cdot \left[\sigma_{v,\text{top}} \cdot h_i + \frac{1}{2} \gamma_i h_i^2\right]$, which is the area of the trapezoid in the i-th layer.
- **Limiting case check:** If all layers have identical γ and φ, the answer must reduce to the standard Rankine formula with resultant at H/3. Use this as a **sanity check** during the exam.
- **Always sketch** the pressure distribution diagram with σh values labelled at every interface — this is a guaranteed credit item in KTU valuation.
- **Real-world significance:** Layered backfill analysis is essential for designing walls against naturally deposited or improved stratified soils in highways, dams, and basement construction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

# Foundation Engineering – Module 1: Numerical Problems

This module covers **Subsoil Exploration** and the **numerical interpretation of field test data**. Numerical problems in KTU exams typically test a student's ability to:
- Apply corrections to raw field data.
- Use empirical correlations to estimate bearing capacity.
- Design the extent of site investigation (depth, number, and spacing of boreholes).

## 1.1 Core Technical Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Site Investigation (Subsoil Exploration):** The process of determining the surface and subsurface conditions of a proposed construction site to design safe and economical foundations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Penetration Test (SPT):** An in-situ dynamic penetration test where a standard split-spoon sampler is driven into the soil by dropping a 63.5 kg hammer from a height of 750 mm. The number of blows required to drive the sampler through **three 150 mm intervals** (the second and third intervals summed up to give the **SPT N-value**) is recorded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plate Load Test (PLT):** A field test conducted to determine the bearing capacity and settlement of soil by loading a rigid plate of standard size (typically 300 mm, 450 mm, 600 mm, or 750 mm square) incrementally and recording the corresponding settlements.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Soil Doctor" Intuition):**
Imagine you are a doctor and the ground beneath a building is your patient. You cannot cut open the earth easily, so you use tools:
- **SPT** is like giving the soil a "hammer reflex test" — counting the bounces tells you how dense or stiff it is.
- **Plate Load Test** is like asking the soil to "hold a weight" and observing how much it "sags" — directly mimicking a real footing.
- **Borehole depth & spacing** decisions are like deciding "how many X-rays and where" — you need enough information to diagnose but not so much that it's wasteful.

---

## 1.2 Scope of Numerical Problems in Module 1

The KTU Module 1 numerical problems generally fall into these families:

| S.No | Problem Family | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | SPT N-value correction | Corrected N-value (N<sub>corr</sub>) |
| 2 | Bearing capacity from SPT | q<sub>ult</sub> or q<sub>allow</sub> |
| 3 | Plate Load Test interpretation | Settlement of actual footing |
| 4 | Depth of exploration | D<sub>borehole</sub> |
| 5 | Number & spacing of boreholes | Layout of investigation |
| 6 | Effective stress & overburden calculations | σ'<sub>vo</sub> at test depth |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Unit weight of water, γ<sub>w</sub> = 9.81 kN/m³** (often taken as **10 kN/m³** in KTU problems for simplicity).
- **Standard hammer weight for SPT = 63.5 kg (140 lbs)**.
- **Free fall height for SPT = 750 mm (30 inches)**.
- **Reference overburden pressure, σ'<sub>vo,ref</sub> = 100 kN/m² (1 kg/cm² or 1 atm)**.
- **Standard plate sizes (PLT): 300 mm, 450 mm, 600 mm, 750 mm** (square plates).

> [!NOTE]
> Always check whether the KTU problem uses **γ<sub>w</sub> = 9.81** or **10 kN/m³** before substituting. Many model answers accept both within ±2% error.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Numerical Workflows

### Workflow A: SPT N-value Correction
Raw N-values from the field must be corrected for **overburden pressure** because the same dense sand at a greater depth appears stronger than at a shallow depth.

**Step 1:** Compute effective overburden pressure at test depth:
$$\sigma'_{vo} = \gamma' \cdot D$$
where *D* is depth of test below ground level and *γ'* is the effective unit weight (use *γ<sub>sat</sub>* − γ<sub>w</sub> below water table).

**Step 2:** Apply the correction factor:
$$C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{2000}{\sigma'_{vo}}\right)$$
or the older formula by **Peck, Hanson & Thornburn (1974)**:
$$C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{20}{\sigma'_{vo}/\text{ksf}}\right)$$

Or the commonly used **Liao-Whitman (1986)** formula:
$$C_N = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\sigma'_{vo}}}$$
(where σ'<sub>vo</sub> is in kN/m² × 10⁻² i.e. kg/cm²). The corrected N-value is:
$$N_{corr} = C_N \cdot N_{field}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** The relation $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}(2000/\sigma'_{vo})$ with σ'<sub>vo</sub> in **kN/m²** is most frequently expected. Ensure C<sub>N</sub> ≥ 1.0 in very loose/shallow cases (per some authors) and cap it at **2.0** for typical designs.

### Workflow B: Bearing Capacity from SPT
For **cohesionless soils**, KTU problems often use the **Meyerhof (1956) / Bowles correlation**:
$$q_{ult} = 30 \cdot N_{corr} \cdot \left(\frac{D_f + B}{B}\right) \quad \text{[kN/m², D}_f\text{ in m]}$$

For **cohesive soils** (Bowles, 1977):
$$q_{ult} = 53 \cdot N_{corr} \quad \text{[kN/m²]}$$

Allowable bearing pressure (using **F.S. = 3**):
$$q_{allow} = \frac{q_{ult}}{F.S.}$$

### Workflow C: Plate Load Test Settlement Extrapolation
Settlement of actual footing can be extrapolated from PLT using:
$$S_f = S_p \cdot \left(\frac{B_f}{B_p}\right)^n$$
where n = 2 (for clays) and n = 1.5 (for sands). Here B<sub>f</sub> = footing width, B<sub>p</sub> = plate width.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | σ'<sub>vo</sub> = γ' × D | Effective overburden at test depth | kN/m² |
| 2 | $C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}(2000/\sigma'_{vo})$ | Overburden correction factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $N_{corr} = C_N \cdot N$ | Corrected SPT N-value | dimensionless |
| 4 | $q_{ult} \text{ (sand)} = 30 \cdot N_{corr} \cdot ((D_f+B)/B)$ | Ultimate bearing capacity (sand) | kN/m² |
| 5 | $q_{ult} \text{ (clay)} = 53 \cdot N_{corr}$ | Ultimate bearing capacity (clay) | kN/m² |
| 6 | $q_{allow} = q_{ult}/F.S.$ | Allowable bearing capacity | kN/m² |
| 7 | $S_f = S_p \cdot (B_f/B_p)^n$ | Settlement extrapolation (n=2 clay, 1.5 sand) | mm |
| 8 | $D_b = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \cdot B$ for square; $D_b = 3 \text{ to } 4 \cdot B$ for rafts | Depth of borehole (KBWT rule) | m |
| 9 | Spacing = 30 m for multistory; 60–100 m for one-story | Borehole spacing | m |
| 10 | $\sigma'_{vo,ref} = 100 \text{ kN/m}^2$ | Reference overburden pressure | kN/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU valuation, **forgetting to convert units** (e.g., depth in mm vs m, kN/m² vs kg/cm²) is the single most common reason for full-mark deduction. Always write units in the final answer.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundation Design:** Pre-design SPT data drives the initial sizing of footings in routine buildings.
- **Liquefaction Assessment:** Corrected SPT N-values are used in the simplified Seed-Idriss method for evaluating seismic liquefaction potential.
- **Site Characterization Reports:** Numerical values from PLT and SPT are tabulated in bore logs which become legal documents for the project.
- **Ground Improvement:** Pre- and post-improvement SPT values are compared to measure the effectiveness of compaction, stone columns, or grouting.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Model Problem 1 — Full SPT Correction and Bearing Capacity

**Problem Statement:**
A Standard Penetration Test was conducted at a depth of 6.0 m below ground level in saturated sand. The recorded N-value was **N = 22**. The saturated unit weight of the sand is γ<sub>sat</sub> = 19.5 kN/m³. The water table is at ground level. A square footing of size **2.0 m × 2.0 m** is to be placed at a depth of **D<sub>f</sub> = 1.5 m** in the same soil. Compute:
1. The corrected SPT N-value.
2. The ultimate and allowable bearing capacity using Bowles' correlation. (Use F.S. = 3)

### Step 1: Compute Effective Overburden Pressure at Test Depth

The water table is at GL, so effective unit weight is:
$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 19.5 - 9.81 = 9.69 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

Therefore, at D = 6.0 m:
$$\sigma'_{vo} = \gamma' \cdot D = 9.69 \times 6.0$$

$$\sigma'_{vo} = 58.14 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> *Reasoning:* Below the water table, the buoyancy effect reduces the effective stress, so we always subtract γ<sub>w</sub> from γ<sub>sat</sub>.

### Step 2: Apply Overburden Correction Factor

$$C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{2000}{\sigma'_{vo}}\right)$$

$$C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{2000}{58.14}\right)$$

$$C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}(34.40)$$

$$C_N = 0.77 \times 1.5366$$

$$C_N = 1.183$$

> *Reasoning:* Since 58.14 < 100, C<sub>N</sub> > 1, meaning the soil is "amplified" because the reference stress is higher than the actual overburden at that depth.

### Step 3: Compute the Corrected N-value

$$N_{corr} = C_N \cdot N_{field} = 1.183 \times 22$$

$$N_{corr} = 26.03 \approx 26$$

> *Reasoning:* The corrected value reflects the in-situ "true" relative density consistent with standard overburden conditions.

### Step 4: Compute Ultimate Bearing Capacity (Bowles, Sand)

For sand:
$$q_{ult} = 30 \cdot N_{corr} \cdot \left(\frac{D_f + B}{B}\right)$$

$$q_{ult} = 30 \times 26 \times \left(\frac{1.5 + 2.0}{2.0}\right)$$

$$q_{ult} = 780 \times \left(\frac{3.5}{2.0}\right)$$

$$q_{ult} = 780 \times 1.75$$

$$q_{ult} = 1365 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 5: Compute Allowable Bearing Capacity

$$q_{allow} = \frac{q_{ult}}{F.S.} = \frac{1365}{3}$$

$$q_{allow} = 455 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> *Reasoning:* Final answer in kN/m² (or kPa) — never in kg/cm² unless explicitly asked.

---

## 3.2 Model Problem 2 — Plate Load Test Settlement Extrapolation

**Problem Statement:**
A Plate Load Test was conducted using a 600 mm × 600 mm plate. At a pressure intensity of 200 kN/m², the plate settled by **S<sub>p</sub> = 10 mm**. The test was carried out on a sandy soil at the level of the proposed foundation, which is **3.0 m × 3.0 m** square. Estimate the settlement of the actual footing at the same pressure intensity.

**Solution:**

For sand, settlement extrapolation index **n = 1.5**:
$$S_f = S_p \cdot \left(\frac{B_f}{B_p}\right)^n$$

$$S_f = 10 \times \left(\frac{3.0}{0.6}\right)^{1.5}$$

$$S_f = 10 \times (5)^{1.5}$$

$$S_f = 10 \times 11.18$$

$$S_f = 111.8 \text{ mm}$$

> *Reasoning:* The settlement increases non-linearly with footing size because stress influence zones are deeper for larger footings.

---

## 3.3 Model Problem 3 — Depth & Number of Boreholes

**Problem Statement:**
A proposed multi-storey building has a raft foundation of size **20 m × 25 m**. Determine:
1. The minimum depth of exploration.
2. The number of boreholes required.

**Solution:**

For a raft, depth of borehole (as per **ASCE / Terzaghi rule**):
$$D_b = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \times B_{min}$$
Take the controlling width B = 20 m:
$$D_b = 1.5 \times 20 = 30 \text{ m (lower bound)}$$
$$D_b = 2.0 \times 20 = 40 \text{ m (upper bound)}$$
$$\boxed{D_b = 30 \text{ to } 40 \text{ m}}$$

For multi-storey building borehole spacing:
$$\text{Spacing} \approx 30 \text{ m (multistory)}$$

Number of boreholes:
$$N = \frac{\text{Perimeter}}{30} = \frac{2(20+25)}{30} = \frac{90}{30} = 3 \text{ boreholes (minimum)}$$

In KTU standard practice, **5 boreholes** (1 at center + 4 at corners) is the typical answer for a raft of this size.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Tree

| Given Data | First Compute | Then Apply | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Depth D, γ<sub>sat</sub>, WT level | γ' = γ<sub>sat</sub> − γ<sub>w</sub> | σ'<sub>vo</sub> = γ' × D | Overburden pressure |
| σ'<sub>vo</sub>, N | C<sub>N</sub> from log formula | N<sub>corr</sub> = C<sub>N</sub>·N | Corrected N |
| N<sub>corr</sub>, soil type, B, D<sub>f</sub> | Pick Bowles formula | q<sub>ult</sub> | Bearing capacity |
| q<sub>ult</sub>, F.S. | q<sub>allow</sub> = q<sub>ult</sub>/F.S. | — | Safe bearing pressure |
| S<sub>p</sub>, B<sub>p</sub>, B<sub>f</sub>, soil type | n = 1.5 (sand) or 2 (clay) | S<sub>f</sub> = S<sub>p</sub>·(B<sub>f</sub>/B<sub>p</sub>)<sup>n</sup> | Footing settlement |
| B of footing, type | D<sub>b</sub> = 1.5B–2B (square) / 3B–4B (raft) | — | Borehole depth |
| Building footprint, storeys | Spacing 30 m (multistor.) or 60 m (1-stor.) | N = Perimeter/Spacing | Number of boreholes |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Numerical Problem Solving Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: KTU Module 1 Numerical Problem] --> B{Type of Field Test?}
    B -->|SPT| C[Compute Effective Overburden sigma_v prime]
    B -->|PLT| D[Record Plate Settlement Sp at given pressure]
    B -->|Site Plan| E[Determine Footing Type and Size B]
    C --> F[Apply CN 0.77 log base 10 of 2000 over sigma_v prime]
    F --> G[Compute N_corr CN times N]
    G --> H{Soil Type?}
    H -->|Sand| I[qult = 30 N_corr times Df plus B over B]
    H -->|Clay| J[qult = 53 N_corr]
    I --> K[qallow = qult over FS 3]
    J --> K
    D --> L[Apply Extrapolation Sf = Sp times Bf over Bp to the power n]
    L --> M{n Value?}
    M -->|Sand| N[n = 1.5]
    M -->|Clay| O[n = 2]
    E --> P[Compute Borehole Depth Db = 1.5B to 2B square 3B to 4B raft]
    P --> Q[Compute Number of Boreholes N = Perimeter over Spacing]
    K --> R[Final Answer with Units kN per m squared]
    N --> R
    O --> R
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — SPT Equipment and Penetration Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[63.5 kg Hammer] -->|Free Fall 750 mm| B[Anvil]
    B --> C[Drill Rod A Rod]
    C --> D[Split Spoon Sampler 50.8 mm OD 35 mm ID]
    D --> E[Soil penetrates 450 mm total in 3 intervals of 150 mm]
    E --> F[Count blows for last 300 mm]
    F --> G[Record as N raw value]
    G --> H[Apply Overburden Correction]
    H --> I[Corrected N used in Correlations]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Plate Load Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rigid Plate 300 to 750 mm] --> B[Hydraulic Jack]
    B --> C[Reaction Beam or Anchored Platform]
    A --> D[Dial Gauges 3 nos symmetrically placed]
    D --> E[Load applied in increments of 20 percent of estimated safe load]
    E --> F[Record settlement at each load stage]
    F --> G[Plot Load versus Settlement curve]
    G --> H{Sand or Clay?}
    H -->|Sand| I[Use n equals 1.5 for settlement extrapolation]
    H -->|Clay| J[Use n equals 2.0 for settlement extrapolation]
    I --> K[Compute Sf for actual footing]
    J --> K
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Borehole Layout for Investigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Building Footprint] --> B{Footing Type}
    B -->|Isolated Footing| C[Borehole Depth Db equals 1.5 B to 2.0 B]
    B -->|Raft Foundation| D[Borehole Depth Db equals 3 B to 4 B]
    C --> E[Spacing equals 30 m multistoried]
    D --> F[Spacing equals 30 m for raft]
    E --> G[Number of Boreholes equals Perimeter over Spacing plus center bore]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Plot Borehole Plan on Site Layout]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

**Q1. Define Standard Penetration Test. Why is the N-value corrected in design?**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **Standard Penetration Test (SPT)** is an in-situ dynamic penetration test in which a standard split-spoon sampler (50.8 mm OD, 35 mm ID) is driven by a **63.5 kg hammer** falling freely from a height of **750 mm**.
- The number of blows required to drive the sampler through **three successive 150 mm penetrations** is recorded; the N-value is the sum of blows for the **last two 150 mm intervals**.
- The N-value is corrected because the raw field N is influenced by the **effective overburden pressure** at the test depth. The same relative density gives a higher N at greater depth due to higher confinement. The corrected N-value, **N<sub>corr</sub> = C<sub>N</sub>·N**, standardizes the data to a reference overburden of **100 kN/m²**, allowing consistent empirical correlation with bearing capacity.

**Q2. List any three corrections applied to the field SPT N-value.**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Overburden Pressure Correction** — using factor C<sub>N</sub> = 0.77·log<sub>10</sub>(2000/σ'<sub>vo</sub>).
2. **Dilatancy Correction** — for fine saturated sands susceptible to negative pore pressure build-up, especially N > 15 in fine sand below WT.
3. **Borehole Diameter Correction** — C<sub>BD</sub> = 1.0 for 65–115 mm dia, 1.05 for 150 mm, 1.15 for 200 mm.

---

## Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 1]**

**(a)** A Standard Penetration Test conducted at a depth of 8 m in a saturated sand deposit (γ<sub>sat</sub> = 20 kN/m³) recorded an N-value of 18. The water table is at 2 m below ground level. A square footing of size 2.5 m × 2.5 m is to be placed at a depth of 1.5 m. Compute the corrected N-value and the ultimate bearing capacity using Bowles' correlation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Plate Load Test was conducted on a sandy soil using a 450 mm square plate. The observed settlement at a pressure of 250 kN/m² is 8 mm. Estimate the settlement of a 3.0 m square footing at the same pressure intensity. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Effective overburden at 8 m:**
The water table is at 2 m depth, so:
- From 0–2 m: dry/moist sand use γ<sub>1</sub>. The problem gives only γ<sub>sat</sub>; we assume γ' below WT = γ<sub>sat</sub> − γ<sub>w</sub> = 20 − 9.81 = 10.19 kN/m³. (Use γ<sub>w</sub> = 9.81 kN/m³)
- From 2–8 m: submerged zone (6 m thick) with γ' = 10.19 kN/m³.

For the first 2 m, we use the bulk unit weight ≈ 17 kN/m³ (typical assumed for moist sand). Let γ = 17 kN/m³ above WT.

$$\sigma'_{vo} = (17 \times 2) + (10.19 \times 6)$$
$$\sigma'_{vo} = 34 + 61.14 = 95.14 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Correction factor:**
$$C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{2000}{95.14}\right) = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}(21.02) = 0.77 \times 1.322 = 1.018$$

**Step 3 — Corrected N:**
$$N_{corr} = 1.018 \times 18 = 18.33 \approx 18$$

**Step 4 — Ultimate bearing capacity (Bowles, sand):**
$$q_{ult} = 30 \times N_{corr} \times \frac{D_f + B}{B}$$
$$q_{ult} = 30 \times 18 \times \frac{1.5 + 2.5}{2.5}$$
$$q_{ult} = 540 \times 1.6 = 864 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Allowable bearing capacity (F.S. = 3):**
$$q_{allow} = \frac{864}{3} = 288 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

#### Solution to (b):

For sand, **n = 1.5**:
$$S_f = S_p \cdot \left(\frac{B_f}{B_p}\right)^{1.5}$$
$$S_f = 8 \times \left(\frac{3.0}{0.45}\right)^{1.5}$$
$$S_f = 8 \times (6.667)^{1.5}$$
$$S_f = 8 \times 17.21 = 137.7 \text{ mm}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Module 1]**

**(a)** A raft foundation of size 25 m × 30 m is proposed for a multi-storey building. Determine (i) the minimum depth of exploration and (ii) the number of boreholes required. Mention the relevant IS code spacing recommendation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 600 mm square plate load test gave a settlement of 12 mm at a pressure of 300 kN/m² in a clayey soil. The proposed square footing is 4.0 m × 4.0 m. Estimate the settlement of the footing at the same pressure. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Depth of exploration for raft:**
Per ASCE / IS 1892 recommendation:
$$D_b = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \times B_{min} \text{ (square footing)}$$
$$D_b = 3 \text{ to } 4 \times B_{min} \text{ (raft)}$$

For raft with B = 25 m (smaller dimension):
$$D_b = 3 \times 25 = 75 \text{ m (lower bound)}$$
$$D_b = 4 \times 25 = 100 \text{ m (upper bound)}$$
$$\boxed{D_b = 75 \text{ to } 100 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 2 — Number of boreholes (IS 1892):**
- For multistoried buildings, spacing = 30 m.
- Number along perimeter:
$$N = \frac{2(25 + 30)}{30} = \frac{110}{30} = 3.67 \approx 4 \text{ boreholes}$$

Including **1 central borehole**, total = **5 boreholes**.

#### Solution to (b):

For clay, **n = 2**:
$$S_f = S_p \cdot \left(\frac{B_f}{B_p}\right)^{2}$$
$$S_f = 12 \times \left(\frac{4.0}{0.6}\right)^{2}$$
$$S_f = 12 \times (6.667)^{2}$$
$$S_f = 12 \times 44.44$$
$$S_f = 533.3 \text{ mm}$$

> *Note:* Such a high settlement is unusual and indicates either the PLT was on a very soft clay or that the test data needs to be reviewed. In KTU, the answer format is what matters.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Confusing kN/m² with kg/cm². Conversion: 1 kg/cm² ≈ 100 kN/m².
> 2. **Forgetting Submerged Unit Weight Below WT (–1 to –2 marks):** Always use γ' = γ<sub>sat</sub> − γ<sub>w</sub> below the water table.
> 3. **Using Wrong Bowles' Formula (–3 marks):** Memorize separate equations for sand (30 N) and clay (53 N) — they are NOT interchangeable.
> 4. **Skipping the CN Step (–2 marks):** Always show the CN calculation explicitly; examiners award step marks.
> 5. **Wrong n in PLT Extrapolation (–2 marks):** Sand n = 1.5, Clay n = 2. Mixing them up is a top mistake.
> 6. **Depth of Borehole Confusion (–1 mark):** For a raft, the multiplier is **3B–4B**, not 1.5B. This is the most often-forgotten rule.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SPT Hammer Specs:** 63.5 kg, 750 mm drop. N = blows for last 300 mm of 450 mm drive.
- **Reference Overburden Pressure:** σ'<sub>vo,ref</sub> = 100 kN/m² (always quote this when applying C<sub>N</sub>).
- **CN Formula (KTU-preferred):** $C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}(2000/\sigma'_{vo})$ with σ'<sub>vo</sub> in kN/m².
- **Corrected N:** $N_{corr} = C_N \times N_{field}$. Typical design range 10–50.
- **Bowles' Bearing Capacity:**
  - Sand → $q_{ult} = 30 N_{corr} (D_f + B)/B$
  - Clay → $q_{ult} = 53 N_{corr}$
- **Allowable Bearing Pressure:** Divide ultimate by F.S. = 3 (standard).
- **Plate Load Test Extrapolation:** $S_f = S_p (B_f/B_p)^n$, with n = 1.5 (sand) and n = 2 (clay).
- **Depth of Borehole Rule:** 1.5B–2B (isolated), 3B–4B (raft). "Wider foundation = deeper exploration."
- **Borehole Spacing:** 30 m (multistoried), 60–100 m (single-storey). Add 1 central borehole for rafts.
- **Submerged Unit Weight:** Always γ' = γ<sub>sat</sub> − γ<sub>w</sub> below water table, where γ<sub>w</sub> = 9.81 (or 10) kN/m³.
- **Square Footing of Size B:** Use B directly in Bowles' formula — no need to convert to equivalent circular.
- **Final Answer Units:** kN/m² (or kPa) for stress; m for depths; mm for settlement.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coulomb's Theory (Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Coulomb's Theory (Concept Only) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition **Coulomb's Earth Pressure Theory (1773)** is a classical limit-equilibrium method for determining the lateral thrust exerted by a cohesionless backfill on a retaining structure. It is a *wedge-method* formulation in which the soil mass bounded by the back of the wall, the backfill surface, and an *assumed planar* failure surface is treated as a free body in static equilibrium. The theory rigorously accounts for three real-world features neglected in simpler methods: (i) **wall friction (δ)** between the soil and the wall, (ii) **inclined back of the wall (α from vertical)**, and (iii) **inclined backfill surface (β from horizontal)**. The theory yields two principal design coefficients — the **Active Earth Pressure Coefficient (Kₐ)** and the **Passive Earth Pressure Coefficient (Kₚ)** — which when multiplied by **½ γ H²** give the resultant earth thrust on the wall. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is marked *"Concept Only"*. The examiner expects a clear understanding of the *wedge equilibrium concept*, the *critical failure plane concept*, the **assumptions**, and the *role of wall friction*. Numerical derivations of Kₐ and Kₚ must be known as a formula, not as a long board-work derivation. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Book-and-Shelf" Intuition Imagine a heavy hardcover book resting against a slightly tilted wooden shelf. The book exerts a horizontal push on the shelf, but the shelf does not feel the full weight of the book — it only feels a *component*. Moreover, the rough surface of the wood exerts a *frictional resistance* along the contact face, so the actual lateral force is less than what a perfectly smooth, frictionless surface would experience. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Summary:** Soil behind a retaining wall behaves exactly like this book. When the wall is rigid and the backfill is granular (sand-like), a *triangular wedge* of soil tries to slide down along an inclined plane. The weight of this wedge, the friction along the wall, the friction along the sliding plane, and the geometry of the wall and backfill together decide how much horizontal push the wall must resist. Coulomb's theory is essentially a *force-balance solution* for this sliding wedge. ### Standardised KTU Notation Table | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range (Cohesionless Soil) | |:------:|:---------|:----------------------------------| | **γ** | Unit weight of backfill | **17 – 20 kN/m³** | | **φ** | Angle of internal friction | **28° – 40°** | | **δ** | Angle of wall friction (mobilised) | **0 to ⅔ φ** | | **α** | Inclination of wall back with vertical | **0° – 20°** | | **β** | Inclination of backfill with horizontal | **0° – ⅓ φ** | | **H** | Height of wall | **3 – 10 m (typical)** | | **ψ** | Angle of trial failure plane with horizontal | **Variable** | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Coulomb's theory is the *workhorse* behind the design of gravity retaining walls, sheet pile bulkheads, bridge abutments, and basement walls where rough wall–soil interface and sloping backfills are routine. Modern software (e.g., PLAXIS, GEO5) still uses Coulomb's closed-form Kₐ/Kₚ as a *benchmark check* against numerical FEA results. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Six Pillars — Assumptions of Coulomb's Theory The theory is built on a specific idealisation of the backfill. Each assumption must be **understood, not memorised**, because every limitation of the method is rooted in one of these assumptions. 1. **Homogeneous, isotropic, dry, cohesionless backfill** — soil has the same γ and φ everywhere; no c (or c = 0) is assumed. 2. **Failure surface is a plane** — the actual curved log-spiral/sliding surface is approximated by a straight line, which is reasonable when φ is moderate and wall friction is present, but introduces error at large δ. 3. **The sliding wedge is a rigid body** — internal strains within the wedge are ignored; only the *boundary* forces matter. 4. **The wall yields sufficiently** to mobilise the limiting active or passive state (i.e., Kₐ or Kₚ). 5. **Wall friction (δ) is fully mobilised** along the entire wall–soil contact at the moment of failure. 6. **Backfill surface is planar** (slope β) and the back of the wall is planar (inclination α from vertical). ### 2.2 The Logic of the Wedge Method — Step by Step For the *active* state (wall moves *away* from backfill): > **Step 1 — Geometry:** Imagine a trial failure plane rising from the *heel* (base) of the wall to the backfill surface at angle **ψ** with the horizontal. The wedge has three boundaries: (a) the wall back, (b) the failure plane, (c) the backfill slope. > **Step 2 — Three Forces on the Wedge:** The wedge is acted upon by: > - Its **self-weight W** (acts vertically downward through the centroid). > - The **reaction R** from the soil below the failure plane (acts at angle φ to the *normal* of the failure plane, i.e., inclined at angle φ from ψ). > - The **wall reaction Pₐ** (acts at angle δ to the *normal* of the wall back, i.e., inclined from the wall-normal). > **Step 3 — Force Polygon Closure:** Since the wedge is in static equilibrium, the three forces must form a *closed triangle*. This gives a unique value of Pₐ for the chosen ψ. > **Step 4 — Critical Plane (Maxima/Minima Principle):** The *true* failure plane is the one that makes Pₐ *maximum* for active state (because the soil will fail along the plane that *most threatens* the wall) and *minimum* for passive state. Mathematically: > > $$\frac{dP_a}{d\psi} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = \psi_{cr}$$ > **Step 5 — Substitute back to get Kₐ.** The result of the optimisation yields the closed-form Coulomb expression. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Print-and-Carry) | # | Expression | Meaning / KTU Use | |:-:|:-----------|:------------------| | 1 | $P_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\gamma\,H^2\,K_a$ | Active thrust on wall (kN/m) | | 2 | $P_p = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\gamma\,H^2\,K_p$ | Passive thrust on wall (kN/m) | | 3 | $K_a = \dfrac{\cos^2(\phi - \alpha)}{\cos^2\alpha \cdot \cos(\alpha + \delta)\left[1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{\sin(\phi+\delta)\sin(\phi-\beta)}{\cos(\alpha+\delta)\cos(\alpha-\beta)}}\right]^2}$ | **General Coulomb Kₐ** | | 4 | $K_p = \dfrac{\cos^2(\phi + \alpha)}{\cos^2\alpha \cdot \cos(\alpha - \delta)\left[1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{\sin(\phi+\delta)\sin(\phi+\beta)}{\cos(\alpha-\delta)\cos(\alpha+\beta)}}\right]^2}$ | **General Coulomb Kₚ** | | 5 | $K_a = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45 - \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | Reduced form (α=0, β=0, δ=0) — equals Rankine | | 6 | $K_p = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45 + \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | Reduced form for passive (α=0, β=0, δ=0) | | 7 | $W = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 \dfrac{\cos(\alpha - \beta)\sin(\psi - \alpha)}{\cos^2\alpha \sin(\psi - \beta)}$ | Weight of trial wedge (active) | | 8 | $P_a = \dfrac{W \sin(\psi - \phi)}{\sin(\psi - \phi - \alpha - \delta)}$ | Trial-wedge thrust expression | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on sign conventions used in KTU textbooks:** Angle **α** is taken *positive* when the wall back leans *into* the backfill (i.e., toward the soil) at the top. Angle **β** is positive when backfill rises away from the wall. Some books use the opposite sign; the *form* of the equation above is the standard textbook form and what KTU expects. ### 2.4 Active vs Passive — The Physical Picture | State | Wall Movement | Failure Plane | Magnitude | Critical Principle | |:------|:--------------|:--------------|:----------|:-------------------| | **Active (Kₐ)** | Wall moves **away** from soil | Wedge slides *down & out* | **Smaller** (≤ ⅓ γH²) | Pₐ is **maximum** → governs design | | **Passive (Kₚ)** | Wall moves **toward** soil | Wedge is pushed **up** | **Larger** (≥ γH²) | Pₚ is **minimum** → governs design | ### 2.5 Coulomb vs Rankine — Examiner's Quick-Reference | Feature | Coulomb (1773) | Rankine (1857) | |:--------|:--------------|:---------------| | Failure surface | **Plane** | **Plane** | | Wall friction δ | **Considered** | **Not considered** | | Wall back inclination | **Allowed (α)** | **Vertical only** | | Backfill slope | **Allowed (β)** | **Horizontal only** | | Surface inclination of failure plane | **Variable (ψ_cr)** | **45° + φ/2** (fixed) | | Result for Kₐ (smooth, level) | Reduces to Rankine | Original | | Suitability for c–φ soil | **Difficult** (c = 0 assumed) | **Possible** | | **Limitation** | Kₚ overestimates at large δ | Less realistic geometry | ### 2.6 Why Coulomb's Kₐ is *Less* Than Rankine's Kₐ Because wall friction δ *delays* the mobilisation of the active state, the soil wedge sits a little more "self-supporting." Numerically: $$K_{a,\,\text{Coulomb}}(\delta > 0) \;\le\; K_{a,\,\text{Rankine}} \;=\; \tan^2\!\left(45 - \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$ > This is *why* using Coulomb gives a more economical and realistic design when the wall is rough. ### 2.7 Engineering Utility - **Design of gravity and cantilever retaining walls** in highway and railway projects. - **Sheet pile anchor systems** — the passive resistance of soil in front of the pile is computed using Kₚ. - **Bridge abutments** with sloped backfill behind. - **Basement and diaphragm walls** in urban excavations. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Concept-Only Mandate:** Per KTU 2024 directive, the emphasis is on the *conceptual derivation* — the equilibrium of the trial wedge — not the closed-form algebraic reduction of Kₐ. We therefore derive Pₐ(ψ) for a general wedge and state (without redoing the full calculus) that maximisation yields the general Kₐ formula already tabulated above. ### 3.1 Geometry of the Trial Active Wedge Consider a retaining wall of height **H** whose back is inclined at **α** to the vertical and which retains a cohesionless backfill of slope **β** to the horizontal. A trial planar failure surface rises from the heel of the wall at an angle **ψ** (measured from horizontal) and meets the backfill slope at a point. The wedge is bounded by three lines: - **Line AB** — along the wall back, of length $L_{AB} = \dfrac{H}{\cos\alpha}$. - **Line BC** — along the failure plane, of length $L_{BC}$ (variable with ψ). - **Line AC** — along the backfill surface, of length $L_{AC}$ (variable with ψ). The interior angle of the triangle at vertex **A** (between wall and backfill) is: $$\theta_A = 90^\circ - \alpha + \beta$$ The interior angle at **B** (between wall and failure plane) is: $$\theta_B = 90^\circ - \alpha + \psi$$ The interior angle at **C** (between failure plane and backfill) is: $$\theta_C = \psi - \beta$$ Check: $\theta_A + \theta_B + \theta_C = (90^\circ - \alpha + \beta) + (90^\circ - \alpha + \psi) + (\psi - \beta)$... > Note: There are several sign conventions in textbooks; KTU typically uses the form below, where the formula is taken as a *standard identity* and need not be re-derived on the board. ### 3.2 Weight of the Trial Wedge Using the sine-area formula for the triangle ABC: $$W \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\gamma \cdot L_{AB} \cdot L_{BC} \cdot \sin\theta_B$$ Substituting $L_{AB} = H/\cos\alpha$ and using the sine rule to eliminate $L_{BC}$: $$L_{BC} \;=\; \frac{L_{AB}\sin\theta_A}{\sin\theta_C} \;=\; \frac{H \sin(90^\circ - \alpha + \beta)}{\cos\alpha \sin(\psi - \beta)} \;=\; \frac{H \cos(\alpha - \beta)}{\cos\alpha \sin(\psi - \beta)}$$ Hence the weight is: $$W \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\gamma \cdot \frac{H}{\cos\alpha} \cdot \frac{H\cos(\alpha - \beta)}{\cos\alpha \sin(\psi - \beta)} \cdot \sin(90^\circ - \alpha + \psi)$$ Simplifying with $\sin(90^\circ - \alpha + \psi) = \cos(\alpha - \psi) = \sin(\psi - \alpha)$ and using $\cos(\alpha - \beta) = \cos(\beta - \alpha)$ (since cosine is even): $$\boxed{\,W \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\gamma\,H^2 \cdot \frac{\cos(\alpha - \beta)\sin(\psi - \alpha)}{\cos^2\alpha \sin(\psi - \beta)}\,}$$ > **Textual interpretation:** As ψ increases from α to (90°+φ), the weight first *increases* (wedge becomes larger) and then *decreases* (wedge slides further out). The maximum weight is *not* the critical condition — the critical condition is the *maximum thrust*, which is the *opposite* of weight behaviour for active case. ### 3.3 Force Polygon — Equilibrium of the Wedge Three forces act on the wedge ABC, all assumed to meet at a common point for moment equilibrium to be trivially satisfied (this is the **culmann's simplification**): 1. **Self-weight W** — vertical, downward. 2. **Reaction R** along failure plane — inclined at angle **φ** to the normal of plane BC; equivalently, R is inclined at angle **ψ − φ** to the *horizontal* (since the normal to BC is horizontal + 90°). 3. **Wall reaction Pₐ** — acting on the wedge from the wall, inclined at angle **δ** to the normal of the wall back; in the chosen sign convention, Pₐ is inclined at angle **α + δ** to the horizontal. Resolving horizontally and vertically (or, more elegantly, using the **Lami's theorem** on the closed force triangle): $$\frac{P_a}{\sin(\psi - \phi)} \;=\; \frac{W}{\sin(\psi - \phi - \alpha - \delta)} \;=\; \frac{R}{\sin(90^\circ + \alpha + \delta)}$$ The middle equality gives the **trial-wedge thrust expression**: $$\boxed{\,P_a(\psi) \;=\; \frac{W \sin(\psi - \phi)}{\sin(\psi - \phi - \alpha - \delta)}\,}$$ ### 3.4 Critical Failure Plane — Maxima Principle The true failure plane is the one for which Pₐ is **maximum** (because the soil will release along the plane that *most threatens* the wall, and the wall must resist this maximum): $$\frac{dP_a(\psi)}{d\psi} \;=\; 0$$ Carrying out the differentiation (treating α, β, δ, φ as constants) on the composite function of W(ψ) and the sine ratio, after substantial trigonometric simplification, the condition reduces to: $$\tan(\psi_{cr} - \beta) \;=\; \tan\!\left[45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2} - \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\delta}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2} + \text{combined term}\right]$$ > [!NOTE] > The full algebraic reduction is lengthy (≈ 15 lines of trig identities) and is **not** required at the board for a "Concept Only" topic. KTU examiners will accept the *form* of the result and the substitution of ψ_cr back into the Pₐ expression. What *is* required is the ability to **state the principle** and write the final Kₐ. ### 3.5 Final Closed-Form Result Substituting ψ_cr back into the Pₐ(ψ) expression and simplifying using the identity: $$\sin(\psi_{cr} - \alpha) = \frac{\cos(\alpha - \beta)}{\cos\alpha} \cdot \frac{\cos(\alpha + \delta)}{\cos(\psi_{cr} - \beta)} \cdot \text{(sine-of-sum reduction)}$$ yields the celebrated **Coulomb Kₐ**: $$K_a \;=\; \frac{\cos^2(\phi - \alpha)}{\cos^2\alpha \cdot \cos(\alpha + \delta) \left[1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{\sin(\phi + \delta)\sin(\phi - \beta)}{\cos(\alpha + \delta)\cos(\alpha - \beta)}}\right]^2}$$ > **Sanity checks (students must verify):** > - α = 0, β = 0, δ = 0 → $K_a = \tan^2(45 - \phi/2)$ — Rankine's result. ✅ > - α = 0, β = 0, δ > 0 → $K_a$ *decreases* — wall friction reduces active thrust. ✅ > - φ = β (backfill at angle of repose) → $\sin(\phi - \beta) = 0$ → $K_a = \dfrac{\cos^2(\phi-\alpha)}{\cos^2\alpha\cos(\alpha+\delta)}$ — the wedge degenerates to the backfill surface itself. ✅ ### 3.6 Passive Case — Brief Derivation Sketch For the **passive state**, the wall pushes into the soil. The same wedge analysis applies with **three sign changes**: | Active Case | Passive Case | |:------------|:-------------| | Failure plane inclines *up & away* from wall | Failure plane inclines *down & into* the wall | | Wedge slides *down* | Wedge is pushed *up* | | Reaction R is *below* the normal of failure plane | Reaction R is *above* the normal | | $P_a$ is the **maximum** | $P_p$ is the **minimum** | | Sign of **δ** in force polygon: opposite | Sign of **δ**: same | The Kₚ expression emerges as: $$K_p \;=\; \frac{\cos^2(\phi + \alpha)}{\cos^2\alpha \cdot \cos(\alpha - \delta) \left[1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{\sin(\phi + \delta)\sin(\phi + \beta)}{\cos(\alpha - \delta)\cos(\alpha + \beta)}}\right]^2}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Critical Limitation:** Coulomb's Kₚ is *unreliable* for δ > φ/3. The error grows rapidly because the *actual* passive failure surface is strongly *curved* (log-spiral), not planar. KTU expects this limitation to be quoted in the answer. ### 3.7 Worked Example — KTU Board Style (14-Mark Style) **Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2023 style):** A 6 m high retaining wall has a vertical back (α = 0), retains a horizontal cohesionless backfill (β = 0), and has a wall friction angle δ = 20°. The soil has γ = 18 kN/m³ and φ = 30°. Compute the active earth thrust using Coulomb's theory and locate its point of application. **Solution:** Given: H = 6 m, α = 0, β = 0, δ = 20°, φ = 30°, γ = 18 kN/m³. **Step 1 — Compute trigonometric terms.** $$\phi + \delta = 50^\circ \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sin(\phi+\delta) = \sin 50^\circ = 0.766$$ $$\phi - \beta = 30^\circ \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sin(\phi-\beta) = \sin 30^\circ = 0.500$$ $$\alpha + \delta = 20^\circ \quad\Rightarrow\quad \cos(\alpha+\delta) = \cos 20^\circ = 0.940$$ $$\alpha - \beta = 0^\circ \quad\Rightarrow\quad \cos(\alpha-\beta) = 1.000$$ $$\phi - \alpha = 30^\circ \quad\Rightarrow\quad \cos^2(\phi-\alpha) = 0.750$$ $$\cos^2\alpha = 1.000$$ **Step 2 — Compute the square-root term.** $$\sqrt{\frac{\sin(\phi+\delta)\sin(\phi-\beta)}{\cos(\alpha+\delta)\cos(\alpha-\beta)}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.766 \times 0.500}{0.940 \times 1.000}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.4074} \;=\; 0.6383$$ **Step 3 — Compute the bracket term.** $$1 + 0.6383 \;=\; 1.6383 \quad\Rightarrow\quad (1.6383)^2 \;=\; 2.6840$$ **Step 4 — Compute Kₐ.** $$K_a \;=\; \frac{0.750}{1.000 \times 0.940 \times 2.6840} \;=\; \frac{0.750}{2.5230} \;=\; 0.2973$$ **Step 5 — Compute the active thrust.** $$P_a \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\gamma\,H^2\,K_a \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \times 18 \times 36 \times 0.2973 \;=\; 96.33\;\text{kN/m}$$ **Step 6 — Point of application.** For a triangular pressure distribution (Coulomb, cohesionless, planar failure), the resultant Pₐ acts at **H/3 from the base** (i.e., 2 m above the base for H = 6 m). > **Comparison with Rankine:** With δ = 0, we get $K_a = \tan^2(45 - 15) = \tan^2 30 = 0.3333$, giving Pₐ = 108 kN/m. **Coulomb gives ~11 % less** — the saving from including wall friction. **Answer:** **Pₐ ≈ 96.3 kN/m, acting at 2 m from the base, inclined at δ = 20° to the wall normal (i.e., 20° below the horizontal).** ## 4.
