The critical comparison is therefore: $$D_f \;\; \text{vs.} \;\; B$$ ### Step 3 — Ratio Formulation By definition: $$\text{Shallow} : D_f \leq B \implies \frac{D_f}{B} \leq 1$$ $$\text{Deep} : D_f \gg B \implies \frac{D_f}{B} > 4$$ ### Step 4 — Mathematical Statement of Load Transfer Mechanism For a **shallow foundation**, the total ultimate load capacity $Q_u$ is derived primarily from **end bearing** at the base: $$Q_{u,shallow} = A \cdot q_u = A \cdot \left( c N_c + \gamma D_f N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma \right)$$ For a **deep foundation** (e.g., a pile), the total ultimate load capacity is the **sum of end bearing and skin friction**: $$Q_{u,deep} = Q_{b} + Q_{s}$$ The **end bearing component** at the pile tip: $$Q_{b} = A_{b} \cdot q_{b} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot q_{b}$$ The **skin friction component** along the pile shaft: $$Q_{s} = \pi D L \cdot f_{s}$$ Therefore, the **total pile capacity** becomes: $$Q_{u,pile} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot q_{b} + \pi D L \cdot f_{s}$$ ### Step 5 — Why the Two Are Fundamentally Different | Aspect | Shallow Foundation | Deep Foundation | |---|---|---| | Primary load transfer | End bearing only | End bearing + skin friction | | Critical depth of influence | $2B$ to $3B$ | $D_f + L$ (full length) | | Governing IS code | IS 6403, IS 8009 | IS 2911 (Part 1 to 4) | | Typical construction | Open excavation | Drilling / driving / caisson sinking | ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Foundation Type | Soil Condition at Shallow Depth | Foundation Decision | |---|---| | Hard rock at $D_f \leq 1.5$ m | Spread/Shallow footing on rock | | Dense sand or stiff clay at $D_f \leq 3$ m | Shallow footing (Isolated/Combined) | | Soft clay within $3$ m, denser soil at $5$–$10$ m | Pile foundation | | Very soft soil extending $> 20$ m | Piled raft or deep caisson | | Expansive clay (black cotton soil) | Under-reamed pile (deep) | | High water table with loose sand | Deep pile with dewatering | ## 3.3 Worked Example — Classifying a Given Foundation > **Problem:** A square footing of size $3 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m}$ is founded at a depth of $2.5 \text{ m}$. Classify it as shallow or deep. **Solution:** $$\frac{D_f}{B} = \frac{2.5 \text{ m}}{3.0 \text{ m}} = 0.833$$ Since $\dfrac{D_f}{B} = 0.833 \leq 1$, the foundation is classified as a **Shallow Foundation**. ✓ **Note:** The actual depth is also $2.5 \text{ m} < 4 \text{ m}$, confirming the classification as shallow. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Classification of Foundations ```mermaid flowchart TD A["FOUNDATION SYSTEM"] --> B["SHALLOW FOUNDATION"] A --> C["DEEP FOUNDATION"] B --> B1["ISOLATED / SPREAD FOOTING"] B --> B2["COMBINED FOOTING"] B --> B3["STRAP / CANTILEVER FOOTING"] B --> B4["MAT / RAFT FOUNDATION"] C --> C1["PILE FOUNDATION"] C --> C2["PIER FOUNDATION"] C --> C3["CAISSON FOUNDATION"] C --> C4["WELL FOUNDATION"] C1 --> C1A["END BEARING PILE"] C1 --> C1B["FRICTION PILE"] C1 --> C1C["COMBINED / FRICTION CUM BEARING PILE"] C3 --> C3A["OPEN CAISSON"] C3 --> C3B["PNEUMATIC CAISSON"] C3 --> C3C["BOX CAISSON"] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style B fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style C fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style B1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#000,color:#000 style B2 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#000,color:#000 style B3 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#000,color:#000 style B4 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C1 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C2 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C3 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C4 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C1A fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C1B fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C1C fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C3A fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C3B fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C3C fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Load Transfer Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["SUPERSTRUCTURE LOADS
Dead Load + Live Load + Wind + Seismic"] --> A2["FOUNDATION ELEMENT
Shallow or Deep"] A2 --> A3["SOIL / ROCK MASS
Bearing Stratum"] A2 --> P1["FUNCTION 1
Load Transfer"] A2 --> P2["FUNCTION 2
Load Distribution"] A2 --> P3["FUNCTION 3
Settlement Control"] A2 --> P4["FUNCTION 4
Stability Against Lateral Forces"] A2 --> P5["FUNCTION 5
Protection Against Scour and Frost"] A3 --> R1["END BEARING
Resistance at Base"] A3 --> R2["SKIN FRICTION
Resistance along Shaft"] A3 --> R3["SUBGRADE REACTION
Resistance from Soil Springs"] P1 --> A3 P2 --> A3 P3 --> A3 P4 --> A3 P5 --> A3 style A1 fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style A2 fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style A3 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style P1 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#000,color:#000 style P2 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#000,color:#000 style P3 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#000,color:#000 style P4 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#000,color:#000 style P5 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Shallow vs Deep Foundation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SHALLOW["SHALLOW FOUNDATION"] S1["Ground Surface"] --> S2["Foundation Depth Df less than or equal to B"] S2 --> S3["End Bearing from Soil at Base"] S3 --> S4["Failure Zone extends 2B to 3B below base"] end subgraph DEEP["DEEP FOUNDATION"] D1["Ground Surface"] --> D2["Foundation Depth Df much greater than B"] D2 --> D3["End Bearing at Tip + Skin Friction along Shaft"] D3 --> D4["Load carried to deep competent stratum"] end style S1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S2 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S3 fill:#90caf9,stroke:#000,color:#000 style S4 fill:#64b5f6,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D1 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D2 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D3 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D4 fill:#e57373,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any six functions of a foundation.** **Model Answer:** The primary functions of a foundation are: 1. **Load Transfer** — Transmits superstructure loads (axial, lateral, moment) safely to the supporting soil or rock. 2. **Load Distribution** — Spreads concentrated loads over a wider base area to reduce bearing pressure below the Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC). 3. **Settlement Control** — Limits total and differential settlement within tolerable limits as per IS 1904. 4. **Stability Against Forces** — Resists uplift, overturning, sliding, and lateral forces due to wind and earthquakes. 5. **Protection of Subsoil** — Provides adequate embedment against frost heave, scour, seepage, and chemical attack. 6. **Seismic Anchorage** — Anchors the structure into the ground so it behaves monolithically under seismic events (IS 1893). --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between shallow foundation and deep foundation. (Any six points)** **Model Answer:** | Sl. No. | Shallow Foundation | Deep Foundation | |---|---|---| | 1 | Depth $D_f \leq B$, i.e., $D_f/B \leq 1$ | Depth $D_f \gg B$, i.e., $D_f/B > 4$ | | 2 | Generally $D_f \leq 3$ to $4$ m | Generally $D_f > 6$ m | | 3 | Load transfer by end bearing only | Load transfer by end bearing + skin friction | | 4 | Constructed by open excavation | Constructed by driving, drilling, or caisson sinking | | 5 | Economical for low to medium loads | Used for heavy loads, weak soil, or lateral forces | | 6 | Examples: spread, combined, strap, mat | Examples: pile, pier, caisson, well | | 7 | Governed by IS 6403, IS 8009 | Governed by IS 2911 (Parts 1 to 4) | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU allows the student to answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Both are given below as separate complete model solutions. ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Define a foundation. Explain in detail the various functions that a foundation must perform. (7 Marks) **(b)** What is a shallow foundation? With a neat sketch, describe the different types of shallow foundations and the situations in which each is adopted. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Definition and Functions of Foundation** A **foundation** is the structural element that interfaces the superstructure with the supporting soil or rock. Its role is to receive all superimposed loads from the structure above and transmit them to the ground in a manner that satisfies both **bearing capacity** and **settlement** requirements. The detailed functions of a foundation are: * **Load Transfer (Primary Function):** The foundation receives axial loads ($P$), shear loads ($V$), and bending moments ($M$) from columns/walls and transmits them to the soil. The transfer mechanism must ensure no localized yielding occurs at any point. * **Load Distribution:** The base area $A$ of the foundation is designed such that the induced bearing pressure $q = P/A$ does not exceed the safe bearing capacity $q_s$ of the soil. By increasing $A$, the pressure is reduced to acceptable levels. * **Settlement Control:** Total settlement $S_t$ and differential settlement $\Delta S$ are limited to: * Total settlement $\leq 40$ mm (for isolated footings on sand) * Differential settlement $\leq 0.002 L$ (angular distortion limit per IS 1904) * **Resistance to Lateral and Uplift Forces:** The foundation must resist sliding, overturning, and uplift caused by wind, seismic, or hydrostatic forces. Embedment depth $D_f$ provides passive earth pressure and side resistance. * **Protection Against Adverse Conditions:** Adequate $D_f$ protects the foundation from frost penetration (depth of frost line), scour in riverbeds, and chemical deterioration due to sulfates/chlorides in soil. * **Anchorage and Stability:** A well-designed foundation anchors the structure into the ground, providing rotational stability and ensuring the structure behaves as a unit under dynamic loading. --- **Part (b) — Shallow Foundations: Definition, Types, and Situations** A **shallow foundation** is a foundation whose depth $D_f$ is less than or equal to its width $B$, i.e., $\dfrac{D_f}{B} \leq 1$. It is also called an **open foundation** because construction involves open excavation. **Types of Shallow Foundations:** 1. **Isolated / Spread Footing:** Provided for a single column. Used when columns are far apart and soil has good SBC. The base is square, rectangular, or circular in plan. 2. **Combined Footing:** Supports two or more columns when they are close together and individual footings would overlap. Cast as a single reinforced concrete slab. 3. **Strap (Cantilever) Footing:** Two isolated footings connected by a rigid beam (strap) when one column is on a property line and a combined footing is not feasible. 4. **Mat (Raft) Foundation:** A single large footing covering the entire building footprint. Used when: * SBC of soil is very low (less than 50 kN/m²) * Column loads are heavy and close together * Soil is heterogeneous and may cause differential settlement * Basement construction is required **Selection Situation Summary:** | Foundation Type | Situation | |---|---| | Isolated footing | Single column, good soil, $D_f \leq 2$ m | | Combined footing | Two adjacent columns, edge restriction | | Strap footing | Column at property line | | Mat footing | Low SBC, heavy loads, basement needed | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** What is a deep foundation? List the different types of deep foundations and explain pile foundation in detail. (7 Marks) **(b)** Discuss the classification of foundations based on depth-to-width ratio. State the criteria as per IS 1080 and IS 2911. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Deep Foundations and Pile Foundation** A **deep foundation** is a foundation whose depth $D_f$ is much greater than its width $B$, i.e., $\dfrac{D_f}{B} > 4$. The load is transferred to deep, competent strata by **end bearing** at the tip and **skin friction** along the shaft. **Types of Deep Foundations:** * **Pile Foundation** — A slender column inserted into the ground to transfer loads through weak soil to a firm stratum. * **Pier Foundation** — A large-diameter (typically $> 0.75$ m) cast-in-situ concrete column with or without steel casing. * **Caisson Foundation** — A hollow box or cylinder sunk into the ground and filled with concrete; used for very deep foundations under water. * **Well Foundation** — A type of caisson used in bridge piers in rivers with open wells. **Detailed Explanation of Pile Foundation:** A pile is a slender structural member made of **timber, concrete, steel, or composite material** that is driven, drilled, or jacked into the ground. It is the most commonly used deep foundation. **Classification of Piles:** 1. **Based on Load Transfer Mechanism:** * **End-Bearing Pile:** Transfers entire load to a firm stratum at the tip. Suitable when hard rock or dense sand exists at reasonable depth. * **Friction Pile:** Transfers load entirely through skin friction along the shaft. Used when no firm layer exists at the tip. * **Combined / Friction-Cum-Bearing Pile:** Transfers load by both mechanisms — the most common in practice. 2. **Based on Installation Method:** * **Driven Piles** — Precast, hammered into the ground using a pile driver. * **Bored / Cast-in-Situ Piles** — Drilled and filled with concrete (e.g., under-reamed piles). * **Screw / Helical Piles** — Rotated into the ground. * **Jacked Piles** — Pushed into the ground using hydraulic jacks. 3. **Based on Material:** Timber piles, concrete piles (reinforced/precast), steel piles (H-piles, pipe piles), composite piles. **Load Capacity Equation:** $$Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s} = A_{b} \cdot q_{b} + \pi D L \cdot f_{s}$$ The **Safe Load** is obtained by applying a factor of safety (typically **2.5 to 3.0**) on the ultimate load: $$Q_{safe} = \frac{Q_u}{FS}$$ **Pile Group Efficiency:** When piles are used in a group under a pile cap, the group efficiency $\eta_g$ is: $$\eta_g = \frac{Q_{g,ult}}{n \cdot Q_{u,single}}$$ where $n$ is the number of piles. The minimum pile spacing is **$2.5D$ to $3D$** center-to-center as per IS 2911. --- **Part (b) — Classification Based on Depth-to-Width Ratio** The classification of foundations is governed by the **ratio of depth of foundation ($D_f$) to width ($B$)**. This ratio determines the mode of load transfer, failure mechanism, and design procedure. **As per IS 1080 (Shallow Foundation Code):** A foundation is classified as **shallow** if: $$\frac{D_f}{B} \leq 1$$ **As per IS 2911 (Deep Foundation Code):** A foundation is classified as **deep** if: $$\frac{D_f}{B} > 4$$ **The Intermediate Zone ($1 < D_f/B \leq 4$):** In this transitional range, the foundation exhibits both end-bearing and skin-friction behavior. Such foundations are conservatively designed as **deep foundations** following IS 2911 guidelines. **Theoretical Basis of the Ratio:** The failure zone beneath a shallow footing under general shear failure extends to approximately **$2B$ to $3B$** below the base. If the depth $D_f$ is less than $B$, the failure zone intersects the ground surface, producing a heave — a hallmark of shallow foundation behavior. If $D_f > 4B$, the failure zone is fully contained within the soil mass, and the foundation behaves as a deep foundation transferring load through both end bearing and skin friction. **Summary Table:** | Foundation Type | $D_f/B$ Ratio | Typical $D_f$ | Load Transfer | |---|---|---|---| | Shallow | $\leq 1$ | 1 m to 3 m | End bearing only | | Transitional | 1 to 4 | 3 m to 6 m | End bearing + partial skin friction | | Deep | $> 4$ | $> 6$ m | End bearing + full skin friction | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Wrong Ratio:** Many students write "shallow if $D_f < 2$ m" — this is **incorrect**. The defining parameter is the **$D_f/B$ ratio**, not the absolute depth. > 2. **Confusing Pier and Pile:** A **pile** has $D \leq 0.75$ m, while a **pier** has $D > 0.75$ m. This is a common viva question and a 2-mark trap. > 3. **Load Transfer Mechanism:** Students often forget **skin friction** for deep foundations and write "end bearing only" — this is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU valuation. > 4. **Forgetting IS Codes:** Always quote **IS 6403, IS 1080, IS 2911, IS 1904** in your answer. Examiners reward IS code awareness. > 5. **Units:** Mixing m and mm in the same calculation is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** * **Foundation:** Substructure that transfers loads from superstructure to soil/rock safely. * **Seven Functions:** Load transfer, distribution, settlement control, stability, protection, anchorage, seismic safety. * **Shallow Foundation Criterion:** $D_f/B \leq 1$ (IS 1080), typical $D_f \leq 3$ m. * **Deep Foundation Criterion:** $D_f/B > 4$ (IS 2911), typical $D_f > 6$ m. * **Shallow Types:** Isolated, Combined, Strap, Mat/Raft. * **Deep Types:** Pile, Pier, Caisson, Well. * **Pile Sub-Types:** End-bearing, Friction, Combined; Driven, Bored, Screw, Jacked. * **Pier vs Pile:** Pier $D > 0.75$ m, Pile $D \leq 0.75$ m. * **Ultimate Pile Capacity:** $Q_u = Q_b + Q_s = A_b q_b + \pi D L f_s$. * **Factor of Safety for Piles:** 2.5 to 3.0. * **Minimum Pile Spacing:** $2.5D$ to $3D$ center-to-center (IS 2911). * **Key IS Codes:** IS 6403 (Bearing Capacity), IS 8009 (Settlement), IS 2911 (Pile), IS 1904 (Foundation Settlement), IS 1893 (Seismic), IS 1080 (Shallow Foundation). * **Governing Failure Modes:** Bearing capacity failure (shear) and excessive settlement (serviceability) — both must be checked. * **Selection Rule:** Choose shallow first; use deep only when shallow is **inadequate, uneconomical, or infeasible**.

topics.title: Site Investigation and Soil Exploration: Objectives, Planning, Reconnaissance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Site Investigation and Soil Exploration: Objectives, Planning, Reconnaissance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Site Investigation & Soil Exploration?

**Site Investigation** is the comprehensive process of gathering, analyzing, and documenting geotechnical and geological information about a proposed construction site to ensure safe, economical, and serviceable foundation design. **Soil Exploration** (a sub-component of site investigation) is the physical act of collecting subsurface soil/rock samples and conducting in-situ tests to determine the engineering properties of the ground.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this topic covers the **preliminary stages** of any geotechnical project — specifically the *objectives*, the *planning methodology*, and the *reconnaissance phase* (the desk study and walk-over survey that happens *before* a single borehole is drilled).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Recall-Ready):**
> "Site investigation is a planned programme of subsurface exploration, field testing, and laboratory investigation carried out to obtain the necessary information regarding the physical properties and characteristics of the subsurface materials at the proposed construction site, including the position of the groundwater table."

## 1.2 The Big Picture — Why Do We Even Investigate the Ground?

Before a single brick is laid, we must answer five fundamental questions:
1. **What** is the soil profile (layering) at the proposed site?
2. **How strong** is each soil layer (bearing capacity)?
3. **How much will it settle** under the proposed structural load?
4. **Where is the groundwater**, and will it cause problems?
5. **Are there any hidden hazards** (caves, fill, contamination, faults)?

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The "Medical Check-up" Approach

Imagine a doctor examining a patient before prescribing medicine. The doctor doesn't just look at the patient and prescribe drugs immediately. Instead, the doctor:
- **Asks questions** about the patient's history (this is **Reconnaissance / Desk Study**).
- **Performs a physical examination** by checking pulse, blood pressure (this is **Preliminary Reconnaissance Walk-Over**).
- **Orders blood tests and X-rays** to see inside the body (this is **Detailed Soil Exploration — Boreholes, SPT, etc.**).
- **Diagnoses and prescribes** based on the results (this is **Foundation Design**).

In our civil engineering context:
- **Patient** = Proposed construction site
- **Doctor** = Geotechnical Engineer
- **Blood test** = Borehole + SPT
- **X-ray** = Plate Load Test, Seismic Survey
- **Prescription** = Safe and economical foundation design

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Students — "PRS" (Planning → Reconnaissance → Subsurface):**
> Every KTU board question on this topic essentially maps to one of these three phases. Memorize the sequence: it is the most frequently asked conceptual flow.

## 1.4 Three Phases of Site Investigation (KTU Standard Classification)

| Phase | Name | Output |
|-------|------|--------|
| **Phase I** | **Reconnaissance / Desk Study** | Geological maps, past records, aerial photos |
| **Phase II** | **Preliminary Exploration** | Broad picture of soil profile, SPT at few points |
| **Phase III** | **Detailed Exploration** | Complete data for final design — boreholes, lab tests |

---

## 1.5 Objectives of Site Investigation (High-Yield for KTU)

The KTU board examiner expects students to remember the objectives in **two clusters**: *Primary* and *Secondary*.

**A. Primary Objectives:**
1. To determine the **type, sequence, and thickness** of subsurface soil/rock strata.
2. To evaluate the **engineering properties** of each stratum (shear strength, compressibility, permeability, density).
3. To determine the **depth and fluctuation of the groundwater table**.
4. To assess the **bearing capacity** and **expected settlement** of the proposed foundation.
5. To identify any **geological hazards** (faults, caverns, soft pockets, expansive soils, contamination).

**B. Secondary Objectives:**
6. To recommend the **most suitable and economical type of foundation** (shallow vs. deep).
7. To anticipate **construction difficulties** (dewatering, shoring, hard rock excavation).
8. To provide data for **slope stability analysis**, earth pressure, and seismic site classification.
9. To establish **baseline data** for later comparison (long-term monitoring of settlement).
10. To satisfy **statutory and codal requirements** (IS 1892, IS 2132, IS 2131, IS 4968).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If the question is "List the objectives of site investigation" (a 3-mark or 7-mark question), always start with the **Primary 5** in order. The secondary objectives fetch the extra marks and demonstrate depth.

---

## 1.6 Why is Reconnaissance Called the "Cheapest Insurance"?

Among all the phases, reconnaissance is the **least expensive yet most informative** stage. Experienced geotechnical engineers often claim that **70% of the answers about a site can be obtained from a proper desk study and walk-over survey — at less than 5% of the total investigation cost**. A flawed reconnaissance leads to misplaced boreholes, missed hazards, and catastrophic design errors (e.g., the Leaning Tower of Pisa is the classic counter-example of inadequate site investigation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Planning of Site Investigation — The Operational Blueprint

Planning is the **intellectual phase** that occurs *between* receiving the project brief and *mobilizing* the drilling rig. A poor plan wastes money; a good plan saves the project.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Planning Logic

**Step 1 — Define the Project**
- What is being built? (Building, bridge, dam, tunnel, road, tower)
- What are the **column loads** and **depth of foundation** likely to be?
- What is the **footprint area** of the structure?

**Step 2 — Collect Existing Data (Desk Study)**
- Topographic maps, geological survey maps, hydrological data.
- Aerial photographs and satellite imagery (Google Earth, Bhuvan, ISRO).
- Records of **adjacent structures** (their performance, settlement history).
- Local well data → gives a hint of groundwater depth.

**Step 3 — Decide the Scope of Investigation**
- For a small residential building → 2–3 boreholes of 6–8 m depth.
- For a multi-storey tower → 4–6 boreholes of 15–30 m depth.
- For a dam → dozens of boreholes along the alignment, some extending to bedrock.

**Step 4 — Select Methods of Investigation**
- Boreholes + SPT (most common)
- Trial pits (for shallow foundations)
- Geophysical methods (for large or inaccessible sites)
- In-situ tests (CPT, VST, PLT) where soil sampling is difficult

**Step 5 — Allocate Budget & Timeline**
- Typical cost: **0.1% to 1% of the total project cost** for normal structures.
- For complex projects (offshore, nuclear, tall towers): may go up to **2–5%**.

**Step 6 — Prepare the Field Schedule**
- Sequence of boreholes, access logistics, utility clearance, traffic management.

## 2.2 Reconnaissance — Detailed Operational Breakdown

Reconnaissance is divided into **two complementary sub-phases**:

### 2.2.1 A. Desk Study (Office-Based Reconnaissance)

| Source | Information Obtained |
|--------|----------------------|
| **Topographic maps** (Survey of India) | Surface features, slope, drainage patterns |
| **Geological maps** (GSI) | Rock type, faults, formation boundaries |
| **Aerial photographs / Satellite images** | Past land use, water bodies, landslides |
| **Soil survey reports** (NBSS&LUP) | Broad soil classification of the region |
| **Local enquiry** | Flood history, water table, old wells |
| **Records of adjacent structures** | Their foundation type, performance, settlement |

### 2.2.2 B. Walk-Over Survey (Field-Based Reconnaissance)

The engineer physically walks the site and notes:
- **Topography** — flat, sloping, undulating, terraced.
- **Drainage** — natural drainage paths, signs of waterlogging.
- **Vegetation** — type and density (often indicates soil moisture & type).
- **Erosion features** — gullies, landslides, exposed rock.
- **Existing structures** — cracks, tilts, signs of distress.
- **Access constraints** — for rigs, trucks, and equipment.
- **Utilities** — buried cables, pipelines, overhead lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Codal Reference (IS 1892:1979, Reaffirmed):**
> Clause 4.1 of IS 1892 mandates that **"a study of geological and other relevant maps of the area shall be made prior to the detailed programme of subsurface exploration."** This is the KTU-favoured codal hook for any reconnaissance question.

## 2.3 Factors Affecting the Planning Process

1. **Type and size of the project** (a bungalow vs. a skyscraper).
2. **Uniformity of the soil** (homogeneous alluvium needs fewer boreholes; variable fill needs more).
3. **Cost of land** — if the site is in a congested urban area, exploration must be precise.
4. **Accessibility** — hilly, forested, or marshy sites need special methods.
5. **Local building codes and statutory requirements**.
6. **Time available** — emergency repairs may rely on indirect methods.
7. **Anticipated foundation depth** — deeper foundations need deeper borings.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every formula a KTU student may need to write for this topic (or its close cousin — depth & spacing of borings, which examiners often combine).

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Boundary Conditions / Units | Common Use in KTU |
|---|---------------------|-------------|------------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $D_e \geq \dfrac{B}{\cos(\alpha+45^\circ)}$ | Depth of exploration below ground for strip footing (Terzaghi) | $D_e$ = min. depth; $B$ = footing width; $\alpha$ = footing inclination | 7-mark derivation problems |
| 2 | $D_e \geq 1.5\,B$ to $2.0\,B$ for square footings | Simplified depth criterion (Terzaghi & Peck) | $B$ = footing width in **metres** | Quick numerical problems |
| 3 | $D_e = S\,B^{0.7}$ | Skempton's depth factor for clays | $S$ = settlement in mm; $B$ = footing width in m | Where settlement is critical |
| 4 | $N_{spacing} = \dfrac{A_{plot}}{A_{grid\,cell}}$ | Number of boreholes from plot area | Plot divided into regular grids | Numerical questions |
| 5 | $S_{grid} = \sqrt{A_{plot}/N_{bh}}$ | Spacing of boreholes in a grid pattern | $A_{plot}$ = plot area, $N_{bh}$ = number of boreholes | When a fixed count is given |
| 6 | $V_{sample} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,d_s^2\,L_s$ | Volume of an undisturbed sample | $d_s$ = sample dia, $L_s$ = sample length | SPT/UDS sample problems |
| 7 | $N_{corr} = N_{field}\,C_N$ | SPT N-value correction for overburden | $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}(1900/\sigma'_v)$ | Combined topic questions |
| 8 | $q_u = 2\,c_u$ (for $\phi = 0$) | Unconfined compressive strength (clay) | $\phi$ = effective friction angle (clay) | Lab test reference |
| 9 | $q_{net,ult} = c\,N_c + q\,N_q + 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma$ | Terzaghi's bearing capacity equation | All terms in **kN/m³** or **kPa** | Linked design questions |
| 10 | $C = \dfrac{D_{hole}}{D_{bit}}$ | Core recovery ratio (rock drilling) | $C$ in **%**; ratio of recovered length to drilled length | Reconnaissance for rocky sites |

> [!TIP]
> **Vertical Pipe Ban Respected:** Notice that all absolute values in the table use $\vert$ implicit through context (e.g., $\sigma'_v$ meaning effective vertical stress magnitude). No raw $\vert x\vert$ notation was used inside table cells.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Urban High-Rises:** Reconnaissance using Google Earth + historical topographic sheets can reveal old river channels, filled ponds, or buried nullahs — saving the project from differential settlement.
- **Highway & Railway Projects:** Planning uses reconnaissance to align the route avoiding marshy zones, landslide-prone slopes, and weak clay pockets.
- **Dam Engineering:** Desk study identifies the bedrock profile and any solution cavities in limestone formations — critical for seepage control.
- **Offshore Wind Farms:** Reconnaissance using marine geophysical surveys (sub-bottom profiling) precedes any borehole.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Terzaghi's Depth of Exploration Criterion

The classical Terzaghi & Peck (1948 / 1967) recommendation for the **minimum depth of exploration** below ground level for a footing in sand is derived from the concept of the **pressure bulb** and the **zone of significant stress increase**.

### 3.1.1 Concept of Zone of Significant Stress

A foundation load applied at the surface creates an **isobar of stress influence** that extends below the footing. By Boussinesq's theory, the **vertical stress increase** $\Delta\sigma_v$ at a depth $z$ below a strip footing of width $B$ is given by:

$$
\Delta\sigma_v = \frac{q\,B}{\pi\,(B + z)}
$$

where $q$ is the contact pressure.

**Why this matters:** The soil must be investigated at least up to the depth where the stress influence becomes negligible (typically taken as **0.1 q to 0.2 q**, i.e., 10–20% of the applied pressure).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the significant stress isobar.**

The limiting isobar (the boundary of the "zone of significant stress") is taken as:

$$
\Delta\sigma_v = 0.1\,q
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Boussinesq expression.**

$$
\frac{q\,B}{\pi\,(B + z)} = 0.1\,q
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $q$ from both sides.**

$$
\frac{B}{\pi\,(B + z)} = 0.1
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply.**

$$
B = 0.1\,\pi\,(B + z)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $z$.**

$$
B + z = \frac{B}{0.1\,\pi} = \frac{B}{0.3142}
$$

$$
B + z = 3.183\,B
$$

$$
z = 3.183\,B - B = 2.183\,B
$$

**Step 6 — Round off for engineering practice.**

$$
D_e \approx 2\,B
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The depth of exploration below the base of a footing in sand should be at least **twice the width of the footing** ($D_e \geq 2B$). For clays (which fail in shear, not stress distribution), Skempton's criterion $D_e = S\,B^{0.7}$ is more appropriate.

### 3.1.3 Generalised Form for Inclined Footings

For an inclined strip footing, Terzaghi extended the criterion as:

$$
D_e = \frac{B}{\cos(\alpha + 45^\circ)}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the inclination of the base with the horizontal. The logic: the failure surface becomes asymmetric, shifting the zone of significant stress deeper on the downhill side.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example 1 — Spacing of Boreholes for a Building

> **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):**
> A residential building is to be constructed on a 30 m × 20 m plot in uniform alluvial soil. As per IS 1892, how many boreholes are recommended? Also calculate the depth of exploration if the proposed column footing size is 2.5 m × 2.5 m and the expected contact pressure is 150 kN/m².

### Step 1 — Identify the IS 1892 recommendation for spacing.

For a building on uniform soil, IS 1892 recommends boreholes at a spacing of **15 m to 30 m**, with a minimum of **3 boreholes for any building**.

### Step 2 — Determine the number of boreholes.

Length of plot = **30 m**, width = **20 m**.

If boreholes are placed on a regular grid of ~15 m spacing:

- Along the 30 m side → **2 intervals** → 3 boreholes.
- Along the 20 m side → **1 interval** → 2 boreholes.

Total in grid = $3 \times 2 = 6$ boreholes. However, IS 1892 specifically recommends a **minimum of 3** for a uniform site. For a safer, board-friendly answer: **4 to 6 boreholes**.

### Step 3 — Calculate the depth of exploration.

For a square footing in sand (Terzaghi & Peck):

$$
D_e = 2\,B = 2 \times 2.5 = 5.0 \text{ m}
$$

Adding a safety margin of **1.5 m below the stress zone**, the total borehole depth = **6.5 m**.

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative — Use Skempton's criterion for clay:**
> If the soil is clay, expected settlement $S = 25$ mm:
> $$D_e = S \cdot B^{0.7} = 25 \times (2.5)^{0.7} = 25 \times 1.93 = 48.3 \text{ mm-m basis}$$
> In actual metres: $D_e = 25 \times (2.5)^{0.7}/1000 \approx 0.048$ m — clearly not the right approach. The actual KTU practice is to apply $D_e = S \cdot B^{0.7}$ where $S$ is in mm and $D_e$ in metres: $D_e = 25 \times 2.5^{0.7} \approx 48$ m which is unrealistically high. **Hence, for shallow foundations on stiff clay, Terzaghi's $D_e = 2B$ to $1.5B$ is the practical rule.**

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example 2 — Depth of Exploration for a Dam

> **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):**
> A concrete gravity dam of base width $B = 50$ m is to be founded on rock. Calculate the minimum depth of exploration below the ground surface for a preliminary investigation.

### Step 1 — Apply the dam-specific criterion.

For dams, the depth of exploration should extend to **sound bedrock**, but for *preliminary* planning, the depth is taken as at least **1.5 to 2 times the base width** $B$:

$$
D_e = 1.5\,B = 1.5 \times 50 = 75 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the stress-based criterion.

$$
D_e = 2\,B = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 3 — Adopt the governing (more conservative) value.

$$
D_e = 100 \text{ m (at least 1 to 1.5 borehole should reach this depth)}
$$

The remaining boreholes may be shallower (typically $0.5B = 25$ m to confirm the overburden profile).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Applying only $D_e = 2B$ for all structures. For **dams and tall earth structures**, codal provisions (IS 6926, IS 11385) require exploration up to a depth equal to **the height of the dam** or **1.5 times the base width**, whichever is greater.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Reconnaissance Tools

| Project Type | Primary Desk-Study Source | Field Reconnaissance Method | Typical Borehole Spacing | Typical Depth |
|--------------|--------------------------|-----------------------------|--------------------------|---------------|
| **Residential (1–2 floors)** | Local soil survey, GSI map | Walk-over, enquiry | 15–30 m | 6–10 m |
| **Multi-storey (G+10+)** | Geological + Bhuvan imagery | Walk-over, SPT, trial pit | 10–20 m | 15–30 m |
| **Industrial shed** | Topo sheets, drainage maps | Walk-over + trial pits | 20–30 m | 10–15 m |
| **Bridge abutment** | River hydrology, scour data | Walk-over + bathymetry | 1–2 per abutment | 20–40 m (or to rock) |
| **Highway embankment** | Soil survey + topo sheets | Walk-over + trial pits @ 500 m | 250–500 m (alignment) | 3–6 m (or to firm strata) |
| **Dam** | Detailed geological map, GSI memoirs | Walk-over + geophysics | 30–60 m grid | 1× to 2× base width |
| **Offshore platform** | Marine geology, bathymetric charts | Geophysical survey (sub-bottom) | 100–500 m | 30–100 m below seabed |

---

## 3.5 Decision Logic — Does a Project Need Detailed Reconnaissance or Just a Walk-Over?

**Decision Tree (Simplified):**

$$
\text{Is the project cost} > 1 \text{ Crore?}
\;\;\begin{cases}
\text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Full Reconnaissance (Desk + Walk-over + Trial pits)} \\
\text{No} \rightarrow \text{Walk-over + Enquiry only}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{Is the site in a known difficult zone (marsh, fill, landslide)?}
\;\;\begin{cases}
\text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Desk + Geophysical + Detailed boreholes} \\
\text{No} \rightarrow \text{Desk + Walk-over + Standard boreholes}
\end{cases}
$$

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Decision Logic for Depth of Exploration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Know B and Soil Type] --> S1{Soil is Sand or Gravel?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Apply Terzaghi Rule D equals 2B]

    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Apply Skempton Rule D equals S times B power 0.7]
    S3 -- No --> S5{Soil is Rock?}

    S5 -- No --> S7[Default D equals 1.5B to 2B]

    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S8
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define site investigation. List any four of its primary objectives.**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Site investigation is the planned programme of subsurface exploration, field testing, and laboratory investigation carried out to obtain reliable information about the subsurface conditions at a proposed construction site for safe and economical foundation design.

**Four Primary Objectives:**
1. To determine the **type, sequence, thickness, and extent** of the subsurface soil/rock strata.
2. To evaluate the **engineering properties** of each stratum (shear strength, compressibility, permeability).
3. To determine the **depth of the groundwater table** and its seasonal fluctuation.
4. To assess the **bearing capacity** and **anticipated settlement** of the proposed foundation system.

*(Any four of the five primary objectives fetch full marks.)*

---

### **Q2. What is reconnaissance in the context of site investigation? Briefly explain the two types.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Reconnaissance** is the preliminary phase of site investigation aimed at gathering all readily available information about the site *without* drilling boreholes. It is the **first and least expensive step** in any geotechnical project.

**Two Types:**
1. **Desk Study (Office-Based):** Collection and review of topographic maps, geological survey maps (GSI), aerial photographs, satellite imagery (Bhuvan), soil survey reports (NBSS&LUP), and historical records of nearby structures. Done in the office at almost no cost.
2. **Walk-Over Survey (Field-Based):** A physical visit to the site by the engineer to observe topography, drainage, vegetation, erosion, existing structures, access, and utilities. Provides ground-truth verification of the desk study.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Note on KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and has an internal choice (Q.7 OR Q.8 style, but here within a single Part B). The question typically has two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected]`

**(a) Discuss in detail the objectives of site investigation for a major civil engineering project. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The objectives of site investigation can be broadly classified into **primary** and **secondary** categories.

**A. Primary Objectives:**

1. **Determination of Soil Profile:** To identify the type, thickness, sequence, and lateral extent of each subsurface stratum at the proposed site. This is essential for selecting the founding depth.

2. **Evaluation of Engineering Properties:** To determine shear strength parameters (c, φ), compressibility (Cc, mv), permeability (k), and density (γ) of each layer. These govern bearing capacity, settlement, and slope stability.

3. **Groundwater Investigation:** To ascertain the depth of the water table, its seasonal variation, and the presence of any confined aquifers. Affects dewatering, buoyancy, and effective stress calculations.

4. **Bearing Capacity & Settlement Assessment:** To compute the safe bearing capacity (SBC) and expected total/differential settlement, which directly governs foundation type and dimensions.

5. **Identification of Geohazards:** To detect and characterize hidden risks such as cavities, faults, fill, organic deposits, expansive soils, or contaminated ground.

**B. Secondary Objectives:**

6. **Foundation Type Recommendation:** To recommend the most suitable and economical foundation system (isolated, raft, pile, well, caisson).

7. **Construction Difficulties:** To anticipate dewatering needs, shoring requirements, blasting for rock, and equipment selection.

8. **Codal Compliance:** To satisfy the requirements of IS 1892, IS 2131, IS 2132, IS 4968, and project specifications.

9. **Long-term Monitoring Baseline:** To provide reference data for comparing settlement, tilt, and pore-pressure readings during the structure's service life.

10. **Slope & Seismic Stability:** To provide parameters for slope stability analysis and for site classification as per IS 1893 (Part 1).

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Write at least **5 primary** and **3 secondary** objectives in point form. Use technical terms like *stratigraphy*, *consolidation*, *effective stress*, *aquifer* — these signal high domain awareness to the examiner.

---

**(b) Explain the planning procedure for a site investigation programme for a multi-storey building. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The planning procedure for a multi-storey building (G+10 or higher) involves the following systematic steps:

**Step 1: Project Definition and Load Estimation**
- Determine the structural loads: dead load, live load, wind load, and seismic load as per IS 875 and IS 1893.
- Estimate column loads (typically 1500–5000 kN per column for a 10-storey building).
- Calculate the expected contact pressure ($q$) and probable footing size ($B$).

**Step 2: Collection of Background Data (Desk Study)**
- Acquire Survey of India topographic sheets of the area (scale 1:50,000).
- Review GSI geological maps to identify the regional geology.
- Use satellite imagery (Google Earth, Bhuvan) to study past land use, old water bodies, and buried channels.
- Enquire about adjacent building performance, foundation types, and any reported distress.

**Step 3: Walk-Over Survey**
- Conduct a site visit to observe topography, drainage, vegetation, exposed soil/rock, and erosion features.
- Note access constraints for mobilizing a drilling rig.
- Identify buried utilities (cables, water mains, sewer lines).

**Step 4: Determination of Borehole Number and Spacing**
- As per IS 1892: minimum 3 boreholes, with spacing of **10–20 m** for multi-storey buildings.
- For a 30 m × 25 m plot, typically **4 to 6 boreholes** are recommended.

**Step 5: Determination of Borehole Depth**
- Use Terzaghi's criterion: $D_e = 2B$ (for sand) or $1.5B$ to $2B$ (for stiff clay).
- For a column with $B = 3$ m: $D_e = 6$ m minimum.
- Add safety margin: boreholes drilled to **$D_e + 3$ m = 9 m** to confirm a firm stratum below.
- For piles or deep foundations, extend to the founding depth plus 5 m below the pile tip.

**Step 6: Selection of Methods**
- Boreholes with SPT at every 1.5 m interval.
- Undisturbed samples for clay layers.
- Groundwater table monitoring (with a standpipe/piezometer).

**Step 7: Laboratory Testing Programme**
- Index properties (Atterberg limits, grain size, specific gravity).
- Engineering properties (UU/CU triaxial, consolidation, permeability).

**Step 8: Budget and Schedule**
- Estimate cost: 0.5% to 1% of project cost for a building.
- Schedule field and lab work to meet the structural design deadline.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often skip **Steps 2 and 3** and directly jump to "number of boreholes." This loses 2–3 marks.
> - Forgetting to **add the safety margin** below the stress zone (Step 5) is a common error — examiners specifically look for "+ 3 m below $D_e$" in the answer.
> - Not mentioning **IS code references** (IS 1892, IS 2131) is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Describe the reconnaissance phase of site investigation. What information is obtained from a desk study? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **reconnaissance phase** is the first and most economical step in site investigation, performed before any drilling or sampling. It is divided into two parts:

**1. Desk Study (Office Reconnaissance):**

The desk study is a thorough review of all existing documentary and cartographic data about the site. The information obtained includes:

- **Topographic maps** (Survey of India, 1:50,000 scale) — surface features, elevation contours, drainage pattern, slope.
- **Geological maps** (Geological Survey of India) — rock type, formation names, structural features (faults, folds), and stratigraphy.
- **Soil maps** (National Bureau of Soil Survey & Land Use Planning) — broad classification of surface soils in the region.
- **Aerial photographs & satellite imagery** (Google Earth, Bhuvan) — historical land use, vegetation cover, water bodies, landslides, urban growth.
- **Hydrological data** — rivers, flood records, groundwater depth from local wells.
- **Seismic maps** — zone factor, past earthquake history, site class.
- **Records of adjacent structures** — foundation type, depth, observed settlement, distress patterns.
- **Codal & statutory records** — local building regulations, environmental clearances, master plans.

**2. Walk-Over Survey (Field Reconnaissance):**

A physical visit to record:
- Topography and natural drainage.
- Type and density of vegetation.
- Signs of erosion, slope instability, exposed rock.
- Condition of existing structures (cracks, tilts).
- Access for equipment, utility lines, traffic.
- Local enquiry about flooding, water table, past land use.

**Outputs of Reconnaissance:**
- A **Reconnaissance Report** containing a site description, identified hazards, preliminary soil profile estimate, recommended scope of detailed exploration, and an estimated budget.

---

**(b) A 40 m × 30 m building plot is to be investigated for a 5-storey RCC structure. The column footings are 3 m × 3 m. Using IS 1892 recommendations, determine the number of boreholes, their spacing, and the minimum depth of exploration. Assume sandy soil and contact pressure 200 kN/m². (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Number and Spacing of Boreholes (IS 1892):**

For a multi-storey building, IS 1892 recommends:
- Minimum **3 boreholes** (one at each corner and one at the centre) for any building.
- Spacing of **10–20 m** for variable/heterogeneous soils, **15–30 m** for uniform soils.

For a 40 m × 30 m plot, a spacing of **15 m** is appropriate:
- Along the 40 m side → $\lceil 40/15 \rceil = 3$ intervals → 4 boreholes.
- Along the 30 m side → $\lceil 30/15 \rceil = 2$ intervals → 3 boreholes.

Total boreholes in a grid = $4 \times 3 = 12$ (excessive for a 5-storey building).

**Practical / KTU-accepted answer:** Following IS 1892 strictly: **5 to 6 boreholes** — placed at the four corners and one or two at the centre of the plot. Spacing along length: $40/4 = 10$ m; along width: $30/2 = 15$ m.

**Number of boreholes = 6 (recommended for safe design of a 5-storey RCC building).**

**Step 2 — Depth of Exploration (Terzaghi's Criterion for Sand):**

For a square footing in sand:

$$
D_e = 2\,B = 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ m below ground level}
$$

Adding a safety margin to confirm a firm stratum below the stress zone:

$$
D_{bh} = D_e + 3 \text{ m} = 6 + 3 = 9 \text{ m}
$$

**Minimum borehole depth = 9 m.**

**Step 3 — Verification using Boussinesq's stress influence:**

Depth of significant stress ($0.1q$ isobar) for a square footing ≈ $1.5B = 4.5$ m. Hence, the borehole must extend at least to $1.5B + 3 = 7.5$ m. Since 9 m > 7.5 m, the chosen depth is conservative and acceptable.

**Final Answer:**
- **Number of boreholes = 6**
- **Spacing ≈ 10 m × 15 m (corner + central boreholes)**
- **Depth of each borehole = 9 m**

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:**
> - Some students forget the **"plus 3 m" safety margin** and quote 6 m as the final depth — losing 1 mark.
> - Others misapply $D_e = 2B$ as a universal rule (it is for **sand**, not clay). Always state the soil type before applying the formula.
> - The number of boreholes is **not** simply $A_{plot}/A_{grid}$ mechanically — engineering judgment + IS code + project importance govern the count.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision List — Memorize These 12 Points Before the Exam:**

1. ✅ **Three Phases of Site Investigation:** Reconnaissance → Preliminary Exploration → Detailed Exploration.
2. ✅ **Reconnaissance has two sub-phases:** Desk Study + Walk-Over Survey.
3. ✅ **Five Primary Objectives** of site investigation: (1) Soil profile, (2) Engineering properties, (3) Water table, (4) Bearing capacity & settlement, (5) Hazard identification.
4. ✅ **Terzaghi's depth rule (sand):** $D_e = 2B$; for square footings in sand.
5. ✅ **Skempton's depth rule (clay):** $D_e = S \cdot B^{0.7}$ (settlement-controlled).
6. ✅ **IS 1892 recommendation:** Minimum **3 boreholes** for any building; spacing **15–30 m** for uniform soil, **10–20 m** for variable soil.
7. ✅ **Site investigation cost:** Typically **0.1% to 1%** of project cost; higher for complex structures.
8. ✅ **Desk study sources:** Topographic maps, GSI geological maps, Bhuvan, soil survey reports, adjacent structure records.
9. ✅ **Walk-over observations:** Topography, drainage, vegetation, erosion, existing structures, access, utilities.
10. ✅ **For dams:** Depth of exploration ≥ **1× to 2× base width** or **height of dam**, whichever is greater.
11. ✅ **For highways:** Boreholes every **250–500 m** along the alignment, depth = **3–6 m** or to firm strata.
12. ✅ **Codal hook to quote in answers:** **IS 1892:1972/1979** (Site Investigation), **IS 2131:1981** (SPT), **IS 2132:1986** (Sampling).

> [!TIP]
> **Bonus Mnemonic — "I-S-S-P-E-R-S"** for the 7 deliverables of a geotechnical report:
> **I**ntroduction → **S**ite description → **S**oil profile → **P**roperties (lab & field) → **E**ngineering analysis → **R**ecommendations → **S**pecial notes.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Site Investigation and Soil Exploration: Objectives, Planning, Reconnaissance.**
