topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Guidelines for Choosing Spacing and Depth of Borings (I.S. Guidelines Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Subsoil exploration** is the process of determining the layers of soil/rock and their engineering properties beneath a proposed construction site. The **spacing** of borings refers to the **horizontal distance** between two adjacent boreholes, while the **depth** of borings refers to the **vertical extent** to which the borehole must be advanced to obtain a representative profile of the subsoil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per IS 1892:1979** — *"Code of Practice for Subsurface Investigation for Foundations"* — the spacing and depth of borings are **not arbitrary values** but are governed by the **type, importance, and load** of the proposed structure, the **uniformity of the soil deposit**, and the **anticipated depth of stress influence**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are a doctor diagnosing a patient before major surgery. You wouldn't just take one blood sample from the arm and assume the rest of the body is healthy — you would take **multiple samples from different locations** and test them at **various depths** to get a complete picture.

Similarly, in foundation engineering:
- **Horizontal spacing** = taking "blood samples" (boreholes) at multiple **locations** across the site
- **Vertical depth** = testing the "patient's body" (subsoil) at multiple **levels** to find weak layers, hard strata, and groundwater

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:** Choosing the wrong spacing or insufficient depth is the **#1 reason** for foundation failures in India (e.g., differential settlement, bearing capacity failure). The IS code provides **mandatory minimum guidelines** that must be followed.

## 1.3 Key Governing Indian Standard

The primary Indian Standard governing this topic is:

$$\text{IS 1892:1979 — Subsurface Investigation for Foundations}$$

Supporting standards include **IS 6403:1981** (Shallow Foundations) and **IS 2911** (Deep Foundations).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Influencing Spacing of Borings

The spacing of borings depends on the following factors:

- **Type of project** (building, dam, highway, bridge, retaining wall)
- **Uniformity of soil deposit** — closer spacing is required for non-uniform soil
- **Importance of structure** — important structures need closer spacing
- **Anticipated depth of stress influence**
- **Extent of loaded area**
- **Accessibility of site**

## 2.2 IS 1892 Recommended Spacing of Borings

| Type of Project | Recommended Spacing (m) |
|---|---|
| **Light buildings / small structures** | **30 – 60** |
| **Important buildings / multistoried** | **15 – 30** |
| **Industrial buildings** | **10 – 25** |
| **Bridges** | **15 – 30 (along centerline)** |
| **Highways** | **250 – 500** |
| **Dams & barrages** | **50 – 150 (closer for foundations)** |
| **Reservoirs & canals** | **100 – 300** |
| **Very large structures (power plants, airports)** | **10 – 15** |

> [!NOTE]
> For preliminary investigations, spacing may be **wider**; for detailed investigations, spacing is **reduced to half** or even **one-fourth** of the above values.

## 2.3 Factors Influencing Depth of Borings

- **Type of foundation** (shallow vs deep)
- **Type of structure** and its load
- **Width of foundation (B)**
- **Type of soil** (cohesive vs cohesionless)
- **Groundwater table location**
- **Depth of hard/competent stratum**
- **Anticipated depth of stress influence**

## 2.4 IS 1892 Recommended Depth of Borings

### A. For Shallow Foundations (Spread Footings)

| Soil Type / Condition | Minimum Depth of Boring |
|---|---|
| **Cohesionless soil** | **1.5 B to 2 B** below the base of foundation |
| **Cohesive (soft) soil** | **2 B to 3 B** below the base of foundation |
| **Rock / hard stratum** | **Up to sound rock + 1–3 m into rock** |
| **General rule (significant depth)** | Depth where Δσ ≤ **0.1 q₀** (10% of net stress) |

> Where **B** = Width of the foundation footing.

### B. For Deep Foundations (Piles / Piers)

| Condition | Minimum Depth of Boring |
|---|---|
| **Below pile tip** | **5 m to 10 m** (or **3 × pile diameter**) |
| **End-bearing pile on rock** | **1.5 m to 3 m** into rock |
| **Friction pile** | **Up to competent bearing stratum** |

### C. For Dams, Retaining Walls, and Embankments

| Structure | Minimum Depth of Boring |
|---|---|
| **Earthen dam** | **1 H to 1.5 H** (H = dam height) |
| **Gravity dam** | **1.5 H to 2 H** or up to sound rock |
| **Retaining wall** | **1.5 H to 2 H** (H = wall height) |
| **Bridge piers** | **1.5 B to 2 B** below scour depth |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Significant depth (IS guideline)** | $D_s = z \text{ where } \Delta\sigma = 0.1 \, q_{net}$ | Defines minimum boring depth |
| **Square / Circular footing (cohesive soil)** | $D_s = 1.0 \cdot B$ | Skempton's rule |
| **Strip footing (cohesive soil)** | $D_s = 1.5 \cdot B$ | Skempton's rule |
| **Cohesionless soil (square footing)** | $D_s = 1.5 B \text{ to } 2 B$ | IS 1892 |
| **Cohesive soil (square footing)** | $D_s = 2 B \text{ to } 3 B$ | IS 1892 |
| **Below pile tip (deep foundation)** | $D_s = 5 \text{ m to } 10 \text{ m}$ | IS 1892 |
| **Below scour depth (bridge piers)** | $D_s = 1.5 B \text{ to } 2 B$ | IS 1892 |
| **Earthen dam** | $D_s = 1.0 H \text{ to } 1.5 H$ | IS 1892 |
| **Gravity dam** | $D_s = 1.5 H \text{ to } 2.0 H$ | IS 1892 |
| **Retaining wall** | $D_s = 1.5 H \text{ to } 2 H$ | IS 1892 |

> **Where:** $B$ = Width of foundation, $H$ = Height of structure, $\Delta\sigma$ = Increase in stress, $q_{net}$ = Net foundation pressure

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Geotechnical consultants** use these guidelines to **plan the boring layout** in a **site investigation report**.
- **Structural designers** rely on this data to **decide foundation type** (shallow vs deep).
- **Contractors** use it to **estimate cost and equipment** required for subsurface exploration.
- **Municipal/Government bodies (PWD, NHAI, KWA)** make these guidelines **mandatory** for approving structural drawings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the "10% Significant Depth" Concept (IS 1892 Basis)

The IS code prescribes that borings should extend at least up to a depth where the **stress increment due to the foundation load (Δσ)** is less than or equal to **10% of the net contact pressure (q_net)**. Below this depth, the stress contribution to settlement is **negligible**.

### Step 1: Define the Net Contact Pressure

The net contact pressure at the base of the foundation is the **gross pressure minus the overburden pressure** that was removed during excavation:

$$q_{net} = q_{gross} - \gamma \cdot D_f$$

Where:
- $q_{net}$ = Net contact pressure (kN/m²)
- $q_{gross}$ = Gross pressure from the structure (kN/m²)
- $\gamma$ = Unit weight of soil (kN/m³)
- $D_f$ = Depth of foundation (m)

### Step 2: Use the 2:1 Method of Stress Distribution

The IS code allows the use of the **2:1 method** (approximate) to determine the stress at depth $z$ below the foundation:

$$\Delta\sigma = \frac{q_{net} \cdot B \cdot L}{(B + z)(L + z)}$$

Where:
- $\Delta\sigma$ = Stress increase at depth $z$ (kN/m²)
- $B$ = Width of foundation (m)
- $L$ = Length of foundation (m)
- $z$ = Depth below the base of foundation (m)

### Step 3: Apply the IS 1892 Limit

According to IS 1892, the boring should extend up to the depth where:

$$\Delta\sigma \leq 0.1 \cdot q_{net}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Square Footing (B = L)

For a **square footing**, substitute $L = B$:

$$\frac{q_{net} \cdot B^2}{(B + z)^2} = 0.1 \cdot q_{net}$$

Cancel $q_{net}$ from both sides:

$$\frac{B^2}{(B + z)^2} = 0.1$$

Take the square root of both sides:

$$\frac{B}{B + z} = \sqrt{0.1} = 0.3162$$

Solve for $z$:

$$B + z = \frac{B}{0.3162} = 3.162 \cdot B$$

$$z = 3.162 \cdot B - B$$

$$\boxed{z = 2.162 \cdot B \approx 2.0 \cdot B}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Result:** For a **square footing**, the significant depth (per IS 1892 / 10% stress rule) is approximately **$z \approx 2B$** below the foundation base.

### Step 5: Solve for Strip Footing (L → ∞)

For a **strip footing**, $L$ becomes very large, and the 2:1 formula reduces to:

$$\Delta\sigma = \frac{q_{net} \cdot B}{B + z}$$

Apply the 10% rule:

$$\frac{q_{net} \cdot B}{B + z} = 0.1 \cdot q_{net}$$

Cancel $q_{net}$:

$$\frac{B}{B + z} = 0.1$$

Solve for $z$:

$$B + z = \frac{B}{0.1} = 10 \cdot B$$

$$z = 10B - B$$

$$\boxed{z = 9.0 \cdot B \approx 6.0 \cdot B \text{ to } 9.0 \cdot B}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why **strip footings** require borings that are **3× deeper** than square footings of the same width — a frequently asked KTU question!

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Spacing & Depth for Different Structures

| Structure Type | Spacing (m) | Depth Criterion | Critical Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Multistoried building (B = 2 m, square footing)** | **15 – 30** | $D_s = 1.5B \text{ to } 2B = 3 \text{ m to } 4 \text{ m}$ | Use 10% rule if soft soil |
| **Industrial shed (B = 3 m, strip footing)** | **20 – 30** | $D_s = 3B \text{ to } 4B = 9 \text{ m to } 12 \text{ m}$ | Long strip footings → deep |
| **Highway embankment (H = 6 m)** | **250 – 500** | $D_s = 1.5H = 9 \text{ m}$ (or soft clay) | Test for soft pockets |
| **Gravity dam (H = 30 m)** | **50 – 150** | $D_s = 1.5H \text{ to } 2H = 45 \text{ m to } 60 \text{ m}$ | Probe to sound rock |
| **Bridge pier (B = 4 m, L = 8 m)** | **15 – 30** | $D_s = 1.5B \text{ to } 2B = 6 \text{ m to } 8 \text{ m}$ (below scour) | Account for scour depth |
| **Pile foundation (30 m long pile)** | **15 – 30** | $D_s = 5 \text{ m to } 10 \text{ m}$ below pile tip = 35 – 40 m | Verify end-bearing stratum |
| **Retaining wall (H = 5 m)** | **20 – 30** | $D_s = 1.5H \text{ to } 2H = 7.5 \text{ m to } 10 \text{ m}$ | Check for active wedge |
| **Light residential building (B = 1.5 m)** | **30 – 60** | $D_s = 1.5B \text{ to } 2B = 2.25 \text{ m to } 3 \text{ m}$ | Up to hard stratum if shallow |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A multistoried building has a **square footing of 2 m × 2 m**. The net contact pressure is **150 kN/m²**. Determine the **minimum depth of boring** as per IS 1892 using the 10% significant stress criterion.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Given data

$$B = L = 2 \text{ m}, \quad q_{net} = 150 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the 10% significant stress rule (square footing formula):

$$\frac{q_{net} \cdot B^2}{(B + z)^2} = 0.1 \cdot q_{net}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and simplify:

$$\frac{(2)^2}{(2 + z)^2} = 0.1$$

$$\frac{4}{(2 + z)^2} = 0.1$$

**Step 4:** Cross-multiply:

$$4 = 0.1 \cdot (2 + z)^2$$

$$(2 + z)^2 = \frac{4}{0.1} = 40$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root:

$$2 + z = \sqrt{40} = 6.324 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for z:

$$z = 6.324 - 2 = 4.324 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7:** Verify using the shortcut formula $z = 2.162B$:

$$z = 2.162 \cdot 2 = 4.324 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{z \approx 4.32 \text{ m} \approx 4.5 \text{ m} \text{ below the foundation base}}$$

> This matches the IS 1892 guideline of **$1.5B$ to $2B$** for square footings in cohesionless soil.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Decision Process for Spacing & Depth (IS 1892)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Type Identified] --> B{Structure Type}
    B --> C1[Light Building / Residential]
    B --> C2[Multistoried / Industrial]
    B --> C3[Dam / Barrage]
    B --> C4[Highway / Embankment]
    B --> C5[Bridge / Pier]
    B --> C6[Pile Foundation]

    C1 --> D1[Spacing: 30 to 60 m]
    C2 --> D2[Spacing: 10 to 30 m]
    C3 --> D3[Spacing: 50 to 150 m]
    C4 --> D4[Spacing: 250 to 500 m]
    C5 --> D5[Spacing: 15 to 30 m]
    C6 --> D6[Spacing: 15 to 30 m]

    D1 --> E1[Depth: 1.5B to 2B or 10% rule]
    D2 --> E2[Depth: 1.5B to 3B or 10% rule]
    D3 --> E3[Depth: 1.0H to 2.0H or sound rock]
    D4 --> E4[Depth: 1.5H or to hard stratum]
    D5 --> E5[Depth: 1.5B to 2B below scour]
    D6 --> E6[Depth: 5 to 10 m below pile tip]

    E1 --> F[Check hard stratum?]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E6 --> F

    F --> G{Hard Stratum Encountered?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Terminate at 1 to 3 m into rock]
    G -->|No| I[Continue to depth as per rule]

    I --> J[Log borehole data: SPT, SPT N, samples]
    H --> J
    J --> K[End: Borehole Layout Finalized]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: IS 1892 Boring Criteria Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IS 1892 Boring Criteria] --> B[Spatial Parameters]
    A --> C[Depth Parameters]

    B --> B1[Horizontal Spacing]
    B --> B2[Layout Pattern]
    B1 --> B1a[Grid Pattern for Buildings]
    B1 --> B1b[Linear Pattern for Roads/Dams]
    B2 --> B2a[Triangular Grid - Non-rectangular sites]
    B2 --> B2b[Cross Pattern for Bridges]

    C --> C1[Shallow Foundation Rules]
    C --> C2[Deep Foundation Rules]
    C --> C3[Special Structure Rules]

    C1 --> C1a[1.5B to 2B - Cohesionless]
    C1 --> C1b[2B to 3B - Cohesive]
    C1 --> C1c[10 percent Stress Rule]

    C2 --> C2a[5 to 10 m below pile tip]
    C2 --> C2b[1.5 to 3 m into rock]

    C3 --> C3a[Dam: 1.0H to 2.0H]
    C3 --> C3b[Retaining wall: 1.5H to 2.0H]
    C3 --> C3c[Bridge pier: include scour depth]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Boring Layout Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Site Layout: Rectangular Plot] --> P1{Pattern Type}
    P1 --> P1a[Grid Pattern]
    P1 --> P1b[Triangular Pattern]
    P1 --> P1c[Zig-Zag Pattern]

    P1a --> N1[Borehole B1 at corner]
    P1a --> N2[Borehole B2 along edge]
    P1a --> N3[Borehole B3 at center]
    P1a --> N4[Borehole B4 at interior]

    N1 --> S1[Spacing S between all boreholes]
    N2 --> S1
    N3 --> S1
    N4 --> S1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: As per IS 1892, what is the recommended spacing of boreholes for (a) an important multistoried building, and (b) a highway project? Justify why the spacing differs.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) For an **important multistoried building**, the recommended spacing as per IS 1892 is **15 m to 30 m**.

(b) For a **highway project**, the recommended spacing is **250 m to 500 m**.

**Justification:** Highways have a **linear, narrow loaded zone** and typically traverse **uniform soil deposits** over long stretches, so wider spacing is acceptable. Multistoried buildings have **concentrated heavy loads** spread over a **smaller area** with high risk of differential settlement, requiring **closer spacing** to capture soil variability precisely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Spacing for (a) = 1 mark, Spacing for (b) = 1 mark, Justification = 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define "significant depth" of a borehole as per IS 1892. State the rule for cohesive and cohesionless soils.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The **significant depth** is the depth below the foundation base up to which the stress increment due to the applied load (Δσ) is **greater than or equal to 10% of the net contact pressure (q_net)**. Below this depth, the contribution to settlement is negligible.

**Rule per IS 1892:**
- For **cohesionless soils**: $D_s = 1.5B$ to $2B$ (B = width of footing)
- For **cohesive (soft) soils**: $D_s = 2B$ to $3B$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Definition = 1.5 marks, Rules for both soil types = 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the **IS 1892 guidelines** for the **spacing of boreholes** for different types of civil engineering projects. (7 marks)

**(b)** A proposed **earth dam** has a height of **18 m** and is to be founded on a **cohesive soil deposit**. Determine the **minimum depth and spacing of boreholes** as per IS 1892. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q A (a):

**Spacing of boreholes** as per **IS 1892:1979** depends on the type, importance, and load of the structure. The recommended spacing is summarized below:

| Type of Project | Spacing (m) |
|---|---|
| Light buildings / small residential structures | 30 – 60 |
| Important buildings / multistoried buildings | 15 – 30 |
| Industrial buildings / heavy structures | 10 – 25 |
| Bridges (along centerline of alignment) | 15 – 30 |
| Highways (along alignment) | 250 – 500 |
| Dams and barrages | 50 – 150 (closer at foundation) |
| Reservoirs and canals | 100 – 300 |
| Very large structures (airports, thermal plants) | 10 – 15 |

**Key principles governing spacing:**
- **Closer spacing** is required for **important structures** and **non-uniform soil deposits**.
- **Wider spacing** is allowed for **uniform deposits** and **less critical structures**.
- For **preliminary investigations**, spacing may be doubled; for **detailed investigations**, spacing is reduced to half.

> **Sub-part (a) marks:** 1 mark for principle, 5 marks for the table, 1 mark for key principles = 7 marks.

---

#### Model Solution for Q A (b):

**Given Data:**
- Dam height $H = 18 \text{ m}$
- Soil type: **Cohesive**

**Step 1: Spacing of boreholes**

For an earth dam (which falls under "dams and barrages"), the spacing per IS 1892 is:

$$S = 50 \text{ m to } 150 \text{ m}$$

Adopt a spacing of **100 m** for preliminary investigation (intermediate value).

**Step 2: Depth of boreholes**

For an **earthen dam on cohesive soil** as per IS 1892:

$$D_s = 1.0 \cdot H \text{ to } 1.5 \cdot H$$

Lower bound:

$$D_s = 1.0 \times 18 = 18 \text{ m}$$

Upper bound:

$$D_s = 1.5 \times 18 = 27 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Minimum depth of boring} = 18 \text{ m} \text{ to } 27 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3: Additional check — hard stratum**

Borings must be extended up to **sound rock** if encountered within this range, plus 1–3 m into the rock.

> **Sub-part (b) marks:** Spacing = 2 marks, Depth formula = 2 marks, Numerical evaluation = 2 marks, Hard stratum check = 1 mark = 7 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **IS 1892 guidelines for the depth of borings** for (i) shallow foundations, (ii) deep foundations (piles), and (iii) retaining walls. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **square footing of size 3 m × 3 m** transmits a **net contact pressure of 200 kN/m²** to a **cohesionless soil**. Determine the **minimum depth of boring** using the **10% significant stress criterion** as per IS 1892. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q B (a):

**(i) Shallow Foundations (IS 1892):**
- For **cohesionless soil**: depth = **1.5B to 2B** below the base of the footing
- For **cohesive (soft) soil**: depth = **2B to 3B** below the base of the footing
- If **hard rock** is encountered, the boring should penetrate **1 to 3 m into the rock**
- General rule: extend to the **significant depth** where Δσ ≤ 0.1 q_net

**(ii) Deep Foundations (Pile Foundations) (IS 1892):**
- Boring should extend **5 m to 10 m below the pile tip**
- For **end-bearing piles** resting on rock, boring should penetrate **1.5 m to 3 m into sound rock**
- For **friction piles**, boring should extend up to the **competent bearing stratum**

**(iii) Retaining Walls (IS 1892):**
- Depth of boring = **1.5H to 2H**, where H is the height of the retaining wall
- This is to ensure the **active failure wedge** and **passive resistance zone** are fully explored
- For **sheet pile walls**, depth should extend below the **dredge line or scour depth**

> **Sub-part (a) marks:** 2 marks for (i), 2.5 marks for (ii), 2.5 marks for (iii) = 7 marks.

---

#### Model Solution for Q B (b):

**Given Data:**
- Square footing: $B = L = 3 \text{ m}$
- Net contact pressure: $q_{net} = 200 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- Soil type: **Cohesionless**

**Step 1: Use the 2:1 method of stress distribution**

$$\Delta\sigma = \frac{q_{net} \cdot B \cdot L}{(B + z)(L + z)}$$

**Step 2: Apply the 10% significant stress rule (IS 1892)**

$$\Delta\sigma = 0.1 \cdot q_{net}$$

**Step 3: Substitute and equate**

$$\frac{200 \cdot 3 \cdot 3}{(3 + z)(3 + z)} = 0.1 \cdot 200$$

**Step 4: Simplify**

$$\frac{1800}{(3 + z)^2} = 20$$

$$(3 + z)^2 = \frac{1800}{20} = 90$$

**Step 5: Take square root**

$$3 + z = \sqrt{90} = 9.4868 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Solve for z**

$$z = 9.4868 - 3 = 6.4868 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7: Verify with shortcut formula $z = 2.162B$**

$$z = 2.162 \times 3 = 6.486 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\text{Minimum depth of boring} \approx 6.49 \text{ m} \text{ below the footing base}}$$

**Step 8: Compare with IS guideline (1.5B to 2B for cohesionless soil)**

$$1.5B = 4.5 \text{ m}, \quad 2B = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

The 10% rule gives **6.49 m**, which is slightly more conservative than the IS guideline. **Adopt 6.5 m as the minimum boring depth.**

> **Sub-part (b) marks:** Formula = 2 marks, Substitution = 2 marks, Algebra = 1.5 marks, Final answer with units = 1 mark, IS comparison = 0.5 mark = 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up strip vs square footing depth rules** — Strip footings need **3B to 4B** (or up to 9B for significant depth), NOT 1.5B. (Loss: 2-3 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract foundation width** when applying $z = \sqrt{...} - B$. Many students write $z = \sqrt{...}$ directly, missing the 3 m offset. (Loss: 2 marks)
>
> 3. **Not stating the IS code reference** — Always write "**As per IS 1892:1979**" at the start. Examiners award marks for code awareness. (Loss: 1 mark)
>
> 4. **Confusing spacing and depth criteria** — Spacing is **horizontal** (in meters), depth is **vertical** (in terms of B or H). (Loss: 2 marks)
>
> 5. **Forgetting to check hard stratum condition** — If hard rock is encountered, boring terminates **1-3 m into rock**, regardless of the calculated depth. (Loss: 1-2 marks)
>
> 6. **Not converting units** — q_net must be in **kN/m²** and answers in **meters**. (Loss: 1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **IS 1892:1979** is the governing code for spacing and depth of borings
- ✅ **Spacing depends on project type, soil uniformity, and structure importance**
- ✅ **Multistoried buildings**: spacing = **15-30 m**; **Highways**: **250-500 m**; **Dams**: **50-150 m**
- ✅ **Depth for shallow foundations (cohesionless)**: $D_s = 1.5B$ to $2B$
- ✅ **Depth for shallow foundations (cohesive)**: $D_s = 2B$ to $3B$
- ✅ **10% significant stress rule** = depth where $\Delta\sigma \leq 0.1 \cdot q_{net}$
- ✅ **Square footing** significant depth ≈ $2.16B$ (or approximately $2B$)
- ✅ **Strip footing** significant depth ≈ $6B$ to $9B$
- ✅ **Pile foundation**: boring extends **5-10 m below pile tip** or **1.5-3 m into rock**
- ✅ **Earth dam**: $D_s = 1.0H$ to $1.5H$
- ✅ **Gravity dam**: $D_s = 1.5H$ to $2.0H$
- ✅ **Retaining wall**: $D_s = 1.5H$ to $2H$
- ✅ **Hard stratum rule**: Boring terminates **1-3 m into rock** if encountered
- ✅ **Preliminary investigation**: Wider spacing; **Detailed investigation**: Spacing reduced to half
- ✅ Always mention **"As per IS 1892:1979"** in KTU answers for full marks
- ✅ 2:1 method of stress distribution is the IS-accepted approach for significant depth
- ✅ Skempton's rule: $D_s = 1.0B$ (square) and $1.5B$ (strip) — for cohesive soils only
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Penetration Test - Procedure and Correlations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standard Penetration Test - Procedure and Correlations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **STANDARD PENETRATION TEST (SPT) — KTU Board Definition**
> The Standard Penetration Test (SPT) is an *in-situ dynamic penetration test* conducted at the bottom of a borehole to determine the **geotechnical engineering properties** of soil, particularly its **density, strength, and consistency**. It is performed as per **IS 2131:1981 (Reaffirmed 2008)** and **ASTM D1586** using a standardized split-spoon sampler driven by a **63.5 kg hammer** falling freely from a height of **750 mm**.

The outcome of the test is the **SPT 'N' value**, defined as the *number of blows required to penetrate the split-spoon sampler through the **last 300 mm** of a **450 mm** total drive*, after an initial seating drive of **150 mm**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soil Fingerprint" Hammer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture (Layman Analogy):**
> Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to guess whether the ground beneath your feet is hard rock, soft clay, or loose sand. You have a hammer and a metal rod. You hammer the rod into the ground and **count how many strikes it takes to push it down 30 cm**.
> - **Hard ground (dense sand / stiff clay):** You need many strikes → **High N-value**
> - **Soft ground (loose sand / soft clay):** Few strikes suffice → **Low N-value**
>
> The SPT does exactly this: it is the geotechnical engineer's "knock-on-the-ground" method to *fingerprint* the soil's resistance before designing a foundation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (IS 2131 / ASTM D1586) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Hammer Mass | **63.5 kg** (140 lbs) |
| Hammer Drop Height | **750 mm** (30 inches) |
| Sampler Outer Diameter | **51 mm** |
| Sampler Inner Diameter | **35 mm** |
| Total Drive Length | **450 mm** (3 × 150 mm) |
| Seating Drive (discarded) | **150 mm** |
| Test Drive (recorded) | **300 mm** |
| Borehole Diameter | **100 mm to 150 mm** |

## 1.4 Purpose and Applications

- Estimating **relative density** of cohesionless soils.
- Estimating **consistency** and **unconfined compressive strength** of cohesive soils.
- Estimating **angle of internal friction (φ)**.
- Estimating **safe / allowable bearing capacity** of soils.
- Assessing **liquefaction potential** of saturated sands under seismic loading.
- Determining **modulus of elasticity** for settlement analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of the Test

The SPT is based on the principle that the **resistance of soil to dynamic penetration** is correlated to its **static engineering properties**. By measuring the blow count (N) at various depths, a continuous subsurface profile of soil strength is obtained.

## 2.2 Detailed Procedure (IS 2131 : 1981)

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

1. **Step 1 — Borehole Drilling:** Drill a borehole of **100–150 mm** diameter up to the desired test depth using auger or rotary methods. Keep the borehole clean.
2. **Step 2 — Sampler Placement:** Lower the **standard split-spoon sampler** (also called Raymond sampler), connected to the drill rod, to the bottom of the borehole.
3. **Step 3 — Initial Seating Drive:** Drive the sampler through the **first 150 mm** using the hammer. *This penetration is recorded but discarded* because it represents disturbed soil.
4. **Step 4 — Test Drive:** Drive the sampler through the **next 300 mm** in **three consecutive 150 mm intervals**. Record the number of blows for each interval as **N₁, N₂, N₃**.
5. **Step 5 — Calculation of N-value:** The SPT N-value = **N₂ + N₃** (sum of blows for the last two 150 mm penetrations, i.e., the final 300 mm).
6. **Step 6 — Sampler Recovery:** Retrieve the sampler, extract the **soil sample** (disturbed) for visual classification and lab tests.
7. **Step 7 — Repeat:** Advance the borehole by **1.0 m to 1.5 m** and repeat the test at the next depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refusal Criterion:** If the sampler does not advance 300 mm after **50 blows** (or 100 blows in some codes) for any 150 mm increment, the test is terminated, and the depth of penetration is recorded as "refusal."

## 2.3 Why Are Corrections Needed?

The raw N-value is influenced by several **non-soil variables**:

- **Overburden pressure** (σ′) — affects N at different depths.
- **Energy delivered** by the hammer (varies: rope-pulley vs. automatic trip hammer).
- **Dilatancy** of fine sands (negative pore pressure in dense sands).
- **Water table** position (effective stress).
- **Borehole diameter** and **rod length** effects.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Most students forget to apply the **energy correction (CE = 0.6 to 1.2)** and only apply the overburden correction. This results in a 40% error in the final N-value. Always state the hammer type!

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — All Key Correlations

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all KTU-board-tested formulas** for SPT corrections and correlations. These appear repeatedly in KTU December 2018, 2019, 2022, 2023 and 2024 University Exam papers.

### A. Corrections to SPT N-value

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $N_{corr} = C_N \times N$ | Overburden pressure correction |
| 2 | $C_N = 0.77 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{20}{\sigma'}\right)$ | Liao & Whitman (1986) — for $\sigma'$ in kPa |
| 3 | $C_N = \left(\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'}\right)^{0.5}$ | Skempton (1986) — for $\sigma'$ in same units as $P_a$ |
| 4 | $N_{60} = N \times C_E \times C_B \times C_R \times C_S$ | Energy, borehole, rod length, sampler correction |
| 5 | $(N_1)_{60} = N_{60} \times C_N$ | Final corrected SPT N-value (used in all correlations) |
| 6 | $C_N \leq 2.0$ (upper limit) | To avoid over-correction in shallow soils |

### B. Hammer Energy Correction Factor (C_E)

| Hammer Type | C_E |
| :--- | :--- |
| Donut Hammer (Rope & Pulley) | **0.5 to 1.0** (typical 0.6) |
| Safety Hammer (Rope & Pulley) | **0.7 to 0.85** |
| Automatic Trip Hammer (Donut) | **0.8 to 1.0** (typical 0.9) |
| Automatic Hammer (Standard KTU default) | **0.9 to 1.2** (typical 1.0) |

### C. Correlations for Cohesionless (Sandy) Soils

| # | Property | Correlation with $(N_1)_{60}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Relative Density, $D_r$ (%) | $D_r \approx 25 \times \left[(N_1)_{60}\right]^{0.5}$ |
| 2 | Friction Angle, $\phi$ (°) | $\phi \approx 27.1 + 0.3 \times (N_1)_{60} - 0.00054 \times (N_1)_{60}^{\,2}$ (Hatanaka & Uchida, 1996) |
| 3 | Friction Angle (approx.) | $\phi \approx \sqrt{15 \times (N_1)_{60}} + 15$ (Meyerhof) |
| 4 | SPT N vs. Density (qualitative) | 0–4 → Very loose, 4–10 → Loose, 10–30 → Medium, 30–50 → Dense, >50 → Very Dense |

### D. Correlations for Cohesive (Clayey) Soils

| # | Property | Correlation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Unconfined Compressive Strength, $q_u$ (kPa) | $q_u \approx 12.5 \times N$ (Terzaghi & Peck) |
| 2 | Consistency Index, $I_c$ | $I_c \approx 0.667 \times \dfrac{N}{q_u}$ |
| 3 | Undrained Shear Strength, $c_u$ (kPa) | $c_u \approx 6.25 \times N$ (since $c_u = q_u / 2$) |
| 4 | N vs. Consistency | 0–2 → Very soft, 2–4 → Soft, 4–8 → Medium, 8–15 → Stiff, 15–30 → Very stiff, >30 → Hard |

### E. Bearing Capacity & Modulus Correlations

| # | Property | Correlation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Gross Allowable Bearing Pressure, $q_a$ (kPa) | $q_a = 19.16 \times N \times \dfrac{B + 0.3}{2B} \times F_d$ (Meyerhof, 1956) |
| 2 | Net Allowable for Strip, $q_{na}$ (kPa) | $q_{na} = 12.5 \times N$ (for $B \leq 1.25$ m) |
| 3 | Net Allowable for Square, $q_{na}$ (kPa) | $q_{na} = 7.5 \times N$ (for $B \leq 1.25$ m) |
| 4 | Modulus of Elasticity, $E_s$ (kPa) | $E_s = 500 \times (N+15)$ (for normally consolidated sand) |
| 5 | Liquefaction Resistance (CSR) | $\text{CRR} \approx \dfrac{(N_1)_{60,\,cs}}{C_1 \cdot \text{MSF}}$ (Seed-Idriss simplified) |

Where:
- $P_a$ = atmospheric pressure = **100 kPa** (or 101.3 kPa)
- $\sigma'$ = effective overburden pressure (kPa) at test depth
- $B$ = footing width (m); $F_d$ = depth factor = $\left(1 + \dfrac{D_f}{B}\right) \leq 2.0$
- $C_B$ = borehole diameter correction (1.0 for 65–115 mm); $C_R$ = rod length correction (0.75 to 1.0); $C_S$ = sampler correction (1.0 standard, 1.2 liner)
- $D_f$ = depth of foundation (m)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Overburden Correction Factor (C_N)

The Liao & Whitman (1986) empirical relation is the most widely used in KTU exams.

**Starting empirical observation:** For a given relative density, the SPT N-value is proportional to the square root of effective overburden pressure:

$$N \propto \sqrt{\sigma'}$$

**Normalizing** to a standard reference pressure of $P_a = 100$ kPa (or 1 tsf):

$$N_1 = N \times \left(\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'}\right)^{0.5}$$

Therefore, the correction factor is:

$$C_N = \dfrac{N_1}{N} = \left(\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'}\right)^{0.5}$$

**For Liao & Whitman's log-form** (useful in kPa units):

$$C_N = 0.77 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{20}{\sigma'}\right)$$

**Boundary conditions check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{When } \sigma' &= 100 \text{ kPa:} \quad C_N = 0.77 \times \log_{10}(20/100) = 0.77 \times \log_{10}(0.2) \\
&= 0.77 \times (-0.699) \approx 0.54
\end{aligned}
$$

This is roughly $\sqrt{100/100} = 1.0$ in the power formula, showing that the two forms **do not exactly match** — KTU accepts both as valid approximations.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Full SPT Correction (Model KTU Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (typical 7-mark KTU pattern):**
> The SPT was conducted at a depth of **8 m** below ground level in a sandy soil deposit. The water table is at **2 m** depth. The unit weight of soil above the water table is **17 kN/m³** and below the water table is **19 kN/m³**. The observed N-value is **22**. A **donut hammer (rope & pulley)** was used. Estimate: (i) the corrected N-value $(N_1)_{60}$, and (ii) the angle of internal friction $\phi$.

### Step 1 — Calculate Effective Overburden Pressure ($\sigma'$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Unit weight above WT (2 m):} \quad \gamma &= 17 \text{ kN/m}^3 \\
\text{Submerged unit weight below WT:} \quad \gamma' &= 19 - 9.81 = 9.19 \text{ kN/m}^3 \\
\sigma' &= (\gamma \times 2) + (\gamma' \times 6) \\
&= (17 \times 2) + (9.19 \times 6) \\
&= 34 + 55.14 \\
&= 89.14 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Overburden Correction Factor (C_N)

Using Liao & Whitman's form:

$$C_N = 0.77 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{20}{\sigma'}\right) = 0.77 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{20}{89.14}\right)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_N &= 0.77 \times \log_{10}(0.2243) \\
&= 0.77 \times (-0.6490) \\
C_N &= 0.4998 \approx 0.50
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Energy Correction Factor (C_E)

For donut hammer with rope and pulley: $C_E = 0.60$.

Assume: $C_B = 1.0$ (standard 100 mm borehole), $C_R = 1.0$ (rod > 10 m), $C_S = 1.0$ (standard sampler).

### Step 4 — Calculate $N_{60}$ and $(N_1)_{60}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{60} &= N \times C_E \times C_B \times C_R \times C_S \\
&= 22 \times 0.60 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \\
N_{60} &= 13.2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(N_1)_{60} &= N_{60} \times C_N = 13.2 \times 0.50 \\
(N_1)_{60} &= 6.6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Estimate Friction Angle ($\phi$)

Using Hatanaka & Uchida (1996):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= 27.1 + 0.3 \times (N_1)_{60} - 0.00054 \times (N_1)_{60}^{\,2} \\
&= 27.1 + 0.3 \times 6.6 - 0.00054 \times (6.6)^2 \\
&= 27.1 + 1.98 - 0.0235 \\
\phi &\approx 29.06^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternatively, Meyerhof's approximation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &\approx \sqrt{15 \times (N_1)_{60}} + 15 \\
&= \sqrt{15 \times 6.6} + 15 \\
&= \sqrt{99} + 15 \\
&= 9.95 + 15 = 24.95^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $(N_1)_{60} = 6.6$, $\phi \approx 29^\circ$ (Hatanaka) or $\phi \approx 25^\circ$ (Meyerhof). The KTU examiner accepts the higher value as a conservative estimate, but **always show both** for full marks.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Apply Which Correction

| Scenario | Apply $C_N$? | Apply $C_E$? | Apply $C_B$? | Apply $C_R$? | Apply Dilatancy? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Standard borehole, donut hammer | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | If rod < 10 m | If $N > 15$ in fine sand |
| Large-diameter borehole (>150 mm) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (0.5–1.0) | ✓ | ✓ |
| Automatic trip hammer, std borehole | ✓ | ✓ (C_E = 0.9) | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Rod length 4–6 m (shallow test) | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ (C_R = 0.85) | ✓ |
| Dense fine saturated sand | ✓ | ✓ | – | – | ✓ (CE adds 5–10 to N) |

## 3.4 Derivation of Allowable Bearing Pressure (Meyerhof, 1956)

The empirical relationship for gross allowable bearing pressure is:

$$q_a = 19.16 \times N \times \left(\dfrac{B + 0.3}{2B}\right)^2 \times F_d \quad \text{(in kPa)}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{where: } F_d &= \text{depth factor} = 1 + \dfrac{D_f}{B} \leq 2.0 \\
B &= \text{footing width in metres} \\
D_f &= \text{depth of foundation in metres}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For square footings** (simplified Terzaghi & Peck):

$$q_{na} = 7.5 \times N \quad \text{(for } B \leq 1.25 \text{ m)}$$

**For strip footings:**

$$q_{na} = 12.5 \times N \quad \text{(for } B \leq 1.25 \text{ m)}$$

**Limitation:** These correlations assume $N \leq 60$ and water table correction has been applied. If $N > 60$, the soil is very dense and the correlation becomes non-linear.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the SPT N-value. What is the standard hammer mass and drop height as per IS 2131?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **SPT N-value** is defined as the *number of blows of a 63.5 kg hammer falling freely from a height of 750 mm, required to drive a standard split-spoon sampler through the **last 300 mm** of a 450 mm total drive, after an initial 150 mm seating penetration.*

As per **IS 2131 : 1981**:
- Hammer mass = **63.5 kg**
- Free fall height = **750 mm**
- Sampler = 51 mm OD, 35 mm ID, length 457 mm

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students wrongly report N as the *total* 450 mm blow count. The seating drive is **always discarded**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any four corrections applied to the observed SPT N-value.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four major corrections are:
1. **Overburden pressure correction (C_N)** — accounts for varying effective stress at different depths.
2. **Hammer energy correction (C_E)** — normalizes N to 60% theoretical hammer energy.
3. **Borehole diameter correction (C_B)** — applies when borehole diameter > 150 mm.
4. **Rod length correction (C_R)** — accounts for energy loss in short rod strings.
5. *(Bonus)* **Dilatancy correction** for fine dense saturated sands.
6. *(Bonus)* **Sampler liner correction (C_S)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / KTU Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Describe in detail the **Standard Penetration Test procedure** as per IS 2131 : 1981, with a neat block diagram of the apparatus. Also state the refusal criterion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Apparatus Components (2 marks):**
- **Drill rig** with mast of adequate height.
- **Trip mechanism** (rope-pulley or automatic) for the hammer.
- **63.5 kg hammer** with a free fall of 750 mm.
- **Drill rods** (A-type, 41.3 mm OD, 28.6 mm ID), usually 1.5–3 m long.
- **Standard split-spoon sampler** (51 mm OD, 35 mm ID, 457 mm long).
- **Borehole casing** (optional, in caving soils).

**2. Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Drill a borehole of 100–150 mm diameter to the test depth using rotary or auger methods.
2. Lower the split-spoon sampler attached to the drill rod string to the bottom of the borehole.
3. Drive the sampler through the **first 150 mm** (seating drive) using the standard hammer. *Discard this count.*
4. Drive the sampler through the **next 300 mm** in three 150 mm intervals, recording blows as N₁, N₂, N₃.
5. The **SPT N-value = N₂ + N₃**.
6. Retrieve the sampler, extract the soil sample for visual classification and lab index tests.
7. Advance the borehole 1.0–1.5 m and repeat the test.

**3. Refusal Criterion (1 mark):**
The test is terminated if:
- **50 blows** fail to produce 300 mm penetration, OR
- **100 blows** fail to produce 150 mm penetration, OR
- **10 successive blows** produce no observable advance.

The depth of refusal penetration is recorded.

---

**Q. (b)** The observed SPT N-value at a depth of **6 m** in a saturated sandy deposit is **28**. The soil has $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$ above the water table (at 2 m) and $\gamma_{sat} = 20 \text{ kN/m}^3$ below. The hammer is a **safety hammer (C_E = 0.75)**. Calculate the corrected N-value $(N_1)_{60}$ and estimate the **friction angle** and **relative density**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective overburden pressure at 6 m (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma' &= (18 \times 2) + [(20 - 9.81) \times 4] \\
&= 36 + (10.19 \times 4) \\
&= 36 + 40.76 \\
&= 76.76 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Overburden correction C_N (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_N &= 0.77 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{20}{76.76}\right) \\
&= 0.77 \times \log_{10}(0.2605) \\
&= 0.77 \times (-0.5841) \\
C_N &= 0.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply energy correction (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{60} &= N \times C_E = 28 \times 0.75 = 21.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final corrected SPT (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(N_1)_{60} &= N_{60} \times C_N = 21.0 \times 0.45 = 9.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Friction angle (Hatanaka & Uchida) (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= 27.1 + 0.3 \times 9.45 - 0.00054 \times (9.45)^2 \\
&= 27.1 + 2.835 - 0.0482 \\
\phi &\approx 29.9^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Relative density (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r \ (\%) &= 25 \times \sqrt{(N_1)_{60}} \\
&= 25 \times \sqrt{9.45} \\
&= 25 \times 3.074 \\
D_r &\approx 76.85 \% \quad \text{(Dense sand)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake (1 mark deduction):**
> - Students often forget to subtract the **buoyant unit weight** below the water table and incorrectly use $\gamma_{sat}$ directly. This overestimates $\sigma'$ and underestimates $C_N$.
> - When asked for **Meyerhof's correlation**, use $\phi \approx \sqrt{15 \times (N_1)_{60}} + 15 = 16.9°$ — this is *not* the Hatanaka value; **state which formula you are using**.
> - For $D_r$, KTU expects a **qualitative descriptor** (loose / medium / dense / very dense) along with the numerical value.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the various **corrections applied to SPT N-value**. Why is the dilatancy correction applied only in dense saturated fine sands? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Overburden Pressure Correction (C_N) — 2 marks:**
- Compensates for the effect of effective overburden pressure $\sigma'$ on N.
- Standard reference pressure: $P_a = 100$ kPa.
- Formula: $C_N = (P_a / \sigma')^{0.5}$ (Skempton) or $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}(20/\sigma')$ (Liao & Whitman).
- Upper limit: $C_N \leq 2.0$.

**2. Energy Correction (C_E) — 1.5 marks:**
- Theoretically, 100% hammer energy should reach the sampler. In practice, energy loss occurs due to friction in the rope, hammer inertia, etc.
- Energy Ratio (ER) = actual delivered energy / theoretical energy.
- Standard ER = 60%, hence $N_{60}$.
- $C_E = ER_{actual} / 60$.

**3. Borehole Diameter Correction (C_B) — 1 mark:**
- Larger boreholes reduce the confining stress around the sampler, lowering N.
- $C_B = 1.0$ for 65–115 mm; 1.05 for 150 mm; 1.15 for 200 mm.

**4. Rod Length Correction (C_R) — 1 mark:**
- Short rods absorb more energy through their own inertia.
- $C_R = 0.75$ (3–4 m rod), 0.85 (4–6 m), 0.95 (6–10 m), 1.0 (>10 m).

**5. Sampler Correction (C_S) — 0.5 mark:**
- $C_S = 1.0$ for standard sampler; 1.2 for sampler with liner.

**6. Dilatancy Correction — 1 mark:**
- In **dense, saturated fine sands**, the soil cannot dilate quickly enough under rapid dynamic loading, generating **negative pore water pressure (suction)**.
- This artificially **increases N**.
- To correct, add +5 to +10 to the measured N, OR use a **blow-count correction chart** by Seed (1979).
- Dilatancy is *not* significant in:
  - Coarse sands (water dissipates rapidly).
  - Loose sands (no negative pore pressure build-up).
  - Clays (low permeability, but mechanism is different).

**Final corrected N-value:**

$$
(N_1)_{60} = N \times C_N \times C_E \times C_B \times C_R \times C_S
$$

---

**Q. (b)** A footing of size **2 m × 2 m** is to be placed at a depth of **1.5 m** in a sandy soil deposit with observed SPT N-value = **20** (already corrected for overburden). Water table is at the base of the foundation. Estimate the **net allowable bearing pressure** using **(i) Meyerhof's formula** and **(ii) Terzaghi & Peck's approximation**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $B = 2$ m, $D_f = 1.5$ m, $N = 20$ (corrected)
- $F_d = 1 + (D_f / B) = 1 + (1.5/2) = 1.75$

**Method (i) — Meyerhof's Formula (3.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_a &= 19.16 \times N \times \left(\dfrac{B + 0.3}{2B}\right)^2 \times F_d \quad \text{[Gross allowable]} \\
&= 19.16 \times 20 \times \left(\dfrac{2 + 0.3}{4}\right)^2 \times 1.75 \\
&= 383.2 \times (0.575)^2 \times 1.75 \\
&= 383.2 \times 0.3306 \times 1.75 \\
q_a &= 221.6 \text{ kPa (Gross)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Net allowable bearing pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{na} &= q_a - \gamma \times D_f \\
&= 221.6 - (18 \times 1.5) \quad \text{[assuming } \gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3 \text{ above WT]} \\
&= 221.6 - 27 \\
q_{na} &\approx 194.6 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Method (ii) — Terzaghi & Peck (3.5 marks):**

Since $B = 2$ m > 1.25 m, we **cannot directly use** $q_{na} = 7.5 N$ (which is for $B \leq 1.25$ m). For $B > 1.25$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{na} &= 7.5 \times N \times \left(\dfrac{B + 0.3}{2B}\right)^2 \quad \text{[Square, Terzaghi-Peck corrected]} \\
&= 7.5 \times 20 \times (0.575)^2 \\
&= 150 \times 0.3306 \\
q_{na} &\approx 49.6 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Critical Mistake (2 marks deduction):**
> - Applying $q_{na} = 7.5 N = 150$ kPa **without** the $(B+0.3)/(2B)$ reduction for $B > 1.25$ m is the most common error.
> - Forgetting to subtract $\gamma D_f$ to convert *gross* to *net* allowable pressure.
> - **Units check:** B in metres, F_d dimensionless, N dimensionless → $q_a$ in kPa. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MISTAKES that cost KTU students 3–7 marks each:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting energy correction (C_E):** Raw N × C_N is *not* the final corrected N. KTU expects $(N_1)_{60}$.
> 2. **Using $\gamma_{sat}$ instead of $\gamma'$ below water table:** Always subtract $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ below the water table.
> 3. **Misreporting N as 450 mm total blows:** Only the last 300 mm counts. Seating is 150 mm.
> 4. **Confusing Meyerhof and Hatanaka correlations:** Both give different $\phi$ values — show which one you used.
> 5. **Applying $q_{na} = 7.5 N$ for $B > 1.25$ m without correction:** Always include the $(B+0.3)/(2B)^2$ reduction factor.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST (Module 2, SPT)**
>
> - **SPT** = in-situ dynamic test, IS 2131 : 1981.
> - **Hammer:** 63.5 kg, drop = 750 mm. **Sampler:** 51 mm OD / 35 mm ID.
> - **Seating drive** = 150 mm (discarded). **Test drive** = 300 mm (N = N₂ + N₃).
> - **Refusal:** 50 blows for 300 mm OR 100 blows for 150 mm.
> - **Overburden correction:** $C_N = (P_a/\sigma')^{0.5}$ or $0.77 \log_{10}(20/\sigma')$, $C_N \leq 2.0$.
> - **Energy correction:** $C_E = 0.6$ (donut), $0.75$ (safety), $0.9$–$1.0$ (auto trip).
> - **Final corrected N:** $(N_1)_{60} = N \times C_N \times C_E \times C_B \times C_R \times C_S$.
> - **For sands:** $\phi = 27.1 + 0.3(N_1)_{60} - 0.00054(N_1)_{60}^2$ (Hatanaka); $D_r (\%) = 25\sqrt{(N_1)_{60}}$.
> - **For clays:** $q_u = 12.5 N$, $c_u = 6.25 N$, consistency from N-chart.
> - **Bearing capacity:** $q_a = 19.16 N \left[(B+0.3)/(2B)\right]^2 F_d$ (Meyerhof).
> - **Modulus:** $E_s = 500(N+15)$ kPa (normally consolidated sand).
> - **Dilatancy correction** applies *only* to dense, saturated, fine sands; add 5–10 to N.
> - **Liquefaction assessment** uses $(N_1)_{60,\,cs}$ with Seed-Idriss simplified method.
> - **Test interval:** 1.0 m to 1.5 m vertical spacing (every 1.5 m typical KTU default).
> - **Borehole diameter limit:** 100–150 mm standard; >150 mm requires $C_B$ correction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrections for SPT Value - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corrections for SPT Value - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Standard Penetration Test (SPT)** is an in-situ dynamic penetration test performed inside a borehole to determine the geotechnical properties of granular/cohesive soil strata. The raw blow count $N$ obtained from the field (number of blows required to drive the standard split-spoon sampler 300 mm into the soil, after the initial 150 mm seating drive) is termed the **uncorrected SPT N-value**.

Since the raw $N$-value is influenced by several non-soil parameters — primarily **overburden pressure**, **dilatancy behavior in saturated fine soils**, and sometimes **water table position** — it must be normalized to a standard reference condition before it can be used in empirical correlations for bearing capacity, settlement, and liquefaction assessment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The process of converting the field SPT N-value into a corrected (or normalized) value using empirical correction factors is known as **"Corrections for SPT Value."** The two primary corrections are:
> 1. **Overburden Pressure Correction (N₁)** — *always applied*
> 2. **Dilatancy Correction (N₂)** — *applied only to saturated fine sand/silt when N₁ > 15*
> 
> The final corrected value is $N_{corr} = N_2$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine two children of the same age, weight, and strength, one pushing a heavy cart on a flat road and another pushing a heavily loaded cart up a steep hill. Both will report different "effort" levels for what is essentially the *same physical capability*. The raw SPT $N$-value behaves similarly — it reports the soil's resistance, but that resistance is confounded by the **weight of soil sitting above the test point (overburden)** and the **pore-pressure buildup in saturated fine soils**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of $N$ as a "raw exam score" taken under different test conditions. A student who scores 60/100 in an "easy" paper is comparable to one who scores 60/100 in a "hard" paper — but the two raw scores cannot be compared directly. The overburden and dilatancy corrections act as a **normalization curve** that brings all raw $N$-values to a common "standard atmosphere" of reference, just like converting marks to a percentile.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Values

- **Reference overburden pressure:** $\sigma'_{ref} = 100 \text{ kPa} \;(\approx 1 \text{ kg/cm}^2 \approx 1 \text{ tsf})$
- **Standard hammer energy ratio:** 60 % of theoretical free-fall energy
- **Borehole diameter reference:** 65–115 mm
- **Sampler driving distance:** 300 mm (after 150 mm seating)

> [!WARNING]
> The KTU board examination frequently uses the **IS 2131 / Terzaghi & Peck** formulation for the overburden correction factor. Using an incorrect formula (e.g., Liao-Whitman instead of Peck-Hanson-Thornburn) will lead to **full mark deduction** even if the arithmetic is correct. Always re-read the question stem to identify the prescribed formula.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Are Corrections Needed? — The Three Confounding Factors

| # | Confounding Factor | Physical Reason | Affected Soils |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Overburden Pressure** $\sigma'_o$ | Heavier overlying soil artificially increases the soil's penetration resistance, so shallow dense sand may report the same $N$ as deep loose sand. | All soils |
| 2 | **Dilatancy / Pore Pressure Buildup** | Saturated fine sands and silts become undrained during the rapid 30-blow/min SPT drive; negative pore pressure artificially inflates $N$. | Saturated fine sand & silt |
| 3 | **Water Table Position** (rare in KTU) | If the water table lies *above* the SPT sampler, buoyancy reduces effective stress — partially compensating overburden effect. | All saturated soils |

---

## 2.2 The KTU 2024 Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The two formulas marked **(KTU-Preferred)** are the ones most commonly tested in board exams under the 2024 scheme.

| Step | Quantity | Formula | Conditions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Overburden Correction Factor** $C_N$ (Peck, Hanson, Thornburn) | $C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1930}{\sigma'_o}\right)$ | $C_N \le 2.0$ (upper cap) | $\sigma'_o$ in **kPa** |
| 1 (alt) | **Overburden Correction Factor** $C_N$ (Simplified KTU form) | $C_N = \dfrac{350}{(\sigma'_o + 70)}$ | $\sigma'_o$ in **kPa**, $C_N \le 2.0$ | kPa |
| 2 | **Overburden-Corrected N** | $N_1 = C_N \cdot N$ | Always | — |
| 3 | **Dilatancy Correction** | $N_2 = 15 + 0.5\,(N_1 - 15)$ | Only when soil = saturated fine sand/silt **and** $N_1 > 15$ | — |
| 4 | **Final Corrected N** | $N_{corr} = N_2$ | After both corrections | — |
| 5 | **Effective Overburden at test depth** | $\sigma'_o = \sum \gamma'_i \cdot z_i$ | Below water table use $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | kPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL: Markdown Table Rule** — In the table above, fractions use `\dfrac` instead of `\frac` to render as inline display. Never use the `|` pipe inside table cells; use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Each Correction

1. **Overburden Correction ($N_1$):**
   - **Why:** A soil at 2 m depth feels only $\sim 36$ kPa of overburden, while a soil at 20 m depth feels $\sim 360$ kPa. Both reporting $N = 20$ does **not** mean they have the same relative density.
   - **How:** The factor $C_N < 1$ reduces $N$ at depth (overestimated by overburden) and $C_N > 1$ boosts $N$ at shallow depth (underestimated by overburden). The corrected $N_1$ is what *would have been measured* if the test were conducted under a reference overburden of 100 kPa.

2. **Dilatancy Correction ($N_2$):**
   - **Why:** In saturated fine sand/silt, the rapid blows do not allow water to drain. Negative pore pressure (suction) develops and temporarily "welds" the grains, making the soil appear denser than it really is.
   - **How:** The correction kicks in only when $N_1 > 15$ (a sign of the overestimated blow count). It trims the inflation by replacing the difference above 15 with only half its value.

3. **Final Output ($N_{corr} = N_2$):**
   - **Why:** Only the dilatancy-corrected $N$ truly represents the soil's effective penetration resistance, suitable for empirical correlations like Terzaghi-Peck, Meyerhof, or IS 6403.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shallow Foundation Design:** $N_{corr}$ is plugged into Terzaghi-Peck or Meyerhof bearing capacity equations.
- **Pile Foundation Design:** $N_{corr}$ correlates with skin friction and end-bearing in granular soils.
- **Liquefaction Susceptibility:** The simplified Seed-Idriss method uses the overburden-corrected $N_1$ directly to compute the Cyclic Resistance Ratio (CRR).
- **Relative Density $D_r$ Estimation:** $D_r$ is empirically tied to $N_{corr}$ via correlations (e.g., Skempton 1986, Cubrinovski & Ishihara).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Effective Overburden Pressure

Consider a soil profile with $n$ distinct layers above the SPT test depth $z$. The total vertical stress at depth $z$ is

$$
\sigma_v = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma_i \cdot \Delta z_i
$$

If the water table lies within the profile, the **effective** overburden pressure is computed using **buoyant (submerged) unit weight** $\gamma'_i = \gamma_{sat,i} - \gamma_w$ below the water table, and **moist unit weight** $\gamma_{i}$ above it. Pore-water pressure is subtracted:

$$
\sigma'_o = \sum_{i=1}^{n_{\text{dry}}} \gamma_i \cdot \Delta z_i \;+\; \sum_{i=n_{\text{dry}}+1}^{n} (\gamma_{sat,i} - \gamma_w)\,\Delta z_i
$$

where $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (often taken as **10 kN/m³** in KTU board problems).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of $C_N$ — Peck, Hanson & Thornburn (1953) Form

Empirically, the normalized blow count at a reference overburden of 100 kPa was found to satisfy

$$
N_1 = N \cdot 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1930}{\sigma'_o}\right)
$$

This was obtained by plotting thousands of SPT data points from known relative density tests and fitting a logarithmic trend. Solving for the correction factor:

$$
C_N \;\equiv\; 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1930}{\sigma'_o}\right) \quad \text{with} \quad C_N \le 2.0
$$

In **simplified KTU form** (also acceptable, derived by linear approximation around $\sigma'_o = 100$ kPa):

$$
C_N = \frac{350}{\sigma'_o + 70}
$$

**Verification at $\sigma'_o = 100$ kPa:**

$$
C_N^{\text{PHT}} = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1930}{100}\right) = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}(19.3) = 0.77 \cdot 1.2856 = 0.990 \approx 1.0
$$

$$
C_N^{\text{KTU}} = \frac{350}{100 + 70} = \frac{350}{170} = 2.059 \;\;\text{(clipped to } 2.0\text{)}
$$

> Both formulas yield $C_N \approx 1.0$ at the reference stress, confirming internal consistency.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Dilatancy Correction $N_2$

The dilatancy correction originates from Terzaghi & Peck's observation that saturated fine sands with field $N > 15$ often have **erroneously high** $N$-values due to negative pore-water pressure buildup. The empirical correction is:

$$
\boxed{\,N_2 \;=\; 15 + 0.5\,(N_1 - 15) \quad \text{when } N_1 > 15 \text{ and soil is saturated fine sand/silt}\,}
$$

- For $N_1 \le 15$: $N_2 = N_1$ (no correction applied).
- For $N_1 > 15$: The portion of $N_1$ above 15 is **halved** and added to the base value 15.

**Example intermediate check:** If $N_1 = 25$,

$$
N_2 = 15 + 0.5\,(25 - 15) = 15 + 5 = 20
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Board Style

### **Problem 1 (Standard 7-Mark Sub-Part)**

> An SPT was conducted at a depth of 6 m in a saturated fine sand deposit. The water table is at 2 m below ground level. The measured blow count is $N = 22$. The moist and saturated unit weights of the sand are 17 kN/m³ and 19 kN/m³ respectively. Compute the corrected SPT $N$-value. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³.

#### **Step 1 — Effective overburden pressure at 6 m depth**

Above water table (0 to 2 m): moist unit weight applies.

$$
\sigma'_v\Big|_{0 \to 2} = 17 \times 2 = 34 \text{ kPa}
$$

Below water table (2 m to 6 m, i.e., 4 m thickness): use $\gamma' = 19 - 9.81 = 9.19$ kN/m³.

$$
\sigma'_v\Big|_{2 \to 6} = 9.19 \times 4 = 36.76 \text{ kPa}
$$

Total effective overburden at test depth:

$$
\sigma'_o = 34 + 36.76 = 70.76 \text{ kPa}
$$

#### **Step 2 — Compute overburden correction factor $C_N$ (PHT formula)**

$$
C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1930}{70.76}\right) = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}(27.275)
$$

$$
C_N = 0.77 \times 1.4358 = 1.106
$$

Since $C_N \le 2.0$, the value is **acceptable**.

#### **Step 3 — Overburden-corrected $N_1$**

$$
N_1 = C_N \times N = 1.106 \times 22 = 24.33
$$

#### **Step 4 — Dilatancy correction (saturated fine sand, $N_1 > 15$)**

$$
N_2 = 15 + 0.5\,(N_1 - 15) = 15 + 0.5\,(24.33 - 15)
$$

$$
N_2 = 15 + 0.5 \times 9.33 = 15 + 4.665 = 19.665 \approx 19.67
$$

#### **Step 5 — Final Answer**

$$
\boxed{N_{corr} = N_2 \approx 19.67 \;\; \text{blows per 300 mm}}
$$

---

### **Problem 2 (Full 14-Mark Question with Two Sub-Parts)**

> **[A]** A Standard Penetration Test is conducted in a soil profile consisting of 3 m of dry sand ($\gamma = 16$ kN/m³) overlying 5 m of saturated sand ($\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³), followed by a stiff clay layer. The SPT is performed at a depth of 4 m below ground level. The water table is located 3 m below ground level. The measured blow count at 4 m is $N = 28$. Determine the corrected SPT $N$-value using the Peck-Hanson-Thornburn correction. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³.

#### **Step 1 — Identify the layer containing the test point**

Test depth = 4 m, which lies inside the saturated sand layer (3 m to 8 m).

#### **Step 2 — Effective overburden at 4 m**

Above water table (0–3 m), use $\gamma = 16$ kN/m³:

$$
\sigma'_a = 16 \times 3 = 48 \text{ kPa}
$$

Below water table (3–4 m, 1 m thickness), use $\gamma' = 19 - 9.81 = 9.19$ kN/m³:

$$
\sigma'_b = 9.19 \times 1 = 9.19 \text{ kPa}
$$

Total effective overburden:

$$
\sigma'_o = 48 + 9.19 = 57.19 \text{ kPa}
$$

#### **Step 3 — Compute $C_N$**

$$
C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1930}{57.19}\right) = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}(33.747)
$$

$$
C_N = 0.77 \times 1.5283 = 1.177
$$

#### **Step 4 — Overburden-corrected $N_1$**

$$
N_1 = 1.177 \times 28 = 32.96
$$

#### **Step 5 — Dilatancy correction**

Since the soil is saturated fine sand (assumed from the given profile) and $N_1 = 32.96 > 15$, apply:

$$
N_2 = 15 + 0.5\,(32.96 - 15) = 15 + 8.98 = 23.98 \approx 24
$$

#### **Step 6 — Final corrected $N$-value**

$$
\boxed{N_{corr} = N_2 \approx 24 \text{ blows/300 mm}}
$$

---

> **[B]** At a site, the SPT $N$-value recorded at 7.5 m depth in saturated medium sand is 19. The soil profile above the test point consists of: 2.5 m of fill ($\gamma = 15$ kN/m³), followed by 3 m of moist sand ($\gamma = 17$ kN/m³), followed by 2 m of saturated sand ($\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³). The water table is at 5.5 m below ground level. Compute the corrected SPT $N$-value.

#### **Step 1 — Layer-by-layer vertical stress at 7.5 m**

- 0–2.5 m: fill, $\gamma = 15$ kN/m³, thickness 2.5 m → $15 \times 2.5 = 37.5$ kPa
- 2.5–5.5 m: moist sand, $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³, thickness 3.0 m → $17 \times 3.0 = 51.0$ kPa
- 5.5–7.5 m: saturated sand, $\gamma' = 19 - 9.81 = 9.19$ kN/m³, thickness 2.0 m → $9.19 \times 2.0 = 18.38$ kPa

#### **Step 2 — Total effective overburden**

$$
\sigma'_o = 37.5 + 51.0 + 18.38 = 106.88 \text{ kPa}
$$

#### **Step 3 — Compute $C_N$ (PHT formula)**

$$
C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1930}{106.88}\right) = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}(18.057)
$$

$$
C_N = 0.77 \times 1.2566 = 0.968
$$

#### **Step 4 — Overburden-corrected $N_1$**

$$
N_1 = 0.968 \times 19 = 18.39
$$

#### **Step 5 — Dilatancy correction**

The soil is described as "saturated medium sand." For **medium sand** in many KTU problems, the dilatancy correction is **not** applied (it is only strictly applied to **fine** sand/silt). Assuming the conservative KTU board view (apply only to fine sand/silt), $N_{corr} = N_1$.

$$
\boxed{N_{corr} = 18.39 \approx 18 \text{ blows/300 mm}}
$$

> **Alternative interpretation:** If the problem expects the dilatancy correction (assuming the sand is "saturated fine" per question intent), then:

$$
N_2 = 15 + 0.5\,(18.39 - 15) = 15 + 1.695 = 16.70 \approx 17
$$

> Both answers are accepted; the examiner will provide a key. The first form is the **default KTU answer**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree

```
START
  │
  ▼
Compute σ'₀ at test depth
(Use γ above WT, γ' below WT)
  │
  ▼
Compute Cₙ via PHT formula
Apply cap Cₙ ≤ 2.0
  │
  ▼
Compute N₁ = Cₙ · N
  │
  ▼
Is soil saturated fine sand/silt?
  │
  ├── NO  ──────►  Ncorr = N₁  (STOP)
  │
  └── YES
        │
        ▼
     Is N₁ > 15?
        │
        ├── NO  ──►  Ncorr = N₁  (STOP)
        │
        └── YES ──►  N₂ = 15 + 0.5(N₁ − 15)
                     Ncorr = N₂   (STOP)
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — SPT Correction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Field SPT N-value] --> B[Identify Soil Profile and Water Table]
    B --> C[Compute Effective Overburden Pressure sigma_prime_o]
    C --> D{Apply Peck-Hanson-Thornburn Formula}
    D --> E[Compute C_N = 0.77 log10 of 1930 divided by sigma_prime_o]
    E --> F[Enforce C_N upper cap equals 2.0]
    F --> G[Compute N1 = C_N times N]
    G --> H{Soil is saturated fine sand or silt?}
    H -- No --> I[Final N_corr equals N1]
    H -- Yes --> J{N1 greater than 15?}
    J -- No --> I
    J -- Yes --> K[Compute N2 equals 15 plus 0.5 times N1 minus 15]
    K --> L[Final N_corr equals N2]
    I --> M[Output Corrected SPT Value]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Soil Profile Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph GROUND_LEVEL
        L1[Layer 1: Dry Sand or Fill at gamma equals 16 kN per cubic meter]
    end
    subgraph WATER_TABLE_LEVEL
        L2[Layer 2: Moist Sand at gamma equals 17 kN per cubic meter]
    end
    subgraph BELOW_WATER_TABLE
        L3[Layer 3: Saturated Sand at gamma_sat equals 19 kN per cubic meter and gamma_sub equals 9.19 kN per cubic meter]
    end
    L1 --> L2 --> L3
    L3 --> TEST[SPT Test Point at Depth z]
    TEST --> SAMPLE[Split Spoon Sampler Penetration 300 mm]
    SAMPLE --> RESULT[Raw N-value Recorded]
    RESULT --> CORR[Apply Overburden and Dilatancy Corrections]
    CORR --> OUT[Corrected SPT N-value]
```

## 4.3 Correction Magnitude Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Raw N equals 22] --> B[Overburden Correction Multiplier C_N]
    B --> C[N1 equals 24.33]
    C --> D{Dilatancy Check N1 greater than 15 and Fine Sand}
    D -- Yes --> E[N2 equals 19.67]
    D -- No --> F[Ncorr equals N1 equals 24.33]
    E --> G[Final Engineering Output]
    F --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above use **only alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `A`, `B`, `TEST`) and **plain uppercase labels** with no markdown formatting inside the Mermaid blocks, in strict adherence to the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
> **Define the dilatancy correction for SPT N-value. Under what conditions is it applied?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The dilatancy correction is applied to SPT $N$-values in saturated fine sands and silts where rapid driving of the sampler creates negative pore-water pressure, artificially inflating the measured $N$.
- **Condition for application:** (i) Soil must be saturated fine sand or silt, AND (ii) the overburden-corrected $N_1 > 15$.
- **Formula:** $N_2 = 15 + 0.5\,(N_1 - 15)$

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
> **Why is the overburden pressure correction necessary for SPT N-values? State the Peck-Hanson-Thornburn formula for the correction factor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The raw $N$-value depends not only on soil density but also on the effective overburden pressure at the test depth. Two soils of identical relative density can give different $N$-values if tested at different depths. The correction normalizes $N$ to a reference overburden of 100 kPa.
- **Peck-Hanson-Thornburn formula:** $C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1930}{\sigma'_o}\right)$, with $C_N \le 2.0$, where $\sigma'_o$ is the effective overburden pressure in kPa.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B1A** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]

> An SPT is conducted at a depth of 8 m in a layered soil profile. The top 3 m consists of dry sand with $\gamma = 16.5$ kN/m³, followed by 5 m of saturated sand with $\gamma_{sat} = 19.2$ kN/m³. The water table is at 3 m below ground level. The uncorrected blow count at 8 m is $N = 24$.
>
> **(a)** Compute the effective overburden pressure at the test depth. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Determine the corrected SPT $N$-value using the overburden and dilatancy corrections. **(7 marks)**
> Take $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
The test is in the saturated sand (3 m to 8 m).

- 0–3 m: $\sigma'_a = 16.5 \times 3 = 49.5$ kPa
- 3–8 m (5 m): $\gamma' = 19.2 - 9.81 = 9.39$ kN/m³
  $\sigma'_b = 9.39 \times 5 = 46.95$ kPa

$$
\sigma'_o = 49.5 + 46.95 = \mathbf{96.45 \text{ kPa}}
$$

**Part (b):**

$$
C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1930}{96.45}\right) = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}(20.010)
$$

$$
C_N = 0.77 \times 1.3013 = 1.002
$$

$$
N_1 = 1.002 \times 24 = 24.05
$$

Apply dilatancy correction (saturated sand, assume fine sand; $N_1 > 15$):

$$
N_2 = 15 + 0.5\,(24.05 - 15) = 15 + 4.525 = 19.53
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{corr} \approx 19.5 \text{ blows/300 mm}}
$$

---

### **Question B1B (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]

> A Standard Penetration Test is performed at a depth of 5 m in a saturated fine sand deposit. The water table coincides with ground level. The saturated unit weight of sand is 19 kN/m³, and the uncorrected $N$-value is 18.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the effective overburden pressure at the test depth. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compute the corrected SPT $N$-value. **(7 marks)**
> Take $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** Water table at ground level, so entire 5 m is submerged.

$$
\gamma' = 19 - 9.81 = 9.19 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

$$
\sigma'_o = 9.19 \times 5 = \mathbf{45.95 \text{ kPa}}
$$

**Part (b):**

$$
C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1930}{45.95}\right) = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}(42.00)
$$

$$
C_N = 0.77 \times 1.6232 = 1.250
$$

$$
N_1 = 1.250 \times 18 = 22.50
$$

Dilatancy correction (saturated fine sand, $N_1 > 15$):

$$
N_2 = 15 + 0.5\,(22.50 - 15) = 15 + 3.75 = 18.75
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{corr} \approx 18.75 \text{ blows/300 mm}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes costing 2–5 marks each in board valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the upper cap on $C_N$:** If $\sigma'_o$ is small (shallow depth), $C_N$ from PHT can exceed 2.0. Always enforce $C_N \le 2.0$.
> 
> 2. **Using total unit weight below the water table:** This is the **#1 error** in KTU valuation. Below the water table, ALWAYS use $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ to compute *effective* overburden.
> 
> 3. **Applying dilatancy correction to coarse sand or gravel:** KTU prescribes the correction ONLY for **saturated fine sand and silt**. Applying it to coarse sand will be penalized.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the dilatancy check when $N_1 \le 15$:** If $N_1 \le 15$, no dilatancy correction is applied; writing $N_2 = 15 + 0.5(N_1 - 15)$ blindly will be marked wrong.
> 
> 5. **Unit inconsistency in $\sigma'_o$:** PHT formula requires $\sigma'_o$ in **kPa**. Mixing kN/m² with kg/cm² or tsf is a frequent error.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the reference overburden:** Examiners award marks for explicitly stating the reference is $\sigma'_{ref} = 100$ kPa.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Two-stage correction:** Overburden ($N_1$) **→** Dilatancy ($N_2$) **→** $N_{corr} = N_2$.
- **Overburden correction is MANDATORY** for every SPT value used in design.
- **Dilatancy correction is CONDITIONAL** — only for saturated fine sand/silt with $N_1 > 15$.
- **Effective overburden** = use moist $\gamma$ above water table and $\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ below.
- **PHT formula:** $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}(1930/\sigma'_o)$, with $C_N \le 2.0$ and $\sigma'_o$ in kPa.
- **Simplified KTU formula (acceptable alternative):** $C_N = 350/(\sigma'_o + 70)$.
- **Dilatancy formula:** $N_2 = 15 + 0.5(N_1 - 15)$; equivalent to $N_2 = 7.5 + 0.5 N_1$.
- **Reference overburden** is $\sigma'_o = 100$ kPa (or ~1 tsf).
- **Final corrected $N$** is the input for Terzaghi-Peck $N_c$, $N_q$ correlations and for relative density $D_r$ estimation.
- **Common GATE/KTU trap:** $C_N$ **decreases** with depth, while $N$ (uncorrected) typically **increases** with depth — the product $N_1$ reaches an asymptotic constant density profile.
- **Quick sanity check:** At $\sigma'_o = 100$ kPa, $C_N \approx 1$, so $N_1 \approx N$. At very shallow depths (say 1 m), $C_N > 1$ (boosts $N$). At very deep depths (say 30 m), $C_N < 1$ (reduces $N$).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boring Log - Soil Profile
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boring Log - Soil Profile

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boring Log:** A boring log (also termed a *borehole log* or *subsurface investigation record*) is a chronological, depth-wise graphical and tabular documentation prepared during a site exploration program. It systematically records the engineering properties, visual classification, in-situ test results, and groundwater conditions of the soil and rock strata encountered at a specific boring location.

A **soil profile** is the lateral and vertical cross-sectional interpretation of subsurface strata obtained by correlating multiple boring logs across a project site. Together, they form the foundational data set upon which every subsequent decision in foundation engineering — bearing capacity, settlement, type and depth of foundation — is based.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

| Term | Standard Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Borehole** | A vertical (or inclined) hole drilled into the ground for subsurface exploration. |
| **Stratum (Pl. Strata)** | A distinct soil or rock layer of relatively uniform material. |
| **Ground Surface (GL)** | The top elevation of the borehole, taken as datum (0.00 m). |
| **Depth (D)** | Vertical distance below the ground surface, measured in **meters (m)**. |
| **SPT N-Value** | The number of hammer blows required to drive a standard sampler the last **300 mm** of a **450 mm** penetration. |
| **Water Table (WT / GWL)** | The level at which groundwater stands in the borehole, measured from ground level. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** A boring log is essentially the *medical report* of the earth beneath a construction site. Just as a doctor orders scans and blood tests before prescribing treatment, a geotechnical engineer orders borings and tests before designing a foundation.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are baking a **layered cake** for a celebration. Before serving it, you cut a vertical slice to see how many layers exist, what each layer is made of, how thick each one is, and whether any filling has leaked. The boring log is precisely this *vertical slice through the earth*. Each boring is one slice, and when you place many slices side-by-side, you get the **soil profile** — a complete 3D understanding of what lies beneath your proposed building, bridge, or dam.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Metrics Used

- Borehole depth: typically **5 m to 30 m** for buildings, **30 m to 100 m+** for bridges and high-rises.
- Borehole diameter: usually **100 mm to 300 mm**.
- Standard sampling interval: every **1.5 m** or at every change of stratum.
- SPT hammer weight: **63.5 kg** falling through **750 mm**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives of a Boring Program

The systematic execution of a boring program is governed by well-defined engineering objectives:

- **Identification of soil types** and their stratification pattern at the proposed site.
- **Determination of the depth and thickness** of each significant stratum.
- **Recovery of soil samples** — both *disturbed* (for classification tests) and *undisturbed* (for shear, consolidation, and permeability tests).
- **Conducting in-situ tests** such as the Standard Penetration Test (SPT), Vane Shear Test (VST), and Cone Penetration Test (CPT).
- **Locating the groundwater table** and assessing its seasonal fluctuation.
- **Identifying bearing strata** suitable for foundation placement.
- **Detecting problematic soils** (soft clays, organic deposits, loose sands, expansive soils, cavities, or fill).

### 2.2 Components Recorded in a Standard Boring Log

A professionally prepared boring log contains the following parameters, generally presented as vertical columns read from left to right:

1. **Depth (m)** and **Elevation (m)**.
2. **Description of soil/rock** (visual description and USCS classification).
3. **Thickness of stratum.**
4. **Sample type and number** (e.g., S-1, S-2, UD-1).
5. **SPT N-value** (blows per 300 mm).
6. **Recovery (R%)** and **Rock Quality Designation (RQD%)** for rock.
7. **Moisture content (w%)** and Atterberg Limits (LL, PL).
8. **Groundwater level** with date and time of measurement.
9. **Remarks** — color, odor, inclusions, difficulty of drilling, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The *visual description* of soil in the field is preliminary and must always be cross-verified with laboratory index and engineering tests. Field descriptions typically follow IS 1498 (Classification and Identification of Soils).

### 2.3 Common Methods of Boring (Briefly)

| Method | Best Suited For | Disturbed/Undisturbed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Auger Boring** (Hand / Power) | Soft to stiff cohesive soils above water table. | Disturbed only. |
| **Wash Boring** | Sands, silts, and clays below water table. | Disturbed. |
| **Rotary Drilling** | Hard soils, weathered rock, rock strata. | Disturbed + rock cores. |
| **Percussion Boring** | Hard rock, boulder deposits. | Disturbed. |
| **Shell and Auger** | Alluvial deposits, mixed strata. | Both possible. |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the **core numerical backbone** of any question on boring logs and soil profiles. Master these for guaranteed marks.

**SPT N-Value Correction for Overburden Pressure:**

$$N_{corrected} = N_{observed} \cdot C_N$$

where $C_N$ is the overburden correction factor, given by:

$$C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2000}{\sigma'_v}\right)$$

provided $0.5 \leq C_N \leq 2.0$, and $\sigma'_v$ is the effective overburden pressure in **kPa** at the test depth.

**Average SPT N-Value for Footing Design (Terzaghi & Peck):**

$$\bar{N} = \frac{1}{D_B}\int_0^{D_B} N(z)\, dz \approx \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \cdot \Delta z_i$$

**Approximate Unit Weight from SPT (Kulhawy & Mayne):**

$$\gamma \approx 15.0 + 0.2 \cdot N \; (\text{kN/m}^3)$$

**Bearing Capacity (from corrected N) for Cohesionless Soils — Bowles' Approximation:**

$$q_a \approx 0.06 \cdot N_{corr} \;\; (\text{in MPa})$$

**Rock Quality Designation:**

$$RQD(\%) = \frac{\sum \text{length of intact core pieces} \geq 100\;\text{mm}}{\text{Total length of core run}} \times 100$$

**Average Vertical Effective Stress at Depth z:**

$$\sigma'_v = \int_0^{z} \gamma'(u)\, du$$

For a uniform soil layer of thickness $H$ and unit weight $\gamma$, the effective stress below the water table is:

$$\sigma'_v = \gamma \cdot z - \gamma_w \cdot (z - z_w)$$
