**Non-compressible soil strata** at shallow depth > - **Low compressibility soils** (Modified Compression Index relationships per Vesić) **Telltale signs in the field:** - Sudden, catastrophic failure - Distinct heave of the soil surface adjacent to the footing - Tilting of the foundation - Clear, well-defined failure surface reaching the ground surface ### 2.2 Local Shear Failure (LSF) — When to Expect > [!IMPORTANT] > **Local Shear Failure is expected in:** > - **Medium-dense cohesionless soils** (Dr = 35%–70%, SPT N = 10–30) > - **Medium stiff clays** (cu = 50–100 kPa) > - **Footings at moderate depths** (Df / B ≈ 1 to 2) > - **Sandy clays or silty sands** of medium compressibility > - **Footings resting on layered soils** where the upper layer is moderately compact **Telltale signs in the field:** - Progressive failure - Mild heave at the surface (only immediately adjacent to the footing) - No clear peak on load-settlement curve - Failure plane does not reach the ground surface ### 2.3 Punching Shear Failure (PSF) — When to Expect > [!IMPORTANT] > **Punching Shear Failure is expected in:** > - **Loose cohesionless soils** (Dr < 35%, SPT N < 10) > - **Soft, normally consolidated clays** (cu < 50 kPa) > - **Deep foundations or footings on loose deposits** (Df / B > 4) > - **Highly compressible soils** where the failure surface cannot develop due to large vertical compression > - **Foundations on stratified deposits** with a loose/soft layer overlying a stiff/dense stratum > - **Smooth-based footings** (steel plate footings, very lightly reinforced bases) > - **Footings under dynamic or cyclic loading** in loose sands (liquefaction-prone) **Telltale signs in the field:** - Continuous settlement without a peak - No surface heave observed - The footing progressively punches into the soil - Failure is by vertical shearing along the perimeter of the footing - Large settlement values before failure # 3. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 3.1 Theoretical Background — The Bearing Capacity Framework The classical **Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Equation** for a strip footing under general shear failure is: $$q_u = c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$$ Where: - $q_u$ = ultimate bearing capacity (kN/m²) - $c$ = cohesion of the soil (kN/m²) - $q$ = overburden pressure at the level of the foundation base, $q = \gamma \cdot D_f$ (kN/m²) - $\gamma$ = unit weight of soil (kN/m³) - $B$ = width of the footing (m) - $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors (dimensionless, function of $\phi$) ### 3.2 Terzaghi's Modified Equation for Local Shear Failure For **local shear failure**, Terzaghi proposed modifying the soil strength parameters and the bearing capacity factors: $$c' = \frac{2}{3} \cdot c \quad \text{and} \quad \tan\phi' = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \tan\phi$$ The modified equation becomes: $$q_u = \frac{2}{3} \cdot c \cdot N_c' + q \cdot N_q' + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma'$$ Where $N_c', N_q', N_\gamma'$ are the bearing capacity factors corresponding to the modified friction angle $\phi'$. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Significance of the Modification** > This reduction captures the **partial mobilization of shear strength** in local shear failure. The mechanism does not develop a fully plastic zone; therefore, the soil's full strength cannot be relied upon. Using the unmodified factors would **overestimate** the bearing capacity, leading to unsafe designs. ### 3.3 Vesić's Classification — A More Comprehensive Framework **Vesić (1973)** proposed a refined classification based on the relationship between soil compressibility and failure surface development. He introduced the concept of a **critical rigidity index (Irc)**, below which the failure is by punching shear: $$I_r = \frac{G}{c + \sigma' \cdot \tan\phi} = \frac{E_s}{2 \cdot (1 + \nu) \cdot (c + \sigma' \cdot \tan\phi)}$$ Where: - $I_r$ = rigidity index of the soil - $G$ = shear modulus - $E_s$ = modulus of elasticity - $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio - $\sigma'$ = effective overburden stress at the foundation level The **critical rigidity index** is: $$I_{rc} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{(3.30 - 0.45 \cdot B/L) \cdot \cot(45^\circ - \phi'/2)}$$ Where $B/L$ is the footing shape factor (rectangularity). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vesić's Failure Mechanism Criterion** > - If $I_r \geq I_{rc}$ → **General Shear Failure** is expected. > - If $I_r < I_{rc}$ → **Punching Shear Failure** is expected. > - **Local Shear Failure** lies as a transition between the two extremes. ### 3.4 De Beer's Chart (1970) — Practical Identification **De Beer (1970)** correlated the failure mechanism with the **SPT N-value (corrected for overburden)** and the **depth ratio (Df / B)**. This chart is widely used in practice for cohesionless soils. | SPT N-value (corrected) | Depth Ratio (Df / B) | Expected Failure Mode | |---|---|---| | N > 30 (Dense) | Df / B < 1 | General Shear | | N = 10–30 (Medium) | Df / B = 1–2 | Local Shear | | N < 10 (Loose) | Df / B > 4 | Punching Shear | | N < 10 (Loose) | Df / B < 1 | Transitional (LSF → PSF) | ### 3.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **complete high-yield formula bank** for the topic — memorize and master these for KTU 2024 examinations. | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Conditions / Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Terzaghi's Ultimate Bearing Capacity (GSF) | $q_u = c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$ | General shear failure, strip footing | | 2 | Modified Bearing Capacity (LSF) | $q_u' = (2/3)c \cdot N_c' + q \cdot N_q' + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma'$ | Local shear failure | | 3 | Modified Shear Parameters (LSF) | $c_m = (2/3)c; \quad \phi_m = \tan^{-1}[(2/3)\tan\phi]$ | Used in LSF calculations | | 4 | Overburden Pressure | $q = \gamma \cdot D_f$ | At base of footing | | 5 | Vesić's Rigidity Index | $I_r = E_s / [2(1+\nu)(c+\sigma'\tan\phi)]$ | Classification of failure mode | | 6 | Critical Rigidity Index | $I_{rc} = 0.5 \cdot e^{(3.30 - 0.45B/L)\cot(45^\circ-\phi'/2)}$ | Threshold for GSF vs PSF | | 7 | Shape Factor for $I_{rc}$ | $B/L$ ratio | Rectangular footing correction | | 8 | General Shear Condition | $I_r \geq I_{rc}$ AND dense/stiff soil, Df/B < 1 | GSF expected | | 9 | Punching Shear Condition | $I_r < I_{rc}$ AND loose/soft soil, Df/B > 4 | PSF expected | | 10 | Local Shear Condition | Transitional state | LSF expected | ### 3.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Foundation Design**: Selection of appropriate failure mode ensures neither over- nor under-design of footings. - **Numerical Modelling**: The choice of constitutive model (Mohr-Coulomb with associated flow for GSF vs. non-associated flow for LSF/PSF) directly depends on the failure mechanism expected. - **Site Investigation**: SPT N-values, CPT qc values, and laboratory UU/CU tests are interpreted in light of the expected failure mechanism. - **Retrofit & Repair**: Identifying the failure mechanism post-collapse is essential to selecting the right retrofitting strategy (e.g., micropiles for PSF, geosynthetics for LSF). - **Liquefaction Studies**: Loose sands under cyclic loading — typically associated with PSF — are flagged as liquefaction-prone. --- # 4. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 4.1 Derivation of the Relationship Between Failure Mechanism and Relative Density The relationship between the **SPT N-value (corrected)** and the **internal friction angle ($\phi$)** for cohesionless soils is given by **Peck, Hanson, and Thornburn (1974)**: For $\phi \leq 30^\circ$: $$\phi = 27.1^\circ + 0.3 \cdot N_{corr} - 0.00054 \cdot (N_{corr})^2$$ For $\phi > 30^\circ$: $$\phi = 27.1^\circ + 0.3 \cdot N_{corr}$$ The corrected SPT N-value is: $$N_{corr} = N_{field} \cdot C_N \cdot C_W \cdot C_S \cdot C_R$$ Where the correction factors are: - $C_N$ = overburden correction factor - $C_W$ = water table correction - $C_S$ = sampler correction - $C_R$ = rod length correction The **relative density** is then computed from: $$D_r = \sqrt{\frac{N_{corr}}{0.679 \cdot \sigma'_v / 100}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step-by-Step Application** > > **Step 1**: Compute corrected SPT N-value using all correction factors. > > **Step 2**: Calculate the effective overburden pressure $\sigma'_v$ at the test depth. > > **Step 3**: Compute the relative density $D_r$. > > **Step 4**: Classify the soil as **loose (Dr < 35%)**, **medium (35% < Dr < 70%)**, or **dense (Dr > 70%)**. > > **Step 5**: Cross-reference with depth ratio $D_f / B$ using the De Beer Chart to determine the failure mechanism. ## 4.2 Analytical Matrix — Soil Properties vs. Failure Mechanism The following **analytical matrix** rigorously maps soil and foundation parameters to the expected failure mechanism: | Soil Property / Foundation Parameter | General Shear Failure (GSF) | Local Shear Failure (LSF) | Punching Shear Failure (PSF) | |---|---|---|---| | **Relative Density (Dr)** for sands | Dr > 70% | 35% ≤ Dr ≤ 70% | Dr < 35% | | **Consistency of clay** | Stiff (cu > 100 kPa) | Medium (50 < cu < 100 kPa) | Soft (cu < 50 kPa) | | **SPT N-value (corrected)** | N > 30 | 10 < N < 30 | N < 10 | | **Friction angle ($\phi$)** | $\phi > 38^\circ$ | $28^\circ < \phi \leq 38^\circ$ | $\phi \leq 28^\circ$ | | **Depth ratio (Df / B)** | < 1 | 1 to 2 | > 4 | | **Compressibility** | Low | Medium | High | | **Footing base roughness** | Rough | Rough to semi-rough | Smooth to semi-rough | | **Soil stratification** | Homogeneous | Layered (medium over stiff) | Loose/soft over stiff/dense | | **Loading rate** | Static, monotonic | Static, monotonic | Static, cyclic, dynamic | | **Groundwater table** | Deep (D_w / B > 2) | Moderate (D_w / B ≈ 1) | Shallow (D_w / B < 0.5) | | **Vesić Rigidity Index** | $I_r \geq I_{rc}$ | Transitional | $I_r < I_{rc}$ | ## 4.3 Worked Example: Identifying the Failure Mechanism > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example — Step-by-Step Derivation** > > **Given**: > - Soil: Medium-dense sand > - Corrected SPT N-value: $N_{corr} = 18$ > - Depth of foundation: $D_f = 1.5$ m > - Width of foundation: $B = 2.0$ m > - Unit weight of soil: $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³ > - Cohesion: $c = 0$ (sandy soil) > - Effective overburden at 1.5 m: $\sigma'_v = 17 \times 1.5 = 25.5$ kN/m² > > **Step 1**: Compute relative density > > $$D_r = \sqrt{\frac{N_{corr}}{0.679 \cdot \sigma'_v / 100}} = \sqrt{\frac{18}{0.679 \cdot 25.5 / 100}}$$ > > Computing the denominator: > > $$0.679 \cdot 25.5 / 100 = 0.173145$$ > > Substituting: > > $$D_r = \sqrt{\frac{18}{0.173145}} = \sqrt{103.95} \approx 53.1\%$$ > > **Step 2**: Classify the soil > > Since 35% < 53.1% < 70%, the soil is **medium-dense**. > > **Step 3**: Compute depth ratio > > $$D_f / B = 1.5 / 2.0 = 0.75$$ > > **Step 4**: Cross-reference with De Beer Chart > > For $N_{corr} = 18$ and $D_f / B = 0.75$, the chart indicates **Local Shear Failure (LSF)**. > > **Step 5**: Compute the modified bearing capacity factors > > For $N_{corr} = 18$, the friction angle is: > > $$\phi = 27.1^\circ + 0.3 \times 18 = 27.1^\circ + 5.4^\circ = 32.5^\circ$$ > > Modified friction angle for LSF: > > $$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{2}{3} \cdot \tan(32.5^\circ)\right] = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{2}{3} \times 0.6371\right] = \tan^{-1}(0.4247) = 23.04^\circ$$ > > **Step 6**: Compute modified bearing capacity factors > > For $\phi_m = 23.04^\circ$, using Terzaghi's chart: > - $N_q' \approx 8.0$ > - $N_c' \approx 15.6$ > - $N_\gamma' \approx 2.6$ > > **Step 7**: Compute ultimate bearing capacity (LSF equation) > > $$q_u' = (2/3) \cdot 0 \cdot N_c' + \gamma \cdot D_f \cdot N_q' + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma'$$ > > Substituting values: > > $$q_u' = 0 + (17 \times 1.5 \times 8.0) + (0.5 \times 17 \times 2.0 \times 2.6)$$ > > $$q_u' = 204 + 44.2 = 248.2 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ > > **Result**: Ultimate bearing capacity under LSF = **248.2 kN/m²**. > > If general shear had been assumed (using $\phi = 32.5^\circ$), the values would be $N_q \approx 23.2$, $N_c \approx 35.5$, $N_\gamma \approx 22.0$, leading to: > > $$q_u = (17 \times 1.5 \times 23.2) + (0.5 \times 17 \times 2.0 \times 22.0) = 591.6 + 374.0 = 965.6 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ > > **Conclusion**: Using GSF parameters when the actual mechanism is LSF would **overestimate** the bearing capacity by a factor of nearly **3.9×**, which is unsafe. # 5. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 5.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Failure Mechanism Identification The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision-making process** for identifying the correct failure mechanism based on soil and foundation parameters. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Foundation Design Problem]) --> Soil[Step 1: Identify Soil Type] Soil --> SoilType{Soil Type?} SoilType --> Cohesionless[Cohesionless Sand/Silt] SoilType --> Cohesive[Cohesive Clay/Silty Clay] Cohesionless --> DrCheck[Step 2: Compute Relative Density Dr] DrCheck --> DrCat{Dr Category?} DrCat --> Loose[Dr is less than 35 percent
Loose Sand] DrCat --> Medium[Dr between 35 and 70 percent
Medium Dense Sand] DrCat --> Dense[Dr greater than 70 percent
Dense Sand] Cohesive --> CuCheck[Step 2b: Determine Undrained Cohesion cu] CuCheck --> CuCat{cu Category?} CuCat --> Soft[cu less than 50 kPa
Soft Clay] CuCat --> MedStiff[cu between 50 and 100 kPa
Medium Stiff Clay] CuCat --> Stiff[cu greater than 100 kPa
Stiff Clay] Loose --> DepthCheck{Depth Ratio Df by B?} Medium --> DepthCheck Dense --> DepthCheck Soft --> DepthCheck MedStiff --> DepthCheck Stiff --> DepthCheck DepthCheck --> DfCat{Df by B Value?} DfCat --> Shallow[Df by B less than 1] DfCat --> Moderate[Df by B between 1 and 4] DfCat --> Deep[Df by B greater than 4] Shallow --> Mech1{Mechanism Decision} Moderate --> Mech2{Mechanism Decision} Deep --> Mech3{Mechanism Decision} Mech1 --> GSF[General Shear Failure GSF
Use full Terzaghi equation] Mech2 --> LSF[Local Shear Failure LSF
Use modified factors 2/3 c and 2/3 tan phi] Mech3 --> PSF[Punching Shear Failure PSF
Use Vesić analysis or deep foundation theory] GSF --> Output([Final Bearing Capacity qu]) LSF --> Output PSF --> Output style Start fill:#2E86AB,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Soil fill:#A23B72,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Cohesionless fill:#F18F01,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Cohesive fill:#C73E1D,stroke:#000,color:#fff style DrCheck fill:#6A994E,stroke:#000,color:#fff style CuCheck fill:#6A994E,stroke:#000,color:#fff style DepthCheck fill:#BC4749,stroke:#000,color:#fff style GSF fill:#386641,stroke:#000,color:#fff style LSF fill:#DDA15E,stroke:#000,color:#000 style PSF fill:#BC6C25,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Output fill:#283618,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 5.2 Comparative Schematic — Failure Surface Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph GSF_DIAG[General Shear Failure] G1[Footing] --> G2[Well defined curved failure surface] G2 --> G3[Heave on both sides of footing] G3 --> G4[Sudden catastrophic failure] end subgraph LSF_DIAG[Local Shear Failure] L1[Footing] --> L2[Partial failure surface] L2 --> L3[Minor heave only near footing] L3 --> L4[Progressive failure] end subgraph PSF_DIAG[Punching Shear Failure] P1[Footing] --> P2[Vertical shear zone around footing] P2 --> P3[No surface heave observed] P3 --> P4[Continuous settlement with no peak] end style GSF_DIAG fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20 style LSF_DIAG fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17 style PSF_DIAG fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C style G1 fill:#386641,color:#fff style L1 fill:#DDA15E style P1 fill:#BC6C25,color:#fff ``` ## 5.3 Footing-Soil Interaction Flow — Stress Path ```mermaid flowchart TD Load([Applied Load Q]) --> Stress[Stress Increases Below Footing] Stress --> Path{Stress Path Trajectory?} Path --> Elastic[Elastic Region
Small settlement] Elastic --> Yield[Yield Initiation
Local plasticity] Yield --> Fully[Full Plasticity Reached] Fully --> ModeCheck{Failure Mode Selection} ModeCheck --> Mode1[Dense Sand or Stiff Clay
AND Df by B less than 1] ModeCheck --> Mode2[Medium Dense Sand or Medium Stiff Clay
AND Df by B between 1 and 4] ModeCheck --> Mode3[Loose Sand or Soft Clay
AND Df by B greater than 4] Mode1 --> GSF2[General Shear Failure
Peak at qu with sudden drop] Mode2 --> LSF2[Local Shear Failure
No clear peak, gradual failure] Mode3 --> PSF2[Punching Shear Failure
Continuous settlement to qu] GSF2 --> Peak1[Peak Load qu reached] LSF2 --> Peak2[Local yield at qu with continued settlement] PSF2 --> Peak3[Gradual mobilization with large settlement] Peak1 --> Collapse1[Catastrophic collapse] Peak2 --> Collapse2[Progressive failure] Peak3 --> Collapse3[Serviceability failure] style Load fill:#1A535C,color:#fff style GSF2 fill:#386641,color:#fff style LSF2 fill:#DDA15E style PSF2 fill:#BC6C25,color:#fff style Collapse1 fill:#660708,color:#fff style Collapse2 fill:#A4161A,color:#fff style Collapse3 fill:#BA181B,color:#fff ``` # 6. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 6.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Short-Answer Conceptual Question **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** > **Q1. List the three modes of bearing capacity failure as per Terzaghi. Under what soil and geometric conditions is *punching shear failure* expected?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The three modes of bearing capacity failure as per **Terzaghi (1943)** are: 1. **General Shear Failure (GSF)**: A well-defined failure surface develops and reaches the ground surface. The failure is sudden and accompanied by heave of soil on both sides of the footing. 2. **Local Shear Failure (LSF)**: The failure surface develops only partially, and significant bulging of soil occurs but does not reach the ground surface. The failure is progressive. 3. **Punching Shear Failure (PSF)**: The foundation punches vertically into the soil with no surface heave, accompanied by vertical shearing around the perimeter of the footing. **Conditions for Punching Shear Failure (PSF)**: - **Loose cohesionless soils** with $D_r < 35\%$ (SPT N < 10) - **Soft, normally consolidated clays** with $c_u < 50$ kPa - **Deep foundations** with depth ratio $D_f / B > 4$ - **Highly compressible soils** where failure surface cannot develop - **Smooth-based footings** or in cases of dynamic/cyclic loading --- ### Question 2: Short-Answer Conceptual Question **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2 - Practice Set]** > **Q2. Explain the modification of bearing capacity factors proposed by Terzaghi for *local shear failure*. Why is this modification necessary?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** For local shear failure, Terzaghi proposed modifying the soil shear strength parameters as follows: $$c_m = \frac{2}{3} \cdot c \quad \text{and} \quad \phi_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{3} \cdot \tan\phi\right)$$ The modified bearing capacity factors $N_c', N_q', N_\gamma'$ corresponding to $\phi_m$ are then used in the equation: $$q_u = \frac{2}{3} \cdot c \cdot N_c' + q \cdot N_q' + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma'$$ **Why this modification is necessary**: - In local shear failure, the failure surface does not develop fully. Therefore, the soil's full shear strength cannot be mobilized. - Using the original shear parameters (c and $\phi$) corresponding to general shear failure would **overestimate** the bearing capacity. - The reduction factors (2/3) account for the **partial mobilization of shear strength** and ensure a **safe and realistic** estimate of the bearing capacity. - This modification aligns the analytical prediction with the observed field behavior of medium-dense soils and medium-stiff clays. --- ## 6.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, End Semester Examination]** > **Q3(a). With neat sketches, explain the three modes of bearing capacity failure of shallow foundations as per Terzaghi. List the field situations in which each failure mechanism can be expected. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The three modes of bearing capacity failure of shallow foundations, as classified by **Terzaghi (1943)**, are described below. **1. General Shear Failure (GSF)**: *Sketch*: ``` ↑ ↑ ↑ (Heave of soil on both sides) | | | ________|__|__|________ Ground Surface \\ // \\ // \\ // \\____// (Curved failure surface) | | | | Footing ───── ───── Failure Zone | | ↑ Load ``` **Characteristics**: - A well-defined, continuous failure surface develops below the footing. - The failure surface consists of three zones: (i) a triangular zone directly beneath the footing, (ii) a radial shear zone (log-spiral), and (iii) a passive Rankine zone. - **Sudden, catastrophic failure** with a pronounced peak in the load-settlement curve. - The footing tilts and bulges of soil appear on both sides. **Situations in which GSF is expected**: - Dense cohesionless soils ($D_r > 70\%$, SPT N > 30) - Stiff, overconsolidated clays ($c_u > 100$ kPa) - Shallow foundations ($D_f / B < 1$) - Rough-based footings (cast-in-situ concrete) - Low compressibility soils with a clearly defined failure surface **2. Local Shear Failure (LSF)**: *Sketch*: ``` (Minor heave near footing) ________|__|________ Ground Surface \\ // \\ ? // (Partial failure surface) \\____// | | | | Footing ─────────── Failure Zone (Partial) | | ``` **Characteristics**: - The failure surface develops **only partially** and does not reach the ground surface. - Significant bulging occurs only **immediately adjacent** to the footing. - The failure is **progressive** with no clear peak in the load-settlement curve. - The ultimate load is **lower** than that predicted by GSF analysis. **Situations in which LSF is expected**: - Medium-dense cohesionless soils ($D_r = 35\%-70\%$, SPT N = 10-30) - Medium stiff clays ($c_u = 50-100$ kPa) - Footings at moderate depth ($D_f / B = 1-2$) - Sandy clays or silty sands of medium compressibility - Footings on layered soils (medium over stiff) **3. Punching Shear Failure (PSF)**: *Sketch*: ``` ________________________ Ground Surface (no heave) | | | | (Vertical shear zone) | | Footing (punches down) | | | | ─────────── Failure Zone (Vertical) | | ``` **Characteristics**: - The foundation **punches vertically downward** into the soil. - No bulging or heave is observed on the ground surface. - Failure occurs by **vertical shearing** along the perimeter of the footing. - The load-settlement curve shows **continuous, large settlements** with no clear peak. **Situations in which PSF is expected**: - Loose cohesionless soils ($D_r < 35\%$, SPT N < 10) - Soft, normally consolidated clays ($c_u < 50$ kPa) - Deep foundations ($D_f / B > 4$) - Highly compressible soils - Foundations on stratified deposits with a loose/soft layer over a stiff/dense stratum - Smooth-based footings or under dynamic/cyclic loading --- > **Q3(b). A square footing of 2 m × 2 m is to be placed at a depth of 1.5 m in a medium-dense sand with the following properties: $\gamma = 17.5$ kN/m³, $\phi = 32°$, $c = 0$. The corrected SPT N-value is 18. Determine the failure mechanism and compute the ultimate bearing capacity using the appropriate theory. (7 Marks)** **Given**: - Footing: Square, $B = L = 2$ m - Depth: $D_f = 1.5$ m - Soil: Medium-dense sand - $\gamma = 17.5$ kN/m³, $\phi = 32°$, $c = 0$ - Corrected SPT N-value: $N_{corr} = 18$ **Step 1: Identify the Failure Mechanism** Since the soil is medium-dense sand (SPT N = 18, between 10 and 30) and $D_f / B = 1.5 / 2 = 0.75$ (less than 1, but the soil is medium-dense), the expected mechanism is **Local Shear Failure (LSF)** as per the De Beer Chart. Alternatively, for $N_{corr} = 18$, the relative density is: $$D_r = \sqrt{\frac{18}{0.679 \times 17.5 \times 1.5 / 100}} = \sqrt{\frac{18}{0.1783}} = \sqrt{100.96} \approx 60\%$$ Since 35% < 60% < 70%, the soil is **medium-dense** → **Local Shear Failure (LSF)**. **Step 2: Compute Modified Friction Angle** $$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{3} \tan 32°\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{3} \times 0.6249\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.4166) = 22.62°$$ **Step 3: Determine Modified Bearing Capacity Factors** For $\phi_m = 22.62°$, using Terzaghi's chart: - $N_q' \approx 7.7$ - $N_\gamma' \approx 2.5$ - $N_c' \approx 15.3$ (not required since c = 0) **Step 4: Compute Overburden Pressure** $$q = \gamma \cdot D_f = 17.5 \times 1.5 = 26.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 5: Compute Ultimate Bearing Capacity (Square Footing)** For a square footing, Terzaghi's equation is: $$q_u = 1.3 \cdot c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.4 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$$ Applying with modified factors (c = 0): $$q_u' = 0 + q \cdot N_q' + 0.4 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma'$$ $$q_u' = 26.25 \times 7.7 + 0.4 \times 17.5 \times 2.0 \times 2.5$$ $$q_u' = 202.13 + 35.0 = 237.13 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Result**: Ultimate bearing capacity under LSF ≈ **237.13 kN/m²**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often forget to use the shape factor (1.3 for $N_c$ and 0.4 for $N_\gamma$) for square footings. KTU examiners deduct 1-2 marks if the strip footing formula is used directly. > - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to apply the modified factors (2/3) for local shear failure. Always confirm the failure mode **first** before computing the bearing capacity. > - **Common Mistake 3**: Misreading the De Beer chart. For medium-dense sand, the failure mechanism depends on **both** the SPT N-value and the depth ratio. If only the SPT N-value is considered, the answer may be wrong. > - **Common Mistake 4**: Using $N_\gamma$ from a general shear chart when the modified $N_\gamma'$ for local shear should be used. Always re-evaluate the friction angle first, then read the corresponding bearing capacity factors from the chart. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, End Semester Examination]** > **Q4(a). Explain Vesić's classification of bearing capacity failure mechanisms. How is the rigidity index ($I_r$) used to determine the failure mode? List the field situations for each mechanism as per Vesić. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Vesić's Classification (1973)**: Vesić proposed a more rigorous classification of bearing capacity failure mechanisms based on the **rigidity index** ($I_r$) of the soil and the **critical rigidity index** ($I_{rc}$). This framework is more comprehensive than Terzaghi's classical classification. **Rigidity Index ($I_r$)**: $$I_r = \frac{G}{c + \sigma' \cdot \tan\phi} = \frac{E_s}{2 \cdot (1 + \nu) \cdot (c + \sigma' \cdot \tan\phi)}$$ Where: - $G$ = shear modulus of soil - $E_s$ = modulus of elasticity - $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio - $c$ = cohesion - $\sigma'$ = effective overburden pressure at foundation level - $\phi$ = angle of internal friction **Critical Rigidity Index ($I_{rc}$)**: $$I_{rc} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{(3.30 - 0.45 \cdot B/L) \cdot \cot(45^\circ - \phi/2)}$$ **Failure Mechanism Criterion**: | Condition | Failure Mechanism | |---|---| | $I_r \geq I_{rc}$ | **General Shear Failure (GSF)** | | $I_r < I_{rc}$ | **Punching Shear Failure (PSF)** | | Transitional state | **Local Shear Failure (LSF)** | **Vesić's Field Situations for Each Mechanism**: **1. General Shear Failure (GSF) — $I_r \geq I_{rc}$**: - Dense sands and stiff, overconsolidated clays - Low compressibility soils - Shallow foundations ($D_f / B < 1$) - Soils with high shear modulus relative to confining stress **2. Local Shear Failure (LSF) — Transitional**: - Medium-dense sands and medium-stiff clays - Medium compressibility soils - Footings at moderate depth ($D_f / B = 1-2$) **3. Punching Shear Failure (PSF) — $I_r < I_{rc}$**: - Loose sands and soft, normally consolidated clays - High compressibility soils - Deep foundations ($D_f / B > 4$) - Soils with low shear modulus relative to confining stress - Footings on layered deposits with a compressible upper layer --- > **Q4(b). A strip footing of 3 m width is placed at 1 m depth in a soft clay with $c_u = 25$ kPa, $\phi = 0°$, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. The shear modulus $G = 5000$ kN/m². Determine the expected failure mechanism using Vesić's framework. (7 Marks)** **Given**: - Footing: Strip footing, $B = 3$ m - Depth: $D_f = 1$ m - Soil: Soft clay - $c_u = 25$ kPa, $\phi = 0°$, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³ - $G = 5000$ kN/m² **Step 1: Compute Effective Overburden Pressure at Foundation Level** $$\sigma' = \gamma \cdot D_f = 18 \times 1 = 18 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 2: Compute Rigidity Index ($I_r$)** For $\phi = 0°$: $$I_r = \frac{G}{c_u + \sigma' \cdot \tan(0°)} = \frac{G}{c_u} = \frac{5000}{25} = 200$$ **Step 3: Compute Critical Rigidity Index ($I_{rc}$)** For $\phi = 0°$: $$\cot\left(45° - \frac{0°}{2}\right) = \cot(45°) = 1.0$$ For a strip footing: $B/L = 3/3 = 1.0$ (using $L \to \infty$, $B/L \to 0$ for strip): $$I_{rc} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{(3.30 - 0.45 \times 0) \times 1.0} = 0.5 \cdot e^{3.30}$$ $$I_{rc} = 0.5 \times 27.11 = 13.56$$ **Step 4: Compare $I_r$ and $I_{rc}$** $$I_r = 200 \geq I_{rc} = 13.56$$ **Conclusion**: Since $I_r \gg I_{rc}$, the failure mechanism is **General Shear Failure (GSF)**. > [!NOTE] > **Note**: Even though the clay is "soft" (c_u = 25 kPa), the **low shear modulus** combined with the **low cohesion** results in a rigidity index that is still high enough to develop a general shear failure mechanism. The classification depends on the **relative** values of $I_r$ and $I_{rc}$, not on the absolute values of $c_u$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often classify the failure mechanism based on $c_u$ alone (e.g., "soft clay → punching shear"). This is **incorrect**. Vesić's framework requires computing both $I_r$ and $I_{rc}$ and comparing them. > - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to use effective overburden pressure $\sigma'$ in the rigidity index formula, not total stress. For clays below the water table, the effective stress is critical. > - **Common Mistake 3**: For a strip footing, students sometimes use $B/L = 0.5$ instead of $B/L = 0$ (since $L \to \infty$). The correct value for a strip footing is $B/L = 0$. --- ## 6.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Failure Mechanisms** > > ☐ **Three Modes of Failure**: General Shear (GSF), Local Shear (LSF), Punching Shear (PSF) — must be memorized with field situations. > > ☐ **General Shear Failure**: Dense sand ($D_r > 70\%$), stiff clay ($c_u > 100$ kPa), $D_f / B < 1$, rough footings, sudden catastrophic failure with surface heave. > > ☐ **Local Shear Failure**: Medium-dense sand ($35\% < D_r < 70\%$), medium-stiff clay ($50 < c_u < 100$ kPa), $D_f / B = 1-2$, progressive failure with partial failure surface. > > ☐ **Punching Shear Failure**: Loose sand ($D_r < 35\%$), soft clay ($c_u < 50$ kPa), $D_f / B > 4$, smooth footings, vertical punching, large settlement. > > ☐ **Terzaghi's Modification for LSF**: Always use $c_m = (2/3)c$ and $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}[(2/3)\tan\phi]$; re-read bearing capacity factors from the chart for the modified $\phi_m$. > > ☐ **Vesić's Rigidity Index**: $I_r = E_s / [2(1+\nu)(c+\sigma'\tan\phi)]$; critical rigidity index $I_{rc} = 0.5 \cdot e^{(3.30-0.45B/L)\cot(45°-\phi/2)}$. > > ☐ **Vesić's Criterion**: $I_r \geq I_{rc}$ → GSF; $I_r < I_{rc}$ → PSF; Transitional → LSF. > > ☐ **De Beer Chart**: Cross-reference corrected SPT N-value with depth ratio ($D_f / B$) to identify the failure mechanism for cohesionless soils. > > ☐ **Footing Shape Factors** (Terzaghi): Square — $N_c$ × 1.3, $N_\gamma$ × 0.4, $N_q$ × 1.0; Circular — $N_c$ × 1.3, $N_\gamma$ × 0.3, $N_q$ × 1.0; Strip — all × 1.0. > > ☐ **Always confirm the failure mode FIRST, then compute the bearing capacity** using the appropriate equation and factors. > > ☐ **Vesić's framework is more rigorous** than Terzaghi's classical classification and is preferred for modern design. > > ☐ **For LSF/PSF, never use unmodified shear strength parameters** — overestimation can lead to catastrophic failure.

topics.title: Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Theory for Strip Footing (No Derivation Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Theory for Strip Footing (No Derivation Required) # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Theory** (1943) is the classical, pioneering analytical method in geotechnical engineering used to compute the **ultimate bearing capacity (q_u)** of a shallow strip footing resting on a homogeneous, isotropic, c-φ (cohesive-frictional) soil mass. The theory models the soil beneath a continuously loaded strip footing as failing along a well-defined three-zone failure mechanism comprising an **active zone (Zone I)**, a **radial shear zone (Zone II)**, and a **passive zone (Zone III)**. The soil is assumed to behave as a **rigid-plastic material** governed by the **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** This topic falls under **Module 2 – Bearing Capacity of Shallow Foundations**. The university expects students to memorize the **final equation**, list the **assumptions**, identify the **failure zones**, compute **bearing capacity factors (N_c, N_q, N_γ)**, and apply **shape and local-shear correction factors** to extend the strip-footing solution to square, rectangular, and circular footings. **Derivation is officially excluded from the assessment.** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🍕 The Pizza Analogy (Easy Memory Trick):** > Imagine pressing a flat, wide object (a **cutting board**, say) down onto a thick layer of soft dough or sand. The board is the **footing**, and the sand is the **soil**. As you press harder, the sand directly beneath the board tries to **escape sideways**. It cannot go through the board, so it bulges upward and sideways along curved slip surfaces. The board is being held up by three "regions" of resistance: > - A **triangular wedge** of soil directly under the board (this is the *active Zone I* that pushes downward and tries to move with the footing). > - A **curved shear zone** of soil that flows sideways (the *radial Zone II* — like a revolving door). > - A **large mass of soil** on the sides that resists being pushed outward (the *passive Zone III*). > > The footing will fail when the **combined resistance** of these three zones is overcome. **Terzaghi's equation simply quantifies this resistance using soil cohesion (c), surcharge (q), and unit weight (γ).** ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Unit weight of soil | **γ** | **kN/m³** | Weight per unit volume of soil | | Cohesion of soil | **c** | **kN/m²** | Inter-particle electrochemical bond strength | | Footing width | **B** | **m** | Smaller plan dimension of the foundation | | Depth of footing | **D_f** | **m** | Depth of foundation below ground surface | | Surcharge pressure | **q = γ·D_f** | **kN/m²** | Effective overburden pressure at base level | | Angle of internal friction | **φ** | **degrees (°)** | Shearing resistance from grain-to-grain friction | # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — How the Theory Works (Step-by-Step Reasoning) Terzaghi assumed that the soil beneath a **strip footing** (length L → ∞, width B) fails along a **prescribed failure surface**, which is a simplification of the actual complex slip surface. The reasoning is built as follows: - **Step 1 — Wedge Logic:** Directly beneath the footing, soil is confined and cannot fail vertically. Instead, a **triangular elastic wedge (Zone I)** forms, with its inclined faces making angles of **φ** with the horizontal. This wedge acts as part of the footing and pushes downward. - **Step 2 — Radial Transition:** The downward-moving wedge is resisted by soil that must shear along **logarithmic spiral / curved surfaces** (Zone II). Here the principal stress directions rotate gradually, hence the name *radial shear zone*. - **Step 3 — Passive Resistance:** The radial shear zone terminates in a large **passive Rankine zone (Zone III)** that is pushed up and outward, eventually reaching the ground surface and producing a **heave**. - **Step 4 — Equilibrium:** Applying the static equilibrium of forces (using Mohr-Coulomb failure along every surface), Terzaghi obtained a closed-form expression for the ultimate load the soil can carry per unit area of the footing. > [!NOTE] > **Why the Three Zones Matter:** This failure mechanism is a **lower-bound idealization** that converts a complex 3-D plasticity problem into a 1-D bearing capacity expression involving only **three dimensionless numbers** (N_c, N_q, N_γ) and the basic soil properties (c, q, γ, B). This elegance is why it is the **cornerstone of all foundation design** taught worldwide. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **🎯 MEMORIZE THIS BLOCK — It appears in 80%+ of KTU numerical questions.** ### **A. Ultimate Bearing Capacity of a Strip Footing (Terzaghi's Equation)** $$ q_{u(\text{strip})} \;=\; c\,N_{c} \;+\; q\,N_{q} \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_{\gamma} $$ Where the surcharge is $q = \gamma \cdot D_{f}$ (in case of soil above footing base). ### **B. Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Factors (Functions of φ only)** $$ N_{q} \;=\; \frac{e^{\,2\pi \tan\phi}\,\big(1+\sin\phi\big)^{2}}{1-\sin\phi} \cdot \frac{1}{2} $$ Or equivalently (more common form): $$ N_{q} \;=\; \frac{a^{2}}{2\,(1-\sin\phi)} \quad \text{where} \quad a = e^{\pi \tan\phi}\tan\!\left(45^{\circ}+\tfrac{\phi}{2}\right) $$ $$ N_{c} \;=\; \big(N_{q}-1\big)\cot\phi $$ $$ N_{\gamma} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\big(K_{p}\,\cos^{2}\phi - 1\big)\tan\phi \quad \text{(approximate form widely used)} $$ Where $K_{p} = \tan^{2}\!\left(45^{\circ}+\tfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ is the Rankine passive earth-pressure coefficient. ### **C. Standard Bearing Capacity Factor Table (for Quick Reference)** | φ (°) | N_c | N_q | N_γ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 5.70 | 1.00 | 0.00 | | 5 | 7.30 | 1.60 | 0.50 | | 10 | 9.60 | 2.70 | 1.20 | | 15 | 12.90 | 4.40 | 2.50 | | 20 | 17.70 | 7.40 | 5.00 | | 25 | 25.10 | 12.70 | 9.70 | | 30 | 37.20 | 22.50 | 19.70 | | 35 | 57.80 | 41.40 | 42.40 | | 40 | 95.70 | 81.30 | 100.40 | | 45 | 172.30 | 173.30 | 297.50 | ### **D. Terzaghi's Shape Correction Factors (for Square, Rectangular, Circular Footings)** For **square** footing: $$ q_{u} \;=\; 1.3\,c\,N_{c} \;+\; q\,N_{q} \;+\; 0.4\,\gamma\,B\,N_{\gamma} $$ For **circular** footing: $$ q_{u} \;=\; 1.3\,c\,N_{c} \;+\; q\,N_{q} \;+\; 0.3\,\gamma\,B\,N_{\gamma} $$ For **rectangular** footing ($B \times L$): $$ q_{u} \;=\; c\,N_{c}\!\left(1+0.3\tfrac{B}{L}\right) + q\,N_{q} + 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_{\gamma}\!\left(1-0.2\tfrac{B}{L}\right) $$ ### **E. Local Shear Failure Modification (for soft/loose soils)** When the soil is suspected to fail by **local shear** (not general shear), Terzaghi recommended using **reduced** strength parameters: $$ c' \;=\; \tfrac{2}{3}\,c \qquad \phi' \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\tfrac{2}{3}\tan\phi\right) $$ The bearing capacity factors are then computed at the **modified** $\phi'$. ### **F. Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity** $$ q_{nu} \;=\; q_{u} \;-\; \gamma D_{f} $$ ### **G. Safe / Allowable Bearing Capacity** $$ q_{s} \;=\; \frac{q_{nu}}{F} \;+\; \gamma D_{f} $$ Where **F** is the factor of safety (typically **F = 3** for buildings as per IS 6403 / IS 1904). ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Building Footing Design:** Determines the **safe load per unit area** a soil can carry, directly controlling the **size of the footing**. - **Bridge Pier Foundations:** Verifies that piers on spread footings will not punch through soft alluvium. - **Tower & Wind-Mill Bases:** Critical for tall structures where settlement control is essential. - **Dam Foundations:** Used as a preliminary check before detailed slip-circle analysis. - **Industrial Floors & Storage Tanks:** Ensures that heavy equipment loads do not cause bearing failure. # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Directive — "No Derivation Required"** means students are **NOT** expected to reproduce the classical Terzaghi derivation in the exam. However, a clear **conceptual walkthrough** of how the three bearing-capacity terms arise is provided below for **conceptual clarity**, followed by an **exhaustive numerical worked example** that forms the bulk of typical KTU problems. ## 3.1 Conceptual Walkthrough of the Three Terms in the Equation The three terms $cN_c$, $qN_q$, and $0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$ have **distinct physical origins**: | Term | Physical Origin | Dominant Soil Type | |---|---|---| | $c\,N_c$ | **Cohesive resistance** — bonding of soil particles resists shearing along the slip surface. | Pure clay (φ = 0) | | $q\,N_q$ | **Surcharge (overburden) resistance** — weight of soil adjacent to the footing provides a confining normal stress on the failure planes. | Sandy soils with φ large | | $0.5\,\gamma B\,N_\gamma$ | **Self-weight (depth) resistance** — weight of the soil wedge below the footing provides resistance proportional to footing width. | Sandy soils with φ large | The dimensionless factors $N_c$, $N_q$, $N_\gamma$ encode the **geometry of the failure surface** and the **frictional strength parameter φ**. ## 3.2 How the Bearing Capacity Factors are Derived (Logical Outline Only) For a **strip footing under vertical, centric load**, Terzaghi analyzed each of the three zones using the **Mohr-Coulomb yield condition** $\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$: - **Zone I (Active Wedge):** The wedge ABC is in **active Rankine state**. The major principal stress is vertical (equal to $q_u$ at the footing base). The minor principal stress acts on the inclined faces. Mohr-circle geometry yields the stress on the inclined plane at angle φ. - **Zone III (Passive Wedge):** The passive Rankine state gives a horizontal major principal stress $\sigma_p$ related to the vertical stress through $K_p$, the passive earth-pressure coefficient. - **Zone II (Radial Shear):** This is the region between the two wedges where slip surfaces are **logarithmic spirals**. The curved boundary integrates to a force resultant that, combined with vertical equilibrium of the wedge, produces the final expression. - **Vertical Equilibrium** of the footing base (sum of vertical forces = applied load) gives: $$ q_{u} \cdot B \;=\; \underbrace{2\,P_{p}\cos(\phi-\alpha)}_{\text{passive resistance}} \;+\; \underbrace{c B \tan\phi}_{\text{cohesion term}} \;+\; \underbrace{0.5\,\gamma B^{2}K_{p}\sin^{2}\!\phi}_{\text{self-weight term}} \;+\; \underbrace{\gamma D_{f} B N_{q}}_{\text{surcharge term}} $$ After simplification, the standard form $q_u = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$ emerges. > [!TIP] > **KTU Tip:** You do **not** need to reproduce the above algebra. Memorize the final equation and the assumption list. Focus your energy on **solving numericals** — that is where marks are scored. ## 3.3 Fully Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem (KTU University Exam Pattern — 14 Marks):** > A strip footing of width **B = 2 m** is to be placed at a depth of **D_f = 1.5 m** in a soil with unit weight **γ = 18 kN/m³**, cohesion **c = 15 kN/m²**, and angle of internal friction **φ = 25°**. Calculate the **ultimate bearing capacity** and the **net safe bearing capacity** with a **factor of safety F = 3**. ### **Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step** **Step 1 — Calculate the surcharge pressure (overburden) at the level of the footing base.** $$ q \;=\; \gamma \cdot D_{f} \;=\; 18 \times 1.5 \;=\; 27 \;\text{kN/m}^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Identify the bearing capacity factors for φ = 25° (from the standard table).** $$ N_{c} \;=\; 25.1 \qquad N_{q} \;=\; 12.7 \qquad N_{\gamma} \;=\; 9.7 $$ *(If a table is not permitted in the exam, students are expected to compute these using the formulas in Section 2.2.)* **Step 3 — Apply Terzaghi's ultimate bearing capacity equation for a strip footing.** $$ q_{u} \;=\; c\,N_{c} \;+\; q\,N_{q} \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_{\gamma} $$ Substituting the numerical values: $$ q_{u} \;=\; (15)(25.1) \;+\; (27)(12.7) \;+\; 0.5 \times 18 \times 2 \times 9.7 $$ $$ q_{u} \;=\; 376.5 \;+\; 342.9 \;+\; 174.6 $$ $$ \boxed{q_{u} \;=\; 894.0 \;\text{kN/m}^{2}} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the net ultimate bearing capacity.** $$ q_{nu} \;=\; q_{u} \;-\; \gamma\,D_{f} \;=\; 894.0 \;-\; 27.0 \;=\; 867.0 \;\text{kN/m}^{2} $$ **Step 5 — Apply the factor of safety to obtain the net safe bearing capacity.** $$ q_{ns} \;=\; \frac{q_{nu}}{F} \;=\; \frac{867.0}{3} \;=\; 289.0 \;\text{kN/m}^{2} $$ **Step 6 — Compute the gross safe bearing capacity (if asked).** $$ q_{s} \;=\; q_{ns} \;+\; \gamma\,D_{f} \;=\; 289.0 \;+\; 27.0 \;=\; 316.0 \;\text{kN/m}^{2} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer (for the 14-mark question):** > - Ultimate bearing capacity $q_u = 894.0$ kN/m² > - Net ultimate bearing capacity $q_{nu} = 867.0$ kN/m² > - Net safe bearing capacity $q_{ns} = 289.0$ kN/m² > - Gross safe bearing capacity $q_{s} = 316.0$ kN/m² ## 3.4 Worked Example for Local Shear Failure > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** The same soil as above is in a **loose state** and is expected to fail by **local shear**. Determine the modified ultimate bearing capacity. **Step 1 — Compute the reduced (modified) strength parameters.** $$ c^{*} \;=\; \tfrac{2}{3}\,c \;=\; \tfrac{2}{3} \times 15 \;=\; 10 \;\text{kN/m}^{2} $$ $$ \phi^{*} \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\tfrac{2}{3}\tan 25^{\circ}\right) \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\tfrac{2}{3} \times 0.4663\right) \;=\; \tan^{-1}(0.3109) \;\approx\; 17.3^{\circ} $$ **Step 2 — Read the bearing capacity factors at φ* = 17.3° (interpolating from the table).** For φ = 15°: N_c = 12.9, N_q = 4.4, N_γ = 2.5 For φ = 20°: N_c = 17.7, N_q = 7.4, N_γ = 5.0 Linear interpolation at φ* = 17.3° (≈ 2.3° above 15°, span 5°): $$ N_{c}^{*} \;\approx\; 12.9 + \tfrac{2.3}{5}(17.7-12.9) \;=\; 12.9 + 0.46 \times 4.8 \;\approx\; 15.11 $$ $$ N_{q}^{*} \;\approx\; 4.4 + \tfrac{2.3}{5}(7.4-4.4) \;=\; 4.4 + 0.46 \times 3.0 \;\approx\; 5.78 $$ $$ N_{\gamma}^{*} \;\approx\; 2.5 + \tfrac{2.3}{5}(5.0-2.5) \;=\; 2.5 + 0.46 \times 2.5 \;\approx\; 3.65 $$ **Step 3 — Apply the modified equation.** $$ q_{u}^{*} \;=\; c^{*}N_{c}^{*} \;+\; q\,N_{q}^{*} \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_{\gamma}^{*} $$ $$ q_{u}^{*} \;=\; (10)(15.11) \;+\; (27)(5.78) \;+\; 0.5 \times 18 \times 2 \times 3.65 $$ $$ q_{u}^{*} \;=\; 151.1 \;+\; 156.06 \;+\; 65.7 \;=\; 372.86 \;\text{kN/m}^{2} $$ > [!TIP] > **Observation:** The local-shear ultimate bearing capacity ($q_u^* = 372.9$ kN/m²) is **less than half** of the general-shear value ($q_u = 894.0$ kN/m²). This is why **identifying the failure mode** is critical — assuming general shear on loose soil leads to **unsafe, over-optimistic** designs. # 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Failure Zone Architecture Under a Strip Footing ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Applied Load per unit length
P per B at footing base] --> B[Soil Reaction q_u
Ultimate Bearing Pressure] B --> C[Active Wedge Zone I
Triangular wedge angle phi with horizontal] B --> D[Radial Shear Zone II
Curved log-spiral slip surface] B --> E[Passive Wedge Zone III
Pushed upward and outward heave] C --> F[Force resultant cN_c term
Cohesion resistance] D --> G[Force resultant qN_q term
Surcharge overburden resistance] E --> H[Force resultant 0.5 gamma B N_gamma term
Self-weight resistance] F --> I[Equilibrium Check
Sum of vertical forces equals P] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Final Equation q_u equals cN_c plus qN_q plus 0.5 gamma B N_gamma] J --> K[Apply Shape Factors
Square Circular Rectangular] K --> L[Apply Local Shear Reduction
c becomes 2c over 3 phi becomes modified] L --> M[Final Design Bearing Capacity
q_s equals q_nu by F plus gamma D_f] ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** The applied load (top) is resisted by **three independent but interacting** soil mechanisms (middle). Each mechanism gives rise to **one of the three terms** in Terzaghi's equation. After **equilibrium**, the equation is **post-processed** with shape and local-shear corrections (bottom-right) to produce the final allowable bearing capacity used in foundation design. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Design Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Input: Soil Properties c phi gamma] --> B1[Step 1: Compute q equals gamma D_f] B1 --> C1[Step 2: Look up or compute N_c N_q N_gamma] C1 --> D1[Step 3: Compute q_u strip] D1 --> E1[Step 4: Decide shape of footing] E1 --> F1[Square: q_u sq equals 1.3 c N_c plus q N_q plus 0.4 gamma B N_gamma] E1 --> F2[Circular: q_u circle equals 1.3 c N_c plus q N_q plus 0.3 gamma B N_gamma] E1 --> F3[Rectangular: shape factors 1 plus 0.3 B by L and 1 minus 0.2 B by L] F1 --> G1[Step 5: Decide failure mode] F2 --> G1 F3 --> G1 G1 --> H1[General Shear: use original c and phi] G1 --> H2[Local Shear: use 2c over 3 and modified phi] H1 --> I1[Step 6: Compute q_nu equals q_u minus gamma D_f] H2 --> I1 I1 --> J1[Step 7: Apply F equals 3 to get q_s] J1 --> K1[Output: Safe Bearing Capacity for Footing Design] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Utility:** This flowchart is an excellent **mnemonic device** to recall the 7-step sequence in any numerical problem. Memorize the order: **Input → Surcharge → BC Factors → q_u → Shape → Failure Mode → FOS → q_s**. # 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1:** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **State Terzaghi's ultimate bearing capacity equation for a strip footing and explain the physical meaning of each term.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Terzaghi's ultimate bearing capacity equation for a strip footing is: $$ q_{u} \;=\; c\,N_{c} \;+\; q\,N_{q} \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_{\gamma} $$ Physical meaning of each term: - **$c N_c$:** The **cohesion term** — represents the resistance offered by the **inter-particle cohesive bonds** of the soil. Dominant in **clayey** soils. (1 Mark) - **$q N_q$:** The **surcharge term** — represents resistance from the **weight of the soil** lying above the level of the footing base (overburden), which increases confining stress on the failure surface. (1 Mark) - **$0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$:** The **self-weight term** — represents resistance from the **weight of the soil wedge** that must be displaced beneath the footing; this term is proportional to the footing width **B** and dominates in **sandy** soils. (1 Mark) --- ### **Question A2:** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List any six assumptions of Terzaghi's bearing capacity theory.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.5 Marks each):** 1. The footing is a **strip footing** (length-to-width ratio L/B → ∞), so plane-strain conditions apply. 2. The soil is **homogeneous, isotropic, and semi-infinite**. 3. The footing base is **rough** (shear stresses develop between soil and footing base). 4. The failure surface in the soil mass is **presumed** (not derived from first principles) and consists of the three zones described. 5. The soil fails by **general shear failure** with the **Mohr-Coulomb criterion** governing strength. 6. The footing is placed at **shallow depth** (D ≤ B), with the overburden replaced by an equivalent surcharge q = γD_f. 7. Surcharge **q** on either side of the footing is **uniform and horizontal**. 8. The water table is **deep enough** to be ignored in the analysis. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations:** A 14-mark question in Module 2 will have **two sub-parts (a) and (b)** typically carrying 7 marks each, OR a single numerical worth 14 marks. You are given **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B** below. Attempt the one for which you are best prepared. --- ### **🔷 Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] A continuous (strip) footing of width **B = 1.8 m** is to be constructed at a depth of **D_f = 1.2 m** in a sandy clay having the following properties: unit weight **γ = 19 kN/m³**, cohesion **c = 20 kN/m²**, and angle of internal friction **φ = 20°**. Determine: **(a)** The ultimate bearing capacity using Terzaghi's equation. (7 Marks) **(b)** The net safe bearing capacity with a factor of safety **F = 3**, and comment on the contribution of each term. (7 Marks) #### **Model Solution — Question A:** **Part (a) — Ultimate Bearing Capacity (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Calculate surcharge pressure q.** $$ q \;=\; \gamma \cdot D_{f} \;=\; 19 \times 1.2 \;=\; 22.8 \;\text{kN/m}^{2} \qquad \text{(1 mark)} $$ **Step 2 — Read the bearing capacity factors for φ = 20° from the standard Terzaghi table.** $$ N_{c} \;=\; 17.7 \qquad N_{q} \;=\; 7.4 \qquad N_{\gamma} \;=\; 5.0 \qquad \text{(2 marks)} $$ **Step 3 — Apply Terzaghi's equation for a strip footing.** $$ q_{u} \;=\; c\,N_{c} \;+\; q\,N_{q} \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_{\gamma} $$ $$ q_{u} \;=\; (20)(17.7) \;+\; (22.8)(7.4) \;+\; 0.5 \times 19 \times 1.8 \times 5.0 $$ $$ q_{u} \;=\; 354.0 \;+\; 168.72 \;+\; 85.5 \;=\; 608.22 \;\text{kN/m}^{2} \qquad \text{(4 marks)} $$ $$ \boxed{q_{u} \;\approx\; 608.2 \;\text{kN/m}^{2}} $$ **Part (b) — Net Safe Bearing Capacity (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Compute net ultimate bearing capacity.** $$ q_{nu} \;=\; q_{u} \;-\; \gamma D_{f} \;=\; 608.22 \;-\; 22.8 \;=\; 585.42 \;\text{kN/m}^{2} \qquad \text{(2 marks)} $$ **Step 2 — Apply factor of safety F = 3.** $$ q_{ns} \;=\; \frac{q_{nu}}{F} \;=\; \frac{585.42}{3} \;=\; 195.14 \;\text{kN/m}^{2} \qquad \text{(2 marks)} $$ **Step 3 — Contribution analysis of each term.** - Cohesion contribution: $cN_c = 354.0$ kN/m² (≈ **58.2%** of $q_u$) - Surcharge contribution: $qN_q = 168.72$ kN/m² (≈ **27.7%** of $q_u$) - Self-weight contribution: $0.5\gamma B N_\gamma = 85.5$ kN/m² (≈ **14.1%** of $q_u$) **Comment:** Since the soil has both cohesion (c = 20 kN/m²) and friction (φ = 20°), the **cohesion term dominates** (≈ 58%), followed by the **surcharge term**. The footing is **safe** at the design load of 195 kN/m². (3 marks) $$ \boxed{q_{ns} \;\approx\; 195.14 \;\text{kN/m}^{2}} $$ --- ### **🔷 Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Explain the **failure zones** considered by Terzaghi beneath a strip footing with a neat sketch, and derive the **modified Terzaghi's equation** for a **square footing** from the strip-footing solution. (14 Marks) #### **Model Solution — Question B:** **Part 1 — Description of Failure Zones (7 Marks)** Terzaghi postulated that the soil mass beneath a strip footing fails along a **composite failure surface** consisting of three distinct zones: - **Zone I — Active Wedge (Triangular Zone):** A triangular wedge of soil directly beneath the footing base, bounded by the footing base and two inclined planes rising at an angle **φ** with the horizontal. This wedge remains in the **active Rankine state** and acts as if it is part of the footing, moving downward with it. (2 Marks) - **Zone II — Radial Shear Zone (Log-Spiral Zone):** A zone of soil on either side of Zone I, bounded by a **curved (logarithmic spiral)** slip surface. In this zone, the principal stress directions rotate continuously. The radial shear zone **transfers** the load from the active wedge to the passive wedge. (2.5 Marks) - **Zone III — Passive Wedge (Rankine Zone):** A large mass of soil on either side of Zone II, bounded by an inclined plane rising at an angle **(45° − φ/2)** with the horizontal. This zone is in the **passive Rankine state** and is pushed up and outward, producing a **visible ground heave** at the surface. (2.5 Marks) **Part 2 — Square Footing Modification (7 Marks)** For a **strip footing**, the bearing capacity equation is: $$ q_{u(\text{strip})} \;=\; c\,N_{c} \;+\; q\,N_{q} \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_{\gamma} \qquad \text{(2 marks)} $$ For a **square footing**, Terzaghi introduced **empirical shape correction factors** (denoted $\alpha_c$, $\alpha_q$, $\alpha_\gamma$) to account for the 3-D geometry: - For the **cohesion term**, shape factor: $\alpha_c = 1.3$ - For the **surcharge term**, shape factor: $\alpha_q = 1.0$ (unchanged) - For the **self-weight term**, shape factor: $\alpha_\gamma = 0.4$ Substituting these into the strip equation, the **square footing equation** becomes: $$ q_{u(\text{square})} \;=\; (1.3)\,c\,N_{c} \;+\; q\,N_{q} \;+\; (0.4)\,\gamma\,B\,N_{\gamma} \qquad \text{(4 marks)} $$ **Physical Justification (1 Mark):** The cohesion and self-weight resistances are **larger for a square footing** than a strip footing (per unit area) because the failure surface is **3-D** and more soil mass is mobilized laterally. The surcharge term is unaffected because it depends only on the overburden pressure $q$ at the base level. > [!NOTE] > **Note on derivation:** Terzaghi's original derivations for the 3-D shape factors were **empirical**, based on laboratory model tests on square and circular footings, not on closed-form plasticity. Students should state this in the exam to score full marks. --- > [!WARNING] > ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Zones:** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract $\gamma D_f$ for net bearing capacity:** Many students compute $q_s = q_u / F$ instead of the correct $q_s = (q_u - \gamma D_f)/F + \gamma D_f$. Lose 1-2 marks easily. > 2. **Using wrong shape factor for rectangular footing:** It is **not** the average of square and strip; use the **explicit formula** $1 + 0.3 B/L$ and $1 - 0.2 B/L$. > 3. **Reading N_c, N_q, N_γ from the wrong column:** These are **functions of φ only**, not of c, q, or γ. Use the row corresponding to the given φ value. > 4. **Not mentioning failure mode:** For local shear, the reduced $c^* = 2c/3$ and $\phi^* = \tan^{-1}((2/3)\tan\phi)$ must be **clearly stated**. Using original c and φ with a local-shear label is a 1-mark penalty. > 5. **Units:** Ensure c, q are in **kN/m²** and B, D_f in **metres** for consistency. Always write units in the final answer. > 6. **Sketch missing in theory questions:** A **neat labelled diagram** of the three failure zones is **mandatory** for full marks in 7-mark theory questions. --- ## 5.3 🌟 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Terzaghi (1943)** is the **father of bearing capacity theory**; his equation is the **foundation** of all modern foundation design. - ✅ The **strip-footing equation** is: $q_u = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$. - ✅ The three terms correspond to **cohesion**, **surcharge**, and **self-weight** resistances — know their **physical meaning** cold. - ✅ **$N_c$, $N_q$, $N_\gamma$** are **dimensionless** functions of **φ only** — memorize the table for φ = 0°, 10°, 20°, 30°, 40°. - ✅ For φ = 0° (pure clay), $N_c = 5.7$, $N_q = 1.0$, $N_\gamma = 0.0$ — the equation reduces to $q_u = 5.7c$. - ✅ **Shape factors:** Square (1.3, 1.0, 0.4), Circular (1.3, 1.0, 0.3), Rectangular (1 + 0.3 B/L, 1.0, 1 − 0.2 B/L). - ✅ **Local shear** uses **reduced strength parameters**: $c' = 2c/3$ and $\phi' = \tan^{-1}((2/3)\tan\phi)$. - ✅ The **three failure zones** are: (i) **Active Wedge** (triangular, angle φ), (ii) **Radial Shear** (log-spiral), (iii) **Passive Wedge** (inclined at 45° − φ/2). - ✅ Assumptions: **homogeneous, isotropic, semi-infinite, rough-base, general-shear, shallow-depth, strip geometry**. - ✅ The **net bearing capacity** is $q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f$ — never forget to subtract the overburden. - ✅ **Factor of Safety (F)** is typically **3** for buildings (per IS 1904 / IS 6403). - ✅ The final **safe bearing capacity** is $q_s = (q_u - \gamma D_f)/F + \gamma D_f = q_{ns} + \gamma D_f$. - ✅ Limitations of Terzaghi's theory: **does not account for** water-table effects, layered soils, inclined loads, eccentric loads, or footing flexibility — these require **Meyerhof / Hansen / Vesic** extensions (covered in Module 3). - ✅ **Mermaid mnemonic:** "**C-Q-Gamma**" → Cohesion, Overburden (Q), Self-weight (Gamma) → memorize in that order. - ✅ Always **draw a labelled diagram** of failure zones in 7-mark theory questions — it typically earns **1–2 easy marks**.

topics.title: Assumptions - Gross and Net Bearing Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assumptions - Gross and Net Bearing Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Bearing Pressure Concepts

In Foundation Engineering, before we plunge into the formidable mathematics of **Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Theory**, it is absolutely essential to understand the *fundamental assumptions* upon which the entire theory rests and the *precise definitions* of the pressures acting on a footing. This module lays the conceptual bedrock for every bearing capacity and settlement calculation you will perform as a practicing geotechnical engineer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> *"Bearing capacity of shallow foundations – Terzaghi's theory – Assumptions and Gross/Net bearing pressure."*
> This topic is a guaranteed **8 to 14 mark** question in every KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 What is Gross Bearing Pressure ($q_g$)?

**Formal Definition:**
The **Gross Bearing Pressure** ($q_g$) is defined as the *total pressure acting at the base of the foundation* due to the entire structural load, including the self-weight of the foundation, the weight of the soil resting on the footing, and any surcharge that is transmitted to the base level. It is the total intensity of load per unit area at the base of the footing.

$$q_g = \frac{P}{A}$$

where:
- $P$ = Total vertical load on the footing (including all overburden, self-weight)
- $A$ = Area of the footing

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Elephant on the Mat"**
> Imagine a heavy elephant standing on a thin rubber mat placed over soft mud. The pressure exerted by the elephant's feet on the mud is the **Gross Pressure**. It includes the weight of the elephant *plus* the weight of the mat itself. In foundation terms, "the elephant" is your superstructure (column load) and "the mat" is the footing. The pressure you measure at the base of the mat is the gross pressure.

## 1.3 What is Net Bearing Pressure ($q_{net}$)?

**Formal Definition:**
The **Net Bearing Pressure** ($q_{net}$) is the *excess pressure* available at the base of the foundation after subtracting the *original overburden pressure* (the weight of the soil that was originally present at the foundation level before excavation). In other words, it is the **additional** or **incremental** pressure that the foundation applies to the subsoil beyond the pressure that was already there due to the natural soil column.

$$q_{net} = q_g - q_o = \frac{P}{A} - \gamma \cdot D_f$$

where:
- $q_o = \gamma \cdot D_f$ = Surcharge / Overburden pressure at foundation level
- $\gamma$ = Unit weight of the soil above the footing base
- $D_f$ = Depth of foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Same Elephant, but now consider what was already there"**
> Before the elephant stepped onto the mat, the mat itself was lying on the mud and the mud was already pressing *upward* on the mat with some pressure (the overburden). The *additional* pressure the elephant adds to the system (above and beyond what the mud was already bearing) is the **Net Pressure**. The soil doesn't "feel" the gross pressure alone; it feels the **net increase** in stress.

## 1.4 The Critical Assumptions in Bearing Capacity Analysis

For Terzaghi's classical theory to be mathematically tractable, the following **assumptions** are made:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU MUST-KNOW — Terzaghi's Assumptions (Frequently Asked in 2 & 14 mark questions):**

1. **Soil is homogeneous and isotropic:** The same engineering properties (cohesion, friction angle) exist at every point and in every direction.
2. **Semi-infinite soil mass:** The soil extends infinitely in all lateral directions and to a great depth.
3. **The footing is a strip footing (length $\gg$ width):** The theory is rigorously derived for a 2D plane-strain case.
4. **Base of footing is rough:** The soil does not slip relative to the base of the footing (no relative displacement at the interface).
5. **Failure surface is planar (simplified):** The actual curved failure surface (log-spiral) is replaced by a straight line for mathematical simplicity.
6. **General shear failure mode:** The theory is derived assuming general shear failure (Type I failure, per Vesić).
7. **Soil above the base is replaced by a uniform surcharge ($q_o = \gamma D_f$):** The weight of soil above the footing base is treated as an equivalent uniform surcharge and its shearing strength is ignored.
8. **Footing is rigid:** The footing does not bend or deflect under load.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Assumptions

Each assumption in Terzaghi's theory exists to convert a complex real-world problem into a solvable static equilibrium problem. Let us dissect the *logic* behind each:

| # | Assumption | Engineering "Why" (Purpose) | Real-World Trade-off |
|---|------------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Homogeneous & Isotropic | Makes $\phi$, $c$ constants in equations | Real soils are layered & anisotropic |
| 2 | Semi-infinite medium | Justifies ignoring far-field boundaries | Excavations are finite |
| 3 | Strip footing | Reduces to 2D plane-strain (one variable) | Real footings are square/circular |
| 4 | Rough base | Prevents slip — fully mobilizes shear strength | Smooth bases exist (e.g., plain concrete) |
| 5 | Planar failure surface | Allows trigonometric equilibrium of wedges | True failure is log-spiral |
| 6 | General shear failure | Gives an upper-bound, conservative estimate | Dense sand/dense clay only |
| 7 | Surcharge substitution | Removes the variable weight of overburden from wedge analysis | Ignores shear strength of soil above base |
| 8 | Rigid footing | Ensures load is uniformly distributed | Flexible footings redistribute load |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:**
> Assumptions 1, 2, 5, and 6 are the **most commonly violated** in the field. This is why KTU examiners love to ask *"Discuss the limitations of Terzaghi's assumptions"* as a 7-mark sub-question.

## 2.2 The Concept of Surcharge ($q_o$)

When we excavate to the depth of the foundation ($D_f$), the *natural soil* that used to occupy that space is gone. The remaining soil at the base "feels" the relief. To restore equilibrium in our mathematical model, Terzaghi replaced the excavated soil with an **equivalent uniform surcharge**:

$$q_o = \gamma \cdot D_f$$

For a **layered** overburden (very common in Kerala's lateritic/alluvial profiles):

$$q_o = \gamma_1 \cdot H_1 + \gamma_2 \cdot H_2 + \dots + \gamma_n \cdot H_n$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these are the only equations you need for Module 2 derivation questions.**

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units (SI) | Boundary Condition |
|----------|--------|---------|------------|---------------------|
| Gross Pressure | $q_g$ | $\dfrac{P}{A}$ | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | Always positive |
| Overburden / Surcharge | $q_o$ | $\gamma \cdot D_f$ | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | $\gamma \geq 0$, $D_f > 0$ |
| Net Pressure | $q_{net}$ | $q_g - q_o = \dfrac{P}{A} - \gamma D_f$ | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | Can be zero or negative |
| Effective Net Pressure | $q'_{net}$ | $q_{net} - u$ | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | Used below water table |
| Ultimate Net Bearing Capacity | $q_{nu}$ | $q_u - q_o$ | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | Used in settlement-based design |
| Safe Bearing Capacity (Net) | $q_{ns}$ | $\dfrac{q_{nu}}{FOS}$ | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | FOS = 3 (typical) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Pitfall:** In KTU exams, $\gamma$ is usually given in **kN/m³** and $D_f$ in **metres**, giving $q_o$ in **kN/m²** (= kPa). Mixing g/cm³ with metres is a common blunder. Always convert: $\gamma = 1.8 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Pressure Concepts

In practical geotechnical design, the distinction between gross and net pressure is not just academic:

1. **Settlement Calculations:** Only the *net* (incremental) stress causes *additional* settlement of the underlying strata. Gross pressure would erroneously predict the total settlement including the soil's own weight settlement (which has already happened).
2. **Factor of Safety Application:** FOS in bearing capacity is applied to the *net* ultimate capacity, not the gross.
3. **Ground Improvement Design:** When using techniques like sand compaction piles or stone columns, the *net* stress governs the design of the improved zone.
4. **Scour and Buoyancy:** Below water table, the **effective** net pressure (using buoyant unit weight $\gamma'$) is used.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Net Bearing Pressure from First Principles

Let us formally derive the relationship between gross and net pressure, as expected in a KTU 14-mark question.

### Step 1: Set up the problem geometry

Consider a strip footing of width $B$ embedded to a depth $D_f$ below the ground surface. A column load $P$ (per unit length, since it is a strip footing) is applied centrally.

Let the unit weight of the soil *above* the footing base be $\gamma$ (assumed constant for simplicity).

### Step 2: Define the Gross Bearing Pressure

The total vertical load transmitted to the base of the footing is $P$. The contact area per unit length is $B \times 1 = B$.

Therefore, by definition of pressure (Force / Area):

$$q_g = \frac{P}{B}$$

### Step 3: Define the original Overburden Pressure

Before excavation, the soil at depth $D_f$ was supporting the weight of the soil column above it. By the principle of effective stress in a homogeneous soil:

$$q_o = \gamma \cdot D_f$$

> **Why does this matter?** Because this pressure was already there! The soil grains were *already* in equilibrium under $q_o$. Only the *extra* pressure will cause additional deformation (settlement) or potential failure.

### Step 4: Subtract to obtain Net Pressure

By direct subtraction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{net} &= q_g - q_o \\[6pt]
q_{net} &= \frac{P}{B} - \gamma \cdot D_f
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Physical Interpretation

- If $q_{net} = 0$ → The foundation is "floating" (no net stress increase). Settlement is theoretically zero.
- If $q_{net} < 0$ → The foundation has *unloaded* the soil (e.g., an underground reservoir). The soil *heaves*.
- If $q_{net} > 0$ → The foundation imposes additional stress → settlement and possible shear failure.

## 3.2 Derivation of Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

The **Ultimate Bearing Capacity** ($q_u$) is the maximum gross pressure the soil can support before shear failure. Subtract the overburden (which the soil was already supporting) to get the **Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{nu} &= q_u - q_o \\[6pt]
q_{nu} &= q_u - \gamma \cdot D_f
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Terzaghi's strip footing** equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= c \cdot N_c + q_o \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_{\gamma} \\[6pt]
q_{nu} &= \big(c \cdot N_c + q_o \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_{\gamma}\big) - q_o \\[6pt]
q_{nu} &= c \cdot N_c + (q_o \cdot N_q - q_o) + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_{\gamma} \\[6pt]
q_{nu} &= c \cdot N_c + q_o \cdot (N_q - 1) + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_{\gamma}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:**
> A common examiner trick: *"Why do we subtract $q_o$ from $q_u$ to get $q_{nu}$?"*
> **Model Answer:** "The surcharge pressure $q_o$ was already being sustained by the soil before the footing was constructed. It is the *net* additional pressure that the soil can now sustain against shear failure."

## 3.3 Derivation of Safe Bearing Pressure (Net)

Applying the **Factor of Safety (FOS)** to the *net ultimate* capacity gives the *net safe* capacity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{ns} &= \frac{q_{nu}}{FOS} \\[6pt]
q_{ns} &= \frac{c \cdot N_c + q_o \cdot (N_q - 1) + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_{\gamma}}{FOS}
\end{aligned}
$$

The *gross* safe bearing capacity is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{s} &= q_{ns} + q_o \\[6pt]
q_{s} &= \frac{c \cdot N_c + q_o \cdot (N_q - 1) + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_{\gamma}}{FOS} + \gamma \cdot D_f
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Many KTU answer scripts add the FOS to $q_o$ *before* subtraction, which is **wrong**. FOS is applied **only to the net ultimate capacity**, not to the overburden.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Design Situation | Governing Pressure | Why? |
|------------------|--------------------|------|
| Settlement analysis | $q_{net}$ | Only the *increment* causes new settlement |
| Bearing capacity check | $q_{nu}$ | Failure is governed by *additional* shear stress |
| Working stress design | $q_{ns} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FOS}$ | Safe additional stress |
| Foundation dimensions | $q_s = q_{ns} + q_o$ | Total safe pressure including overburden |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

Since the topic involves physical geometry (a footing in soil), we use a **Block-Level Architecture Flow** to map how the loads transform from structural to geotechnical components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Superstructure Load P] --> B[Column]
    B --> C[Footing - Width B, Depth Df]
    C --> D[Base Contact Interface]
    D --> E[Subsoil Failure Zone]
    H[Soil Column above Footing] --> I[Overburden Pressure qo = gamma x Df]
    C --> J[Total Vertical Force at Base = P]
    J --> K[Gross Bearing Pressure qg = P / A]
    I --> L[Subtract qo from qg]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Net Bearing Pressure qnet]
    M --> N[Settlement Calculation]
    M --> O[Shear Failure Check qnu = qu - qo]
    O --> P[Apply FOS to qnu]
    P --> Q[Net Safe Capacity qns]
    Q --> R[Add qo back to get Gross Safe qns + qo]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — From Load to Design Pressure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Total Load P on Footing] --> P2[Step 2: Compute qg = P / A]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Compute qo = gamma x Df]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Compute qnet = qg - qo]
    P4 --> P5A{Bearing Capacity Check?}
    P4 --> P5B{Settlement Check?}
    P5A --> P6[Compare qnet with qnu / FOS]
    P5B --> P7[Use qnet in Elastic Settlement Formula]
    P6 --> P8[Safe or Redesign]
    P7 --> P8
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — Terzaghi's Assumptions Visualized

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TA[Terzaghi Bearing Capacity Theory] --> TA1[Assumption 1: Homogeneous Isotropic Soil]
    TA --> TA2[Assumption 2: Semi Infinite Medium]
    TA --> TA3[Assumption 3: Strip Footing]
    TA --> TA4[Assumption 4: Rough Footing Base]
    TA --> TA5[Assumption 5: Planar Failure Surface]
    TA --> TA6[Assumption 6: General Shear Failure]
    TA --> TA7[Assumption 7: Surcharge Substitution for Overburden]
    TA --> TA8[Assumption 8: Rigid Footing]
    TA7 --> QO[Surcharge qo = gamma Df]
    QO --> QG[qg = P / A]
    QG --> QNET[qnet = qg - qo]
    QNET --> QU[Ultimate Net qnu = qu - qo]
    QU --> QSAFE[Safe Net qns = qnu / FOS]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define the terms:**
**(a) Gross bearing pressure**
**(b) Net bearing pressure**
**(c) Surcharge**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Gross bearing pressure ($q_g$):** It is the total pressure acting at the base of the foundation due to the entire load (column load + self-weight of footing + weight of soil on footing) transmitted to the base level, expressed per unit area.

$$
q_g = \frac{P}{A}
$$

**(b) Net bearing pressure ($q_{net}$):** It is the *excess* pressure at the base of the foundation after subtracting the overburden pressure from the gross pressure. It represents the additional stress imposed on the subsoil.

$$
q_{net} = q_g - \gamma D_f
$$

**(c) Surcharge ($q_o$):** It is the equivalent uniform pressure assumed to act at the foundation level in place of the weight of the soil removed during excavation. For uniform soil, $q_o = \gamma D_f$.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**State any three assumptions of Terzaghi's bearing capacity theory.**

**Model Answer:**

1. The soil is **homogeneous and isotropic** with constant shear parameters $c$ and $\phi$ throughout.
2. The footing is a **strip footing** of width $B$ with a **rough base**, and the theory is derived under plane-strain conditions.
3. The soil mass is **semi-infinite** and the failure zone is assumed to extend up to the ground surface.
4. *(Any other valid assumption from Section 1.4 is acceptable.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**(a)** Define the terms *Gross bearing pressure* and *Net bearing pressure*. Derive the relationship between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State the assumptions of Terzaghi's bearing capacity theory and explain the role of surcharge in the analysis. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Definition of Gross Bearing Pressure (2 marks):**
The **gross bearing pressure** ($q_g$) is defined as the total intensity of pressure at the base of the foundation, which is the total vertical load (column load, self-weight of footing, and overburden soil weight) divided by the effective area of the footing.

$$
q_g = \frac{P}{A}
$$

where $P$ is the total vertical load (in kN) and $A$ is the effective base area (in $\text{m}^2$).

**Step 2 — Definition of Net Bearing Pressure (2 marks):**
The **net bearing pressure** ($q_{net}$) is defined as the *additional* or *incremental* pressure at the foundation base after deducting the original overburden pressure ($q_o = \gamma D_f$) that was already being supported by the soil prior to construction.

**Step 3 — Derivation (3 marks):**
For a strip footing of width $B$ at depth $D_f$ with a column load $P$ per unit length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross pressure at base: } q_g &= \frac{P}{B} \\[6pt]
\text{Overburden pressure removed: } q_o &= \gamma \cdot D_f \\[6pt]
\text{Net pressure (excess over original): } q_{net} &= q_g - q_o \\[6pt]
\therefore \quad q_{net} &= \frac{P}{B} - \gamma D_f
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Physical meaning:** The net bearing pressure is the *change* in vertical stress at the foundation level compared to the pre-construction condition. It is this *change* that drives both additional settlement and potential shear failure of the subsoil.

### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — List of Assumptions (4 marks):**
The key assumptions of Terzaghi's bearing capacity theory are:

1. The soil is **homogeneous and isotropic** with engineering properties ($c$, $\phi$, $\gamma$) constant throughout.
2. The **footing is a strip footing** (length $\gg$ width) and the analysis is 2-D plane strain.
3. The footing base is **rough** (no slip between base and soil).
4. The soil mass is **semi-infinite** and the failure surface extends to ground level.
5. The failure surface is approximated as **planar** (straight lines) for mathematical simplicity.
6. The footing is **rigid** and the soil undergoes **general shear failure**.
7. The soil above the base is **replaced by a uniform surcharge** $q_o = \gamma D_f$; its shear strength contribution is ignored.
