**Step 2 — Role of Surcharge (3 marks):**
The **surcharge** $q_o = \gamma D_f$ is the equivalent pressure that accounts for the weight of the soil column excavated to reach the foundation level. Its role is twofold:

- It is **subtracted** from the gross ultimate bearing capacity $q_u$ to obtain the **net ultimate bearing capacity** $q_{nu}$ — because the soil was already in equilibrium under $q_o$.
- It is **added back** to the net safe bearing capacity to obtain the **gross safe bearing capacity** — for use in actual foundation design where the engineer needs the *total* allowable pressure on the soil.

The surcharge simplifies the analysis by treating the complex weight of the overburden as a simple distributed load on the failure surface.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 Alternative]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain how surcharge ($q_o = \gamma D_f$) is incorporated in Terzaghi's bearing capacity equation. Derive the expression for net ultimate bearing capacity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A strip footing of width 2 m is placed at a depth of 1.5 m in a soil with $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $c = 15 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\phi = 30°$. Using Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors ($N_c = 37.2$, $N_q = 22.5$, $N_{\gamma} = 19.7$), calculate the **net ultimate** and **net safe** bearing capacities. Take FOS = 3. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Sketch Description (2 marks):**
Terzaghi visualised a strip footing of width $B$ at depth $D_f$ with three distinct failure zones beneath it:
- Zone I (Elastic wedge): Triangular wedge directly beneath the base, bounded by the base of the footing and lines at angle $\phi$ with the horizontal.
- Zone II (Radial shear zone): Log-spiral-shaped region (approximated as planar for analysis).
- Zone III (Passive Rankine zone): Triangular zones on either side, bounded by lines at $45° - \phi/2$ with the horizontal.

The soil above the base of the footing is replaced by a uniform surcharge pressure $q_o = \gamma D_f$ acting on the horizontal plane at the level of the footing base.

**Step 2 — Gross Ultimate Bearing Capacity Equation (2 marks):**

$$
q_u = c N_c + q_o N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_{\gamma}
$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{nu} &= q_u - q_o \\[4pt]
q_{nu} &= (c N_c + q_o N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_{\gamma}) - q_o \\[4pt]
q_{nu} &= c N_c + q_o(N_q - 1) + 0.5 \gamma B N_{\gamma}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the final expression for the **net ultimate bearing capacity** — the additional pressure (beyond the original overburden) the soil can support before shear failure.

### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Width $B = 2$ m
- Depth $D_f = 1.5$ m
- $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- $c = 15 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- $\phi = 30°$
- $N_c = 37.2$, $N_q = 22.5$, $N_{\gamma} = 19.7$
- FOS = 3

**Step 1: Calculate Surcharge (1 mark)**

$$
q_o = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Gross Ultimate Bearing Capacity (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= c N_c + q_o N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_{\gamma} \\[4pt]
q_u &= (15)(37.2) + (27)(22.5) + 0.5(18)(2)(19.7) \\[4pt]
q_u &= 558.0 + 607.5 + 354.6 \\[4pt]
q_u &= 1520.1 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{nu} &= q_u - q_o \\[4pt]
q_{nu} &= 1520.1 - 27 \\[4pt]
q_{nu} &= 1493.1 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Net Safe Bearing Capacity (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{ns} &= \frac{q_{nu}}{FOS} = \frac{1493.1}{3} \\[4pt]
q_{ns} &= 497.7 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $q_{nu} = 1493.1 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- $q_{ns} = 497.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $q_o$ from $q_u$** when the question asks for *net* capacity. This single error costs **3 marks**.
> 2. **Applying FOS incorrectly** to the *gross* $q_u$ instead of the *net* $q_{nu}$ — loses another **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not showing the surcharge calculation** $q_o = \gamma D_f$ as a separate step. Examiners expect to see this line explicitly.
> 4. **Unit errors:** Writing $q_o = 18 \times 1.5 = 27$ without stating units $\text{kN/m}^2$ loses **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 5. **Wrong assumption:** Stating "soil is cohesionless" when $c$ is given. The student loses 1–2 marks for not recognising that Terzaghi's *general* equation handles both $c-\phi$ soils.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Module 2 Assumptions & Bearing Pressure:**

- **Gross pressure** = Total load at base / Area = $P/A$. Includes everything.
- **Net pressure** = Gross - Overburden = $q_g - \gamma D_f$. Only the *extra* stress.
- **Surcharge** $q_o = \gamma D_f$ (homogeneous soil) or $\sum \gamma_i H_i$ (layered). It is an *equivalent* pressure, not a real one.
- **Net ultimate capacity** $q_{nu} = q_u - q_o$ — what the soil can resist *additionally*.
- **Net safe capacity** $q_{ns} = q_{nu} / FOS$ — design value (apply FOS = 3 typically).
- **Gross safe capacity** $q_s = q_{ns} + q_o$ — what you compare with the actual $q_g$ applied.
- **Terzaghi's 8 Assumptions:** Homogeneous, Isotropic, Semi-infinite, Strip footing, Rough base, Planar failure, General shear, Surcharge substitution, Rigid footing.
- **Terzaghi's Strip Footing Equation:** $q_u = cN_c + q_o N_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$. Memorise it.
- **For $\phi = 0$ (purely cohesive soil):** $N_c = 5.7$, $N_q = 1.0$, $N_\gamma = 0.0$. So $q_u = 5.7c + \gamma D_f$.
- **For square/circular footings:** Shape factors must be applied (Meyerhof, Hansen, IS code). Terzaghi's *original* formula is for **strip footings only**.
- **Below the water table:** Use $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ for the soil below the base in the $N_\gamma$ term, but use total $\gamma_{sat}$ for the $N_q$ surcharge term if water is inside the surcharge soil.
- **Settlement uses $q_{net}$** — never $q_g$ — because only the *increment* causes additional settlement.
- **KTU 2024 answer writing tip:** Always draw a labelled sketch of the footing with $B$, $D_f$, $q_o$, and the three failure zones. This guarantees **2–3 marks** even if the derivation has an arithmetic slip.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultimate and Safe Bearing Capacity - Allowable Soil Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ultimate and Safe Bearing Capacity - Allowable Soil Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ultimate Bearing Capacity (q_ult)

The **Ultimate Bearing Capacity** of a soil is defined as the **maximum gross pressure** that the soil can support without undergoing **shear failure**. It is the limiting intensity of loading at which the underlying soil mass is on the verge of collapse due to the development of continuous shear failure surfaces.

Mathematically, it represents the vertical stress $q$ acting on the footing base at the instant the soil beneath fails along the critical slip surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Ultimate Bearing Capacity is the *gross* stress at the footing base corresponding to shear failure of the supporting soil. It is **NOT** the net stress, and it **INCLUDES** the weight of the soil surcharge (overburden) above the foundation level.

## 1.2 Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC / q_s)

The **Safe Bearing Capacity** is the maximum net intensity of loading that the soil can carry **safely** without risk of shear failure AND with acceptable settlements for the structure.

$$q_s = \frac{q_{ult}}{FOS}$$

where **FOS** is the **Factor of Safety** against shear failure. A typical KTU-recommended FOS ranges from **2.5 to 3.0** for shallow foundations on homogeneous soils.

## 1.3 Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity (q_nu) and Net Safe Bearing Capacity (q_ns)

- **Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity** ($q_{nu}$): Ultimate bearing capacity *exclusive* of the overburden pressure $q_0 = \gamma D_f$ at the footing level.

$$q_{nu} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f$$

- **Net Safe Bearing Capacity** ($q_{ns}$): Safe bearing capacity expressed as a net stress (i.e., the *extra* pressure over and above the existing overburden that the soil can carry safely).

$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{FOS} = q_s - \gamma D_f$$

## 1.4 Allowable Soil Pressure (q_a) / Allowable Bearing Pressure (ABP)

The **Allowable Bearing Pressure** is the maximum net pressure that can be applied on the foundation such that **two** conditions are simultaneously satisfied:
1. **Shear failure** is prevented (Factor of Safety $\geq 3$).
2. **Settlements** (total and differential) remain within permissible limits as per **IS 1904 (1986)**.

$$q_a = \min \left( \frac{q_{nu}}{FOS}, \; q_{settlement} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Picture):** Imagine placing a heavy book on a stretched rubber sheet. The **Ultimate Bearing Capacity** is the weight at which the sheet *tears* (shear failure). The **Safe Bearing Capacity** is a smaller weight (divided by FOS) that prevents tearing. The **Allowable Bearing Pressure** is an *even smaller* weight, chosen not only to avoid tearing but also to keep the *deflection* (settlement) of the rubber sheet within the legroom of the table — because even without tearing, excessive bending can break the table legs! In foundation design, **settlement often governs** over shear strength, especially in sands and stiff clays.

## 1.5 Gross vs. Net — A Critical Distinction

| Quantity | Symbol | Includes Overburden? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Gross Ultimate Bearing Capacity | $q_{ult}$ | Yes |
| Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity | $q_{nu}$ | No |
| Gross Safe Bearing Capacity | $q_s$ | Yes |
| Net Safe Bearing Capacity | $q_{ns}$ | No |
| Allowable Bearing Pressure | $q_a$ | Net (always) |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** KTU question papers almost always ask for **net** values when the word *bearing capacity* is qualified by *net*. If a question simply says "ultimate bearing capacity," it means the **gross** value. Always re-read the wording!

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modes of Shear Failure Beneath a Footing

When a footing is progressively loaded, the underlying soil fails in one of three distinct modes (Vesic's classification, also adopted by IS 6403):

- **General Shear Failure (GSF):** Applicable to **dense sand** and **stiff (overconsolidated) clay**. Failure surfaces reach the ground surface; a clear heave appears adjacent to the footing; a sudden, catastrophic failure occurs at a well-defined peak load.
- **Local Shear Failure (LSF):** Applicable to **medium-dense sand** and **medium stiff clay**. Failure surfaces do not reach the ground surface; heave is barely visible; failure is gradual.
- **Punching Shear Failure (PSF):** Applicable to **loose sand** and **soft clay**. The footing punches vertically downward; failure is highly progressive with no defined peak.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Bearing Capacity Computation

1. **Step 1:** Identify the soil profile and obtain shear parameters $c, \phi, \gamma$ for each stratum.
2. **Step 2:** Determine the failure mode (GSF, LSF, or PSF) using SPT-N, $D_r$, or $I_p$ correlations.
3. **Step 3:** Select the appropriate bearing capacity equation (Terzaghi / Meyerhof / IS 6403).
4. **Step 4:** Compute bearing capacity factors $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ as functions of $\phi$.
5. **Step 5:** Apply shape, depth, inclination, and water-table corrections.
6. **Step 6:** Compute $q_{ult}$ (gross) and $q_{nu} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f$ (net).
7. **Step 7:** Apply FOS (typically 3) to obtain $q_s$ or $q_{ns}$.
8. **Step 8:** Compute allowable bearing pressure $q_a$ by also checking the settlement criterion.

## 2.3 Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Factors

Terzaghi (1943) developed theoretical bearing capacity factors based on the superposition principle and the assumption of a strip footing with General Shear Failure. The factors are:

$$N_c = \cot\phi \cdot (N_q - 1)$$

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

$$N_\gamma = \frac{1}{2}(N_q - 1)\tan\phi \quad \text{(Terzaghi's semi-empirical form)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why semi-empirical?** Terzaghi's expression for $N_\gamma$ was derived by combining Rankine's passive resistance with a logarithmic-spiral adjustment, then calibrating against model tests. Modern variants (Meyerhof, Hansen, Vesic) use slightly different forms, but KTU 2024 Scheme still expects **Terzaghi's original expressions** in the syllabus context.

## 2.4 Terzaghi's General Equation

For a **strip footing** of width $B$ at depth $D_f$ with general shear failure:

$$q_{ult} = cN_c + qN_q + \frac{1}{2}\gamma B N_\gamma$$

For **square** ($B \times B$) and **circular** (diameter $B$) footings, Terzaghi proposed modified shape coefficients:

| Footing Type | Equation |
| :--- | :--- |
| Strip ($L/B \to \infty$) | $q_{ult} = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\,\gamma B N_\gamma$ |
| Square ($L = B$) | $q_{ult} = 1.3\,cN_c + qN_q + 0.4\,\gamma B N_\gamma$ |
| Circular ($B =$ diameter) | $q_{ult} = 1.3\,cN_c + qN_q + 0.3\,\gamma B N_\gamma$ |

where $q = \gamma D_f$ is the surcharge (overburden) at the footing base level.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $q_{ult} = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$ | Terzaghi gross ultimate BC (strip) | kN/m² |
| 2 | $q_{nu} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f$ | Net ultimate BC | kN/m² |
| 3 | $q_s = q_{ult} / FOS$ | Gross safe BC | kN/m² |
| 4 | $q_{ns} = q_{nu} / FOS$ | Net safe BC | kN/m² |
| 5 | $q_a = q_{ns} \; \vert \; q_{settlement}$ | Allowable bearing pressure | kN/m² |
| 6 | $N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45 + \phi/2)$ | Terzaghi $N_q$ factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | Terzaghi $N_c$ factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $N_\gamma = 0.5(N_q - 1)\tan\phi$ | Terzaghi $N_\gamma$ factor | dimensionless |
| 9 | $q = \gamma D_f$ | Surcharge at base | kN/m² |
| 10 | $FOS = 2.5 \text{ to } 3.0$ | Recommended range | dimensionless |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of bearing capacity is the **backbone of shallow foundation design**. Practising geotechnical engineers in Kerala (especially along the coastal alluvium of Ernakulam, Alappuzha, and the lateritic soils of midland regions) routinely use these equations to size isolated footings for residential buildings, combined footings for column pairs, and raft foundations for high-rise structures. The **allowable soil pressure** is the single most important parameter communicated to the structural designer as the safe contact stress.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Terzaghi's Assumptions (Pre-Derivation Setup)

To derive the bearing capacity equation, Terzaghi made the following simplifying assumptions:

1. The footing is of **infinite strip** length (plane-strain condition) and rests on a **homogeneous, isotropic** soil mass.
2. The soil above the base of the footing is replaced by an equivalent **surcharge** $q = \gamma D_f$ (no shear strength considered above base).
3. Failure occurs by **General Shear** with a well-defined failure surface comprising three zones:
   - **Zone I** (triangular wedge directly beneath the footing, angle $\phi$ with horizontal at base edges).
   - **Zone II** (radial shear zone — bounded by a logarithmic spiral).
   - **Zone III** (passive Rankine zone — triangular, with failure surface inclined at $45^\circ - \phi/2$ to the horizontal).
4. The surcharge $q$ acts vertically on the horizontal plane at the level of the footing base.
5. The footing base is **rough** (no slip between footing and soil).

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Bearing Capacity Equation

### Step A: Geometry of the Failure Surface

The footing of width $B$ is at depth $D_f$. The triangular wedge **Zone I** beneath the base has its inclined sides making an angle $\phi$ with the horizontal (because the soil-footing interface is rough and the wedge moves downwards as a rigid body with the footing).

The base of the wedge lies at a depth $D_f + (B/2)\tan\phi$ below ground level. The radial shear **Zone II** extends outward and upward, terminating in the **Zone III** passive wedge whose failure surface is inclined at $45^\circ - \phi/2$ to the horizontal (Rankine's passive case).

### Step B: Superposition of Three Resistances

Terzaghi applied the **superposition principle** to the resisting stresses on a horizontal plane at the base of the footing. The total ultimate load per unit length $P_{ult}$ on a strip footing is the sum of three contributions:

$$P_{ult} = P_c + P_q + P_\gamma$$

- $P_c$ = resistance due to **cohesion** $c$ of the soil (no surcharge, no weight).
- $P_q$ = resistance due to **surcharge** $q = \gamma D_f$ (no cohesion, no weight).
- $P_\gamma$ = resistance due to **weight** of the soil wedge (no cohesion, no surcharge).

### Step C: Cohesion Term ($P_c$)

For the case of $\phi = 0$ (purely cohesive soil, $\phi$-u condition), the failure surface becomes a **circular arc** (since the Mohr-Coulomb envelope is horizontal). By modifying Prandtl's solution for a punch indenting a weightless, purely cohesive material, Terzaghi obtained:

$$P_c = c \cdot B \cdot N_c$$

where $N_c$ is a dimensionless bearing capacity factor. For $\phi = 0$, $N_c = 5.7$ (for a strip footing with rough base), which is **exactly** the well-known result:

$$N_c = 5.14 \text{ (smooth base)} \quad ; \quad N_c = 5.7 \text{ (rough base — Terzaghi)}$$

### Step D: Surcharge Term ($P_q$)

Treating the surcharge as a load on the failure surface, and using Rankine's passive earth pressure theory for the equivalent weightless, cohesionless material, Terzaghi obtained:

$$P_q = q \cdot B \cdot N_q$$

The factor $N_q$ emerges from the geometry of the radial-shear and passive zones. Substituting $\beta = 45^\circ + \phi/2$, the result is:

$$N_q = \frac{e^{\left(\frac{3\pi}{2} - \phi\right)\tan\phi}}{2\cos\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)}$$

which simplifies to the more familiar form used in textbooks and adopted by KTU:

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

### Step E: Self-Weight Term ($P_\gamma$)

For a cohesionless, weighty, surchargeless soil, the resistance offered by the weight of the soil in the failure wedge is:

$$P_\gamma = \frac{1}{2} \gamma B^2 \cdot N_\gamma$$

Since $q_{ult} = P_{ult}/B$ (load per unit length divided by width), we get:

$$q_{ult} = cN_c + qN_q + \frac{1}{2}\gamma B N_\gamma$$

This is **Terzaghi's General Bearing Capacity Equation** for a strip footing under General Shear Failure.

### Step F: Conversion to Net and Safe Bearing Capacity

Once $q_{ult}$ is determined, the net ultimate bearing capacity is obtained by subtracting the overburden pressure at the footing base:

$$q_{nu} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f$$

Dividing by the factor of safety $F$ (typically 3 for general shear, 2.5 for local shear) gives the net safe bearing capacity:

$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Full Computation)

> **Problem (Typical KTU 2024 Pattern — 14 Marks):**
> A strip footing of width **2.5 m** rests at a depth of **1.5 m** in a soil with unit weight **18 kN/m³**, cohesion **15 kN/m²**, and angle of internal friction **25°**. Compute the ultimate, net ultimate, and net safe bearing capacities using Terzaghi's theory. Take Factor of Safety = 3. Assume general shear failure.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify inputs.

$$B = 2.5 \text{ m}, \quad D_f = 1.5 \text{ m}, \quad \gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3, \quad c = 15 \text{ kN/m}^2, \quad \phi = 25^\circ$$

**Step 2:** Compute bearing capacity factors using Terzaghi's equations.

For $\phi = 25^\circ$:

$$\tan\phi = \tan 25^\circ = 0.4663$$

$$\pi \tan\phi = \pi \times 0.4663 = 1.4649$$

$$e^{\pi \tan\phi} = e^{1.4649} = 4.327$$

$$\tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{25^\circ}{2}\right) = \tan(57.5^\circ) = 1.5697$$

$$\tan^2(57.5^\circ) = 2.464$$

$$N_q = 4.327 \times 2.464 = 10.66$$

$$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi = (10.66 - 1) \times \cot 25^\circ = 9.66 \times 2.1445 = 20.72$$

$$N_\gamma = 0.5(N_q - 1)\tan\phi = 0.5 \times 9.66 \times 0.4663 = 2.25$$

**Step 3:** Compute surcharge at base.

$$q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Compute ultimate bearing capacity (strip footing).

$$q_{ult} = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$$

$$q_{ult} = (15 \times 20.72) + (27 \times 10.66) + (0.5 \times 18 \times 2.5 \times 2.25)$$

$$q_{ult} = 310.80 + 287.82 + 50.63 = 649.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Compute net ultimate bearing capacity.

$$q_{nu} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f = 649.25 - 27 = 622.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 6:** Compute net safe bearing capacity.

$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{FOS} = \frac{622.25}{3} = 207.42 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Final Tabulated Results

| Quantity | Value (kN/m²) |
| :---: | :---: |
| $q_{ult}$ (Gross Ultimate BC) | **649.25** |
| $q_{nu}$ (Net Ultimate BC) | **622.25** |
| $q_{ns}$ (Net Safe BC) | **207.42** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Notice that $q_{ult}$ and $q_{nu}$ differ by only $\gamma D_f$ (the overburden), which is small (27 kN/m²) compared to the dominant cohesion term. In dense sands ($\phi > 35^\circ$, $c \approx 0$), the $q$ and $\gamma B$ terms dominate, and the distinction becomes more significant.

## 3.4 Local Shear Failure Modification

For **local shear failure**, Terzaghi recommended the use of *modified* shear parameters:

$$c_m = \frac{2}{3} c \quad ; \quad \phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2}{3}\tan\phi\right)$$

These reduced values are substituted into the standard formulas to obtain $N_c', N_q', N_\gamma'$, which are then used in the same general equation. The **factor of safety** for local shear is typically taken as **2.5** (lower than for general shear, since the failure is more gradual).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Terzaghi's Three-Zone Failure Mechanism (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid block represents the functional architecture of Terzaghi's failure surface and the superposition-based computation flow. It maps the *logical flow* of stress contributions rather than the literal soil geometry (which is cumbersome in Mermaid).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply Footing Load P] --> B[Soil Beneath Fails by Shear]
    B --> C{Failure Mode?}
    C -- "Dense sand / Stiff clay" --> D[General Shear Failure]
    C -- "Medium dense / Medium stiff" --> E[Local Shear Failure]
    C -- "Loose sand / Soft clay" --> F[Punching Shear Failure]
    D --> G[Compute q_ult via Terzaghi]
    E --> H[Use modified c_m phi_m]
    F --> I[Use IS 6403 Skempton]
    G --> J[Sum three stress contributions]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> J1[Term 1: c N_c - Cohesion]
    J --> J2[Term 2: q N_q - Surcharge]
    J --> J3[Term 3: 0.5 gamma B N_gamma - Self weight]
    J1 --> K[Get q_ult gross]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[Subtract gamma D_f to get q_nu]
    L --> M[Divide by FOS to get q_ns]
    M --> N{Check Settlement Criterion}
    N -- "q_ns < q_settle" --> O[q_a = q_ns - Shear Governs]
    N -- "q_settle < q_ns" --> P[q_a = q_settle - Settlement Governs]
    O --> Q[Final Allowable Bearing Pressure]
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — Decision Logic for Selecting Bearing Capacity Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Given: c, phi, gamma, B, D_f, FOS] --> B[Step 1: Compute N_q]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute N_c = N_q-1 x cot phi]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute N_gamma = 0.5 N_q-1 x tan phi]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute q = gamma D_f]
    E --> F[Step 5: Compute q_ult using superposition]
    F --> G[Step 6: Compute q_nu = q_ult - q]
    G --> H[Step 7: Apply FOS to obtain q_ns]
    H --> I[Step 8: Cross-check with settlement limit]
    I --> J[Output: q_a Allowable Bearing Pressure]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Relation Between Gross, Net, Safe, and Allowable Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ULT["Ultimate Stage"]
        QULT[Q_ult Gross]
        QNU[Q_nu Net = Q_ult - gamma D_f]
    end
    subgraph SAFE["Safe Stage"]
        QS[Q_s Gross = Q_ult divided by FOS]
        QNS[Q_ns Net = Q_nu divided by FOS]
    end
    subgraph ALLOW["Allowable Stage"]
        QA[Q_a = min of Q_ns and Q_settle]
    end
    QULT -- "Minus overburden" --> QNU
    QULT -- "Divide by FOS" --> QS
    QNU -- "Divide by FOS" --> QNS
    QNS --> QA
    QS -- "Subtract overburden" --> QNS
    QA --> OUT[Final Design Parameter for Structural Engineer]
```
