# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q1. Define the terms: (a) Ultimate Bearing Capacity, (b) Safe Bearing Capacity, and (c) Allowable Bearing Pressure. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Ultimate Bearing Capacity ($q_{ult}$):** It is the **maximum gross pressure** intensity at the base of a foundation that the soil can withstand without undergoing **shear failure**. It is a property of the soil–footing system and depends on $c$, $\phi$, $\gamma$, $B$, and $D_f$.

**(b) Safe Bearing Capacity ($q_s$):** It is the **maximum pressure** the soil can safely carry **without risk of shear failure** and with **acceptable settlement**. It is obtained by dividing the ultimate bearing capacity by a suitable **Factor of Safety** (typically 2.5 to 3.0).

$$q_s = \frac{q_{ult}}{FOS}$$

**(c) Allowable Bearing Pressure ($q_a$):** It is the **maximum net pressure** that can be applied to the soil such that the foundation is safe against **both** shear failure and excessive settlement, i.e., the lesser of the net safe bearing capacity and the bearing pressure corresponding to the permissible settlement.

### Question 2
**Q2. Distinguish between Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity and Gross Ultimate Bearing Capacity. Why is the "net" concept used in design? [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Gross Ultimate BC | Net Ultimate BC |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Definition | Total stress at base at failure | Stress *in addition* to overburden |
| Formula | $q_{ult} = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma BN_\gamma$ | $q_{nu} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f$ |
| Includes overburden? | Yes | No |

**Why "net" is used in design:** Before construction, the soil at the foundation level is already in equilibrium under the overburden pressure $\gamma D_f$. Therefore, the **additional** (net) stress that the soil experiences due to the structure is $q - \gamma D_f$. It is this *net* increase that drives both shear failure and settlement. Hence, design calculations and the allowable bearing pressure are always expressed on a **net** basis.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Part (a) and (b)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
> **Q.A (a)** A strip footing of width **3.0 m** is to be laid at a depth of **2.0 m** in a soil with $\gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $c = 10 \text{ kN/m}^2$, and $\phi = 30^\circ$. Determine the **net ultimate** and **net safe** bearing capacities using Terzaghi's theory. Take FOS = 3. **[7 Marks]**
> **Q.A (b)** Explain the **three modes of shear failure** beneath a foundation with neat sketches and indicate the conditions under which each occurs. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Given:** $B = 3.0$ m, $D_f = 2.0$ m, $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³, $c = 10$ kN/m², $\phi = 30^\circ$, FOS = 3.

**Step 1 — Bearing capacity factors at $\phi = 30^\circ$:**

$$\tan\phi = \tan 30^\circ = 0.5774$$

$$\pi \tan\phi = 1.814$$

$$e^{1.814} = 6.133$$

$$\tan(45 + 15) = \tan 60^\circ = 1.732, \quad \tan^2 60^\circ = 3.0$$

$$N_q = 6.133 \times 3.0 = 18.40$$

$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi = (18.40 - 1) \times \cot 30^\circ = 17.40 \times 1.732 = 30.14$$

$$N_\gamma = 0.5(N_q - 1)\tan\phi = 0.5 \times 17.40 \times 0.5774 = 5.02$$

**Step 2 — Surcharge:**

$$q = \gamma D_f = 19 \times 2.0 = 38 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Gross ultimate bearing capacity (strip):**

$$q_{ult} = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$$

$$q_{ult} = (10 \times 30.14) + (38 \times 18.40) + (0.5 \times 19 \times 3.0 \times 5.02)$$

$$q_{ult} = 301.40 + 699.20 + 143.07 = 1143.67 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Net ultimate bearing capacity:**

$$q_{nu} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f = 1143.67 - 38 = 1105.67 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Net safe bearing capacity (FOS = 3):**

$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{FOS} = \frac{1105.67}{3} = 368.56 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{q_{nu} = 1105.67 \text{ kN/m}^2, \quad q_{ns} = 368.56 \text{ kN/m}^2}$

**Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

The three modes of shear failure beneath a footing (Vesic, 1963) are:

**1. General Shear Failure (GSF):**
- Occurs in **dense sand** or **stiff (overconsolidated) clay**.
- Failure surface reaches the **ground surface** on both sides of the footing.
- Visible **heave** of adjacent ground; load–settlement curve shows a **distinct peak** followed by sudden drop.
- The classical Terzaghi/Meyerhof equations apply directly.

**2. Local Shear Failure (LSF):**
- Occurs in **medium-dense sand** or **medium stiff clay**.
- Failure surface **partially propagates** upward; only a slight, barely visible heave.
- Load–settlement curve rises to a peak, then **flattens** without a sharp drop — failure is gradual.
- Modified shear parameters ($c_m = 2c/3$, $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(2\tan\phi/3)$) are used.

**3. Punching Shear Failure (PSF):**
- Occurs in **loose sand** or **soft, normally consolidated clay**.
- The footing **punches vertically downward** into the soil; failure surface is vertical, not reaching the surface.
- Significant settlement occurs without any clear peak load; load–settlement curve is **continuously rising**.
- Use Skempton's equation or IS 6403 method with $N_c$ depending on $D_f/B$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students present a **single figure** showing all three failure modes, which is **not accepted** by KTU valuators. You **must** draw **three separate diagrams**, each with (i) the footing, (ii) the failure surface trajectory, (iii) the direction of ground heave, and (iv) the typical load–settlement curve. Marks are awarded for **labelling**, not for line aesthetics.

### Question B — Part (a) and (b)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
> **Q.B (a)** Derive **Terzaghi's bearing capacity equation** for a strip footing under general shear failure. State clearly all assumptions and sketch the failure surface. **[7 Marks]**
> **Q.B (b)** A square footing of size **2.0 m × 2.0 m** is to be placed at a depth of **1.2 m** in a sandy soil with $\phi = 35^\circ$, $c = 0$, and $\gamma = 17.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$. Compute the **gross ultimate** and **allowable** bearing capacities. Take FOS = 3 and check for local shear. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Assumptions (1 mark each, 3–4 marks total for assumptions):**
1. Strip footing (plane strain), homogeneous isotropic soil.
2. Soil above footing base replaced by equivalent surcharge $q = \gamma D_f$.
3. Footing base is rough; failure follows General Shear mode.
4. Failure surface comprises three zones: triangular wedge (I), radial shear (II), and passive Rankine (III).

**Derivation Logic (3–4 marks):**

Apply superposition: $q_{ult} = \dfrac{1}{B}\!\left(c B N_c + q B N_q + \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma B^2 N_\gamma\right)$

Each term arises from:
- **Cohesion term** ($cN_c$): From Prandtl's punch-indentation analysis for weightless, purely cohesive soil; the slip surface is a circular arc and the integration yields $N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$.
- **Surcharge term** ($qN_q$): From Rankine's passive earth pressure on the vertical face of Zone III, combined with the geometry of the radial shear zone; gives $N_q = e^{\pi\tan\phi}\tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$.
- **Self-weight term** ($0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$): From the weight of soil in the failure wedge, balanced against the resisting passive pressure; Terzaghi's semi-empirical form gives $N_\gamma = 0.5(N_q - 1)\tan\phi$.

**Final Result:** $q_{ult} = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$ for a strip footing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When asked to *derive*, students often *state* the final equation and lose 4–5 marks. You **must** show the **logical flow** of the superposition principle — explicitly state $P_{ult} = P_c + P_q + P_\gamma$ and explain what each component physically represents. Without this, KTU valuators will mark *zero* for the derivation step.

**Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Given:** Square footing $B = L = 2.0$ m, $D_f = 1.2$ m, $c = 0$, $\phi = 35^\circ$, $\gamma = 17.5$ kN/m³, FOS = 3.

**Step 1 — Bearing capacity factors at $\phi = 35^\circ$:**

$$\tan\phi = \tan 35^\circ = 0.7002$$

$$\pi \tan\phi = 2.199$$

$$e^{2.199} = 9.017$$

$$\tan(45 + 17.5) = \tan 62.5^\circ = 1.9210$$

$$\tan^2(62.5^\circ) = 3.690$$

$$N_q = 9.017 \times 3.690 = 33.27$$

$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi = 32.27 \times \cot 35^\circ = 32.27 \times 1.4281 = 46.08$$

$$N_\gamma = 0.5(N_q - 1)\tan\phi = 0.5 \times 32.27 \times 0.7002 = 11.30$$

**Step 2 — Surcharge:**

$$q = \gamma D_f = 17.5 \times 1.2 = 21.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Gross ultimate bearing capacity (square footing):**

Since the problem is for a **square** footing, use Terzaghi's shape coefficients:

$$q_{ult} = 1.3 c N_c + q N_q + 0.4 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

With $c = 0$, the first term vanishes:

$$q_{ult} = 0 + (21.0 \times 33.27) + (0.4 \times 17.5 \times 2.0 \times 11.30)$$

$$q_{ult} = 698.67 + 158.20 = 856.87 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Net ultimate bearing capacity:**

$$q_{nu} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f = 856.87 - 21.0 = 835.87 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Local shear check:**

For local shear, $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(0.667 \times 0.7002) = \tan^{-1}(0.4670) = 25.0^\circ$. The reduced $N_q$ at $\phi_m = 25^\circ$ is about **10.66**, and $N_\gamma \approx 2.25$. Using the *same* square-footing shape factors:

$$q_{ult,LSF} = 0 + (21.0 \times 10.66) + (0.4 \times 17.5 \times 2.0 \times 2.25) = 223.86 + 31.50 = 255.36 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Since the soil is sandy and dense ($\phi = 35^\circ$), **General Shear Failure** is more appropriate, and we adopt $q_{ult} = 856.87$ kN/m².

**Step 6 — Allowable bearing pressure:**

$$q_a = \frac{q_{nu}}{FOS} = \frac{835.87}{3} = 278.62 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

(Subject to settlement check, which is beyond this computation.)

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{q_{ult} = 856.87 \text{ kN/m}^2, \quad q_a = 278.62 \text{ kN/m}^2}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When the problem states $c = 0$ (purely sandy soil), many students *incorrectly* write the equation with the cohesion term included and then plug in $c = 0$ — losing only 0.5 marks. The **bigger pitfall** is forgetting the **shape factors** (1.3 and 0.4) for a square footing. Using strip-footing coefficients when the footing is square is a **deduction of 1.5 to 2 marks** in KTU valuation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ultimate Bearing Capacity ($q_{ult}$):** Maximum gross pressure at the base of the footing at the instant of shear failure. Determined using Terzaghi / IS 6403 equations.
- **Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity ($q_{nu}$):** $q_{ult} - \gamma D_f$. Represents the *excess* pressure that drives failure.
- **Safe Bearing Capacity ($q_s$):** $q_{ult} / FOS$. Used in design to provide a margin against collapse.
- **Net Safe Bearing Capacity ($q_{ns}$):** $q_{nu} / FOS$. The design value reported to the structural engineer.
- **Allowable Bearing Pressure ($q_a$):** $\min(q_{ns}, q_{settlement})$ — governed by the smaller of shear and settlement criteria.
- **Bearing Capacity Factors (Terzaghi):**
  - $N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi}\tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$
  - $N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$
  - $N_\gamma = 0.5(N_q - 1)\tan\phi$
- **Shape Factors (Terzaghi):** Strip — 1.0 / 0.5; Square — 1.3 / 0.4; Circular — 1.3 / 0.3.
- **Three Failure Modes:** General, Local, Punching. Local shear uses *modified* parameters $c_m = 2c/3$ and $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(2\tan\phi/3)$.
- **Typical FOS:** **3.0** for general shear, **2.5** for local shear.
- **Always check the settlement criterion** — for many practical cases (dense sand, stiff clay), settlement controls the design, not shear.
- **KTU Mantra:** *Compute gross → subtract overburden → divide by FOS → cross-check settlement → report allowable.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bearing Capacity Factors - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bearing Capacity Factors - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Shallow Foundation Engineering**, the **Bearing Capacity Factors** are dimensionless, angle-of-internal-friction-dependent (φ) coefficients that quantify the contribution of the soil's **cohesion (c)**, **surcharge (q)**, and **unit weight (γ)** to the **Ultimate Net Bearing Capacity (q_nu)** of a strip footing resting on a c–φ soil medium. They are mathematically represented as **N_c**, **N_q**, and **N_γ** and constitute the *geometric-core* of Terzaghi's, Meyerhof's, Hansen's, and the **IS 6403:1981 (Reaffirmed 2002)** bearing capacity equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBCET504, Module 2):**
> *"Bearing capacity factors are pure functions of the angle of shearing resistance (φ). They are empirically/theoretically derived from plastic equilibrium analyses of a soil mass beneath a continuously loaded footing and are essential for computing the ultimate bearing capacity under both general and local shear failure modes."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ice-Cream Scoop" Model

Imagine pressing a spoon down into a tub of firm ice-cream. The resistance you feel comes from **three different things**, and the *magnitude* of each depends on the *angle at which you tilt the spoon and the "stickiness" of the ice-cream*:

1. **The "Stickiness" (Cohesion) — Factor N_c** → The natural grip of the ice-cream resisting the spoon (clay-like behavior).
2. **The "Weight of the Scoop" (Surcharge) — Factor N_q** → The overburdened material already piled on top of the spot you are pressing, helping push back.
3. **The "Density of the Tub" (Unit Weight) — Factor N_γ** → The granular, sandy particles below that jam together and resist the spoon (sand-like behavior).

The angle at which you push the spoon (tilt) is the **φ** — a steeper push on a rougher surface generates *much* higher resistance. This is exactly why the bearing capacity factors are exponential functions of φ.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Values

> [!NOTE]
> - **φ = 0°** (pure clay): N_c = **5.14**, N_q = **1.00**, N_γ = **0.00**
> - **φ = 30°** (medium sand): N_c ≈ **30.14**, N_q ≈ **18.40**, N_γ ≈ **22.40** (Vesic/IS)
> - **φ = 45°** (dense gravel): N_c ≈ **133.87**, N_q ≈ **134.87**, N_γ ≈ **271.75** (Vesic/IS)
> - The factor N_q rises **exponentially** with φ — a small change in φ causes a large change in N_q.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The "Why" Behind the Factors

The three factors are derived from the **Theory of Plastic Equilibrium** (Prandtl-Reissner wedge mechanism). The soil mass beneath a footing, at incipient failure, is idealized into three distinct zones:

- **Zone I (Active Wedge / Elastic Zone):** Sits directly beneath the footing; angle = (45 + φ/2) with horizontal.
- **Zone II (Radial Shear Zone):** A logarithmic spiral transition region; bounded by the radii r₀ and r₁.
- **Zone III (Passive Wedge):** A triangular passive zone pushed upward; angle = (45 − φ/2) with horizontal.

The bearing capacity is the *sum* of three independent resistive contributions evaluated along the failure surface, giving birth to the c, q, and γ terms.

## 2.2 Boundary Conditions & Assumptions (as per KTU expected solutions)

1. Footing is a **strip (B/L → 0)**, shallow (D_f ≤ B).
2. Soil is **homogeneous, isotropic, semi-infinite**.
3. **General shear failure** (default unless specified otherwise → use N_c, N_q, N_γ unmodified).
4. Water table corrections applied separately.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically derived** and used for KTU 2024 numerical answers.

| # | Equation | Form | Validity |
|---|----------|------|----------|
| 1 | $N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi}\cdot \tan^2\left(45 + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | Exponential | All φ ≥ 0° |
| 2 | $N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | Cotangent | All φ > 0° |
| 3 | $N_c = \pi + 2$ (for φ = 0) | Constant | Pure clay |
| 4 | $N_{\gamma} = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$ | Vesic / IS 6403 | 0° < φ ≤ 45° |
| 5 | $N_{\gamma} = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\,\phi)$ | Meyerhof | 0° < φ ≤ 45° |
| 6 | $N_{\gamma} = 1.8(N_q - 1)\tan\phi$ | Terzaghi | Strip footing only |
| 7 | **IS 6403 Ultimate Bearing Capacity** | $q_u = cN_c s_c d_c i_c + q(N_q - 1)s_q d_q i_q + 0.5 B \gamma N_{\gamma} s_{\gamma} d_{\gamma} i_{\gamma}$ | General |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** The 2024 scheme paper (Module 2) predominantly uses the **IS 6403 / Vesic** form for N_γ. Always write down the formula you are using; marks are awarded for the formula statement itself.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundation Design of Buildings & Bridges** → Computes safe load on isolated, strip, or raft footings.
- **Tower & Chimney Foundations** → Determines footing size for high overturning moments.
- **Earth-Retaining Structures** → These same factors appear in **active earth pressure** calculations (K_a, K_p).
- **Offshore Jack-Up Rigs** → Spudcan bearing capacity uses modified N_γ factors for layered seabed soils.
- **Pile Foundations** → N_c, N_q, N_γ are central to the **β-method** and **α-method** of pile capacity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Expression for N_q

We start from the **Reissner-Prandtl solution** for a strip footing under surcharge load **q** (no cohesion, no self-weight). The failure surface is the classical log-spiral. The ultimate surcharge pressure is:

$$q_{nu} = q\cdot N_q$$

Reissner (1924) showed, by equating energy along the log-spiral, that:

$$N_q = e^{\pi\tan\phi}\cdot \tan^2\left(45 + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

### Step-by-Step Algebraic Walkthrough

**Step 1:** Let $K_p = \tan^2\left(45 + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ — this is the **Rankine passive earth pressure coefficient**.

**Step 2:** Express the exponential term:
$$e^{\pi\tan\phi} = \exp(\pi\tan\phi)$$

**Step 3:** Multiply the two terms (they are *not* functions of the same variable, hence the product form):
$$N_q = K_p \cdot \exp(\pi\tan\phi)$$

**Step 4:** **Limiting case** — when φ = 0:
$$K_p = \tan^2(45) = 1, \quad \exp(0) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_q = 1 \;\;\checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Expression for N_c (Cohesion Term)

For a **cohesion-only soil with surcharge**, Prandtl's classical plasticity solution gives the bearing capacity on a weightless medium:

$$q_{nu} = cN_c + qN_q$$

Subtracting the pure-surcharge solution, the cohesion contribution is isolated. By integrating the cohesive resistance along the log-spiral, Terzaghi (1943) showed:

$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$$

### Step-by-Step Verification

**Step 1:** Write the identity:
$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$$

**Step 2:** Apply L'Hôpital's rule as φ → 0:
$$\lim_{\phi\to 0}(N_q - 1)\cot\phi = \lim_{\phi\to 0}\frac{N_q - 1}{\tan\phi}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate numerator and denominator with respect to φ (using the form $N_q = e^{\pi\tan\phi} \sec^2(45+\phi/2)$ — too long for display, but the standard result holds):
$$\lim_{\phi\to 0} = \pi + 2 = 5.14 \;\;\checkmark$$

Hence, the **modified N_c for φ = 0** is the famous **5.14**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Expression for N_γ (Vesic / IS 6403)

This factor is the **most debated** because the self-weight of the soil wedge creates a non-trivial integral along the curved failure surface. Vesic (1973) proposed a closed-form approximation:

$$N_{\gamma} = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$$

### Numerical Worked Example (Comprehensive)

> **Problem:** A strip footing of width **B = 2 m** rests on a sandy soil with **φ = 30°**, **c = 0**, **γ = 18 kN/m³**, and **D_f = 1.5 m**. Calculate N_c, N_q, N_γ and the ultimate bearing capacity using **IS 6403** (assume general shear, no shape / depth / inclination factors).

**Step 1: Calculate tan φ**
$$\tan(30°) = 0.5774$$

**Step 2: Calculate N_q**

$$N_q = e^{\pi \times 0.5774}\cdot \tan^2\left(45 + \frac{30}{2}\right)$$

$$\pi \times 0.5774 = 1.8138$$

$$e^{1.8138} = 6.1325$$

$$\tan(60°) = 1.7321, \quad \tan^2(60°) = 3.0001$$

$$N_q = 6.1325 \times 3.0001 = 18.40$$

**Step 3: Calculate N_c**
$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi = (18.40 - 1) \times \frac{1}{0.5774}$$

$$N_c = 17.40 \times 1.7321 = 30.14$$

**Step 4: Calculate N_γ (Vesic / IS 6403)**
$$N_{\gamma} = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi = 2(18.40 + 1) \times 0.5774$$

$$N_{\gamma} = 2 \times 19.40 \times 0.5774 = 22.40$$

**Step 5: Compute surcharge and self-weight terms**
- Surcharge: $q = \gamma \cdot D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27\ \text{kN/m}^2$
- Self-weight term: $0.5 \times B \times \gamma = 0.5 \times 2 \times 18 = 18\ \text{kN/m}^2$

**Step 6: Apply the IS 6403 Ultimate Bearing Capacity formula**
$$q_u = cN_c s_c d_c i_c + q(N_q - 1)s_q d_q i_q + 0.5 B \gamma N_{\gamma} s_{\gamma} d_{\gamma} i_{\gamma}$$

Since the problem states **no shape / depth / inclination factors** (s = d = i = 1) and **c = 0**:

$$q_u = 0 + 27 \times (18.40 - 1) + 18 \times 22.40$$

$$q_u = 27 \times 17.40 + 18 \times 22.40$$

$$q_u = 469.80 + 403.20 = 873.00\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 7: Net ultimate bearing capacity**
$$q_{nu} = q_u - q = 873.00 - 27 = 846.00\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 8: Safe bearing capacity (FOS = 3)**
$$q_{safe} = \frac{q_u}{F} = \frac{873.00}{3} = 291.00\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

## 3.4 Quick Numerical Verification Table (IS 6403 / Vesic)

| φ (°) | N_q | N_c | N_γ |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|
| 0 | 1.00 | 5.14 | 0.00 |
| 5 | 1.57 | 6.49 | 0.45 |
| 10 | 2.47 | 8.34 | 1.22 |
| 15 | 3.94 | 10.98 | 2.65 |
| 20 | 6.40 | 14.83 | 5.39 |
| 25 | 10.66 | 20.72 | 10.88 |
| 30 | 18.40 | 30.14 | 22.40 |
| 35 | 33.30 | 46.12 | 48.03 |
| 40 | 64.20 | 75.31 | 109.41 |
| 45 | 134.87 | 133.87 | 271.75 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table:** Notice that N_γ rises **super-exponentially** beyond φ = 30°. A 5° increase from 40° to 45° more than *doubles* N_γ. This is why φ is measured carefully in the lab (Direct Shear / Triaxial) — small φ errors → large capacity errors.

## 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Local Shear Failure (Terzaghi Modified Factors)

> **Problem:** Same as before, but the soil is **loose to medium sand** with local shear. Use φ' = $\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\tan\phi\right)$ and modified factors $N_c' = 0.67\,N_c$, $N_q' = 0.67\,N_q$, $N_{\gamma}' = 0.5\,N_{\gamma}$.

**Step 1: Compute reduced φ'**
$$\tan\phi' = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.5774 = 0.3849, \quad \phi' = 21.05°$$

**Step 2: Compute modified factors**
- $N_q' = 0.67 \times N_q(\phi=21.05°)$
- For 21.05°: $\pi\tan\phi' = 1.2095$, $e^{1.2095} = 3.352$, $\tan(60.525°)^2 = 3.825$
- $N_q = 12.82$, so $N_q' = 0.67 \times 12.82 = 8.59$
- $N_c' = 0.67 \times (8.59 - 1) \times \cot(21.05°) = 0.67 \times 7.59 \times 2.599 = 13.22$
- $N_{\gamma}' = 0.5 \times 2 \times (8.59 + 1) \times \tan(21.05°) = 0.5 \times 2 \times 9.59 \times 0.3849 = 3.69$

**Step 3: Apply the formula (c = 0)**
$$q_u = q(N_q' - 1) + 0.5B\gamma N_{\gamma}' = 27 \times 7.59 + 18 \times 3.69 = 204.93 + 66.42 = 271.35\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Comparison:** Local shear gives **271 kN/m²** vs general shear's **873 kN/m²** — a **~70% drop**. KTU questions frequently test this comparison.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Terzaghi's Three-Zone Failure Mechanism (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    footing["Strip Footing of Width B"]
    z1["Zone I - Active Wedge\nAngle: 45 + phi/2 with horizontal"]
    z2["Zone II - Radial Shear Zone\nLog-spiral surface between r0 and r1"]
    z3["Zone III - Passive Wedge\nAngle: 45 - phi/2 with horizontal"]
    gs["Ground Surface"]
    res["Resultant Resistance = c*Nc + q*Nq + 0.5*B*gamma*Ngamma"]
    gwt["Water Table (Correction Layer)"]

    footing --- z1
    z1 --- z2
    z2 --- z3
    z3 --- gs
    footing --> res
    z1 -.-> res
    z2 -.-> res
    z3 -.-> res
    gwt -.-> z2
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — How N_c, N_q, N_γ Feed the Final Capacity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Soil Parameters:\nphi, c, gamma, Df, B"] --> B["Compute Nq\nexp*tan^2(45+phi/2)"]
    A --> C["Compute Nc\n(Nq-1)*cot phi"]
    A --> D["Compute Ngamma\n2(Nq+1)*tan phi"]
    B --> E["Term 1: c*Nc\nCohesion Component"]
    C --> E
    B --> F["Term 2: q*(Nq-1)\nSurcharge Component"]
    B --> G["Term 3: 0.5*B*gamma*Ngamma\nSelf-Weight Component"]
    D --> G
    E --> H["Sum to get qu\nUltimate Bearing Capacity"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["qnu = qu - q\nNet Ultimate Capacity"]
    I --> J["qsafe = qnu / FOS\nSafe Bearing Capacity"]
```

## 4.3 Sensitivity Diagram — Variation of N_c, N_q, N_γ with φ

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Low_Phi["Low phi (0 to 10 degrees) - Clay Dominant"]
        L1["Nc rises from 5.14 to 8.34"]
        L2["Nq rises from 1.00 to 2.47"]
        L3["Ngamma remains below 1.22"]
        L4["Cohesion term DOMINATES"]
    end

    subgraph Med_Phi["Medium phi (10 to 30 degrees) - Silty Sand"]
        M1["Nc rises to 30.14"]
        M2["Nq rises to 18.40"]
        M3["Ngamma rises to 22.40"]
        M4["Surcharge term DOMINATES"]
    end

    subgraph High_Phi["High phi (30 to 45 degrees) - Dense Gravel"]
        H1["Nc rises to 133.87"]
        H2["Nq rises to 134.87"]
        H3["Ngamma rises to 271.75"]
        H4["Self-weight term DOMINATES"]
    end

    Low_Phi --> Med_Phi --> High_Phi
```

## 4.4 IS 6403 / Vesic N_γ — Comparison with Meyerhof and Terzaghi (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Three_Forms["Three Accepted Forms of N_gamma"]
        VESIC["VESIC / IS 6403 FORM:\nN_gamma = 2(Nq + 1) tan phi\nMOST USED IN KTU EXAMS"]
        MEYER["MEYERHOF FORM:\nN_gamma = (Nq - 1) tan(1.4 phi)\nCONSERVATIVE FOR LOW phi"]
        TERZ["TERZAGHI FORM:\nN_gamma = 1.8(Nq - 1) tan phi\nSTRIP FOOTING ONLY"]
    end

    phi["Angle phi (degrees)"] --> VESIC
    phi --> MEYER
    phi --> TERZ
    VESIC --> result["Used to compute\nqu = 0.5 * B * gamma * N_gamma"]
    MEYER --> result
    TERZ --> result
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List any three assumptions made in the derivation of Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The soil is **homogeneous, isotropic, and semi-infinite**, with the footing resting on its horizontal surface.
2. The footing is of **infinite length (strip footing) and shallow** (D_f ≤ B), producing a 2-D plane-strain failure.
3. The soil fails by **general shear**, obeying the **Mohr-Coulomb** failure criterion (c–φ soil), and the self-weight of the soil above the base is treated as a surcharge q = γD_f.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's note: Students who *miss self-weight being converted to surcharge* lose 1 mark.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Why is the N_γ factor the most uncertain of the three bearing capacity factors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The N_γ term arises from the **self-weight of the soil in the failure wedge**, requiring integration of stresses along a curved log-spiral surface — a mathematically intractable problem.
2. Closed-form solutions (Vesic, Meyerhof, Terzaghi) are **empirical fits** to numerical plasticity solutions; they differ by up to **30%** for the same φ.
3. It is the **only factor that does not have a rigorous theoretical basis** — unlike N_c and N_q which stem from Prandtl-Reissner plasticity.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Losing 2 marks:** Forgetting to convert **inches/feet to SI units** (B in meters, D_f in meters, γ in kN/m³).
> - **Losing 2 marks:** Using the **wrong N_γ form** (Meyerhof vs Vesic) — always state the source explicitly.
> - **Losing 1 mark:** Forgetting the **surcharge term** (q = γD_f) when c = 0.
> - **Losing 1 mark:** Forgetting to **subtract q** when computing **net** bearing capacity.
> - **Losing 2 marks:** Not applying the **FOS** correctly — KTU expects F = 3 for clayey soils and F = 2.5 for sandy soils, *unless stated otherwise*.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

A strip footing of width **B = 3 m** is to be placed at a depth of **D_f = 1.5 m** in a sandy soil with **φ = 35°**, **c = 0**, and **γ = 19 kN/m³**. The water table is at the base of the footing. Compute the **ultimate** and **net ultimate** bearing capacity using **IS 6403 (Vesic factors)**. Take FOS = 3 and find the **safe bearing pressure**.

#### (a) Compute the bearing capacity factors N_c, N_q, N_γ [7 marks]

**Solution:**

$$\tan(35°) = 0.7002$$

**Compute N_q:**

$$\pi \tan(35°) = \pi \times 0.7002 = 2.1995$$

$$e^{2.1995} = 9.0176$$

$$\tan\left(45 + \frac{35}{2}\right) = \tan(62.5°) = 1.9210$$

$$\tan^2(62.5°) = 3.6902$$

$$N_q = 9.0176 \times 3.6902 = \mathbf{33.30}$$

**Compute N_c:**

$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot(35°) = (33.30 - 1) \times \frac{1}{0.7002} = 32.30 \times 1.4281 = \mathbf{46.12}$$

**Compute N_γ (Vesic):**

$$N_{\gamma} = 2(N_q + 1)\tan(35°) = 2(33.30 + 1) \times 0.7002 = 2 \times 34.30 \times 0.7002 = \mathbf{48.03}$$

*(Awarded 7 marks — 2 each for N_c, N_q; 3 for N_γ with formula statement)*

#### (b) Compute q_u, q_nu, and q_safe [7 marks]

**Solution:**

Since c = 0, the cohesion term vanishes.

**Surcharge:** $q = \gamma D_f = 19 \times 1.5 = 28.5\ \text{kN/m}^2$

**Self-weight coefficient:** $0.5 B \gamma = 0.5 \times 3 \times 19 = 28.5\ \text{kN/m}^2$

**Ultimate bearing capacity:**

$$q_u = 0 + q(N_q - 1) + 0.5 B \gamma N_{\gamma}$$

$$q_u = 28.5 \times (33.30 - 1) + 28.5 \times 48.03$$

$$q_u = 28.5 \times 32.30 + 28.5 \times 48.03$$

$$q_u = 920.55 + 1368.86 = \mathbf{2289.41\ \text{kN/m}^2}$$

**Net ultimate bearing capacity:**

$$q_{nu} = q_u - q = 2289.41 - 28.5 = \mathbf{2260.91\ \text{kN/m}^2}$$

**Safe bearing capacity (FOS = 3):**

$$q_{safe} = \frac{q_u}{F} = \frac{2289.41}{3} = \mathbf{763.14\ \text{kN/m}^2}$$

*(Awarded 7 marks — 2 for surcharge, 3 for q_u, 1 for q_nu, 1 for q_safe)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

A square footing of **2 m × 2 m** rests at **D_f = 1 m** in a clayey soil with **c = 30 kN/m²**, **φ = 15°**, and **γ = 17.5 kN/m³**. Use **Meyerhof's** bearing capacity factors and **IS 6403 shape factors** (s_c = 1.3, s_q = 1.0, s_γ = 0.8). Calculate the **ultimate bearing capacity** and **safe load** the footing can carry. Use FOS = 3.

#### (a) Compute the bearing capacity factors [7 marks]

**Solution:**

$$\tan(15°) = 0.2679$$

**Compute N_q:**

$$\pi\tan(15°) = 0.8417, \quad e^{0.8417} = 2.3204$$

$$\tan(52.5°) = 1.3032, \quad \tan^2(52.5°) = 1.6983$$

$$N_q = 2.3204 \times 1.6983 = \mathbf{3.94}$$

**Compute N_c:**

$$N_c = (3.94 - 1)\cot(15°) = 2.94 \times 3.7321 = \mathbf{10.98}$$

**Compute N_γ (Meyerhof):**

$$N_{\gamma} = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi) = (3.94 - 1)\tan(21°) = 2.94 \times 0.3839 = \mathbf{1.13}$$

*(Awarded 7 marks — 2 for N_c, 2 for N_q, 3 for N_γ including formula reference to Meyerhof)*

#### (b) Compute the ultimate and safe load [7 marks]

**Solution:**

**Surcharge:** $q = \gamma D_f = 17.5 \times 1 = 17.5\ \text{kN/m}^2$

**Self-weight term:** $0.5 B \gamma = 0.5 \times 2 \times 17.5 = 17.5\ \text{kN/m}^2$

**Cohesion term:**
$$cN_c s_c = 30 \times 10.98 \times 1.3 = 428.22\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Surcharge term:**
$$q(N_q - 1)s_q = 17.5 \times (3.94 - 1) \times 1.0 = 17.5 \times 2.94 = 51.45\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Self-weight term:**
$$0.5 B \gamma N_{\gamma} s_{\gamma} = 17.5 \times 1.13 \times 0.8 = 15.82\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Ultimate bearing capacity:**
$$q_u = 428.22 + 51.45 + 15.82 = \mathbf{495.49\ \text{kN/m}^2}$$

**Safe bearing capacity:**
$$q_{safe} = \frac{495.49}{3} = 165.16\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Safe load on 2 m × 2 m footing:**
$$Q_{safe} = q_{safe} \times A = 165.16 \times 4 = \mathbf{660.66\ \text{kN}}$$

*(Awarded 7 marks — 2 each for q_u components, 1 for q_safe, 2 for safe load with area computation)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2 Exam**

- **Three factors, one variable:** N_c, N_q, N_γ are *purely* functions of φ — c, γ, B, D_f do *not* enter the factor computation.
- **Formula stack (in order):** N_q → N_c → N_γ. Always compute N_q *first* because both N_c and N_γ depend on it.
- **Two N_γ forms to remember:** Vesic (IS 6403): $2(N_q+1)\tan\phi$ and Meyerhof: $(N_q-1)\tan(1.4\phi)$. KTU paper usually specifies — if not, use **Vesic**.
- **Pure-clay case (φ = 0):** N_c = 5.14, N_q = 1, N_γ = 0. The bearing capacity becomes $q_u = 5.14\,c + q$.
- **Local shear modifications:** $N_c' = 0.67 N_c$, $N_q' = 0.67 N_q$, $N_{\gamma}' = 0.5 N_{\gamma}$ and φ' = $\tan^{-1}(0.67 \tan\phi)$.
- **Net vs Gross:** $q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f$ — for net bearing capacity, always **subtract** the overburden.
- **Factor of Safety (FOS):** Default 3 for clays, 2.5 for sands, unless specified.
- **Unit consistency:** B in **m**, γ in **kN/m³**, q in **kN/m²**. Mixing SI and Imperial units is the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers.
- **Always show the formula explicitly** before substitution — KTU award 1 mark for correct formula citation alone.
- **Plot the failure wedge** in descriptive answers (Zones I, II, III) — examiners reward a clear diagram with 1–2 extra marks.
- **N_q grows exponentially**, so a small error in measuring φ (e.g., 2°–3°) leads to a large error in N_q. Always perform **triplicate** lab tests.
- **Strip vs Square footings:** KTU Module 2 is mostly strip (B/L = 0). When square/circular is given, apply the **shape factors** (s_c, s_q, s_γ).
- **Water-table correction:** When the water table is at the base of the footing, use **buoyant unit weight** (γ' = γ_sat − γ_w) in the self-weight term only.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terzaghi's Formulae for Circular and Square Footings - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Terzaghi's Formulae for Circular and Square Footings - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Terzaghi's General Bearing Capacity Equation (1943):**
> The ultimate bearing capacity ($q_u$) of a shallow foundation is the maximum pressure that the soil can sustain at the base of the footing just before shear failure occurs. For shallow foundations, Terzaghi proposed distinct modified equations for **strip**, **square**, and **circular** footings by incorporating empirical **shape factors** ($s_c$, $s_q$, $s_\gamma$) into his original three-term general equation.

For a **square footing** of size $B \times B$:

$$q_u = 1.3 \, c \, N_c + q \, N_q + 0.4 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$

For a **circular footing** of diameter $B$:

$$q_u = 1.3 \, c \, N_c + q \, N_q + 0.3 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$

where $q = \gamma \cdot D_f$ is the overburden pressure at the level of the foundation base, and $N_c$, $N_q$, $N_\gamma$ are **dimensionless bearing capacity factors** that depend solely on the soil's angle of internal friction ($\phi$).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: Pressing Your Palm on a Soft Cake**
> Imagine pushing your palm flat on a soft cake. The cake fails by a triangular wedge slipping out from under your hand. Now compare pressing with:
> - **Open palm (square)** — A bigger, flat surface distributes load over a wider area, so the cake resists more.
> - **Closed fist (circular)** — A smaller contact patch means the wedge is narrower; the cake fails slightly more easily.
> - **Knife edge (strip footing)** — Theoretically infinite in one direction, with no end resistance.
>
> The **shape factors** mathematically capture this "end-resistance effect." The square footing has a slightly higher bearing capacity than the circular footing of the same width $B$, because the square has four corners resisting shear, while the circle has continuously curving edges with no abrupt corners.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Unit weight of soil ($\gamma$):** typically **18 – 20 kN/m³** for sands; **17 – 19 kN/m³** for clays.
> - **Cohesion ($c$):** in **kPa** (kilopascals) or **kN/m²**.
> - **Standard Factor of Safety ($FS$):** **3** for bearing capacity (as per IS 6403 / KTU convention).
> - **Default surcharge approximation:** $q = \gamma \cdot D_f$ (assuming water table is well below the base).
> - All bearing capacity factors ($N_c$, $N_q$, $N_\gamma$) are **dimensionless**.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

This topic is a **high-weightage, high-scoring** module in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) under PBCET504. It is one of the most frequently asked numerical problems because:
- It integrates **theory + computation**.
- The bearing capacity factors are often tabulated in the question paper, making it a friendly numerical.
- Square and circular footings are the **two most common shapes** used for isolated column footings and machine foundations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — How Terzaghi Built the Formula

Terzaghi (1943) developed the bearing capacity equation by **superposing three separate physical effects**:

1. **Cohesion Term ($c \cdot N_c$):** Resistance of soil particles sticking together (dominant in clay).
2. **Surcharge Term ($q \cdot N_q$):** Resistance from the weight of soil sitting on the failure wedge beside the footing.
3. **Self-Weight Term ($0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$):** Resistance from the weight of soil within the failure zone itself (dominant in sand).

He assumed **general shear failure** (soil is dense/stiff) and used **statical equilibrium** of the three-zone failure mechanism:
- **Zone I** (triangular wedge directly under footing) — active state.
- **Zone II** (radial shear zone) — radial shear.
- **Zone III** (passive Rankine wedge) — passive state.

---

## 2.2 Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Factors

These factors depend only on the **soil friction angle $\phi$**:

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

$$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cdot \cot\phi$$

$$N_\gamma = (N_q - 1) \cdot \tan(1.4\phi)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Terzaghi derived $N_c$ and $N_q$ rigorously, but $N_\gamma$ was an **empirical approximation** (since the integral has no closed form). This is why modern codes (IS 6403, Meyerhof, Hansen, Vesic) use slightly different $N_\gamma$ expressions. **For KTU, always use Terzaghi's original form unless the question specifies otherwise.**

---

## 2.3 Shape Factors in Terzaghi's Theory

| Footing Type | $s_c$ | $s_q$ | $s_\gamma$ | Compact Form of $q_u$ |
|--------------|:-----:|:-----:|:----------:|------------------------|
| Strip / Continuous ($L/B \to \infty$) | 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 | $cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$ |
| **Square** ($B \times B$) | **1.3** | **1.0** | **0.8** | $1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.4\gamma B N_\gamma$ |
| **Circular** (Diameter $B$) | **1.3** | **1.0** | **0.6** | $1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.3\gamma B N_\gamma$ |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Definition / Unit |
|---------|----------|-------------------|
| Surcharge (overburden) at base | $q = \gamma \cdot D_f$ | kN/m² |
| Net ultimate bearing capacity | $q_{nu} = q_u - q$ | kN/m² |
| Safe (gross) bearing capacity | $q_s = \dfrac{q_u}{FS}$ | kN/m² |
| Net safe bearing capacity | $q_{ns} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FS}$ | kN/m² |
| Allowable / Safe gross pressure | $q_{sa} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FS} + \gamma D_f$ | kN/m² |
| Bearing capacity factor $N_q$ | $N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2(45 + \phi/2)$ | Dimensionless |
| Bearing capacity factor $N_c$ | $N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi$ | Dimensionless |
| Bearing capacity factor $N_\gamma$ | $N_\gamma = (N_q - 1) \tan(1.4\phi)$ | Dimensionless |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Isolated column footings** for residential/commercial buildings → **square**.
> - **Machine foundations, silos, water tanks, transmission towers, chimneys** → **circular**.
> - **Bridge piers, retaining wall bases, long wall footings** → **strip**.
> - Engineers must pick the right shape factor to avoid **under-design** (collapse) or **over-design** (cost overrun).

---

## 2.6 Important Assumptions (KTU Examiner's Check-List)

- **General shear failure** mode is assumed (dense sand / stiff clay).
- Foundation is **shallow** ($D_f \leq B$).
- **Groundwater table** is well below the base of the foundation (or correction factors are applied separately).
- Soil is **homogeneous and isotropic** down to a depth $\geq B$ below the base.
- The base of the footing is **rough** (Terzaghi's assumption).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Problem Solutions

## 3.1 Derivation: From Strip to Square and Circular Footings

For a **strip footing**, Terzaghi's equation is:

$$q_{u,\text{strip}} = c \, N_c + q \, N_q + 0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$

For a **square footing**, the failure surface in 3-D is a **pyramid + truncated cones + passive wedges** rather than a simple 2-D wedge. This geometry yields a higher resistance per unit width. Terzaghi calibrated the constants empirically:

$$q_{u,\text{square}} = 1.3 \, c \, N_c + 1.0 \, q \, N_q + 0.4 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$

For a **circular footing**, the failure surface is axisymmetric (a cone of revolution), and end resistance is less effective than a square (no corners):

$$q_{u,\text{circular}} = 1.3 \, c \, N_c + 1.0 \, q \, N_q + 0.3 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$

The physical interpretation of the constant $1.3$ in the cohesion term is that the **3-D passive resistance** is $\approx 30\%$ higher than the 2-D case. The constants $0.4$ and $0.3$ in the self-weight term are empirical fits to model tests.

---

## 3.2 WORKED EXAMPLE 1 — Square Footing (Standard KTU Pattern)

> **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam Style]:**
> A square footing of size $2\,\text{m} \times 2\,\text{m}$ is founded at a depth of $1.5\,\text{m}$ in a soil with unit weight $\gamma = 18\,\text{kN/m}^3$, cohesion $c = 15\,\text{kPa}$, and angle of internal friction $\phi = 30^\circ$. Using Terzaghi's theory, calculate the ultimate bearing capacity, net ultimate bearing capacity, and the safe bearing capacity. Adopt a factor of safety of $3$.

### Step 1: Identify the footing type
Square footing → Use $s_c = 1.3$, $s_q = 1.0$, $s_\gamma = 0.8$.

### Step 2: Compute the surcharge (overburden pressure)

$$q = \gamma \cdot D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 3: Compute the bearing capacity factors for $\phi = 30^\circ$

First compute $N_q$:

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan 30^\circ} \cdot \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \tfrac{30^\circ}{2}\right)$$

$$\tan 30^\circ = 0.5774 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \pi \tan 30^\circ = 3.1416 \times 0.5774 = 1.8138$$

$$e^{1.8138} = 6.132$$

$$\tan(45^\circ + 15^\circ) = \tan 60^\circ = 1.732 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan^2 60^\circ = 3.000$$

$$N_q = 6.132 \times 3.000 = 18.40$$

Next, $N_c$:

$$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi = (18.40 - 1) \times \cot 30^\circ = 17.40 \times 1.732 = 30.14$$

Finally, $N_\gamma$ (Terzaghi's empirical form):

$$N_\gamma = (N_q - 1) \tan(1.4\phi) = 17.40 \times \tan(42^\circ) = 17.40 \times 0.9004 = 15.67$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Check with Standard Tables:** For $\phi = 30^\circ$, the standard values are $N_c \approx 30.14$, $N_q \approx 18.40$, $N_\gamma \approx 15.67$. ✅

### Step 4: Compute the ultimate bearing capacity

$$q_u = 1.3 \, c \, N_c + q \, N_q + 0.4 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$

**Cohesion term:**

$$1.3 \times 15 \times 30.14 = 19.5 \times 30.14 = 587.73 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Surcharge term:**

$$q \times N_q = 27.0 \times 18.40 = 496.80 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Self-weight term:**

$$0.4 \times 18 \times 2 \times 15.67 = 0.4 \times 36 \times 15.67 = 14.4 \times 15.67 = 225.65 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Summation:**

$$q_u = 587.73 + 496.80 + 225.65 = 1310.18 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 5: Net ultimate bearing capacity

$$q_{nu} = q_u - q = 1310.18 - 27.0 = 1283.18 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 6: Safe bearing capacities (with $FS = 3$)

$$q_s = \frac{q_u}{FS} = \frac{1310.18}{3} = 436.73 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{FS} = \frac{1283.18}{3} = 427.73 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer (Example 1):** $q_u = 1310.18 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $q_{nu} = 1283.18 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $q_s = 436.73 \text{ kN/m}^2$.

---

## 3.3 WORKED EXAMPLE 2 — Circular Footing (Same Soil Conditions)

> **Problem Statement:**
> Repeat Example 1, but with a **circular footing of diameter $B = 2\,\text{m}$** under identical soil and depth conditions.

### Step 1: Shape factors for circular footing
$s_c = 1.3$, $s_q = 1.0$, $s_\gamma = 0.6$.

### Step 2: Bearing capacity factors remain the same
$N_c = 30.14$, $N_q = 18.40$, $N_\gamma = 15.67$.

### Step 3: Compute $q_u$ for circular footing

$$q_u = 1.3 \, c \, N_c + q \, N_q + 0.3 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$

**Cohesion term (unchanged):**

$$1.3 \times 15 \times 30.14 = 587.73 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Surcharge term (unchanged):**

$$27.0 \times 18.40 = 496.80 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Self-weight term (smaller because $s_\gamma = 0.6$):**

$$0.3 \times 18 \times 2 \times 15.67 = 0.3 \times 36 \times 15.67 = 10.8 \times 15.67 = 169.24 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Summation:**

$$q_u = 587.73 + 496.80 + 169.24 = 1253.77 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 4: Net ultimate and safe capacities

$$q_{nu} = 1253.77 - 27.0 = 1226.77 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$q_s = \frac{1253.77}{3} = 417.92 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer (Example 2):** $q_u = 1253.77 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $q_{nu} = 1226.77 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $q_s = 417.92 \text{ kN/m}^2$.

---

## 3.4 WORKED EXAMPLE 3 — Comparison & Design Insight

> **Problem Statement [KTU Type]:**
> A square footing ($1.5 \times 1.5\,\text{m}$) and a circular footing (diameter $1.5\,\text{m}$) are both placed at a depth of $1.0\,\text{m}$ in a soil with $\gamma = 19\,\text{kN/m}^3$, $c = 10\,\text{kPa}$, $\phi = 25^\circ$. Compare their safe bearing capacities with $FS = 3$.

### Step 1: Bearing capacity factors for $\phi = 25^\circ$

$$\tan 25^\circ = 0.4663 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \pi \tan 25^\circ = 1.4649$$

$$e^{1.4649} = 4.327$$

$$\tan(45 + 12.5) = \tan 57.5^\circ = 1.5697 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan^2 = 2.464$$

$$N_q = 4.327 \times 2.464 = 10.66$$

$$N_c = (10.66 - 1) \cot 25^\circ = 9.66 \times 2.1445 = 20.72$$

$$N_\gamma = 9.66 \times \tan(35^\circ) = 9.66 \times 0.7002 = 6.76$$

### Step 2: Surcharge

$$q = 19 \times 1.0 = 19.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 3: Square footing ($B = 1.5\,\text{m}$)

$$q_{u,sq} = 1.3 \times 10 \times 20.72 + 19 \times 10.66 + 0.4 \times 19 \times 1.5 \times 6.76$$

$$= 269.36 + 202.54 + 77.06 = 548.96 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$q_{s,sq} = \frac{548.96}{3} = 182.99 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 4: Circular footing ($B = 1.5\,\text{m}$)

$$q_{u,circ} = 1.3 \times 10 \times 20.72 + 19 \times 10.66 + 0.3 \times 19 \times 1.5 \times 6.76$$

$$= 269.36 + 202.54 + 57.79 = 529.69 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$q_{s,circ} = \frac{529.69}{3} = 176.56 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 5: Comparison

$$\frac{q_{u,sq} - q_{u,circ}}{q_{u,circ}} \times 100 = \frac{548.96 - 529.69}{529.69} \times 100 = 3.64\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** For the same width, the square footing carries **~3.6% more load** than the circular footing. However, the **circular footing has a smaller perimeter** (less concrete) and a more uniform stress distribution — hence preferred for **water tanks, silos, and chimneys** where bending moments and punching shear need to be minimized.

---

## 3.5 WORKED EXAMPLE 4 — Design-Type Problem (Finding $B$ from Allowable Load)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A square column footing must carry a service load (including column self-weight) of $1500\,\text{kN}$ at a depth of $1.2\,\text{m}$. The soil has $\gamma = 17.5\,\text{kN/m}^3$, $c = 5\,\text{kPa}$, $\phi = 35^\circ$. Find the minimum size $B$ of the footing using Terzaghi's theory with $FS = 3$. Ignore the weight of the footing and soil above base.

### Step 1: Bearing capacity factors for $\phi = 35^\circ$

$$\tan 35^\circ = 0.7002 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \pi \tan 35^\circ = 2.1995$$

$$e^{2.1995} = 9.017$$

$$\tan(45 + 17.5) = \tan 62.5^\circ = 1.9210 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan^2 = 3.690$$

$$N_q = 9.017 \times 3.690 = 33.30$$

$$N_c = (33.30 - 1) \cot 35^\circ = 32.30 \times 1.4281 = 46.13$$

$$N_\gamma = 32.30 \times \tan(49^\circ) = 32.30 \times 1.1504 = 37.16$$

### Step 2: Required net safe bearing capacity

$$q_{ns,req} = \frac{P}{B^2} = \frac{1500}{B^2}$$

### Step 3: Net ultimate bearing capacity (square footing)

$$q_{nu} = 1.3 c N_c + q N_q + 0.4 \gamma B N_\gamma - q$$

$$= 1.3 \times 5 \times 46.13 + (17.5 \times 1.2) \times 33.30 + 0.4 \times 17.5 \times B \times 37.16 - 17.5 \times 1.2$$

$$= 299.85 + 699.30 + 260.12 \, B - 21.00$$

$$= 978.15 + 260.12 \, B \text{ (kN/m}^2)$$

### Step 4: Apply $FS = 3$

$$q_{ns} = \frac{978.15 + 260.12 \, B}{3} = 326.05 + 86.71 \, B$$

### Step 5: Equate to required $q_{ns}$

$$\frac{1500}{B^2} = 326.05 + 86.71 \, B$$

$$1500 = (326.05 + 86.71 \, B) \, B^2 = 326.05 B^2 + 86.71 B^3$$

This is a cubic in $B$. **Trial-and-error method:**

| $B$ (m) | LHS $= 326.05 B^2 + 86.71 B^3$ | RHS = 1500 |
|---------|----------------------------------|------------|
| 1.5 | $733.61 + 292.65 = 1026.26$ | Too small |
| 1.7 | $942.49 + 426.27 = 1368.76$ | Too small |
| 1.8 | $1056.40 + 505.62 = 1562.02$ | Slightly large |
| 1.78 | $1033.07 + 488.79 = 1521.86$ | ≈ 1500 ✓ |

### Step 6: Final design

$$\boxed{B \approx 1.8\,\text{m} \text{ (adopt standard size } 1.8 \times 1.8\,\text{m)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Judgement:** Always round **up** to the next standard construction size, and check that the **permissible settlement** (which is not computed by Terzaghi's theory) is also satisfied.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Computation Flow — Terzaghi's Method for Square / Circular Footings

The Mermaid block below maps the **complete computational workflow** that students must follow when solving KTU numerical problems on this topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Inputs] --> B[Footing Type: Square or Circular]
    B --> C[Soil Properties: c, phi, gamma]
    C --> D[Foundation Geometry: B, D_f]
    D --> E[Step 1: Compute Surcharge q = gamma x D_f]
    E --> F[Step 2: Compute Nq = e power pi tan phi times tan square 45 plus phi/2]
    F --> G[Step 3: Compute Nc = Nq minus 1 times cot phi]
    G --> H[Step 4: Compute Ngamma = Nq minus 1 times tan 1.4 phi]
    H --> I{Choose Footing Shape}
    I -->|Square| J[Use sc = 1.3, sq = 1.0, sgamma = 0.8]
    I -->|Circular| K[Use sc = 1.3, sq = 1.0, sgamma = 0.6]
    J --> L[Step 5: Compute qu = 1.3 c Nc + q Nq + 0.4 gamma B Ngamma]
    K --> M[Step 5: Compute qu = 1.3 c Nc + q Nq + 0.3 gamma B Ngamma]
    L --> N[Step 6: Compute qnu = qu minus q]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Step 7: Apply Factor of Safety FS]
    O --> P[Step 8: Compute qs = qu / FS and qns = qnu / FS]
    P --> Q[Step 9: Final Output - Report qs and qns]
    Q --> R[End]
```

---

## 4.2 Failure-Zone Geometry — 2-D vs 3-D Comparison

The Mermaid block below contrasts the **2-D planar failure wedge** (strip footing) with the **3-D pyramidal / conical failure surface** (square and circular footings).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Strip Footing: 2D Failure] --> B[Triangular Wedge under Base]
    B --> C[Radial Shear Zone Log Spiral]
    C --> D[Passive Rankine Wedge]

    E[Square Footing: 3D Failure] --> F[Pyramidal Wedge under Base]
    F --> G[Curved Shear Surface in 4 Quadrants]
    G --> H[Passive Wedges at 4 Corners]

    I[Circular Footing: 3D Failure] --> J[Cone of Revolution under Base]
    J --> K[Axisymmetric Shear Surface]
    K --> L[Continuous Passive Wedge around Perimeter]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparison of Shape Factors — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Footings [Footing Geometry]
        A1[Strip: B x L, L large]
        A2[Square: B x B]
        A3[Circular: Diameter B]
    end

    subgraph Cohesion [Cohesion Shape Factor sc]
        B1[sc = 1.0]
        B2[sc = 1.3]
        B3[sc = 1.3]
    end

    subgraph Surcharge [Surcharge Shape Factor sq]
        C1[sq = 1.0]
        C2[sq = 1.0]
        C3[sq = 1.0]
    end

    subgraph SelfWeight [Self Weight Shape Factor sgamma]
        D1[sgamma = 1.0]
        D2[sgamma = 0.8]
        D3[sgamma = 0.6]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> C1
    A1 --> D1
    A2 --> B2
    A2 --> C2
    A2 --> D2
    A3 --> B3
    A3 --> C3
    A3 --> D3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The square and circular footings have **identical** cohesion and surcharge factors, but differ in the **self-weight factor** ($0.8$ vs $0.6$). This is the **single most important difference** that students must memorize for KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Define the following terms with units: (a) Ultimate bearing capacity, (b) Net safe bearing capacity, (c) Surcharge.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(a) Ultimate bearing capacity ($q_u$):** The maximum gross pressure at the base of a foundation that the soil can withstand without shear failure. **Unit:** kN/m².
- **(b) Net safe bearing capacity ($q_{ns}$):** The net ultimate bearing capacity divided by an appropriate factor of safety, i.e., $q_{ns} = q_{nu} / FS$. **Unit:** kN/m².
- **(c) Surcharge ($q$):** The effective vertical stress (overburden pressure) at the level of the foundation base, computed as $q = \gamma \cdot D_f$. **Unit:** kN/m².

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**State the Terzaghi bearing capacity equation for a square footing and identify the physical meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$q_u = 1.3 \, c \, N_c + q \, N_q + 0.4 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$

- **$1.3 \, c \, N_c$:** Cohesion contribution — resistance due to inter-particle bonding in cohesive soils.
- **$q \, N_q$:** Surcharge contribution — resistance from the weight of soil beside the failure wedge above the base level.
- **$0.4 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$:** Self-weight contribution — resistance from the weight of soil within the failure zone. The factor $0.4$ is the **shape factor** ($s_\gamma = 0.8$ applied to $0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $D_f$ units** (e.g., using cm instead of m) — instant 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Using $\tan(1.4 \phi)$** with $\phi$ in radians — Terzaghi's $N_\gamma$ requires $\phi$ in **degrees**.
> 3. **Mixing up shape factors** between square and circular (the $s_\gamma$ difference: $0.8$ vs $0.6$ is a **1-mark penalty** zone).
> 4. **Forgetting to subtract $q$** when computing $q_{nu}$.
> 5. **Not reporting units** — IS-code style requires **kN/m² or kPa** explicitly.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
A square footing of size $2.5\,\text{m} \times 2.5\,\text{m}$ rests at a depth of $1.8\,\text{m}$ below the ground surface in a soil with $\gamma = 19\,\text{kN/m}^3$, $c = 20\,\text{kPa}$ and $\phi = 28^\circ$. Using Terzaghi's theory, determine the **ultimate bearing capacity** and the **net safe bearing capacity** with a factor of safety of 3.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
If the same footing is **converted to a circular footing** of diameter $2.5\,\text{m}$ under identical conditions, recompute the safe bearing capacity. By what percentage does the square footing carry more load than the circular footing?

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Surcharge:**

$$q = \gamma \cdot D_f = 19 \times 1.8 = 34.20 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Bearing capacity factors for $\phi = 28^\circ$:**

$$\tan 28^\circ = 0.5317 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \pi \tan 28^\circ = 1.6702$$

$$e^{1.6702} = 5.317$$

$$\tan(45 + 14) = \tan 59^\circ = 1.6643 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan^2 = 2.770$$

$$N_q = 5.317 \times 2.770 = 14.72$$

$$N_c = (14.72 - 1) \cot 28^\circ = 13.72 \times 1.8807 = 25.80$$

$$N_\gamma = 13.72 \times \tan(39.2^\circ) = 13.72 \times 0.8158 = 11.19$$

**Step 3 — Ultimate bearing capacity (square):**

$$q_u = 1.3 \times 20 \times 25.80 + 34.20 \times 14.72 + 0.4 \times 19 \times 2.5 \times 11.19$$

$$= 670.80 + 503.42 + 212.61 = 1386.83 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Net ultimate and net safe:**

$$q_{nu} = 1386.83 - 34.20 = 1352.63 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$q_{ns} = \frac{1352.63}{3} = 450.88 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> **Answer (a):** $q_u = 1386.83 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $q_{ns} = 450.88 \text{ kN/m}^2$.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

For **circular footing**, use $s_\gamma = 0.6$ instead of $0.8$.

**Step 5 — Recompute $q_u$ for circular:**

$$q_u = 1.3 \times 20 \times 25.80 + 34.20 \times 14.72 + 0.3 \times 19 \times 2.5 \times 11.19$$

$$= 670.80 + 503.42 + 159.46 = 1333.68 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$q_{ns,circ} = \frac{1333.68 - 34.20}{3} = \frac{1299.48}{3} = 433.16 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Percentage difference:**

$$\Delta\% = \frac{450.88 - 433.16}{433.16} \times 100 = 4.09\%$$

> **Answer (b):** $q_{ns,circ} = 433.16 \text{ kN/m}^2$; the square footing carries **~4.09% more** load than the circular footing.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
A circular footing of diameter $3.0\,\text{m}$ is to be placed at a depth of $1.5\,\text{m}$ in sandy soil with $\gamma = 18.5\,\text{kN/m}^3$, $c = 0$, and $\phi = 32^\circ$. Using Terzaghi's formula, compute the **ultimate** and **safe bearing capacity** ($FS = 3$). What total load can this footing support?

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
For the same site, if the engineer proposes a **square footing of $3.0\,\text{m} \times 3.0\,\text{m}$** at the same depth, recalculate the safe bearing capacity and determine which footing is more economical (in terms of allowable load per unit volume of concrete).

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bearing capacity factors for $\phi = 32^\circ$:**

$$\tan 32^\circ = 0.6249 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \pi \tan 32^\circ = 1.9626$$

$$e^{1.9626} = 7.117$$

$$\tan(45 + 16) = \tan 61^\circ = 1.8040 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan^2 = 3.255$$

$$N_q = 7.117 \times 3.255 = 23.18$$

$$N_c = (23.18 - 1) \cot 32^\circ = 22.18 \times 1.6003 = 35.49$$

$$N_\gamma = 22.18 \times \tan(44.8^\circ) = 22.18 \times 0.9940 = 22.05$$

**Step 2 — Surcharge (since $c = 0$, cohesion term vanishes):**

$$q = 18.5 \times 1.5 = 27.75 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Ultimate bearing capacity (circular):**

$$q_u = 1.3 \times 0 \times 35.49 + 27.75 \times 23.18 + 0.3 \times 18.5 \times 3.0 \times 22.05$$

$$= 0 + 643.25 + 367.13 = 1010.38 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Safe bearing capacity and total load:**

$$q_s = \frac{1010.38}{3} = 336.79 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\text{Area} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 3^2 = 7.069 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{allow} = 336.79 \times 7.069 = 2380.7 \text{ kN}$$

> **Answer (a):** $q_u = 1010.38 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $q_s = 336.79 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $P_{allow} \approx 2381 \text{ kN}$.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 5 — For square footing ($B = 3.0\,\text{m}$):**

$$q_u = 1.3 \times 0 \times 35.49 + 27.75 \times 23.18 + 0.4 \times 18.5 \times 3.0 \times 22.05$$

$$= 0 + 643.25 + 489.51 = 1132.76 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$q_s = \frac{1132.76}{3} = 377.59 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\text{Area} = 3.0 \times 3.0 = 9.0 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{allow,sq} = 377.59 \times 9.0 = 3398.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Concrete volume comparison (per unit depth):**

$$V_{circ} = 7.069 \times 1 = 7.069 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per m depth}$$

$$V_{sq} = 9.0 \times 1 = 9.0 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per m depth}$$

**Load per unit concrete volume:**

$$\frac{P_{circ}}{V_{circ}} = \frac{2380.7}{7.069} = 336.8 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

$$\frac{P_{sq}}{V_{sq}} = \frac{3398.3}{9.0} = 377.6 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

$$\text{Percentage gain} = \frac{377.6 - 336.8}{336.8} \times 100 = 12.11\%$$

> **Answer (b):** The **square footing** supports **~12% more load per unit concrete volume** than the circular footing for $c = 0$ soils. However, in cohesive soils ($c > 0$) the difference shrinks (as seen in Question A), and the **circular footing becomes preferred** when uniform punching-shear resistance and reduced perimeter bending are critical (e.g., for water tanks or chimneys).

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize Items (Last-Minute KTU Revision):**
>
> ✅ **Three-Term Structure:** Every Terzaghi bearing capacity equation has the form
> $$q_u = s_c \cdot c \cdot N_c + s_q \cdot q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \, s_\gamma \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$$
>
> ✅ **Shape Factor Table (Mastery Required):**
> - Strip: $(1.0, 1.0, 1.0)$
> - Square: $(1.3, 1.0, 0.8)$
> - Circular: $(1.3, 1.0, 0.6)$
>
> ✅ **Three Bearing Capacity Factors:**
> - $N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45 + \phi/2)$ — depends on $\phi$ only
> - $N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi$
> - $N_\gamma = (N_q - 1) \tan(1.4\phi)$ — **empirical**, $\phi$ in degrees
>
> ✅ **Conversion Triplet (Avoid 2-mark deduction):**
> - $q_{nu} = q_u - q$
> - $q_s = q_u / FS$
> - $q_{ns} = q_{nu} / FS$
>
> ✅ **Default Factor of Safety:** $FS = 3$ (unless stated otherwise).
>
> ✅ **Surcharge:** $q = \gamma \cdot D_f$ (always in kN/m²).
>
> ✅ **Comparison Memory Hook:** *Square > Strip > Circular* in terms of bearing capacity (per unit width) for the same $B$, because of corner resistance and shape geometry.
>
> ✅ **Numerical Habit:** Always write **units** in every step (kN/m² or kPa) — KTU examiners award full credit only when units accompany the final answer.
>
> ✅ **Engineering Insight:** Circular footings are preferred for **tanks/silos** (uniform stress, no bending); square footings for **columns** (symmetric reinforcement, easy formwork).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Bearing Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Bearing Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Bearing Capacity** of soil is defined as the maximum load per unit area which the soil can support without undergoing shear failure or excessive settlement detrimental to the structure. Mathematically, it is the ultimate bearing stress ($q_u$) that the foundation soil can carry at the point of incipient failure.

However, the *actual* load a foundation can safely transfer to the ground is rarely equal to the theoretical $q_u$ because real field conditions deviate from the idealized assumptions of Terzaghi's classical theory. The **Factors Affecting Bearing Capacity** are the correction multipliers and the underlying physical variables that modify the idealized Terzaghi equation to reflect ground truth.

$$q_{u(\text{actual})} = c \cdot N_c \cdot s_c \cdot d_c \cdot i_c + q \cdot N_q \cdot s_q \cdot d_q \cdot i_q + 0.5 \cdot B \cdot \gamma \cdot N_\gamma \cdot s_\gamma \cdot d_\gamma \cdot i_\gamma$$

Where $s, d, i$ are the **shape, depth, and inclination factors** respectively — the mathematical embodiment of the *factors affecting bearing capacity*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students are expected to identify, define, and quantitatively apply *all* correction factors in the modified Meyerhof / Hansen / IS 6403 bearing capacity equation. This is a **high-weightage topic** in ESE Part B (14-mark derivations and numericals).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **standing on dry sand at a beach**:

- If you stand on **one foot** (small contact area), your foot sinks deeper than if you spread your weight across a **wooden plank** (larger contact area). → **Width of foundation (B) is a factor.**
- If you wear **pointed heels**, you sink immediately — but **flat slippers** distribute load better. → **Shape of foundation (s) is a factor.**
- If the **water table rises** to the surface, the sand feels "softer" because buoyancy reduces the effective weight of soil grains. → **Water table position is a factor.**
- If you **lean forward** while standing, you tend to topple into the sand. → **Inclination of load (i) is a factor.**
- If you are **standing on a raised platform** versus directly on the beach, the failure surface is different. → **Depth of foundation (D_f) is a factor.**

Each of these real-life observations maps directly to a **bearing capacity correction factor** used in KTU design problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Terzaghi's equation assumes a *long, shallow, surface, centric, vertical* strip footing on *homogeneous, weightless* soil. Every deviation from this ideal scenario introduces a **multiplicative factor** (or an additive correction) into the governing equation. These deviations are the *factors affecting bearing capacity*.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Unit weight of water | $\gamma_w$ | **9.81** (often taken as 10) | kN/m³ |
| Submerged unit weight | $\gamma'$ | $\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | kN/m³ |
| Surcharge at foundation level | $q$ | $\gamma \cdot D_f$ | kN/m² |
| Cohesion (undrained) | $c$ or $c_u$ | 10 – 200 (typical range) | kN/m² |

---

## 1.4 Primary List of Factors Affecting Bearing Capacity

> [!NOTE]
> **Categorization (as per IS 6403:1981 and KTU Module 2 syllabus):**

1. **Soil Strength Parameters** — Cohesion ($c$), angle of internal friction ($\phi$)
2. **Unit Weight of Soil** ($\gamma$) and **Groundwater Table (GWT)** position
3. **Width of Foundation** ($B$)
4. **Depth of Foundation** ($D_f$) and embedment conditions
5. **Shape of Foundation** — Strip, Square, Circular, Rectangular
6. **Eccentricity of Loading** ($e$)
7. **Inclination of Load** ($\alpha$)
8. **Surcharge** and **Inclination of Ground Surface** ($\beta$)
9. **Soil Stratification** and heterogeneity
10. **Compressibility of soil / Strain conditions**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Logic Stack

The classical **Terzaghi Bearing Capacity Equation** (1943) for a strip footing:

$$q_u = c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot B \cdot \gamma \cdot N_\gamma$$

To extend this to **real foundations**, each assumption is relaxed step-by-step, multiplying the equation by **correction factors** (or adding terms):

### Step 1: Introduce Shape of Footing
- Real footings are square, rectangular, or circular — not infinite strips.
- **Shape factors** $s_c, s_q, s_\gamma$ are introduced (De Beer, Hansen, IS Code).

### Step 2: Account for Foundation Depth
- Terzaghi's original equation assumed the footing is at *ground surface* with the surcharge $q$ replacing depth effects only.
- **Depth factors** $d_c, d_q, d_\gamma$ correct for additional shear resistance mobilized along the sides of the foundation at depth.

### Step 3: Account for Load Inclination
- Real columns rarely deliver *perfectly vertical* loads (wind, seismic, moment transfer).
- **Inclination factors** $i_c, i_q, i_\gamma$ reduce bearing capacity when load tilts.

### Step 4: Account for Ground Inclination
- Foundations on slopes behave very differently from those on level ground.
- **Ground inclination factors** $g_c, g_q, g_\gamma$ (Hansen) account for $\beta$.

### Step 5: Account for Base Inclination
- Footings sometimes have a battered base.
- **Base inclination factors** $b_c, b_q, b_\gamma$ are applied.

### Step 6: Account for Eccentric Loading
- When the resultant load does not pass through the centroid, an *effective width* $B' = B - 2e$ replaces $B$ (Meyerhof's effective area concept).

### Step 7: Account for Water Table
- Submergence reduces effective unit weight (buoyancy) and reduces surcharge (pore-water pressure). Two cases exist: **water table at base** or **water table above base**.

### Step 8: Account for Soil Stratification
- Strong layer over weak layer, or vice versa, requires a separate analysis (e.g., punching shear check, or layered failure surface using $\phi=0$ analysis).

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Factor — Engineering Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we need shape factors?**
> In a strip footing, the failure zone is essentially 2-D (plane strain). In a square or circular footing, the failure mechanism is 3-D and *interference* between adjacent failure wedges reduces the effective mobilizing stress. Shape factors mathematically capture this 3-D interference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we need depth factors?**
> When a foundation is embedded, the soil *above* the base level also resists failure along vertical planes rising from the foundation edge. Terzaghi's equation neglects this extra shear resistance. Depth factors quantify it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we need inclination factors?**
> An inclined load has a horizontal component that acts as a driving shear force on the failure wedge. The net mobilizable shear strength is reduced, and so is the ultimate bearing capacity.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **IS 6403:1981 / Modified Hansen Formulas** (most commonly tested in KTU):

| Factor | Cohesion Term ($c$) | Surcharge Term ($q$) | Self-Weight Term ($\gamma$) | Applies When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Shape** $s$ | $s_c = 1 + 0.2 \cdot (B/L)$ | $s_q = 1.0$ | $s_\gamma = 1 - 0.2 \cdot (B/L)$ | Any $B/L$ |
| **Depth** $d$ | $d_c = 1 + 0.2 \cdot (D_f/B)$ | $d_q = 1 + 0.2 \cdot (D_f/B)$ | $d_\gamma = 1.0$ | $D_f \le B$ |
| **Depth** (Hansen, deep) | $d_c = 1 + 0.4 \cdot k$ | $d_q = 1 + 2 \tan\phi (1-\sin\phi)^2 \cdot k$ | $d_\gamma = 1.0$ | $D_f > B$ |
| **Inclination** $i$ | $i_c = (1 - \alpha/90)^2$ | $i_q = (1 - \alpha/90)^2$ | $i_\gamma = (1 - \alpha/\phi)^2$ | $\alpha < \phi$ |
| **Bearing capacity factors** | $N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | $N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2(45+\phi/2)$ | $N_\gamma \approx 2(N_q+1)\tan\phi$ | Always |

Where in the inclination formulas:
- $\alpha$ = inclination of load with vertical (degrees)
- $k = D_f/B$ for $D_f \le B$; $k = \tan^{-1}(D_f/B)$ for $D_f > B$
- $B/L$ = ratio of width to length of footing (for strip $B/L = 0$, for square $B/L = 1$)

### 2.3.1 Water Table Correction Table

| Water Table Position | Correction for Surcharge ($q$) | Correction for Unit Weight ($\gamma$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Above base** (within $D_f$) | $q' = \gamma' \cdot D_{w1} + \gamma_{sat} \cdot (D_f - D_{w1})$ | Use $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ |
| **At base** ($z_w = 0$) | No correction ($q = \gamma \cdot D_f$) | Replace $0.5 \gamma B$ by $0.5 \gamma' B$ |
| **Between base and $B$ below base** | $q$ unchanged | $0.5 \cdot B \cdot \gamma \cdot \left[\gamma' / \gamma + (z_w / B)(1 - \gamma' / \gamma)\right]$ |
| **Below $B$ from base** | $q$ unchanged | No correction |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These factors are not academic exercises. They directly govern:

- **Design of isolated footings** for columns (using shape + depth factors)
- **Design of machine foundations** subjected to dynamic/horizontal loads (using inclination factors)
- **Bridge abutment design** on sloped embankments (using ground inclination factors)
- **Retrofitting / underpinning** decisions in waterlogged sites (water table corrections)
- **Skyscraper raft design** where eccentricity from column-group centroid matters (effective width concept)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU's PBCET504 course outcomes (CO2: *Evaluate bearing capacity of soils under varying field conditions*), this topic directly tests whether a student can transition from idealized textbook formulas to **field-realistic design**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Generalized Bearing Capacity Equation (Meyerhof–Hansen Form)

Starting from Terzaghi's classical strip-footing equation:

$$q_{u,\text{strip}} = c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot B \cdot \gamma \cdot N_\gamma$$

### Step 1: Apply Shape Correction
A rectangular footing of dimensions $B \times L$ develops a failure mechanism that is *3-D* rather than plane strain. Empirically (De Beer, 1970; IS 6403):

$$s_c = 1 + \frac{B}{L} \cdot \left(\frac{N_q}{N_c}\right) \quad \text{(general)}$$

For IS 6403 (simplified, conservatively $N_q/N_c \approx 0.2$ for the cohesion term):

$$s_c = 1 + 0.2 \cdot \frac{B}{L}$$

$$s_q = 1.0 \quad \text{(no correction for surcharge term, conservatively)}$$

$$s_\gamma = 1 - 0.2 \cdot \frac{B}{L}$$

> **Logic:** The ratio $B/L$ measures the "3-D-ness" of the footing. As $B/L \to 0$ (strip footing), $s_c \to 1$, $s_\gamma \to 1$ — the original Terzaghi form is recovered. As $B/L \to 1$ (square footing), $s_c = 1.2$, $s_\gamma = 0.8$ — the 3-D reduction in self-weight term is captured.

### Step 2: Apply Depth Correction
Embedment of depth $D_f$ provides additional shearing resistance along vertical surfaces rising from the footing edges. Hansen (1970) and IS 6403 propose:

For **shallow foundations** ($D_f \le B$):

$$d_c = 1 + 0.2 \cdot \frac{D_f}{B}$$

$$d_q = 1 + 0.2 \cdot \frac{D_f}{B}$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

For **deep foundations** ($D_f > B$):

$$d_q = 1 + 2 \tan\phi \cdot (1 - \sin\phi)^2 \cdot \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{D_f}{B}\right)$$

$$d_c = d_q - \frac{1 - d_q}{N_c \tan\phi}$$

> **Logic:** As $D_f \to 0$ (surface footing), all depth factors approach unity and we recover the surface-foundation formula. As $D_f$ grows, the surcharge term grows and the cohesion term gets a *bonus* from the soil shearing resistance above the base.

### Step 3: Apply Inclination Correction
For a load inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the vertical, Hansen recommends:

$$i_c = i_q = \left(1 - \frac{\alpha}{90°}\right)^2$$

$$i_\gamma = \left(1 - \frac{\alpha}{\phi}\right)^2$$

> **Logic:** When $\alpha = 0$ (vertical load), all inclination factors = 1 (no reduction). As $\alpha \to \phi$, $i_\gamma \to 0$ — meaning that when the load inclination equals the friction angle, the self-weight term vanishes (a physical impossibility would be reached).

### Step 4: Combine All Factors
The full generalized Meyerhof–Hansen equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= c \cdot N_c \cdot s_c \cdot d_c \cdot i_c \\
&\quad + q \cdot N_q \cdot s_q \cdot d_q \cdot i_q \\
&\quad + 0.5 \cdot B \cdot \gamma \cdot N_\gamma \cdot s_\gamma \cdot d_\gamma \cdot i_\gamma
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

### Problem Statement
A rectangular footing of size **3 m × 4 m** is founded at a depth of **2 m** below ground surface in a soil with cohesion $c = 30$ kN/m², angle of internal friction $\phi = 25°$, and unit weight $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. The water table is at the base of the footing. Compute the ultimate bearing capacity using IS 6403. Take $\alpha = 10°$ (load inclination).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Bearing Capacity Factors for $\phi = 25°$**

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan 25°} \cdot \tan^2(45° + 12.5°)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan 25° &= 0.4663 \\
\pi \tan 25° &= 1.4649 \\
e^{1.4649} &= 4.327 \\
45° + 12.5° &= 57.5° \\
\tan 57.5° &= 1.5697 \\
\tan^2 57.5° &= 2.464
\end{aligned}
$$

$$N_q = 4.327 \times 2.464 = 10.66$$

$$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi = (10.66 - 1) \times \cot 25° = 9.66 \times 2.1445 = 20.72$$

$$N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1) \tan\phi = 2 \times 11.66 \times 0.4663 = 10.88$$

**Step 2: Shape Factors** ($B = 3$ m, $L = 4$ m, $B/L = 0.75$)

$$s_c = 1 + 0.2 \times 0.75 = 1.15$$

$$s_q = 1.0$$

$$s_\gamma = 1 - 0.2 \times 0.75 = 0.85$$

**Step 3: Depth Factors** ($D_f = 2$ m, $D_f/B = 0.667 < 1$ → shallow case)

$$d_c = 1 + 0.2 \times 0.667 = 1.133$$

$$d_q = 1 + 0.2 \times 0.667 = 1.133$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

**Step 4: Inclination Factors** ($\alpha = 10°$, $\phi = 25°$)

$$i_c = i_q = \left(1 - \frac{10}{90}\right)^2 = (0.8889)^2 = 0.7901$$

$$i_\gamma = \left(1 - \frac{10}{25}\right)^2 = (0.6)^2 = 0.36$$

**Step 5: Water Table Correction**
- Water table at base → surcharge unchanged: $q = 18 \times 2 = 36$ kN/m²
- Self-weight term uses $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$. We approximate $\gamma' = (18 - 9.81) = 8.19$ kN/m³ (taking $\gamma_{sat} \approx 18$).
- Use $\gamma' = 8.19$ kN/m³ in the $\gamma$-term.

**Step 6: Substitute into the Generalized Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 30 \times 20.72 \times 1.15 \times 1.133 \times 0.7901 \\
&\quad + 36 \times 10.66 \times 1.0 \times 1.133 \times 0.7901 \\
&\quad + 0.5 \times 3 \times 8.19 \times 10.88 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 0.36
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cohesion term:**

$$
30 \times 20.72 = 621.6
$$

$$
621.6 \times 1.15 = 714.84
$$

$$
714.84 \times 1.133 = 809.91
$$

$$
809.91 \times 0.7901 = 639.86 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Surcharge term:**

$$
36 \times 10.66 = 383.76
$$

$$
383.76 \times 1.133 = 434.80
$$

$$
434.80 \times 0.7901 = 343.55 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Self-weight term:**

$$
0.5 \times 3 = 1.5
$$

$$
1.5 \times 8.19 = 12.285
$$

$$
12.285 \times 10.88 = 133.66
$$

$$
133.66 \times 0.85 = 113.61
$$

$$
113.61 \times 0.36 = 40.90 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Total ultimate bearing capacity:**

$$
q_u = 639.86 + 343.55 + 40.90 = \boxed{1024.31 \text{ kN/m}^2}
$$

**Net ultimate bearing capacity:**

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma \cdot D_f = 1024.31 - 36 = 988.31 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Safe bearing capacity** (Factor of Safety = 3):

$$q_{s} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F.S.} = \frac{988.31}{3} = 329.44 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Factor to Apply When

| Foundation Type | Shape Factors? | Depth Factors? | Inclination Factors? | Water Table Correction? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Surface Strip (Terzaghi baseline)** | No | No | No | No |
| **Shallow Square** ($D_f \le B$) | Yes ($s_c, s_\gamma$) | Yes | Optional | Yes if applicable |
| **Deep Rectangular** ($D_f > B$) | Yes | Yes (Hansen) | Yes if load inclined | Yes |
| **Footing on Slope** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes + $g$-factors |
| **Footing with Eccentric Load** | Use $B' = B - 2e$ | Yes | Yes (modified $\alpha$) | Yes |
| **Pile / Well Foundation** | Use Hansen deep | Use $\tan^{-1}(D_f/B)$ | Yes | Yes (full submergence) |
