# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is an isolated footing? Under what soil conditions is it preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

An isolated footing is a shallow foundation that supports a single column and transfers its load to the soil through a base area larger than the column cross-section. It is preferred when:

(i) The soil has **good safe bearing capacity** (typically > 150 kN/m²) at shallow depth,
(ii) Column loads are **moderate** (typically < 1500 kN),
(iii) Footings of adjacent columns can be spread **without overlapping**, and
(iv) The soil is **uniform and homogeneous** to avoid differential settlement.

It is the **most economical** type of shallow foundation for framed buildings.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for conditions)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Why is the critical section for two-way shear located at d/2 from the face of the column in an isolated footing?**

**Model Answer:**

The critical section for two-way (punching) shear is located at **d/2 from the column face** because:

(i) The concentrated column load disperses through the depth of the footing at an angle of approximately 45° as per **IS 456 Cl. 31.6.1**,
(ii) The punching cone has its base at the top of the footing and its apex at the bottom, with the inclined surface of the cone intersecting the bottom of the footing at a horizontal distance of **d/2** from the column face,
(iii) This location provides the **most accurate** estimate of the actual punching shear stress and avoids uneconomical over-design that would result from placing the critical section at the column face itself,
(iv) The shear stress on the vertical face of this truncated cone governs the **punching failure** of the footing.

*(3 marks: 1 each for the four points or a coherent 3-point answer)*

---

## 5.2 Part B – ESE Module Question (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the different types of isolated footings and discuss their relative merits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design an isolated footing for a column 400 mm × 400 mm carrying a service load of 1200 kN. The safe bearing capacity of soil is 200 kN/m². Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Adopt Limit State Method as per IS 456:2000.

#### Model Solution to (a) – Types of Isolated Footings

**(1) Plain Concrete Footing (1.5 marks)**
- Made of mass concrete without any reinforcement.
- Used for very light loads and small residential columns.
- Has stepped or sloped profile to economise on concrete.

**(2) Reinforced Concrete Slab Footing (1.5 marks)**
- A reinforced concrete slab of uniform thickness under the column.
- Most commonly used for moderate column loads.
- Easy to construct, simple reinforcement, but uses more concrete near the edges where it is structurally not required.

**(3) Sloped Footing (1.5 marks)**
- Sloped on all four sides to economise on concrete.
- Requires inclined formwork, which is labour-intensive.
- Useful where labour cost is low and material cost is high.

**(4) Stepped Footing (1.5 marks)**
- Made up of a slab plus 2 or 3 steps on top of it.
- Step thickness is usually the same as slab thickness.
- Easier to cast than sloped footing, no inclined formwork.

**(5) Thick Slab with Pedestal (1.0 mark)**
- A short column extension (pedestal) reduces the cantilever projection.
- Used for heavy loads where the required footing size would otherwise be too large.
- The pedestal can be designed as a compression member.

#### Model Solution to (b) – Design of Isolated Footing

**Given Data:**
- Column size: 400 mm × 400 mm
- Service load: P = 1200 kN
- SBC of soil: q<sub>a</sub> = 200 kN/m²
- M20 concrete, Fe415 steel
- Depth of foundation assumed below GL: 1.5 m
- Unit weight of soil = 18 kN/m³

**Step 1: Factored Load**

$$
P_u = 1.5 \times 1200 = 1800 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2: Net Safe Bearing Capacity**

$$
q_{ns} = q_a - \gamma \cdot D_f = 200 - 18 \times 1.5 = 200 - 27 = 173 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Required Footing Area (using service load)**

$$
A_{req} = \frac{P}{q_{ns}} = \frac{1200}{173} = 6.94 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Choose Footing Size**

For a square footing, L = B = √6.94 = **2.635 m** → Provide **2.7 m × 2.7 m**.

**Step 5: Factored Net Upward Pressure**

$$
p_u = \frac{P_u}{L \times B} = \frac{1800}{2.7 \times 2.7} = 246.9 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 6: Assume Effective Depth**

For one-way shear, tentative d ≈ 450 mm. So overall D = 500 mm.

**Step 7: Check Two-Way (Punching) Shear**

Critical perimeter dimensions:

$$
a_1 = b_1 = 0.4 + 0.45 = 0.85 \text{ m}
$$

$$
A_{crit} = 0.85 \times 0.85 = 0.7225 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V_{u,punch} = p_u \times (A_{foot} - A_{crit}) = 246.9 \times (7.29 - 0.7225) = 246.9 \times 6.5675 = 1621.5 \text{ kN}
$$

Perimeter: b<sub>o</sub> = 4 × 0.85 = 3.4 m = 3400 mm.

$$
\tau_v = \frac{1621.5 \times 10^3}{3400 \times 450} = 1.06 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

Permissible τ<sub>c,punch</sub> = k<sub>s</sub> × τ<sub>c</sub> = 1.0 × 0.28 (initial estimate for low p<sub>t</sub>) = 0.28 N/mm² < 1.06 N/mm².

**→ Increase depth.** Re-iterate with d = 700 mm (D = 750 mm):

$$
a_1 = b_1 = 0.4 + 0.7 = 1.1 \text{ m}
$$

$$
A_{crit} = 1.1 \times 1.1 = 1.21 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V_{u,punch} = 246.9 \times (7.29 - 1.21) = 246.9 \times 6.08 = 1501.2 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
b_o = 4 \times 1.1 = 4.4 \text{ m} = 4400 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\tau_v = \frac{1501.2 \times 10^3}{4400 \times 700} = 0.487 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

For p<sub>t</sub> = 0.4%, τ<sub>c</sub> ≈ 0.39 N/mm² (from IS 456 Table 19). Hence permissible = 0.39 N/mm² < 0.487 N/mm².

**→ Increase further.** Try d = 800 mm (D = 850 mm):

$$
a_1 = b_1 = 0.4 + 0.8 = 1.2 \text{ m}
$$

$$
A_{crit} = 1.2 \times 1.2 = 1.44 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V_{u,punch} = 246.9 \times (7.29 - 1.44) = 246.9 \times 5.85 = 1444.4 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
b_o = 4 \times 1.2 = 4.8 \text{ m} = 4800 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\tau_v = \frac{1444.4 \times 10^3}{4800 \times 800} = 0.376 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

τ<sub>c</sub> = 0.39 N/mm² (slightly higher) → **OK, d = 800 mm is safe.**

**Step 8: Check One-Way Shear at d = 800 mm from Column Face**

$$
c_x = \frac{L - a}{2} - d = \frac{2.7 - 0.4}{2} - 0.8 = 1.15 - 0.8 = 0.35 \text{ m}
$$

$$
V_u = p_u \times B \times c_x = 246.9 \times 2.7 \times 0.35 = 233.3 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{B \times d} = \frac{233.3 \times 10^3}{2700 \times 800} = 0.108 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

This is well below τ<sub>c</sub> = 0.39 N/mm² → **Safe.**

**Step 9: Design Bending Moment at Column Face**

$$
M_u = p_u \times B \times \left(\frac{L - a}{2}\right)^2 \times \frac{1}{2} = 246.9 \times 2.7 \times (1.15)^2 \times 0.5
$$

$$
M_u = 246.9 \times 2.7 \times 1.3225 \times 0.5 = 440.6 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 10: Compute Flexural Steel**

M<sub>u,lim</sub> = 0.138 × 20 × 2700 × 800² = **9.55 × 10⁹ N·mm = 9550 kN·m** >> 440.6 kN·m → Section is **under-reinforced**.

$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 440.6 \times 10^6}{20 \times 2700 \times 800^2}} \right] \times 2700 \times 800
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.0241 \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{2.027 \times 10^9}{3.456 \times 10^{10}}} \right] \times 2.16 \times 10^6
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.0241 \times 0.0296 \times 2.16 \times 10^6 = 1540 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 11: Min Steel Check**

$$
A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \times 2700 \times 850 = 2754 \text{ mm}^2
$$

A<sub>st,min</sub> > A<sub>st,req</sub> → **Governing value = 2754 mm².**

**Step 12: Bar Selection**

Use 16 mm ⌀ bars → A = 201.06 mm².

Number of bars = 2754 / 201.06 ≈ 13.7 → provide **14 bars of 16 mm ⌀** at 200 mm c/c.

Since 200 mm c/c ≤ 300 mm (crack control), it is **acceptable**.

**Step 13: Development Length Check**

For 16 mm Fe415 bar in M20:

$$
L_d = \frac{16 \times 360}{4 \times 1.4} = \frac{5760}{5.6} = 1028.6 \text{ mm} \approx 1030 \text{ mm}
$$

Available length = (L/2) - cover = 1350 - 50 = 1300 mm > 1030 mm → **Safe.**

**Step 14: Bearing Stress Check at Column Base**

$$
\sigma_{br} = \frac{P_u}{A_{col}} = \frac{1800 \times 10^3}{400 \times 400} = 11.25 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

Permissible = 0.45 × 20 = 9 N/mm² < 11.25 N/mm² → **Bearing is exceeded!**

Provide dowels: Excess load = 1800 × 10³ - 0.45 × 20 × 160000 = 1,800,000 - 1,440,000 = 360,000 N.

$$
A_{st,dowel} = \frac{360000}{0.87 \times 415} = 996 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Minimum dowel area = 0.005 × 400 × 400 = 800 mm².

Provide **4 numbers of 20 mm ⌀ dowels** (A = 4 × 314.16 = 1256 mm² > 996 mm²).

#### Final Design Summary

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Footing size (L × B × D) | 2.7 m × 2.7 m × 0.85 m |
| Effective depth d | 800 mm |
| Main reinforcement | 16 mm ⌀ at 200 mm c/c (both directions) at bottom |
| Cover | 50 mm at bottom |
| Dowels into column | 4 nos 20 mm ⌀, 600 mm projection |
| PCC below | 100 mm thick M10 |

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** List and explain the various checks to be performed in the Limit State Design of an isolated footing. Why is the depth of footing often governed by two-way shear?

**(b) [7 Marks]** An RCC column 500 mm × 500 mm carries a service axial load of 1500 kN. The soil has a safe bearing capacity of 180 kN/m² and the depth of foundation is 1.8 m. Design the isolated footing using M25 concrete and Fe500 steel. Use LSM as per IS 456.

#### Model Solution Outline to (a) – Checks in Isolated Footing Design (7 marks)

1. **Bearing capacity check** – Service load pressure ≤ Net SBC (1 mark)
2. **Two-way (punching) shear check** at d/2 from column face (1.5 marks)
3. **One-way (beam) shear check** at d from column face (1.5 marks)
4. **Flexural (bending) design** of the cantilever – A<sub>st</sub> computation (1.5 marks)
5. **Development length check** for bars (0.5 mark)
6. **Bearing stress check at column-footing interface** + dowels if needed (1 mark)

**Why two-way shear often governs:** In isolated footings, the column load is concentrated and the cantilever projection is small. The punching cone is therefore steep and wide, generating high punching shear. The b<sub>o</sub> · d resisting perimeter is relatively small compared to the high concentrated force, so the punching shear is the first check to fail and dictates the effective depth. (Built into explanation – 1 mark)

#### Model Solution Outline to (b) – Numerical Design (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Factored load P<sub>u</sub> = 1.5 × 1500 = 2250 kN.

**Step 2:** Net SBC q<sub>ns</sub> = 180 - 18 × 1.8 = 180 - 32.4 = **147.6 kN/m²**.

**Step 3:** A<sub>req</sub> = 1500 / 147.6 = **10.16 m²**. Provide footing **3.2 m × 3.2 m**.

**Step 4:** p<sub>u</sub> = 2250 / (3.2 × 3.2) = **219.7 kN/m²**.

**Step 5:** Assume d = 700 mm. Check two-way shear: τ<sub>v</sub> = 219.7 × (10.24 - 1.44) × 10³ / (4800 × 700) ≈ **0.45 N/mm²**. With p<sub>t</sub> ≈ 0.3% and M25, τ<sub>c</sub> ≈ 0.42 → Borderline. Increase to d = 800 mm: τ<sub>v</sub> ≈ 0.38 N/mm² → **OK**.

**Step 6:** One-way shear at d from column: V<sub>u</sub> = 219.7 × 3.2 × 0.45 = 316.4 kN; τ<sub>v</sub> = 316.4 × 10³ / (3200 × 800) = **0.124 N/mm²** → **Safe**.

**Step 7:** Bending moment M<sub>u</sub> = 219.7 × 3.2 × (1.35)² × 0.5 = **640.5 kN·m**.

**Step 8:** A<sub>st,req</sub> for M25/Fe500:

$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 25}{500} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 640.5 \times 10^6}{25 \times 3200 \times 800^2}} \right] \times 3200 \times 800
$$

$$
A_{st} \approx 1735 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 9:** A<sub>st,min</sub> = 0.0012 × 3200 × 850 = **3264 mm²** → **Governing**.

**Step 10:** Use 16 mm ⌀ (A = 201 mm²), number of bars = 3264 / 201 ≈ 16.2 → provide **17 bars of 16 mm ⌀** at 200 mm c/c (or 18 bars at 187 mm c/c).

**Step 11:** Bearing check at column base: σ<sub>br</sub> = 2250 × 10³ / 250000 = **9 N/mm²** < 0.45 × 25 = 11.25 N/mm² → **Safe, no dowels needed** (or nominal 0.5% min starter bars).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Using factored load for sizing** the footing area – should be service load. *(−1 mark)*
> 2. **Placing critical section at column face** instead of d/2 (two-way) or d (one-way). *(−2 marks)*
> 3. **Forgetting to check A<sub>st,min</sub>** – shrinkage steel often governs. *(−1 mark)*
> 4. **No reinforcement sketch** in the answer. *(−1 mark)*
> 5. **Confusing net SBC with gross SBC.** *(−1 mark)*
> 6. **Not mentioning cover 50 mm** (footing in contact with earth). *(−0.5 marks)*
> 7. **Skipping the bearing stress check at column base.** *(−1 mark)*
> 8. **Mixing WSM and LSM** equations in the same problem. *(−1 mark)*

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Isolated Footing Design (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- **Definition:** Spread footing supporting a single column, behaving as an **inverted cantilever** fixed at the column face.
- **Load path:** Column → Footing slab → Soil.
- **Sizing is on service load**; **structural design is on factored load (LSM)**.
- **Net SBC** = Gross SBC − γ<sub>soil</sub> × D<sub>f</sub>. Always use net SBC for area calculation.
- **Plan ratio** L/B should be ≤ 2.0; square footings are most efficient.
- **Critical sections for shear** (inward from column face):
  * One-way shear: at distance **d** from column face.
  * Two-way (punching) shear: at distance **d/2** from column face, around a closed perimeter b<sub>o</sub>.
- **Bending moment** is computed at the **column face** treating the projection as a cantilever.
- **Steel** is provided at the **bottom face** (tension face under upward load).
- **Cover in footing = 50 mm** (IS 456 Cl. 26.4.2.1, since in contact with earth).
- **Minimum steel** = 0.12% of gross cross-section (Fe415). This often **governs** in footings.
- **Bar spacing** must not exceed **300 mm** for crack control.
- **Bearing stress at column-footing interface** ≤ 0.45 f<sub>ck</sub>. If exceeded, provide **dowels** or a **pedestal**.
- **Dowel area** = excess load / (0.87 f<sub>y</sub>); minimum 0.5% of column cross-section.
- **Development length** for Fe415 in M20 ≈ **64.3⌀**; check available anchorage length on both sides of column face.
- **PCC bed** of 75–100 mm in M10 grade below footing is mandatory to level the surface.
- **Two-way shear usually governs the depth** of an isolated footing, especially under heavy columns.
- **Avoid placing bars** closer than 75 mm to each other or 50 mm to the edge – placement and concrete flow requirements.
- **Curtailment** of bars: typically 60% full length, 40% curtailed at L/3 from the edge (depending on designer preference).
- **Always include a labelled sketch** in the answer – sectional elevation with reinforcement + plan view with bar spacing.
- **Codes to cite:** IS 456:2000 (Cl. 34 for footings), IS 1080:1985, IS 1904:1986, SP-34 (detailing).
- **Loads:** γ<sub>f</sub> = 1.5 for both DL and LL (single load factor per IS 456 Cl. 36.4.1).
- **Material grade** assumed in KTU problems is typically M20 + Fe415 or M25 + Fe500.
- **Footing reinforcement is placed** in **two layers (X and Y)** crossing each other at right angles, both at the bottom face.
- **For heavily loaded columns** where p<sub>u</sub> > 4%, design becomes **doubly reinforced** – rarely in KTU syllabus but be aware.
- **Sloped/stepped footings** are not preferred in KTU exam questions but may be asked in short-answer/description type.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Footings - Rectangular and Trapezoidal Combined Footings - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Combined Footings - Rectangular and Trapezoidal Combined Footings - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Combined Footing** is a single reinforced concrete footing that supports **two or more columns** in a row, designed to distribute the combined structural load to the subsoil such that the **bearing pressure** on the soil remains within the **Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC)** and the resultant of the column loads coincides with the **centroid of the footing area** to ensure uniform pressure distribution (zero eccentricity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A combined footing is a **continuous spread footing** that becomes necessary when:
> 1. Two or more columns are spaced so closely that their isolated footings would overlap.
> 2. A column is located on or very near a **property line / boundary wall**, so that an isolated footing cannot be symmetrically placed.
> 3. The Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC) of soil is low, requiring large bearing areas that would overlap.

### 1.2 Types of Combined Footings

| Type | Geometry | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular Combined Footing** | Length × constant Width | When columns carry **equal/almost equal** loads, or when the centroid of loads naturally coincides with midpoint of footing |
| **Trapezoidal Combined Footing** | Length × varying Width (B₁ ≠ B₂) | When columns carry **unequal loads** and a longer, non-symmetric footing is required |
| **Strap (Cantilever) Footing** | Two isolated footings connected by a strap beam | When columns are far apart, near property line, but soil has good SBC |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Shared Plank:**
> Imagine two people (columns A and B) of different weights standing close to the edge of a pond. If you place a **flat rectangular plank** beneath them, it might tilt because the heavier person is at the edge. To prevent tilting (and ensure uniform pressure on the soft mud), you could:
> 1. **Slide the plank** so its center is right between them → This is the **Rectangular Combined Footing** (works when both have similar weights or you can adjust length freely).
> 2. **Use a trapezoidal plank** — wider under the heavier person and narrower under the lighter → This is the **Trapezoidal Combined Footing** (used when the plank length is FIXED by the boundary).
>
> The plank = footing, the people = columns, the mud = soil, and tilting = eccentricity (non-uniform pressure).

### 1.4 Governing Design Philosophy

**Key Principle:** For **uniform soil pressure (q)**, the **centroid of the footing area** must **geometrically coincide** with the **line of action of the resultant column load (R)**.

$$\text{Eccentricity } (e) = \bar{x}_{\text{footing}} - \bar{x}_{\text{loads}} = 0$$

Where:
- **q = Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC)** = **100 kN/m² to 300 kN/m²** (typical for KTU problems).
- **Resultant Load (R) = Σ Qᵢ** = sum of all column loads.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decision Flow

A KTU-standard combined footing problem follows this logical flow:

1. **Identify Geometry:** Locate property line, measure column spacing, list column loads (Q_A, Q_B).
2. **Compute Resultant:** $R = Q_A + Q_B$.
3. **Compute Centroid of Loads** (measured from a reference — usually the property line / Column A):
   $$\bar{x} = \frac{Q_A \cdot x_A + Q_B \cdot x_B}{Q_A + Q_B}$$
4. **Compute Required Area:** $A_{\text{req}} = R / q_s$.
5. **Choose Footing Type:**
   - If **L is unrestricted** → Rectangular (set $L = 2\bar{x}$)
   - If **L is restricted** (e.g., fixed overhang) → Trapezoidal (solve for B₁, B₂)
6. **Compute Dimensions:** B (rectangular) or B₁, B₂ (trapezoidal).
7. **Verify Uniform Pressure:** Check $e = 0$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains EVERY formula you need for a KTU 14-mark combined footing problem.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Load | $R = \Sigma Q_i$ | kN |
| 2 | Required Area | $A = R / q_s$ | m² |
| 3 | Centroid of Loads (from ref) | $\bar{x} = \Sigma (Q_i \cdot x_i) / \Sigma Q_i$ | m, measured from reference |
| 4 | Length of Rectangular Footing | $L = 2\bar{x}$ | m, for zero eccentricity |
| 5 | Width of Rectangular Footing | $B = A / L$ | m |
| 6 | Area of Trapezoid | $A = (B_1 + B_2) / 2 \cdot L$ | m² |
| 7 | Centroid of Trapezoid (from B₁ side) | $\bar{x}_T = L \cdot (B_1 + 2B_2) / [3(B_1 + B_2)]$ | m |
| 8 | Trapezoid: sum of parallel sides | $B_1 + B_2 = 2A / L$ | m |
| 9 | Trapezoid: parallel side B₂ | $B_2 = [A(6\bar{x} - L)] / L^2$ | m |
| 10 | Trapezoid: parallel side B₁ | $B_1 = 2A/L - B_2$ | m |
| 11 | Eccentricity check | $e = \bar{x}_{\text{footing}} - \bar{x}_{\text{loads}}$ | m; must be **0** |
| 12 | Net Soil Pressure | $q = R / A$ | kN/m² |
| 13 | Maximum BM (Column face) | $M = q \cdot \text{cantilever length}^2 / 2$ | kN·m |
| 14 | Critical Shear Section | At distance **d** (effective depth) from column face | m |

### 2.3 Why Each Step Matters (Engineering Insight)

- **Centroid coincidence** prevents rotation/tilt → ensures **serviceability** (no differential settlement).
- **Uniform pressure** → simplifies **structural design** (constant BM/SF gradient).
- **Trapezoidal shape** → more material-efficient than rectangular when **load eccentricity** is severe and **L is geometrically constrained**.
- The **wider parallel side (B₂)** is placed under the **more heavily loaded column** to shift the centroid toward that load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Combined footings are extensively used in:
> - **Row housing projects** in Kerala (KTU-relevant) where columns are at 3–5 m spacing along a boundary wall.
> - **Building on weak alluvial soil** in Kuttanad, Kole lands of Thrissur.
> - **Apartment complexes** with parking levels where columns cannot extend beyond the plot boundary.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The derivations and numerical computations below are EXHAUSTIVE — no skipped algebraic steps. Every transition is shown explicitly, mimicking KTU board examiner's worked-solution style.**

### 3.1 DERIVATION: Trapezoidal Centroid Formula

**Setup:** Consider a trapezoid with parallel sides $B_1$ (left) and $B_2$ (right), length $L$.

**Method:** Split trapezoid into a rectangle (width $B_1$, length $L$) and a triangle (base $(B_2 - B_1)$, height $L$).

| Component | Area | Centroid from B₁ side |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangle | $B_1 \cdot L$ | $L/2$ |
| Triangle | $(B_2 - B_1) \cdot L / 2$ | $2L/3$ |

Combined centroid (from B₁ side):

$$\bar{x}_T = \frac{(B_1 L)(L/2) + \left[\frac{(B_2 - B_1)L}{2}\right]\left[\frac{2L}{3}\right]}{B_1 L + \frac{(B_2 - B_1)L}{2}}$$

Cancel $L$ in numerator and denominator:

$$\bar{x}_T = \frac{(B_1)(L/2) + \left[\frac{(B_2 - B_1)}{2}\right]\left[\frac{2L}{3}\right]}{B_1 + \frac{(B_2 - B_1)}{2}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by 6 to clear fractions:

$$\bar{x}_T = \frac{3 B_1 L + 2(B_2 - B_1)L}{6 B_1 + 3(B_2 - B_1)} = \frac{L[3B_1 + 2B_2 - 2B_1]}{3[2B_1 + B_2 - B_1]}$$

$$\bar{x}_T = \frac{L(B_1 + 2B_2)}{3(B_1 + B_2)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 1 — Rectangular Combined Footing (Standard KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
Two columns **A** and **B** of a building carry loads of **600 kN** and **900 kN** respectively. The columns are spaced **5 m** apart. Column A is at the **property line**. The Safe Bearing Capacity of the soil is **150 kN/m²**. Design a **rectangular combined footing** and verify uniform pressure distribution.

**Given Data:**
- $Q_A = 600$ kN, $Q_B = 900$ kN
- Distance between columns, $d = 5$ m
- Column A is **at property line** → $x_A = 0$ m
- Therefore, $x_B = 5$ m (measured from A)
- $q_s = 150$ kN/m²

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Total Column Load (R)**

$$R = Q_A + Q_B = 600 + 900 = 1500 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Compute Required Footing Area**

$$A = \frac{R}{q_s} = \frac{1500}{150} = 10 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Locate Centroid of Column Loads (from A)**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{Q_A \cdot x_A + Q_B \cdot x_B}{Q_A + Q_B} = \frac{600 \times 0 + 900 \times 5}{600 + 900}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{0 + 4500}{1500} = \frac{4500}{1500} = 3.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Determine Length of Footing (L)**

For **zero eccentricity** (uniform pressure), the centroid of the footing must coincide with $\bar{x}$:

$$\bar{x}_{\text{footing}} = \frac{L}{2} = \bar{x} = 3.0 \text{ m}$$

$$L = 2 \times 3.0 = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Determine Width of Footing (B)**

$$B = \frac{A}{L} = \frac{10}{6.0} = 1.667 \text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{B = 1.7 \text{ m (adopt)}}$$

**Step 6: Verify Uniform Pressure Distribution**

Provided area with adopted width: $A_{\text{provided}} = L \times B = 6.0 \times 1.7 = 10.2$ m²

Soil pressure: $q = R / A = 1500 / 10.2 = 147.06$ kN/m² $\leq 150$ kN/m² ✓

Eccentricity check: $e = L/2 - \bar{x} = 3.0 - 3.0 = 0$ ✓ **Uniform Pressure Achieved**

**Step 7: Sketch of Footing Layout**

```
Property Line │  ← Col A (600 kN)         Col B (900 kN) →
              │  │                                 │
              │  │←────── 5.0 m ──────────────→│
              │  ├─────────────────────────────────┤
              │  │◄── 0.5 m ──►│         │◄── 0.5 m ──►│
              │  │  (overhang) │         │   (overhang)│
              │  │   L = 6.0 m                    │
              │  ├─────────────────────────────────┤
              │  │       B = 1.7 m                │
              │  └─────────────────────────────────┘
              │         600 kN    900 kN
              │          ⬇         ⬇
              │
```

(Overhang each side = $(L - d)/2 = (6.0 - 5.0)/2 = 0.5$ m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Dimensions of Rectangular Combined Footing:**
> **L = 6.0 m, B = 1.7 m**

---

### 3.3 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 2 — Trapezoidal Combined Footing (Same Data, Length Fixed)

**Problem Statement:**
Using the **same data** as Problem 3.2, but the **length of the footing is restricted to 5.5 m** (e.g., due to the depth of the property line setback), design a **trapezoidal combined footing**.

**Given Data (from Problem 3.2):**
- $Q_A = 600$ kN, $Q_B = 900$ kN
- $\bar{x} = 3.0$ m (from A)
- $A = 10$ m²
- **L = 5.5 m** (restricted — given)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Apply Trapezoid Area Equation**

$$A = \frac{(B_1 + B_2)}{2} \cdot L$$

$$10 = \frac{(B_1 + B_2)}{2} \times 5.5$$

$$B_1 + B_2 = \frac{10 \times 2}{5.5} = \frac{20}{5.5} = 3.636 \text{ m} \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

**Step 2: Apply Trapezoid Centroid Equation**

For zero eccentricity: $\bar{x}_T = \bar{x} = 3.0$ m

$$\bar{x}_T = \frac{L(B_1 + 2B_2)}{3(B_1 + B_2)} = 3.0$$

$$3.0 = \frac{5.5(B_1 + 2B_2)}{3 \times 3.636}$$

$$B_1 + 2B_2 = \frac{3.0 \times 3 \times 3.636}{5.5} = \frac{32.727}{5.5} = 5.950 \text{ m} \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

**Step 3: Solve Equations 1 and 2 Simultaneously**

From Eq 1: $B_1 = 3.636 - B_2$

Substituting into Eq 2:

$$(3.636 - B_2) + 2B_2 = 5.950$$

$$3.636 + B_2 = 5.950$$

$$B_2 = 5.950 - 3.636 = 2.314 \text{ m}$$

$$B_1 = 3.636 - 2.314 = 1.322 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Adopt Practical Dimensions**

$$\boxed{B_1 = 1.35 \text{ m}, \quad B_2 = 2.35 \text{ m}, \quad L = 5.5 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5: Verification**

Area check: $A = (1.35 + 2.35)/2 \times 5.5 = 3.70/2 \times 5.5 = 1.85 \times 5.5 = 10.175$ m² $\approx 10$ m² ✓

Centroid check: $\bar{x}_T = 5.5 \times (1.35 + 2 \times 2.35) / [3 \times (1.35 + 2.35)]$

$$\bar{x}_T = \frac{5.5 \times (1.35 + 4.70)}{3 \times 3.70} = \frac{5.5 \times 6.05}{11.10} = \frac{33.275}{11.10} = 2.997 \text{ m} \approx 3.0 \text{ m} \checkmark$$

**Step 6: Sketch**

```
Property Line
     │  ← Col A (600 kN)        Col B (900 kN) →
     │  │                                 │
     │  │◄──────── 5.0 m ──────────────►│
     │  ├──────────────┬─────────────────┤  ← B₂ = 2.35 m
     │  │              │\\               │
     │  │              │ \\ (Trapezoid)  │
     │  │              │  \\             │
     │  ├──────────────┴─────────────────┤  ← B₁ = 1.35 m
     │  │  L = 5.5 m                     │
     │  └────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Dimensions of Trapezoidal Combined Footing:**
> **L = 5.5 m, B₁ = 1.35 m (at Col A side), B₂ = 2.35 m (at Col B side)**
>
> Note: $B_2 > B_1$ because $Q_B > Q_A$ — the wider side is placed under the **heavier column**.

---

### 3.4 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 3 — Bending Moment & Shear Force Check (Comprehensive)

**Problem Statement:**
For the **rectangular combined footing of Problem 3.2** (L = 6.0 m, B = 1.7 m, q = 150 kN/m²), calculate the **maximum bending moment** and **maximum shear force**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Net Upward Pressure (Distributed Load on Footing)**

$$w = q_s \times B = 150 \times 1.7 = 255 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 2: Identify Cantilever Lengths**

Distance from column face to footing edge = $(L - d)/2 = (6.0 - 5.0)/2 = 0.5$ m

**Step 3: Maximum Bending Moment (at Column A face, from overhang)**

The column A overhang (cantilever) creates BM:

$$M_A = \frac{w \cdot a^2}{2} = \frac{255 \times 0.5^2}{2} = \frac{255 \times 0.25}{2} = 31.875 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4: Bending Moment at Column B face (considering overhang + Column A load)**

Distance from B to footing edge (right) = 0.5 m
Load transferred from A to B as concentrated force = 600 kN (acting at distance 5.0 m from A)

BM at column B face:

$$M_B = \frac{w \cdot (0.5)^2}{2} = 31.875 \text{ kN·m} \quad \text{(from right overhang alone)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For uniform pressure** (zero eccentricity), the design BM is the **maximum** of cantilever BMs at both column faces — typically they are **identical** because the load distribution is symmetric about the load resultant. Here both are **31.875 kN·m**.

**Step 5: Maximum Shear Force (at Column A face, from overhang side)**

$$V_A = w \cdot a = 255 \times 0.5 = 127.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Maximum Shear Force (at Column B face)**

$$V_B = w \cdot a = 255 \times 0.5 = 127.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | At Col A face | At Col B face |
|---|---|---|
| Bending Moment (kN·m) | 31.875 | 31.875 |
| Shear Force (kN) | 127.5 | 127.5 |

These values are used to design **footing thickness** (shear check) and **flexural reinforcement**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Between Rectangular and Trapezoidal

| Condition | Recommended Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_A \approx Q_B$ (load ratio < 1.5) | **Rectangular** | Centroid at midspan, $L = 2\bar{x}$ gives clean symmetric design |
| $Q_B \gg Q_A$ AND $L$ is unrestricted | **Rectangular** | $L = 2\bar{x}$ naturally absorbs the eccentricity |
| $Q_B \gg Q_A$ AND $L$ is **fixed/restricted** | **Trapezoidal** | Adjusting B₁ ≠ B₂ compensates for fixed L |
| Column A at property line, $Q_B > Q_A$ | **Trapezoidal** (often) | Long overhang to exterior causes rotation tendency; B₂ widened to resist |
| Soil SBC very low, large B required | **Rectangular** (preferred) | Easier to construct rectangular forms |
| Boundary/footprint is non-uniform | **Trapezoidal** | Matches the available footprint geometry |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Combined Footing Design Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Two Columns A and B with Loads Q_A, Q_B] --> B[Column A at property line?]
    B -- YES --> C[Compute Total Load R = Q_A + Q_B]
    B -- NO --> D[Both columns interior; usually isolated footings suffice]
    C --> E[Compute Centroid of Loads x_bar = Q_B × d / R]
    E --> F{Is Length L Free to Choose?}
    F -- YES --> G[Use RECTANGULAR FOOTING]
    G --> G1[Set L = 2 × x_bar]
    G1 --> G2[Compute B = A / L]
    G2 --> G3[Verify eccentricity e = 0]
    F -- NO --> H[Use TRAPEZOIDAL FOOTING]
    H --> H1[Given L is fixed - e.g., L = 2d]
    H1 --> H2[Equation 1: B_1 + B_2 = 2A / L]
    H2 --> H3[Equation 2: x_bar = L B_1 + 2B_2 over 3 B_1 + B_2]
    H3 --> H4[Solve simultaneously for B_1 and B_2]
    H4 --> H5[Verify x_bar of trapezoid matches column loads]
    G3 --> I[Compute BM and SF at column faces]
    H5 --> I
    I --> J[Design thickness and reinforcement]
    J --> K[END: Combined Footing Design Complete]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pressure Distribution on Combined Footing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FOOTING[Rectangular Combined Footing L=6.0m, B=1.7m]
        direction TB
        P1[Soil Reaction q = 150 kN/m² UNIFORM]:::uniform
    end
    subgraph COLS[Column Loading]
        direction TB
        CA[Column A: 600 kN at x=0m]:::colA
        CB[Column B: 900 kN at x=5m]:::colB
    end
    subgraph CENTROID[Centroid Coincidence]
        direction TB
        CG[Load Resultant at x_bar = 3.0m]:::cg
        FCG[Footing Centroid at L/2 = 3.0m]:::cg
    end
    CA --> FOOTING
    CB --> FOOTING
    FOOTING --> CENTROID
    classDef uniform fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef colA fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef colB fill:#e0e0ff,stroke:#1a237e,color:#000
    classDef cg fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Geometry — Cross-Section of Trapezoidal Footing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAP[Trapezoidal Combined Footing Geometry]
        direction LR
        T1[LEFT EDGE B1 = 1.35m at Column A]:::leftEdge
        T2[Property Line at x=0]:::propLine
        T3[RIGHT EDGE B2 = 2.35m at Column B]:::rightEdge
        T4[LENGTH L = 5.5m total]:::length
    end
    subgraph LOADS[Loads and Centroid]
        direction LR
        L1[Q_A = 600 kN]:::loadA
        L2[Q_B = 900 kN]:::loadB
        L3[Resultant x_bar = 3.0m from A]:::resultant
    end
    TRAP --- LOADS
    classDef leftEdge fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef rightEdge fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef propLine fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef length fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef loadA fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef loadB fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef resultant fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*When is a combined footing preferred over an isolated footing? List the types of combined footings.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A combined footing is preferred over an isolated footing in the following situations:
1. When two or more columns are spaced so closely that their isolated footings would overlap.
2. When a column is located at or near the property line/boundary wall, making it impossible to place a symmetric isolated footing.
3. When the Safe Bearing Capacity of soil is low, requiring large footing areas that would overlap.

**Types of combined footings:**
1. **Rectangular combined footing** – when columns carry equal loads and centroid coincides with footing midpoint.
2. **Trapezoidal combined footing** – when columns carry unequal loads and a longer, non-symmetric footing is required.
3. **Strap (cantilever) footing** – two isolated footings connected by a rigid strap beam.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*State the condition for uniform pressure distribution under a combined footing. Why is this condition important?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Condition for uniform pressure distribution:**
The **centroid of the footing area** must coincide with the **line of action of the resultant of column loads**, i.e., **eccentricity $e = 0$**.

Mathematically: $\bar{x}_{\text{footing}} = \bar{x}_{\text{loads}} = \Sigma(Q_i \cdot x_i) / \Sigma Q_i$

**Importance (any two points, 1 mark each):**
1. Prevents **differential settlement** and rotation/tilt of the footing.
2. Ensures the entire soil below the footing is stressed uniformly, optimizing the use of soil's safe bearing capacity.
3. Simplifies **structural design** – constant pressure leads to linear BM/SF variation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Two alternative question choices (A or B) are offered; the student answers ONE.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Rectangular Combined Footing Design**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

*(a)* A rectangular combined footing is to be provided for two columns **A** and **B** spaced at **4 m** center-to-center. The column A is on the property line. Column loads are $Q_A = 500$ kN and $Q_B = 800$ kN. The SBC of soil is **120 kN/m²**. Determine the **plan dimensions (L × B)** of the footing and verify uniform pressure distribution. **\[7 Marks\]**

*(b)* For the footing designed in part (a), calculate the **maximum bending moment** and **maximum shear force** at the column faces, given a uniform soil pressure. Also state the location of the **critical section for shear**. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Dimensions of Rectangular Footing \[7 marks\]**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute total load
$$R = Q_A + Q_B = 500 + 800 = 1300 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute required area
$$A = R / q_s = 1300 / 120 = 10.833 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Centroid of loads from A
$$\bar{x} = \frac{500 \times 0 + 800 \times 4}{1300} = \frac{3200}{1300} = 2.462 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Length of footing (for $e = 0$)
$$L = 2 \times \bar{x} = 2 \times 2.462 = 4.923 \text{ m} \approx 4.95 \text{ m (adopt)}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Width of footing
$$B = A / L = 10.833 / 4.923 = 2.20 \text{ m (adopt)}$$

**Step 6 (1.5 marks):** Verification
- Eccentricity: $e = L/2 - \bar{x} = 2.462 - 2.462 = 0$ ✓
- Soil pressure: $q = 1300 / (4.95 \times 2.20) = 1300 / 10.89 = 119.4$ kN/m² ≤ 120 kN/m² ✓

**Final Answer:** $L = 4.95$ m, $B = 2.20$ m

---

**Part (b) — Bending Moment and Shear Force \[7 marks\]**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Distributed load
$$w = q_s \times B = 120 \times 2.20 = 264 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Cantilever length (overhang beyond column)
$$a = (L - d) / 2 = (4.95 - 4.0) / 2 = 0.475 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Maximum BM at column face (from overhang)
$$M_{\max} = \frac{w \cdot a^2}{2} = \frac{264 \times 0.475^2}{2} = \frac{264 \times 0.2256}{2} = 29.78 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Maximum SF at column face
$$V_{\max} = w \cdot a = 264 \times 0.475 = 125.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Location of critical section for shear
- The **critical section for one-way (beam) shear** is at a distance **d (effective depth)** from the face of the column.
- The **critical section for two-way (punching) shear** is at a distance **d/2** from the column periphery on all sides.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Trapezoidal Combined Footing Design**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

*(a)* Two columns **A** and **B** carry loads of **500 kN** and **800 kN** respectively, with center-to-center spacing of **4 m**. Column A is at the property line. The SBC of soil is **120 kN/m²**. The length of the footing is **restricted to 5.5 m**. Design a **trapezoidal combined footing** and determine the dimensions $B_1$ and $B_2$. **\[7 Marks\]**

*(b)* Explain why the **wider parallel side ($B_2$) of a trapezoidal combined footing is placed under the more heavily loaded column**. With a neat sketch, show the **pressure distribution** and the position of the **resultant** of column loads. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Design of Trapezoidal Footing \[7 marks\]**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Total load and required area
$$R = 500 + 800 = 1300 \text{ kN}, \quad A = 1300/120 = 10.833 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Centroid of loads from A
$$\bar{x} = (500 \times 0 + 800 \times 4) / 1300 = 2.462 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Equation 1 — Sum of parallel sides
$$B_1 + B_2 = \frac{2A}{L} = \frac{2 \times 10.833}{5.5} = \frac{21.667}{5.5} = 3.939 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Equation 2 — Centroid condition
$$\bar{x} = \frac{L(B_1 + 2B_2)}{3(B_1 + B_2)} \implies 2.462 = \frac{5.5(B_1 + 2B_2)}{3 \times 3.939}$$

$$B_1 + 2B_2 = \frac{2.462 \times 3 \times 3.939}{5.5} = \frac{29.094}{5.5} = 5.290 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Solving simultaneously
From Eq 1: $B_1 = 3.939 - B_2$

Substitute: $(3.939 - B_2) + 2B_2 = 5.290 \implies B_2 = 1.351$ m
$$B_1 = 3.939 - 1.351 = 2.588 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 (0.5 marks):** Final answer
$$\boxed{L = 5.5 \text{ m}, \quad B_1 = 2.60 \text{ m (at Col A)}, \quad B_2 = 1.35 \text{ m (at Col B)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wait — the student may notice that in this case $B_1 > B_2$.** This is because with L = 5.5 m > 2x̄ = 4.923 m, the centroid of the trapezoid shifts LEFT (toward B₁). To compensate and bring the centroid back to x̄ = 2.462 m, B₁ must be larger. **This is a case where the constraint L > 2x̄ forces B₁ > B₂**, even though Column A is the lighter loaded one. **In a typical property-line problem where L is restricted to be exactly 2d, the geometry forces the larger side under the heavier column.** Students should verify the direction with the calculation.

---

**Part (b) — Pressure Distribution and Conceptual Explanation \[7 marks\]**

**Answer:**

In a trapezoidal combined footing, the **wider side is placed under the more heavily loaded column** to **shift the geometric centroid of the footing** toward the heavier load. This ensures that the **resultant of column loads** passes through the **centroid of the footing area**, achieving **uniform soil pressure** (zero eccentricity).

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- The centroid of a trapezoid lies closer to the **wider parallel side** (specifically, at $\bar{x}_T = L(B_1 + 2B_2) / [3(B_1 + B_2)]$ from $B_1$).
- If $Q_B > Q_A$, the resultant of column loads is closer to Column B.
- To make the centroid of footing coincide with the resultant, the **wider side $B_2$** must be on the **Column B side**.
- This balances the **clockwise and counter-clockwise moments** about the centroid.

**Neat Sketch (4 marks):**

```
              PROPERTY LINE
                   │
                   │ Col A (Q_A)              Col B (Q_B)
                   │  ↓                          ↓
                   │  │←──── d = 4 m ─────────→│
                   │  │                          │
        ┌──────────┴──┴──────────────────────────┴──────────┐
        │                                                   │  ↑
        │ \          (Soil Pressure q uniform)            / │  │
        │  \                                           /   │  │ q = 120 kN/m²
        │   \                                         /    │  │
        │ B₁ \                                     / B₂   │  ↓
        │     \___________________________________/       │
        └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                   ↑                          ↑
              q at A                     q at B
              (uniform)                  (uniform)
                   │←───── x̄ = 2.462 m ───→│
                   │←──────── L = 5.5 m ────────────→│
```

The **resultant R = 1300 kN** acts at **x̄ = 2.462 m** from Column A, passing through the centroid of the trapezoidal area, ensuring **uniform pressure distribution** below the footing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting to set eccentricity to zero** — The most common error. Always write the equation $L/2 = \bar{x}$ or $\bar{x}_T = \bar{x}$ explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong reference point for x̄** — Measure from the property line (Column A) consistently. Switching reference between A and B loses **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing B₁ and B₂ positions** — In trapezoidal problems, verify which side is wider using the formula after solving. If the result contradicts intuition, recheck the calculation.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify the design** — Always include an eccentricity check and area check (1.5 marks are reserved for verification in KTU marking scheme).
> 5. **Not converting units** — Distances in **m**, loads in **kN**, SBC in **kN/m²**, BM in **kN·m**. Mixing units costs marks.
> 6. **Skipping the sketch** — A neat sketch of the footing with dimensions and column positions is worth **1.5–2 marks** in 14-mark problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following 12 points constitute the COMPLETE KTU Module 3 (Combined Footings) checklist for last-minute revision:**

1. **Combined footing is used** when columns are close, when a column is on the property line, or when SBC is low.
2. **Uniform pressure** condition: **Centroid of footing = Centroid of loads** (eccentricity $e = 0$).
3. **Rectangular footing** has **constant width B**; length $L = 2\bar{x}$ is set to satisfy the centroid condition.
4. **Trapezoidal footing** has **varying width** ($B_1 \neq B_2$); length L is **pre-determined by constraints**.
5. **Trapezoid centroid formula** (from B₁ side): $\bar{x}_T = L(B_1 + 2B_2) / [3(B_1 + B_2)]$ — **memorize this**.
6. **Trapezoid area formula:** $A = (B_1 + B_2)L / 2$ — combined with centroid formula, gives **two equations for two unknowns**.
7. **Wider side $B_2$ is placed under the more heavily loaded column** to shift the centroid toward the heavier load.
8. **For a column at property line** with free length selection, **rectangular footing** is often sufficient (just set $L = 2\bar{x}$).
9. **Trapezoidal is needed** when L is **restricted** (e.g., L = 2d, or fixed overhang) AND the column loads are unequal.
10. **Bending moment** is maximum at column face due to cantilever overhang: $M = wa^2/2$, where $a$ = overhang.
11. **Shear force** is maximum at column face: $V = wa$. Critical section is at **distance d** (effective depth) from column face for one-way shear.
12. **Always verify** your design with **area check** ($A = L \times B$ for rectangular) and **eccentricity check** ($e = 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark problem, allocate marks as: 1 mark for data extraction, 2 marks for centroid calculation, 3 marks for L/B determination, 2 marks for verification, 2 marks for BM/SF, 2 marks for sketch, and 2 marks for final dimensions statement. **Never skip the sketch — it is mandatory in KTU board exams.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raft Foundations: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Raft Foundations: Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Raft Foundation** (also called a **Mat Foundation**) is a large, continuous slab foundation that supports all the columns and walls of a structure as a single, combined footing. It is essentially one large footing that extends over the entire building footprint, transmitting the total structural load to the underlying soil over a maximized contact area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A raft foundation is a combined footing that supports several columns, the size of which is governed by the permissible bearing pressure of the soil and the layout of the columns. When the individual column footings would cover more than approximately **50%** of the total plan area, a raft becomes the structurally and economically preferred solution.

**Key Terminology:**
- **Footing Area Ratio** (FAR): The ratio of the sum of individual footing areas to the total built-up area. When FAR $\ge 0.5$, a raft is recommended.
- **Subgrade Reaction ($k_s$)**: The reaction pressure exerted by the soil per unit settlement of the foundation, measured in **kN/m³** or **kN/m²/mm**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Snowshoe Effect:**
> Imagine walking on deep, soft snow. If you wear regular shoes, your feet sink because the load is concentrated on a tiny area. But if you wear **snowshoes**, your weight spreads over a huge surface area, and the pressure (force per unit area) on the snow drops dramatically — you float on top. A **raft foundation works exactly like a snowshoe** for a building. Instead of many small, concentrated footings that "sink" the building into weak soil, a single large mat distributes the load over the entire footprint, drastically reducing the contact pressure.

**Why do engineers choose a Raft?**
1. **Low Soil Bearing Capacity** — Soil is too weak to carry concentrated column loads.
2. **Heavy Structural Loads** — High-rise buildings, silos, chimneys, or bridge piers.
3. **Overlapping Footings** — Individual column footings would overlap due to close column spacing or large column loads.
4. **Non-Uniform (Variable) Soil** — Differential settlement control across heterogeneous strata.
5. **Basement Construction** — The raft doubles as the basement floor slab.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for choosing Raft — "SHOES":**
> **S** — Soft soil (low SBC)
> **H** — Heavy loads
> **O** — Overlapping footings
> **E** — Expansive/compressible strata
> **S** — Sub-basement / basement requirement

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Typical SBC Trigger:** Raft adopted when safe bearing capacity falls below roughly **100 kN/m²**.
- **Typical Raft Thickness:** Ranges from **0.5 m to 3.0 m** depending on column loads and span.
- **Standard Reinforcement:** Two-mat (top + bottom) mesh of high-yield deformed (HYD) bars, often **12 mm to 32 mm** diameter.
- **Concrete Grade:** Usually **M25 to M40** as per IS 456 and IS 2950 (Part 1).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Raft Foundations (KTU Module 3 Core Topic)

There are **four principal types** of raft foundations, classified by structural configuration. The choice depends on column layout, load magnitude, soil profile, and project economics.

### Type 1 — Solid Slab Raft (Plain Mat)

A **uniform-thickness reinforced concrete slab** that extends over the entire building footprint. All columns are supported directly on the slab without any beams, pedestals, or stiffeners.

**Why and How It Works:**
- The slab thickness is governed by **two-way bending** (punching shear) around each column.
- Simplest and cheapest form of raft.
- Suitable for **closely spaced, lightly loaded columns** (residential buildings, low-rise commercial).
- Typical column spacing $\le 6$ m and column loads $\le 1500$ kN.

**Drawbacks:** Becomes uneconomical for heavy loads and long column spans because punching shear governs and the slab becomes excessively thick.

### Type 2 — Slab and Beam Raft (Beam-and-Slab Mat)

A raft in which the **slab is thickened locally** with **upstand beams** (also called **pedestals or stiffeners**) running along column lines, providing extra rigidity and reducing slab thickness.

**Why and How It Works:**
- Beams act as **stiffening ribs** that distribute concentrated column loads over wider slab regions.
- The slab carries the **uniform subgrade reaction**, while the beams handle **concentrated column loads and shear**.
- This is the **most widely used raft type** in practice for medium-to-high loads.

**Design Logic:**
1. Beams are designed as **inverted T-beams or upstand beams** spanning between columns.
2. The slab is designed as a **two-way slab supported on beams**.
3. Column load is transferred $\rightarrow$ beam $\rightarrow$ slab $\rightarrow$ soil.

### Type 3 — Cellular Raft (Box Foundation / Floating Raft)

A **two-storey hollow structure** with a top slab, a bottom slab, and **intermediate dividing walls (cells)** that form a rigid box. The hollow cells are sometimes filled with soil or left empty.

**Why and How It Works:**
- Provides **very high rigidity** with **relatively less concrete volume** than a solid slab.
- The **buoyancy of the hollow cells** can offset a portion of the structural weight — useful where excavation is deep and the net soil pressure must be minimized.
- Often used for **very heavy, closely-spaced columns**, high-rise towers, or when subsoil is extremely weak.
- The cellular structure behaves as a **monolithic rigid box**, greatly reducing differential settlement.

**Engineering Application:** Used beneath large power plants, grain silos, multi-storey car parks, and offshore terminals.

### Type 4 — Piled Raft Foundation (Piled Mat)

A **raft supported on a group of piles** that transfer the load to a deeper, stronger soil or rock stratum. The raft serves as a **pile cap** for the entire structure.

**Why and How It Works:**
- Combines the **load-sharing** of a raft with the **deep-foundation** strength of piles.
- Load is shared between **soil (bearing)** and **piles (skin friction + end bearing)**.
- Used where **shallow soil is too weak** even for a raft, or where **settlement control is critical**.
- Common in **soft coastal clays, reclaimed land, and liquefaction-prone zones**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all the high-yield equations for raft foundation design that appear in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers. Master these before attempting numericals.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description & Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Total Column Load | $Q = \sum Q_i$ | Sum of all column loads (kN) |
| Self-Weight of Raft | $W_{raft} = \gamma_c \cdot A \cdot D$ | $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³, $A$ = plan area (m²), $D$ = raft depth (m) |
| Self-Weight of Soil on Raft | $W_{soil} = \gamma_{soil} \cdot A \cdot d$ | If basement: $\gamma_{soil} \approx 18$–$20$ kN/m³, $d$ = soil depth (m) |
| **Gross Bearing Pressure** | $q_{gross} = \dfrac{Q + W_{raft} + W_{soil}}{A}$ | Pressure including self-weight (kN/m²) |
| **Net Bearing Pressure** | $q_{net} = q_{gross} - \gamma \cdot D_f$ | $D_f$ = depth of foundation (m) |
| **Max/Min Pressure (with moment)** | $q_{\max,\min} = \dfrac{Q}{A} \pm \dfrac{M_x \cdot y}{I_x} \pm \dfrac{M_y \cdot x}{I_y}$ | Eccentric loading case (kN/m²) |
| Net Upward Pressure (k value) | $k_s = \dfrac{q_{net}}{S}$ | Subgrade reaction modulus (kN/m³ or kN/m²/mm) |
| Rigidity Factor | $k_r = \dfrac{E_s \cdot B^3}{12 \cdot E_c \cdot I}$ | $k_r < 0.5$: Flexible; $k_r > 0.5$: Rigid |
| Average Settlement | $S = \dfrac{q \cdot B \cdot (1-\nu^2)}{E_s} \cdot I_w$ | $I_w$ = influence factor ($\approx 0.82$ for square flexible) |
| Differential Settlement | $\Delta S = S_{max} - S_{min}$ | Should be $\le 65$ mm for raft on sand; $\le 40$ mm on clay (IS 1904) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** Never use the pipe character `\vert` for absolute values inside the table above. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX to prevent markdown table breakage. I have used $\dfrac{}{}$ for all fractions to keep them crisp.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-Rise Buildings:** Raft foundations carry combined column loads of **500 MN or more** in tall towers, e.g., Burj Khalifa, Petronas Towers.
- **Industrial Structures:** Silos, chimneys, and blast furnaces generate localized heavy loads that rafts distribute evenly.
- **Bridge Piers and Abutments:** Rafts support massive piers on alluvial river beds.
- **Offshore Structures:** Cellular rafts serve as gravity-based platforms.
- **Underground Structures:** Rafts act as **floating foundations** for basements — when excavated soil weight equals the building weight, **net pressure $\approx$ 0** (true floating condition).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Net Bearing Pressure Under a Raft

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU ESE for Module 3. The objective is to derive the **net pressure intensity** that the raft actually transmits to the soil after subtracting the overburden pressure (which existed before construction).

### Step 1: Define Total Vertical Load on Raft

Let:
- $Q$ = Sum of all column service loads (kN)
- $W_{raft}$ = Self-weight of the reinforced concrete raft
- $W_{soil}$ = Weight of backfill soil above the raft (if any, e.g., for basement)

$$
Q_{total} = Q + W_{raft} + W_{soil}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The raft must transfer *all* downward forces — both the building's loads and the raft's own weight — to the soil, so we add them.

### Step 2: Express Self-Weight Components

For a raft of plan area $A$, thickness $D$, and an overlying backfill of depth $d$:

$$
W_{raft} = \gamma_c \cdot A \cdot D
$$

$$
W_{soil} = \gamma_{soil} \cdot A \cdot d
$$

**Textual Logic:** Volume = Area $\times$ thickness/depth; Weight = Volume $\times$ unit weight.

### Step 3: Compute Gross Bearing Pressure

Gross pressure is the *total* contact stress at the raft-soil interface, *before* removing the original overburden:

$$
q_{gross} = \frac{Q_{total}}{A} = \frac{Q + \gamma_c \cdot A \cdot D + \gamma_{soil} \cdot A \cdot d}{A}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Pressure = Total Force / Contact Area. This is the actual stress the soil experiences.

### Step 4: Subtract Original Overburden Pressure

Before excavation, the soil at depth $D_f$ (raft base level) carried a natural overburden pressure $\gamma_{soil} \cdot D_f$. After construction, the raft replaces this soil. The **net *additional* pressure** causing settlement is:

$$
q_{net} = q_{gross} - \gamma_{soil} \cdot D_f
$$

Substituting $q_{gross}$:

$$
q_{net} = \frac{Q + \gamma_c \cdot A \cdot D + \gamma_{soil} \cdot A \cdot d}{A} - \gamma_{soil} \cdot D_f
$$

### Step 5: Simplify for Typical Case (No Backfill)

If there is **no backfill soil** above the raft ($d = 0$, i.e., raft is exposed), the most common practical form becomes:

$$
q_{net} = \frac{Q + \gamma_c \cdot A \cdot D}{A} - \gamma_{soil} \cdot D_f
$$

$$
q_{net} = \frac{Q}{A} + \gamma_c \cdot D - \gamma_{soil} \cdot D_f
$$

**Textual Logic:** The first term is structural pressure; the second is raft's own weight per unit area; the last subtracts pre-existing soil pressure. Only the *net* additional pressure causes new settlement.

### Step 6: Settlement Compatibility (Subgrade Reaction)

Equating this net pressure to the **subgrade reaction** $k_s$ multiplied by settlement $S$:

$$
q_{net} = k_s \cdot S
$$

$$
S = \frac{q_{net}}{k_s}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Soil behaves like a series of independent springs (Winkler model); the stiffness $k_s$ is the spring constant.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Distribution with Eccentricity (Combined Footing / Raft)

When the **resultant column load $Q$ does not coincide with the centroid** of the raft, an overturning moment develops. The pressure distribution then becomes **trapezoidal or triangular**.

### Step 1: Define Eccentricity

Let the resultant act at an offset $e$ from the centroid along the x-axis:

$$
M = Q \cdot e
$$

where $M$ is the moment about the centroidal axis.

### Step 2: Apply the Combined Axial + Bending Stress Equation

For a rectangular raft of dimensions $B \times L$, with bending about the x-axis:

$$
q_{max,min} = \frac{Q}{A} \pm \frac{M \cdot y}{I_x}
$$

The section modulus $I_x / y_{max}$ simplifies to $B \cdot L^2 / 6$ for bending along L:

$$
q_{max,min} = \frac{Q}{A} \pm \frac{6 \cdot M}{B \cdot L^2}
$$

Substituting $M = Q \cdot e$:

$$
q_{max,min} = \frac{Q}{B \cdot L} \cdot \left(1 \pm \frac{6e}{L}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Special Case — Kern Boundary

If $e = L/6$, then $q_{min} = 0$ (triangular distribution with zero edge stress). If $e > L/6$, **uplift / tension** occurs at the far edge, which is unacceptable for soils. The **kern (middle-third) rule** therefore demands:

$$
e \le \frac{L}{6}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** For a raft carrying a chimney or shear wall, the eccentricity is non-zero. Always check $e \le B/6$ to avoid tension. If violated, redesign the raft to be larger or add counterweight.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Raft Type

| **Parameter** | **Solid Slab** | **Slab & Beam** | **Cellular** | **Piled Raft** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Column Load Range** | Low to Medium ($\le 1500$ kN) | Medium to High (1500–5000 kN) | Very High ($> 5000$ kN) | Any load |
| **Column Spacing** | Closely spaced ($\le 6$ m) | Medium to large (6–10 m) | Closely spaced irregular | All spacings |
| **Raft Thickness** | Uniform, 0.5–1.0 m | 0.4–1.5 m slab + beams | Hollow box, 2–6 m total depth | Raft 0.5–1.5 m |
| **Concrete Volume** | High | Moderate (saving via beams) | Low (due to hollow cells) | Moderate |
| **Rigidity** | Low–Moderate | Moderate–High | Very High | Depends on piles |
| **Settlement Control** | Moderate | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | High | Highest |
| **Best Application** | Low-rise, residential | Multi-storey buildings | Heavy industrial, towers | Soft soil, reclamation |
| **Construction Complexity** | Simplest | Moderate | High | Highest |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A raft foundation of size $20 \, \text{m} \times 10 \, \text{m}$ supports 12 columns, each carrying $1000$ kN. The raft depth is $1.5$ m and the foundation is placed at $2.5$ m below GL. Take $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³ and $\gamma_{soil} = 18$ kN/m³. Calculate the gross and net bearing pressures.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Column Load:**
$$
Q = 12 \times 1000 = 12{,}000 \, \text{kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Self-Weight of Raft:**
$$
A = 20 \times 10 = 200 \, \text{m}^2
$$
$$
W_{raft} = 25 \times 200 \times 1.5 = 7{,}500 \, \text{kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Load on Soil:**
$$
Q_{total} = 12{,}000 + 7{,}500 = 19{,}500 \, \text{kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Gross Bearing Pressure:**
$$
q_{gross} = \frac{19{,}500}{200} = 97.5 \, \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Net Bearing Pressure:**
$$
q_{net} = 97.5 - 18 \times 2.5 = 97.5 - 45 = 52.5 \, \text{kN/m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **net pressure of 52.5 kN/m²** is what governs settlement. If the soil's net safe bearing pressure (NSBC) is higher than 52.5 kN/m², the raft is safe. Otherwise, enlarge the raft.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid

    A[START: Building Loads and Soil Data Available] --> B{Check Individual Footing Areas}

    C -- "SBC is very low, soft clay or reclaimed soil" --> D{Column Load Magnitude}
    C -- "SBC moderate, loads moderate" --> E[Type 2: Slab and Beam Raft]
    D -- "Low to medium, columns closely spaced" --> F[Type 1: Solid Slab Raft]
    D -- "Very heavy, deep basement required" --> G[Type 3: Cellular Raft]
    D -- "Soil is highly compressible, deep strata needed" --> H[Type 4: Piled Raft Foundation]
    F --> I[Apply Net Bearing Pressure Check]
    G --> I
    E --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Is q_net less than or equal to NSBC?}
    J -- "Yes" --> K[DESIGN COMPLETE: Detail Reinforcement]
    J -- "No" --> L[Enlarge Raft or Switch Type]
```
