# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a raft foundation. Under what conditions is a raft preferred over individual footings?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **raft foundation** is a large continuous reinforced concrete slab supporting all the columns of a structure, transferring the combined load to the soil as a single unit over the entire building footprint.
>
> **Conditions for preferring a raft over isolated footings:**
> 1. The **soil bearing capacity is low** (e.g., q_s < 100 kN/m²).
> 2. The **column loads are very heavy** (typically > 1000 kN each).
> 3. The **combined area of individual footings exceeds 50%** of the building's plan area (footings would overlap).
> 4. The **soil profile contains localized soft/compressible pockets** that could cause excessive differential settlement.
> 5. The structure is **sensitive to differential settlement** (e.g., framed buildings, machinery foundations).
> 6. The project includes a **basement** where the raft doubles as the basement floor.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is the "middle-third rule" (core condition) for a raft foundation? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **middle-third rule (core condition)** states that for full contact between a rigid raft and the underlying soil (with no tension or uplift), the **resultant of all loads must lie within the middle third of the base in both principal directions**. Mathematically:
>
> $$\frac{e_x}{B} \leq \frac{1}{6} \quad \text{AND} \quad \frac{e_y}{L} \leq \frac{1}{6}$$
>
> **Importance:**
> - If the eccentricity exceeds B/6 (or L/6), the contact pressure at the far edge becomes **zero or negative (uplift)**, meaning part of the raft separates from the soil.
> - This leads to **non-uniform contact pressure**, increased **maximum pressure** (which may exceed the SBC), and **structural distress** due to redistribution of stresses.
> - In design practice, the **maximum pressure is doubled** under the triangular contact case, often necessitating a **larger raft dimension** to bring the load back into the kern.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is internally divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. Candidates answer **either Question A OR Question B** (full optionality is provided within Module 3).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

A raft foundation of size **10 m × 12 m** supports 6 columns carrying **600 kN** each. The columns are located at coordinates (in m from the left-bottom corner): (3, 4), (7, 4), (3, 8), (7, 8), (5, 6), and (5, 2). The net allowable bearing pressure is **$q_{na} = 80$ kN/m²** and the unit weight of soil is **18 kN/m³**. Assume the depth of foundation as 1.5 m.

**(a) Determine the contact pressure distribution beneath the raft using the rigid method.** [7 Marks]

**(b) Check whether the maximum contact pressure is within safe limits. If not, suggest a suitable design modification.** [7 Marks]

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Contact Pressure Distribution**

**Step 1: Compute the total column load**

$$Q_{col} = 6 \times 600 = 3600 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Compute the weight of the raft** (assume slab thickness 0.6 m):

$$W_{raft} = 0.6 \times 10 \times 12 \times 25 = 1800 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Total load**

$$Q = 3600 + 1800 = 5400 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Locate the centroid of the column group (x-direction)**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{600(3+7+3+7+5+5)}{3600} = \frac{600 \times 30}{3600} = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Locate the centroid (y-direction)**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{600(4+4+8+8+6+2)}{3600} = \frac{600 \times 32}{3600} = 5.33 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Centroid of raft** is at $x_c = 5$ m, $y_c = 6$ m.

**Step 7: Eccentricities**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_x &= 5.0 - 5.0 = 0 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
e_y &= 5.33 - 6.0 = -0.67 \text{ m} \quad \text{(load is shifted upward from raft centroid)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Check core condition**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{e_x}{B} &= \frac{0}{10} = 0 < \frac{1}{6} \checkmark \\[4pt]
\frac{\vert e_y \vert}{L} &= \frac{0.67}{12} = 0.056 < \frac{1}{6} \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The load lies within the kern — **full contact** with the soil.

**Step 9: Compute corner pressures** at $(x, y) = (B/2, L/2) = (5, 6)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{max,min} &= \frac{Q}{A} \left[ 1 \pm \frac{6 e_x}{B} \pm \frac{6 e_y}{L} \right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{5400}{120} \left[ 1 \pm 0 \pm \frac{6 \times (-0.67)}{12} \right] \\[6pt]
&= 45 \left[ 1 \pm 0.335 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{max} &= 45 \times 1.335 = 60.07 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\[4pt]
q_{min} &= 45 \times 0.665 = 29.92 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b): Safety Check and Design Modification

**Step 10: Compare with net allowable bearing pressure**

$$q_{max} = 60.07 \text{ kN/m}^2 < q_{na} = 80 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum contact pressure of **60.07 kN/m²** is **well within the safe limit** of 80 kN/m². **The design is safe** as proposed, and no modification is necessary.

> **However**, if the design had been unsafe (q_max > q_na), the modifications would be:
> 1. **Increase the raft dimensions** (B or L) to reduce the contact pressure.
> 2. **Shift the column layout** to bring the load resultant closer to the raft centroid (reduce eccentricity).
> 3. **Adopt a slab-and-beam or cellular raft** for higher stiffness.
> 4. **Use a piled raft** to transfer some load to deeper strata.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the self-weight** of the raft in the total load Q. (Most common error — **deduct 1 mark**.)
> 2. **Sign convention error** on eccentricity: always quote $|e|$ with direction specified.
> 3. **Failing to check the core condition** before applying the uniform/linear pressure formula.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert kN/m² to kN/m** (or vice versa) consistently in bending moment calculations.
> 5. **Using gross SBC instead of net SBC** when depth of foundation is significant.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

A column of size **0.5 m × 0.5 m** carries a service load of **1500 kN** on a raft foundation. The **net allowable bearing pressure** is **$q_{na} = 100$ kN/m²** and the **raft slab thickness** is **0.6 m**. The concrete is **M25** and steel is **Fe 415**.

**(a) Determine the minimum plan area of the raft and design the thickness to resist punching shear.** [7 Marks]

**(b) Calculate the maximum bending moment per unit width and design the main reinforcement (Fe 415).** [7 Marks]

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Minimum Plan Area and Punching Shear Design**

**Step 1: Assume raft self-weight = 10% of column load**

$$Q_{total} = 1.1 \times 1500 = 1650 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Minimum raft area**

$$A_{req} = \frac{Q_{total}}{q_{na}} = \frac{1650}{100} = 16.5 \text{ m}^2$$

Choose a square raft of **4.2 m × 4.2 m = 17.64 m²** (slight over-design is standard practice).

$$q_{actual} = \frac{1650}{17.64} = 93.54 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Punching shear check at column location**

Assume effective depth $d = 600 - 50 = 550$ mm (50 mm clear cover). Critical perimeter at $d/2 = 275$ mm from column face:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= 2 \left[ (c_1 + d) + (c_2 + d) \right] = 2 \left[ (0.5 + 0.55) + (0.5 + 0.55) \right] = 4.2 \text{ m} = 4200 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
A_{crit} &= (0.5 + 0.55)^2 = 1.1025 \text{ m}^2 \\[6pt]
V_p &= P - q \cdot A_{crit} = 1500 - 93.54 \times 1.1025 = 1500 - 103.13 = 1396.87 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply limit state of design — factored load**

$$V_{u,factor} = 1.5 \times 1396.87 = 2095.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Design shear stress**

$$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b_0 \cdot d} = \frac{2095.3 \times 10^3}{4200 \times 550} = 0.906 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 6: Permissible shear stress** (IS 456 for M25, $p_t = 0.5\%$ assumed): $\tau_c \approx 0.36 \times \sqrt{25} / \sqrt{...}$ — using a standard value: $\tau_c \approx 1.10$ N/mm².

$$\tau_v = 0.906 < \tau_c = 1.10 \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

**Hence, d = 550 mm is adequate against punching shear.**

#### Part (b): Bending Moment and Reinforcement Design

**Step 1: Critical bending moment per unit width (cantilever from column face to raft edge)**

$$L_1 = \frac{4.2}{2} - \frac{0.5}{2} = 2.1 - 0.25 = 1.85 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Factored bending moment per meter width**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_u &= 1.5 \times \frac{q \cdot L_1^2}{2} \quad \text{(per meter width)} \\[6pt]
&= 1.5 \times \frac{93.54 \times 1.85^2}{2} = 1.5 \times \frac{320.27}{2} \\[6pt]
&= 240.20 \text{ kN·m/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Required reinforcement using IS 456 limit state design**

For M25, Fe 415, the design constants are:
- $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm²
- $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2$ for Fe 415.

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 1000 \times 550^2 \times 10^{-6} = 1043.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_u = 240.2 < M_{u,lim} = 1043.5$ kN·m, the section is **under-reinforced** — singly reinforced design is sufficient.

**Step 4: Calculate $A_{st}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{st} &= \frac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}} \right] b d \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.5 \times 25}{415} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 240.2 \times 10^6}{25 \times 1000 \times 550^2}} \right] \times 1000 \times 550 \\[6pt]
&= 0.0301 \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.1458} \right] \times 550{,}000 \\[6pt]
&= 0.0301 \times 0.0763 \times 550{,}000 = 1263 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Provide 12 mm diameter bars at 85 mm c/c** (provides $A_{st,prov} = 12.66 \times 1000 / 85 = 1325$ mm²) — **safe**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B Specific:**
> 1. **Not applying the load factor of 1.5** for limit state design (results in gross under-design — lose 2 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting clear cover** when computing effective depth $d$.
> 3. **Using gross unit stress method** instead of limit state method (penalty as per KTU 2024 scheme).
> 4. **Not checking $M_u < M_{u,lim}$** to confirm singly-reinforced section.
> 5. **Omitting distribution steel** (minimum 0.12% of gross cross-section) — common omission.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision):**

- ✅ **Raft = combined footing for ALL columns** of a structure, designed as a single mat.
- ✅ **Rigid method** is the conventional KTU approach; IS 2950 Part 1 governs design.
- ✅ **Core condition:** $e_x \leq B/6$ AND $e_y \leq L/6$ — required for full contact (no uplift).
- ✅ **Contact pressure (corner):** $q = (Q/A) [1 \pm (6 e_x / B) \pm (6 e_y / L)]$.
- ✅ **Permissible angular distortion:** $\beta = \Delta / L \leq 1/500$ for framed R.C. structures.
- ✅ **Bending moment (cantilever):** $M = q L_1^2 / 2$ per unit width.
- ✅ **Bending moment (simply supported):** $M = q L_1^2 / 8$ per unit width.
- ✅ **Punching shear** is critical at column locations — check at $d/2$ from column face.
- ✅ **Effective depth** $d = $ total depth $-$ clear cover (50 mm for raft slabs).
- ✅ **Limit state factor** for load: 1.5 (D.L.) and 1.5 (L.L.) in combination as per IS 456.
- ✅ **$M_{u,lim}$ for Fe 415:** $0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ — confirm singly reinforced.
- ✅ **Distribution steel minimum:** 0.12% of gross cross-sectional area.
- ✅ **Coefficient of subgrade reaction** $k_s = q/s$ — Winkler model parameter (units: kN/m³).
- ✅ **Raft types to know:** Flat slab, slab-and-beam, slab-and-pedestal, cellular, piled raft.
- ✅ **Self-weight of raft** = thickness $\times$ area $\times$ 25 kN/m³ (for R.C.C.).
- ✅ **Net vs. Gross SBC:** $q_{ns} = q_{s,gross} - \gamma_{soil} \cdot D_f$.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Design Principles of Raft Foundation.**
> *Best of luck with your KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examination!*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bearing Capacity Equations for Raft on Sand (Teng's Equation Based on SPT Value) and for Raft on Clay (Skempton's Formula)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bearing Capacity Equations for Raft on Sand (Teng's Equation Based on SPT Value) and for Raft on Clay (Skempton's Formula)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bearing Capacity of Raft Foundations — The Big Picture

A **Raft Foundation** (also called a *mat foundation*) is a large continuous slab supporting the entire structure. Unlike an isolated footing, the raft mobilizes a much larger area of soil, which fundamentally changes the bearing capacity equations. The classical Terzaghi/Meyerhof/Vesic equations (developed for shallow *isolated* footings) are **not directly reliable** for rafts because rafts behave closer to a **"general shear failure"** at a much larger scale, and the soil mass involved is significantly deeper.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why separate equations for rafts?**
> For rafts, the failure zone extends to a depth roughly equal to the raft width $B$. Empirical evidence shows that bearing capacity does **not increase indefinitely** with width for sands — this size effect must be captured empirically. For clays, the shape and depth factors derived for small footings do not hold for large rafts.

## 1.2 Teng's Equation for Raft on Sand (1962)

**Formal Definition:**
> *Teng's equation is a semi-empirical formula that estimates the **net ultimate bearing capacity** of a square or circular raft foundation resting on a cohesionless soil (sand) by directly correlating it with the **corrected Standard Penetration Test (SPT) N-value**.*

**Teng's Ultimate Net Bearing Capacity Equation:**

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, N \, R_w$$

**Where:**
- $\gamma$ = effective unit weight of sand (in **kN/m³**)
- $B$ = width of raft (in **m**)
- $N$ = **corrected** SPT N-value (blow count)
- $R_w$ = **water table reduction factor** (dimensionless, 0 ≤ $R_w$ ≤ 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Constraint (Teng's Limitation):**
> The equation is **valid only for B ≤ 4.5 m**. For larger rafts, the equation is **unconservative** because laboratory and field data show that the bearing capacity **does not increase linearly beyond B = 4.5 m**. Hence, for B > 4.5 m, the value of B in the formula is **capped at 4.5 m** (some references say 4.0 m). This is a classic KTU exam trap.

## 1.3 Skempton's Formula for Raft on Clay (1951)

**Formal Definition:**
> *Skempton's formula is a **semi-empirical bearing capacity equation** for foundations on **saturated clay (φ = 0 condition)**, specifically developed because classical theories significantly **underestimate** the bearing capacity factor $N_c$ for large rafts at moderate depths.*

**Skempton's Bearing Capacity Equation:**

$$q_{ult} = c_u \, N_c + \gamma \, D_f$$

**Where:**
- $c_u$ = undrained cohesion of clay (in **kPa**)
- $N_c$ = modified bearing capacity factor (depends on shape and depth)
- $\gamma$ = unit weight of soil above the base
- $D_f$ = depth of foundation

**Skempton's $N_c$ Factor:**

$$N_c = 5.0 \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{B}{L}\right) \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{D_f}{B}\right) \quad \text{for} \quad \frac{D_f}{B} \leq 2.5$$

$$N_c = 7.5 \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{B}{L}\right) \quad \text{for} \quad \frac{D_f}{B} > 2.5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Note:** Skempton's $N_c$ is **NOT a constant 5.14** like Terzaghi. It **increases with depth ratio $D_f/B$** up to a limit of $D_f/B = 2.5$, capturing the fact that **deeper rafts on clay are stronger** than classical theory predicts.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Crater Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Crater Formation):**
> Imagine dropping a heavy object onto sand versus onto wet clay:
>
> - **Sand (Teng's):** When you press down on sand, the resistance comes from **friction and packing** of the grains. The deeper you probe, the more grains you engage — but only up to a point. Beyond ~4.5 m, the sand "forgets" the load, and extra width doesn't help. SPT N-value measures *how many blows* it takes to push a spoon through the sand — more blows = stiffer sand = higher bearing capacity. **Teng's equation is essentially a direct translation of "blow count × sand weight × depth" into a bearing pressure.**
>
> - **Clay (Skempton's):** When you press on wet clay, the resistance comes from the **cohesive water-soil bonds** squeezing the water out. The more clay mass you mobilize (larger raft), and the more clay is **confined** by overburden (deeper raft), the stronger it is. Skempton's $N_c$ recognizes that **larger rafts are disproportionately stronger** on clay — a phenomenon classical φ=0 theory misses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Teng's Equation

Teng's reasoning when developing his 1962 equation:

1. **Step 1 — Empirical observation:** For square/circular rafts on sand, the net ultimate bearing capacity is observed to be approximately proportional to the **SPT N-value**.
2. **Step 2 — Dimensional analysis:** The bearing pressure (kN/m²) must contain the unit weight of soil $\gamma$ (kN/m³) multiplied by a length scale. The natural length scale is the **raft width B**.
3. **Step 3 — Calibration:** By correlating 200+ field load tests with SPT data, Teng determined the empirical constant to be **0.5**.
4. **Step 4 — Water table correction:** Buoyancy reduces effective unit weight. Teng introduced $R_w$ to discount the effective contribution.
5. **Step 5 — Width cap:** For $B > 4.5$ m, the failure mechanism shifts from "general shear" to "local shear," and the linear relationship breaks down. Hence the cap.

### Water Table Reduction Factor ($R_w$)

For a water table located at depth $z_w$ below the base of the raft:

$$R_w = 0.5 \left(1 + \frac{z_w}{B}\right)$$

**Special Cases:**
- Water table at base level ($z_w = 0$): $R_w = 0.5$
- Water table at depth $z_w = B$ or more: $R_w = 1.0$ (no reduction)
- Water table at ground surface ($z_w = -D_f$): $R_w = 0.5(1 - D_f/B)$ — may even reduce below 0.5

### SPT Correction (Background — Frequently Tested in KTU)

The "N" used in Teng's equation is the **corrected N-value**:

$$N_{corrected} = N_{observed} \times C_N \times C_R \times \eta_H$$

Where:
- $C_N$ = overburden correction (Liao & Whitman): $C_N = \sqrt{\frac{98}{\sigma'_v}}$ (kPa) or $\sqrt{\frac{2000}{\sigma'_v}}$ (psf)
- $C_R$ = rod length correction
- $\eta_H$ = hammer efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Most exam problems give you the **already-corrected N-value** directly. Do not over-correct unless specified.

### Safe Bearing Capacity (Teng's Method)

$$q_s = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} + \gamma D_f$$

**Factor of Safety (F):**
- F = **3** for normal structures
- F = **4 to 5** for important structures (e.g., hospitals, towers)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Skempton's Formula

1. **Step 1 — Why modify Terzaghi?** Terzaghi's $N_c = 5.14$ was derived for **strip footings at the surface**. Field tests on rafts showed actual failure loads 30–50% higher.
2. **Step 2 — Shape effect:** A square or circular raft mobilizes soil on all sides, so it is stronger than a strip. Skempton added the $(1 + 0.2B/L)$ factor.
3. **Step 3 — Depth effect:** A raft buried in clay has additional confinement from the surrounding soil mass. The factor $(1 + 0.2D_f/B)$ accounts for this, capped at $D_f/B = 2.5$.
4. **Step 4 — Upper limit:** When $D_f/B > 2.5$, the raft is so deep that the failure mechanism no longer reaches the ground surface. $N_c$ saturates at $7.5(1 + 0.2B/L)$.

### Special $N_c$ Values for KTU Calculations

| Raft Shape | B/L | $N_c$ (at surface, $D_f=0$) | $N_c$ (deep, $D_f/B>2.5$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strip | → 0 | **5.0** | **7.5** |
| Square | 1.0 | **6.0** | **9.0** |
| Circular | ≈1.0 | **6.0** | **9.0** |
| Rectangular (B/L = 0.5) | 0.5 | **5.5** | **8.25** |

### Net vs. Gross Bearing Capacity (Skempton)

**Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity:**

$$q_{nu} = c_u \, N_c$$

**Gross Ultimate Bearing Capacity:**

$$q_{ult} = c_u \, N_c + \gamma \, D_f$$

**Safe Bearing Pressure (Allowable):**

$$q_s = \frac{c_u \, N_c}{F} + \gamma D_f$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Teng's Equation (Sand)** | **Skempton's Formula (Clay)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Ultimate Net BC** | $q_{nu} = 0.5 \gamma B N R_w$ | $q_{nu} = c_u N_c$ |
| **Ultimate Gross BC** | $q_{ult} = q_{nu} + \gamma D_f$ | $q_{ult} = c_u N_c + \gamma D_f$ |
| **$N_c$ Factor** | Not applicable (cohesionless) | $5.0(1+0.2\frac{B}{L})(1+0.2\frac{D_f}{B})$ if $\frac{D_f}{B}\leq 2.5$ |
| **Upper cap of $N_c$** | Not applicable | $7.5(1+0.2\frac{B}{L})$ if $\frac{D_f}{B} > 2.5$ |
| **Width limitation** | $B \leq 4.5$ m (cap B = 4.5 m) | None |
| **Water table factor** | $R_w = 0.5(1+\frac{z_w}{B})$ | None (use effective $c_u$) |
| **Safe BC formula** | $q_s = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} + \gamma D_f$ | $q_s = \frac{c_u N_c}{F} + \gamma D_f$ |
| **Typical F** | 3 to 4 | 3 |
| **Required input** | SPT N (corrected), $\gamma$, B, $D_f$, $z_w$ | $c_u$ (undrained), B, L, $D_f$ |
| **Soil type** | Sand/SPT-based cohesionless | Saturated clay ($\phi = 0$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:**
> - **Teng's equation** is used during *preliminary design* when only SPT data is available (very common in India).
> - **Skempton's formula** is the **de facto standard** for design of mat foundations on soft to medium clays in coastal and backwater regions of Kerala — extremely relevant to KTU students.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation Logic — Teng's Equation from Classical Bearing Capacity Theory

Teng's equation can be partially motivated from the general bearing capacity equation by **substituting empirical correlations** between SPT N and the Terzaghi parameters.

**Starting Point — Meyerhof & Hanna's General Bearing Capacity:**

$$q_{ult} = c' N_c q_c' N_q s_c d_c + 0.5 \gamma' B N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma$$

For **cohesionless sand** ($c' = 0$):

$$q_{ult} = q N_q s_q d_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma$$

Now, **SPT correlations** (Bowles, 1996) provide:

$$N_q \approx N \quad \text{and} \quad N_\gamma \approx N$$

Substituting and using **shape and depth factors for square rafts** ($s_q s_\gamma \approx 0.6$, $d_q d_\gamma \approx 1.0$):

$$q_{ult} = \gamma D_f (0.6)(1.0) N + 0.5 \gamma B (0.6)(1.0) N$$

**Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity** (subtract overburden $\gamma D_f$):

$$q_{nu} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f$$

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \gamma B N (0.6) (1.0)$$

$$q_{nu} \approx 0.3 \gamma B N$$

To account for **three-dimensional effects and water table correction**, Teng calibrated his constant empirically:

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, N \, R_w \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Skempton's $N_c$ — Empirical Foundation

Skempton (1951) compiled **field load test data on 21 foundations** in saturated clay. He plotted the **actual measured $N_c$** against the **depth ratio $D_f/B$** and observed:

1. At $D_f/B = 0$ (surface footing, strip), $N_c \approx 5.0$
2. As $D_f/B$ increases, $N_c$ rises linearly
3. Slope = $5.0 \times 0.2 = 1.0$ per unit of $D_f/B$
4. At $D_f/B = 2.5$, $N_c$ reaches a maximum and saturates
5. Maximum $N_c$ (strip) = $5.0 \times (1 + 0.2 \times 2.5) = 5.0 \times 1.5 = 7.5$

**Therefore for strip footing:**

$$N_c = 5.0 \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{D_f}{B}\right), \quad \frac{D_f}{B} \leq 2.5$$

$$N_c = 7.5, \quad \frac{D_f}{B} > 2.5$$

**Adding shape factor** based on the same test data (square vs. strip):

$$N_c = 5.0 \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{B}{L}\right) \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{D_f}{B}\right), \quad \frac{D_f}{B} \leq 2.5 \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Exam Style)

### **Example 1 — Teng's Equation on Sand**

**Problem:**
A square raft of size **8 m × 8 m** is founded at a depth of **2 m** below ground surface in a sandy soil with corrected SPT N = **20** and unit weight $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. The water table is at **3 m** below ground surface. Determine the safe bearing capacity using F = 3.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Check width condition.

$$B = 8 \text{ m} > 4.5 \text{ m}$$

So use **B = 4.5 m** (Teng's cap).

**Step 2:** Water table depth below base of raft.

$$z_w = 3 - 2 = 1 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $R_w$.

$$R_w = 0.5 \left(1 + \frac{z_w}{B}\right) = 0.5 \left(1 + \frac{1}{4.5}\right)$$

$$R_w = 0.5 \times 1.222 = 0.611$$

**Step 4:** Net ultimate bearing capacity.

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \times \gamma \times B \times N \times R_w$$

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \times 18 \times 4.5 \times 20 \times 0.611$$

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \times 18 \times 4.5 \times 20 \times 0.611$$

Calculating step by step:

$$0.5 \times 18 = 9$$

$$9 \times 4.5 = 40.5$$

$$40.5 \times 20 = 810$$

$$810 \times 0.611 = 494.91 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Gross safe bearing capacity.

$$q_s = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} + \gamma D_f$$

$$q_s = \frac{494.91}{3} + 18 \times 2$$

$$q_s = 164.97 + 36$$

$$\boxed{q_s = 200.97 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 201 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

---

### **Example 2 — Skempton's Formula on Clay**

**Problem:**
A square raft **10 m × 10 m** is placed at **3 m depth** in a saturated clay with undrained cohesion $c_u = 60$ kPa and $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³. Find the safe bearing capacity with F = 3.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Check depth ratio.

$$\frac{D_f}{B} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.3$$

Since $0.3 \leq 2.5$, use:

$$N_c = 5.0 \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{B}{L}\right) \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{D_f}{B}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Calculate shape factor (square → B/L = 1).

$$1 + 0.2 \frac{B}{L} = 1 + 0.2 \times 1 = 1.2$$

**Step 3:** Calculate depth factor.

$$1 + 0.2 \frac{D_f}{B} = 1 + 0.2 \times 0.3 = 1 + 0.06 = 1.06$$

**Step 4:** Calculate $N_c$.

$$N_c = 5.0 \times 1.2 \times 1.06$$

$$N_c = 5.0 \times 1.272 = 6.36$$

**Step 5:** Net ultimate bearing capacity.

$$q_{nu} = c_u N_c = 60 \times 6.36 = 381.6 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 6:** Gross safe bearing capacity.

$$q_s = \frac{c_u N_c}{F} + \gamma D_f$$

$$q_s = \frac{381.6}{3} + 19 \times 3$$

$$q_s = 127.2 + 57$$

$$\boxed{q_s = 184.2 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

---

### **Example 3 — Comparative Analysis (Concept KTU Loves)**

For a **rectangular raft 4 m × 8 m** at $D_f = 2$ m on clay with $c_u = 80$ kPa, $\gamma = 20$ kN/m³:

$$\frac{D_f}{B} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5 \leq 2.5$$

$$\frac{B}{L} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5$$

$$N_c = 5.0 (1 + 0.2 \times 0.5)(1 + 0.2 \times 0.5)$$

$$N_c = 5.0 \times 1.1 \times 1.1 = 6.05$$

$$q_{nu} = 80 \times 6.05 = 484 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Compare with **Terzaghi (B/L = 0)** for the same raft: $N_c = 5.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.1 = 5.5$, giving $q_{nu} = 440$ kN/m².

**Observation:** Skempton's correction gives **10% higher capacity** for the rectangular shape factor alone.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What?

| **Soil Condition** | **Equation** | **Key Parameter** | **KTU Exam Hint** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sand + SPT data available | Teng's | Corrected N-value | Apply $B \leq 4.5$ m cap |
| Saturated clay | Skempton's | $c_u$ (undrained) | Check $D_f/B$ for $N_c$ formula |
| Clay with both $c$ and $\phi$ | Use general equation | Both $c$ and $\phi$ | Not Skempton's direct domain |
| Stratified soil | Weighted average | Multiple layers | Beyond Module 3 scope |
| Water table in sand | Apply $R_w$ | $z_w$ below base | Always check WT position |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Methodology Flowchart for Both Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Soil Type] --> B{Is soil cohesionless?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use TENG's Equation]
    B -- No --> D[Check phi equals zero]
    D -- Yes --> E[Use SKEMPTON's Formula]
    D -- No --> F[Use General Bearing Capacity Eqn]
    
    C --> C1[Collect corrected SPT N-value]
    C1 --> C2[Check raft width B]
    C2 --> C3{B greater than 4.5 m?}
    C3 -- Yes --> C4[Cap B to 4.5 m]
    C3 -- No --> C5[Use actual B]
    C4 --> C6[Locate water table zw]
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C7[Calculate Rw factor]
    C7 --> C8[Compute q_nu equals 0.5 gamma B N Rw]
    C8 --> C9[Add gamma Df for gross q_ult]
    C9 --> C10[Divide by F for safe q_s]
    
    E --> E1[Collect undrained cu]
    E1 --> E2[Compute B by L ratio]
    E2 --> E3[Compute Df by B ratio]
    E3 --> E4{Df by B less than 2.5?}
    E4 -- Yes --> E5[Nc equals 5.0 times 1 plus 0.2 B by L times 1 plus 0.2 Df by B]
    E4 -- No --> E6[Nc equals 7.5 times 1 plus 0.2 B by L]
    E5 --> E7[Compute q_nu equals cu Nc]
    E6 --> E7
    E7 --> E8[Add gamma Df for gross q_ult]
    E8 --> E9[Divide by F for safe q_s]
    
    C10 --> Z[Final Answer: q_s]
    E9 --> Z
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TENG[Teng Equation for Sand]
        T1[Input: N SPT, gamma, B, Df, zw] --> T2[Constraint: B leq 4.5 m]
        T2 --> T3[Water Factor Rw]
        T3 --> T4[q_nu equals 0.5 gamma B N Rw]
        T4 --> T5[q_s equals q_nu by F plus gamma Df]
    end
    
    subgraph SKMP[Skempton Formula for Clay]
        S1[Input: cu, B, L, Df] --> S2[Shape Factor B by L]
        S2 --> S3[Depth Factor Df by B]
        S3 --> S4{Threshold: Df by B vs 2.5}
        S4 -- leq 2.5 --> S5[Use first equation for Nc]
        S4 -- greater than 2.5 --> S6[Use upper limit for Nc]
        S5 --> S7[q_nu equals cu Nc]
        S6 --> S7
        S7 --> S8[q_s equals cu Nc by F plus gamma Df]
    end
    
    T5 --> OUT[Comparison Output]
    S8 --> OUT
    OUT --> TEND[Decision: For sand use Teng, for clay use Skempton]
```

## 4.3 Physical Setup Schematic (Text-Based Block Diagram)

```
    ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
                   GROUND SURFACE
    ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
                   |       |
    D_f = 2m       |  SAND | (or CLAY)
                   |       |
    ───────────────┴───────┴────────────────
                   |       |
    z_w = 1m       |  SAND |     ← Water Table
                   |       |
    ───────────────┴───────┴────────────────
                   |       |  B = 8 m (sand)
                   |  SAND |  B = 10 m (clay)
                   |       |
                   |       |
                   |       |   Failure zone extends
                   |       |   to depth ≈ B below
                   |       |   raft base
                   |       |
    ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Schematic:**
> - For **sand rafts**, the failure wedge extends to roughly **0.5B to B** below the base.
> - For **clay rafts**, the failure is a **deeper, more circular slip** that goes to depth ~B.
> - The 4.5 m width cap in Teng's equation reflects that beyond this depth, the failure mechanism no longer scales with width.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q:** State Teng's equation for the net ultimate bearing capacity of a raft foundation on sand. What is the limitation of this equation regarding the width of the raft?

**Model Answer:**

Teng's equation (1962) for the net ultimate bearing capacity of a square or circular raft on cohesionless soil (sand) is:

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, N \, R_w$$

Where $\gamma$ = unit weight of sand, $B$ = width of raft, $N$ = corrected SPT N-value, and $R_w$ = water table reduction factor.

**Limitation:** The equation is valid only for **B ≤ 4.5 m**. For larger widths, the value of B in the formula must be **capped at 4.5 m** because the bearing capacity does not increase linearly with width beyond this limit (size effect / local shear failure mechanism).

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Q:** Write Skempton's formula for bearing capacity of a raft on saturated clay and explain the variation of $N_c$ with $D_f/B$.

**Model Answer:**

Skempton's formula for a raft on saturated clay ($\phi = 0$):

$$q_{ult} = c_u N_c + \gamma D_f$$

Where $N_c$ is given by:

$$N_c = 5.0 \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{B}{L}\right) \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{D_f}{B}\right) \quad \text{for} \quad \frac{D_f}{B} \leq 2.5$$

$$N_c = 7.5 \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{B}{L}\right) \quad \text{for} \quad \frac{D_f}{B} > 2.5$$

**Variation of $N_c$ with $D_f/B$:** For a strip footing (B/L → 0), $N_c$ increases linearly from **5.0** at $D_f/B = 0$ to **7.5** at $D_f/B = 2.5$, after which it remains constant at **7.5**. This reflects the increased confinement and reduced likelihood of the failure surface reaching the ground surface for deeper rafts.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q (a)** A square raft of size **6 m × 6 m** is founded at **1.5 m** depth in sand with corrected SPT N = **25**, $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³, and water table at the **ground surface**. Determine the **net ultimate** and **safe bearing capacity** using Teng's equation. Take F = 3. (7 Marks)

**Q (b)** A raft foundation of size **12 m × 18 m** rests on saturated clay at depth 4 m. Given $c_u = 90$ kPa, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. Calculate the **safe bearing capacity** using Skempton's formula. Take F = 3. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q(a):**

**Step 1:** Check width.

$$B = 6 \text{ m} > 4.5 \text{ m}$$

**Therefore**, use B = 4.5 m in Teng's equation.

**Step 2:** Water table location.

Water table is at the ground surface, so depth below raft base:

$$z_w = -D_f = -1.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $R_w$ using Teng's convention (with $z_w$ measured from base; if WT is above base, $R_w$ is reduced):

$$R_w = 0.5 \left(1 + \frac{z_w}{B}\right) = 0.5 \left(1 + \frac{-1.5}{4.5}\right)$$

$$R_w = 0.5 \times (1 - 0.333) = 0.5 \times 0.667 = 0.333$$

**Step 4:** Net ultimate bearing capacity.

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \times \gamma \times B \times N \times R_w$$

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \times 17 \times 4.5 \times 25 \times 0.333$$

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \times 17 \times 4.5 \times 25 \times 0.333$$

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \times 17 = 8.5$$

$$8.5 \times 4.5 = 38.25$$

$$38.25 \times 25 = 956.25$$

$$956.25 \times 0.333 = 318.43 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Gross safe bearing capacity.

$$q_s = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} + \gamma D_f$$

$$q_s = \frac{318.43}{3} + 17 \times 1.5$$

$$q_s = 106.14 + 25.5$$

$$\boxed{q_s = 131.64 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

**Net ultimate bearing capacity = 318.43 kN/m²**

---

**Model Solution to Q(b):**

**Step 1:** Calculate ratios.

$$\frac{B}{L} = \frac{12}{18} = 0.667$$

$$\frac{D_f}{B} = \frac{4}{12} = 0.333$$

**Step 2:** Since $D_f/B = 0.333 \leq 2.5$:

$$N_c = 5.0 \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{B}{L}\right) \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{D_f}{B}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Shape factor.

$$1 + 0.2 \times 0.667 = 1 + 0.1333 = 1.1333$$

**Step 4:** Depth factor.

$$1 + 0.2 \times 0.333 = 1 + 0.0667 = 1.0667$$

**Step 5:** $N_c$.

$$N_c = 5.0 \times 1.1333 \times 1.0667$$

$$N_c = 5.0 \times 1.2089 = 6.044$$

**Step 6:** Net ultimate bearing capacity.

$$q_{nu} = c_u \times N_c = 90 \times 6.044 = 543.96 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 7:** Safe bearing capacity.

$$q_s = \frac{c_u N_c}{F} + \gamma D_f$$

$$q_s = \frac{543.96}{3} + 18 \times 4$$

$$q_s = 181.32 + 72$$

$$\boxed{q_s = 253.32 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q (a)** What are the **assumptions and limitations** of Skempton's formula for bearing capacity of rafts on clay? Compare it with Terzaghi's classical equation. (7 Marks)

**Q (b)** A square raft of size **5 m × 5 m** is to be constructed on sand with $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³, corrected SPT N = 30, water table at **2.5 m** depth, and foundation depth $D_f = 1.5$ m. Using Teng's equation, find the **safe bearing pressure** (F = 3). (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q(a):**

**Assumptions of Skempton's Formula:**
1. Soil is **saturated clay** with $\phi = 0$ (undrained condition).
2. The bearing capacity depends only on **undrained cohesion $c_u$**.
3. Soil mass behaves as a **rigid-plastic** material (Tresca criterion).
4. Failure is by **general shear** (or local shear for very soft clays).

**Limitations:**
1. Not applicable to **partially saturated** or **overconsolidated** clays where $\phi \neq 0$.
2. Valid only for **homogeneous clay deposits** — fails in layered soils.
3. Assumes **no tension crack** formation at the surface.
4. The empirical constants (5.0, 0.2) are based on **limited field data** (21 tests).
5. The cap at $D_f/B = 2.5$ is **arbitrary** and may not be valid for very soft clays.

**Comparison with Terzaghi's Equation:**

| **Feature** | **Terzaghi (1943)** | **Skempton (1951)** |
|---|---|---|
| $N_c$ value (surface strip) | 5.7 | 5.0 |
| $N_c$ for square/circular | 6.0 (constant) | 6.0 (matches at surface) |
| Depth effect | Negligible (assumed $D_f/B$ small) | **Explicit $(1+0.2D_f/B)$ factor** |
| Empirical basis | Theory + small-scale tests | **Field load test data on full-scale rafts** |
| Raft size | Valid for small footings | **Developed specifically for rafts** |
| Upper limit on $N_c$ | None | 7.5 (strip) or 9.0 (square) |
| Cohesion input | $c$ (general) | $c_u$ (undrained only) |
| Use case | General shallow foundations | **Mat/raft foundations on soft clay** |

**Conclusion:** Skempton's formula is **more realistic** for large rafts on clay because it accounts for the **size effect** and **depth confinement** observed in field load tests, which classical Terzaghi theory underestimates.

---

**Model Solution to Q(b):**

**Step 1:** Check width.

$$B = 5 \text{ m} > 4.5 \text{ m}$$

**Therefore**, use B = 4.5 m in Teng's formula.

**Step 2:** Water table depth below raft base.

$$z_w = 2.5 - 1.5 = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $R_w$.

$$R_w = 0.5 \left(1 + \frac{z_w}{B}\right) = 0.5 \left(1 + \frac{1.0}{4.5}\right)$$

$$R_w = 0.5 \times 1.222 = 0.611$$

**Step 4:** Net ultimate bearing capacity.

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \times \gamma \times B \times N \times R_w$$

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \times 19 \times 4.5 \times 30 \times 0.611$$

$$q_{nu} = 0.5 \times 19 = 9.5$$

$$9.5 \times 4.5 = 42.75$$

$$42.75 \times 30 = 1282.5$$

$$1282.5 \times 0.611 = 783.61 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Safe bearing pressure.

$$q_s = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} + \gamma D_f$$

$$q_s = \frac{783.61}{3} + 19 \times 1.5$$

$$q_s = 261.20 + 28.5$$

$$\boxed{q_s = 289.7 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the B = 4.5 m cap in Teng's equation** — This is the #1 mistake. If $B = 8$ m is given, students plug 8 into the formula. **This is wrong and loses 2–3 marks immediately.**
>
> 2. **Wrong sign of $z_w$ in $R_w$** — Water table *below* base: $z_w$ is positive. Water table *above* base (flooded site): $z_w$ is negative. The convention must be strictly applied.
>
> 3. **Confusing $N_c$ in Skempton vs. Terzaghi** — Terzaghi's $N_c = 5.7$ (strip) is NOT used in Skempton's formula. Skempton uses **5.0** as the base constant.
>
> 4. **Using the wrong $N_c$ formula for $D_f/B > 2.5$** — When the depth ratio exceeds 2.5, the second formula must be used. Students often ignore this and continue with the first.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to convert net → gross for $q_s$** — The safe bearing pressure requires the overburden $\gamma D_f$ to be **added back**, not subtracted. Students often divide $q_{nu}$ by F and forget the addition.
>
> 6. **Shape ratio B/L confusion** — Some students write L/B instead of B/L. The Skempton formula specifically uses B/L. **For a square (B=L), both give 1, so this trap is hidden.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

### **Teng's Equation for Raft on Sand:**
- ✅ Formula: $q_{nu} = 0.5 \gamma B N R_w$
- ✅ Valid only for **B ≤ 4.5 m**; cap B at 4.5 m otherwise
- ✅ N is the **corrected** SPT N-value
- ✅ $R_w = 0.5(1 + z_w/B)$ where $z_w$ is depth of water table **below raft base**
- ✅ Safe BC: $q_s = q_{nu}/F + \gamma D_f$
- ✅ Factor of safety: F = 3 (normal), 4–5 (important structures)
- ✅ Used in **preliminary design** with SPT data

### **Skempton's Formula for Raft on Clay:**
- ✅ Formula: $q_{ult} = c_u N_c + \gamma D_f$
- ✅ Soil must be **saturated clay** with $\phi = 0$
- ✅ Base constant: **5.0** (not 5.7 from Terzaghi)
- ✅ $N_c = 5.0(1+0.2B/L)(1+0.2D_f/B)$ for $D_f/B \leq 2.5$
- ✅ $N_c = 7.5(1+0.2B/L)$ for $D_f/B > 2.5$
- ✅ For square raft at surface: $N_c = 6.0$; for strip at surface: $N_c = 5.0$
- ✅ Maximum $N_c$ for square raft = **9.0** (deep, $D_f/B > 2.5$)
- ✅ Safe BC: $q_s = c_u N_c/F + \gamma D_f$
- ✅ Use **$c_u$ (undrained cohesion)** — not drained cohesion $c'$

### **Comparative Snapshot:**
| **Aspect** | **Teng** | **Skempton** |
|---|---|---|
| Soil | Sand | Clay |
| Key test | SPT (N) | Unconfined Compression / Vane ($c_u$) |
| Width limit | B ≤ 4.5 m | None |
| Water table | $R_w$ correction | Effective stress analysis |
| Empirical basis | 200+ SPT correlations | 21 field load tests |

### **Critical Numerical Values to Memorize:**
- Teng's constant: **0.5**
- Skempton base $N_c$ (strip, surface): **5.0**
- Skempton $N_c$ (square, surface): **6.0**
- Skempton $N_c$ (strip, deep): **7.5**
- Skempton $N_c$ (square, deep): **9.0**
- Teng's width cap: **4.5 m**
- Skempton depth cap: **D_f/B = 2.5**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floating Foundations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Floating Foundations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Floating Foundation (Compensated Foundation / Pontoon Foundation):**
> A *floating foundation* is a type of deep foundation in which the **weight of the soil excavated for the foundation pit is made approximately equal to the weight of the structure (including the foundation itself)** that will rest on it. The resulting **net increase in vertical stress on the subsoil at foundation level is zero (or negligible)**, so the building is said to "float" in the surrounding earth mass.

**Synonyms used in KTU textbooks:**
- Compensated Foundation
- Buoyant Foundation
- Pontoon Foundation
- Balanced Foundation
- Fully / Partially Compensated Raft

The classical definition (Terzaghi, Peck, Mesri) states:
> *"A raft is designed as a floating foundation when the weight of the excavated soil equals the weight of the proposed structure at the level of the base of the raft."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition 🧠

Imagine a **large steel ship** floating in the ocean. The ship is enormously heavy (thousands of tonnes), yet it does NOT sink into the seabed because:
1. The ship **displaces a volume of water** equal to its own weight (Archimedes' Principle).
2. The net force on the seabed is therefore **nearly zero**.

A **floating foundation works on the same principle**:
- The soil beneath a proposed building site is dug out (excavated).
- The excavated soil weighed X tonnes.
- The building (made lighter by using hollow cells, light-weight concrete, or air-filled basements) is designed so that **the building also weighs approximately X tonnes**.
- The soil underneath experiences almost the **same stress as it did before construction** — it has not been loaded additionally. The structure is "buoyed up" by the soil it displaced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples:**
> - Multi-storey buildings in **Kerala backwaters / coastal soft clay zones** (Kochi, Kuttanad).
> - **High-rise buildings in Mumbai** on marine clay.
> - **Underground metro stations** with deep basements.
> - **Sea-front hotel basements** that double as parking floors (acting as hollow buoyant boxes).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Standard)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol |
|---|---|
| Unit weight of water, $\gamma_w$ | **9.81 kN/m³** (≈ 10 kN/m³ for KTU numericals) |
| Submerged unit weight, $\gamma'$ | $\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ |
| Specific gravity of soil solids, $G_s$ | 2.65 – 2.75 (assumed 2.70 in KTU problems) |
| Depth range where float is adopted | $D_f \ge 5$ m (typical) |
| Net stress objective | $\Delta\sigma \approx 0$ kN/m² |

---

## 1.4 When is a Floating Foundation Adopted? (Necessity Conditions)

A floating foundation is selected when **ALL** the following exist:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Bearing capacity of shallow soil is very low** ($q_u \le 100$ kN/m², soft clay / loose silt).
> 2. **Depth of competent bearing stratum is large** (deep soil profile shows no hard layer near the surface).
> 3. **Piling is uneconomical or impractical** (long piles, high cost, deep water table, artesian conditions).
> 4. **A large structural footprint** is needed (the whole basement/raft covers the building area, so the contact pressure becomes manageable).

The condition can be mathematically expressed as the **Relative Depth Ratio**:
$$\frac{D_f}{B} \ge 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5$$

where $B$ = width of foundation. When this ratio is high, the weight of the excavated soil is large enough to "compensate" the building weight.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Floating Foundation Works — Step-by-Step Logic

**Before excavation (original ground condition):**
- Vertical effective stress at the depth of future foundation level:
$$\sigma'_0 = \gamma \cdot D_f$$
- (For soil below water table: use $\gamma'$ instead of $\gamma$).

**After excavation only (before placing structure):**
- Stress relieved to ~0 at base of pit.
- Net reduction in stress: $\sigma'_0$.

**After placing structure + backfill load:**
- Stress at base = pressure imposed by structure = $q_{avg} = \dfrac{W_{structure}}{A}$.
- **Net change in stress on subsoil:**
$$\Delta\sigma = q_{avg} - \gamma D_f \quad \text{(dry soil case)}$$
$$\Delta\sigma = q_{avg} - \gamma' D_f \quad \text{(submerged soil case)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For a "perfectly floating" foundation:**
> $$\boxed{\;\Delta\sigma = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; q_{avg} = \gamma D_f \;\; \text{or} \;\; q_{avg} = \gamma' D_f\;}$$
> The subsoil is *not stressed any more than it was before construction* → **zero additional settlement** (theoretically).

---

## 2.2 Types of Floating Foundations

| # | Type | Description | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Compensated Mat / Raft** | Solid RC raft cast at depth; excavation weight ≈ building weight | Multi-storey on soft soil |
| 2 | **Hollow Box / Pontoon Raft** | Raft with hollow chambers/cells; air/light fill inside | Buoyancy reduces weight |
| 3 | **Basement-type Floating Foundation** | Submerged hollow basement = air-filled buoyancy tank | Reduces net weight to 60–80% |
| 4 | **Semi-floating / Partially Compensated** | Only 70–90% compensation, small net pressure allowed | Where some settlement tolerable |

---

## 2.3 🎯 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | **Original effective overburden pressure** | $p_0 = \gamma D_f$ (or $\gamma' D_f$ submerged) | $D_f$ = depth of foundation |
| F2 | **Gross bearing pressure (Avg.)** | $q_{avg} = \dfrac{W}{A}$ | $W$ = total weight, $A$ = area |
| F3 | **Net bearing pressure** | $q_{net} = q_{avg} - \gamma D_f$ (or $-\gamma' D_f$) | Excess pressure on soil |
| F4 | **Condition for full float (dry)** | $D_f = \dfrac{q_{avg}}{\gamma}$ | Depth for $\Delta\sigma=0$ |
| F5 | **Condition for full float (submerged)** | $D_f = \dfrac{q_{avg}}{\gamma'}$ | Uses $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat}-\gamma_w$ |
| F6 | **Depth for partial float (submerged)** | $D_f = \dfrac{q_{net}}{\gamma - \gamma_w}$ | Net = $(q_{avg} - \gamma' D_f)$ |
| F7 | **Submerged unit weight** | $\gamma' = \dfrac{(G_s - 1)\gamma_w}{1+e} = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | Standard soil-mechanics |
| F8 | **Modified ultimate bearing capacity (submerged)** | $q_{u(sub)} = c' N_c + \gamma' D_f (N_q - 1) + 0.5 \gamma' B N_\gamma$ | Terzaghi / Meyerhof / IS form |
| F9 | **Submerged factor in BC** | $W' = W_{soil} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma'}{\gamma_{sat}}$ | Used in weight-volume checks |
| F10 | **Net allowable bearing pressure** | $q_{a,net} = \dfrac{q_{u}}{FOS} - \gamma' D_f$ | After FOS |
| F11 | **% Compensation** | $\%\text{Compensation} = \dfrac{\gamma D_f}{q_{avg}} \times 100$ | 100% = fully floating |
| F12 | **Weight-reduction to achieve float** | $W_{target} = \gamma' D_f \cdot A$ | Max allowable structure weight |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:**
> Whenever the foundation depth is **below the water table**, ALWAYS use **submerged unit weight $\gamma'$** in the bearing capacity equation. This **increases the effective stress and bearing capacity** of the soil, but it also changes the design depth.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **High-Rise Construction on Soft Marine Clay (Kerala, Mumbai, Singapore, Dubai Marina):** Avoids extremely long, expensive piles.
2. **Underground Metro Stations & Pump Houses:** The void created during excavation = buoyancy; station acts as a hollow pontoon.
3. **Offshore Concrete Platforms (Gravity-Based Structures):** Hollow base cells provide both stability and buoyancy during towing.
4. **Recreational / Commercial Basements:** Underground parking + mechanical floors become structural floats.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> **Exhaustive Mandate:** Every algebraic transition, every substitution, every numerical evaluation is explicitly written. No skipped steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Depth Equation for a Fully Compensated (100% Floating) Foundation in Submerged Soil

### Step 1 — Set up the problem symbolically

Let:
- $A$ = area of raft at depth $D_f$ (m²)
- $W$ = total weight of the proposed structure including raft (kN)
- $\gamma$ = bulk unit weight of soil above water table (kN/m³)
- $\gamma_{sat}$ = saturated unit weight of soil below water table (kN/m³)
- $\gamma_w$ = unit weight of water = **9.81 kN/m³** (or 10 for KTU)
- $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ = submerged (effective) unit weight

### Step 2 — Average contact pressure (gross) at raft base

By definition of contact pressure:
$$q_{avg} = \dfrac{W}{A}$$

### Step 3 — Original overburden pressure that existed at depth $D_f$ BEFORE excavation

If the water table coincides approximately with the foundation level (typical assumption for floating foundations in soft clays), the soil was saturated:
$$p_0 = \gamma' \cdot D_f$$

### Step 4 — Define the "Net Pressure Increase" on the subsoil

This is the increase in vertical effective stress on the soil beneath the raft:
$$\Delta\sigma = q_{avg} - p_0 = \dfrac{W}{A} - \gamma' D_f$$

### Step 5 — Apply the floating (fully compensated) condition

> A *floating* foundation is defined as one producing **zero net increase** in subsoil stress:
$$\Delta\sigma = 0$$

$$\dfrac{W}{A} - \gamma' D_f = 0$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $D_f$

$$\dfrac{W}{A} = \gamma' D_f$$

$$\boxed{\;D_f = \dfrac{W}{A \, \gamma'} = \dfrac{W}{A \, (\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w)}\;}$$

### Step 7 — Interpretation

This is the **minimum depth** at which a raft of area $A$ and total weight $W$ must be placed **to be in a state of perfect floatation** in a submerged soil deposit. If $D_f$ is provided (e.g., by site constraint) and is **less** than this value, the foundation is *partially compensated* and a small net stress acts on the subsoil. If $D_f$ is **greater**, the structure tends to "heave" upward — anchors or tension piles may be needed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Modified Bearing Capacity Equation Below Water Table

### Step 1 — General form of Terzaghi's bearing capacity equation (for a strip footing, then extended)

$$q_u = c' N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, N_\gamma$$

where $q = \gamma D_f$ is the surcharge at the base level.

### Step 2 — Recognize that, below the water table, the soil is submerged

The effective (submerged) unit weight is $\gamma'$, and the effective surcharge is $q' = \gamma' D_f$.

### Step 3 — Replace $\gamma$ by $\gamma'$ in the bearing capacity equation

$$\boxed{\;q_{u(sub)} = c' N_c + \gamma' D_f (N_q - 1) + 0.5 \, \gamma' \, B \, N_\gamma\;}$$

> **Why $(N_q - 1)$ and not $N_q$?**
> Because the surcharge $q' = \gamma' D_f$ replaces the $\gamma D_f$ term. The original $q N_q$ becomes $\gamma' D_f N_q$. But the original overburden weight $\gamma D_f$ (when the footing was originally above water) is no longer there — we *subtract* $\gamma D_f$ implicitly. The classical IS-code and Skempton form is:
> $$q_{u(sub)} = c' N_c + \gamma' D_f (N_q - 1) + 0.5 \gamma' B N_\gamma$$

### Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU standard)

**Given:** $c' = 20$ kN/m², $\phi' = 25°$, $B = 6$ m, $D_f = 4$ m, $\gamma' = 9$ kN/m³.
Bearing capacity factors for $\phi = 25°$: $N_c = 25.1$, $N_q = 12.7$, $N_\gamma = 9.7$ (Meyerhof).

Substitute:
$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{u(sub)} &= (20)(25.1) + (9)(4)(12.7 - 1) + 0.5(9)(6)(9.7) \\
&= 502.0 + 9 \times 4 \times 11.7 + 0.5 \times 9 \times 6 \times 9.7 \\
&= 502.0 + 421.2 + 261.9 \\
&= 1185.1 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Net ultimate bearing capacity:
$$q_{nu} = q_{u(sub)} - \gamma' D_f = 1185.1 - (9)(4) = 1185.1 - 36 = 1149.1 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: % Compensation and Partial-Float Depth Equation

### Step 1 — Define % Compensation

$$\%\text{Compensation} = \dfrac{\text{Weight of soil removed}}{\text{Weight of structure}} \times 100$$
$$= \dfrac{\gamma' D_f \cdot A}{W} \times 100$$

### Step 2 — When % Compensation = 100% → Full Float (already derived)

$$D_{f,full} = \dfrac{W}{A \gamma'}$$

### Step 3 — When % Compensation < 100% → Partial Float

Let $q_{net}$ be the allowable net pressure on subsoil (decided by settlement criteria).

Required depth:
$$D_f = \dfrac{q_{avg} - q_{net}}{\gamma'} = \dfrac{q_{net}}{\gamma' \cdot (1 - \%\text{Compensation}/100)}$$

Or equivalently, if depth is given and we want to know the net pressure:
$$q_{net} = \dfrac{W}{A} - \gamma' D_f$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU ESE Style — 14 Mark Pattern)

**Question (Numerical):** A proposed building has a total weight of **120,000 kN** (including foundation). The raft is to be placed at depth $D_f = 4$ m below ground level in a soft clay deposit where the water table is at the ground surface. The saturated unit weight of the clay is $\gamma_{sat} = 18$ kN/m³, cohesion $c' = 25$ kN/m², $\phi' = 0°$ (saturated clay), and $\gamma_w = 10$ kN/m³. The raft is square, $20 \text{ m} \times 20 \text{ m} = 400$ m². Take FOS = 3. Check whether the foundation is floating and compute the factor of safety against bearing failure.

### Solution — Exhaustive

**Step A — Submerged unit weight:**
$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 18 - 10 = 8 \;\text{kN/m}^3$$

**Step B — Required depth for full floatation:**
$$D_{f,full} = \dfrac{W}{A \cdot \gamma'} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{400 \times 8} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{3200} = 37.5 \;\text{m}$$

**Step C — Compare with provided depth:**

Provided $D_f = 4$ m $\;\ll\;$ 37.5 m. **Therefore, the foundation is NOT fully floating.** It is *partially compensated* with a large net pressure on the soil.

**Step D — Actual net pressure exerted on subsoil:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{avg} &= \dfrac{W}{A} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{400} = 300 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
\sigma'_0 &= \gamma' D_f = 8 \times 4 = 32 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
q_{net} &= q_{avg} - \sigma'_0 = 300 - 32 = 268 \;\text{kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Ultimate bearing capacity (Skempton, $\phi = 0$):**
For $\phi = 0$, $N_c = 5.7$ (square, $S_c = 1.3$, so $N_c^{sq} = 6.17$). Use IS-code form:
$$q_u = c' N_c = 25 \times 5.7 = 142.5 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \;(strip)$$
$$q_u^{sq} = 1.3 \times c' N_c = 1.3 \times 142.5 = 185.25 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step F — Net ultimate bearing capacity:**
$$q_{nu} = q_u^{sq} - \gamma' D_f = 185.25 - 32 = 153.25 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step G — Allowable net bearing pressure:**
$$q_{a,net} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FOS} = \dfrac{153.25}{3} = 51.08 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step H — Conclusion:**
$$q_{net,applied} = 268 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \;\gg\; q_{a,net} = 51.08 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**The raft of only 4 m depth is unsafe** — the soil will fail. To achieve floatation, we must either:
- **Increase $D_f$ to 37.5 m** (impractical here), OR
- **Use a much larger raft area** to reduce $q_{avg}$: $A_{min} = \dfrac{W}{\gamma' \cdot D_f} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{8 \times 4} = 3750$ m² (square of ~61 m), OR
- **Reduce the building weight** by using lightweight materials / hollow basements.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic (Exam Quick Reference)

| Observation | Diagnosis | Action |
|---|---|---|
| $D_f < D_{f,full}$ | Under-compensated | Add more depth OR enlarge raft |
| $D_f = D_{f,full}$ | Fully floating | Ideal — but check bearing capacity separately |
| $D_f > D_{f,full}$ | Over-compensated / would heave | Use tension piles / anchors |
| $q_{net} > q_{a,net}$ | Bearing failure likely | Enlarge raft OR reduce weight |
| Settlement > tolerance | Time-rate too slow | Use vertical drains / preloading |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Floating Foundation Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Brief and Soil Report] --> B{Soil Bearing Capacity q_u}
    B -- Low q_u < 100 kN/m2 --> C{Depth of firm stratum > 10 m}
    B -- Adequate q_u --> Z[Adopt Shallow Footing - Stop]
    C -- Yes --> D[Floating Foundation Considered]
    C -- No --> P[Adopt Deep Pile Foundation - Stop]
    D --> E[Compute Submerged Unit Weight gamma prime]
    E --> F[Compute Weight of Structure W and Raft Area A]
    F --> G[Compute Required Float Depth Df full = W divided by A times gamma prime]
    G --> H{Is Provided Df greater or equal to Df full}
    H -- Yes --> I[Fully Floating - Design as Buoyant Box]
    H -- No --> J[Partially Compensated - Compute Net Stress]
    J --> K[Check Net Pressure q_net less than Allowable q_a net]
    K --> L{q_net less than q_a net}
    L -- Yes --> M[Accept Design - Detail Raft]
    L -- No --> N[Increase Df OR Enlarge Raft OR Lighten Structure]
    I --> O[Check Uplift and Buoyancy Stability]
    O --> M
    N --> K
    M --> Y[Final Design Drawings and Specifications]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Cross-Section of a Floating Foundation in Submerged Clay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ground Surface and Water Table at GL] --> B[Excavation Pit Depth Df]
    B --> C[Soft Clay Layer Gamma sat and c prime]
    C --> D[Raft Base Level - Floating Interface]
    D --> E[Compensated Raft 20 m by 20 m]
    E --> F[Superstructure Columns and Walls Above]
    F --> G[Buoyant Submerged Volume Provides Lift]
    G --> H[Net Vertical Stress Change on Subsoil equals ZERO]
    H --> I[No Additional Settlement Occurs]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Types of Floating Foundations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Floating Foundation Types] --> B[1. Compensated Mat Raft - Solid RC Slab]
    A --> C[2. Hollow Box Pontoon - Cellular Voids inside]
    A --> D[3. Basement Type - Submerged Hollow Chambers]
    A --> E[4. Semi Floating - Partial Compensation 70 to 90 percent]
    B --> F[Use in Multi Storey on Soft Clay]
    C --> G[Use where Weight Reduction Critical]
    D --> H[Use in High Water Table Coastal Sites]
    E --> I[Use where Some Settlement is Permissible]
```

## 4.4 Force / Stress Equilibrium Diagram (Text Schematic)

```
         ↑ σ_avg = W/A   (downward force per unit area)
         │
   ┌─────┴─────┐
   │  STRUCTURE │  ← Weight W = ρ_structure × g × V_structure
   │  + RAFT    │
   └─────┬─────┘
         │
─────────┴─────────  ← Raft base at depth Df
   SOIL  ↑  SOIL      ↑ γ' Df  (buoyancy / soil stress relieved)
   (sub-            (submerged overburden that EXISTED before)
    merged)
         │
         ▼ Net Δσ = W/A − γ'·Df = 0   ⟹ FLOATING CONDITION
```

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a *floating foundation*. Under what soil conditions is it preferred over a pile foundation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **floating foundation** is a deep foundation in which the **weight of soil excavated from the foundation pit is made equal to the weight of the proposed structure (including the raft)**. As a result, the **net increase in vertical stress on the subsoil at the foundation level is zero**, and theoretically, the building undergoes no additional settlement.

**Preferred over pile foundation when:**
1. The bearing capacity of the upper soil is very low (soft clay / loose silt, $q_u \le 100$ kN/m²).
2. The depth to a firm stratum is very large (piling becomes uneconomical).
3. The structure has a large footprint (mat covers most of the area).
4. Pile installation is difficult (artesian water, very soft strata, environmental restrictions).

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for conditions + 1 mark for justification = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is **% compensation**? What does 100% compensation signify?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Percentage compensation** is defined as the ratio (in percentage) of the **weight of the soil removed (excavated) to the weight of the proposed structure**, both calculated at the foundation level.

$$\%\text{Compensation} = \dfrac{\gamma' \cdot D_f \cdot A}{W} \times 100$$

- **100 % compensation** signifies a **fully floating foundation** in which the structure's weight exactly equals the weight of the excavated soil — producing **zero net increase in subsoil stress**, and hence no additional settlement in theory.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for explanation of 100% = 3 marks)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ CHOICE A — 14 Marks

#### Question A(a) [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – July 2023]** A building weighing **180,000 kN** (including the foundation) is to be supported by a **square raft of 30 m × 30 m** at a depth of **6 m** below ground level. The water table is at the ground surface. For the soft clay:
- $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³
- $c' = 30$ kN/m²
- $\phi' = 0°$
- $\gamma_w = 10$ kN/m³
- FOS = 3 (against bearing failure)

**Determine:**
1. Whether the foundation is **fully floating**, partially floating, or not floating.
2. The **net bearing pressure** on the subsoil.
3. The **factor of safety** against bearing failure.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Submerged unit weight:**
$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 19 - 10 = 9 \;\text{kN/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Required depth for full floatation:**
$$D_{f,full} = \dfrac{W}{A \cdot \gamma'} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{900 \times 9} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{8100} = 22.22 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with provided $D_f = 6$ m:**
$$6 \;\text{m} \;\ll\; 22.22 \;\text{m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{Foundation is NOT floating (severely under-compensated)}.$$

**Step 4 — Average contact pressure:**
$$q_{avg} = \dfrac{W}{A} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{900} = 200 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Net pressure on subsoil:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{net} &= q_{avg} - \gamma' D_f \\
&= 200 - (9)(6) \\
&= 200 - 54 = 146 \;\text{kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Ultimate bearing capacity (Skempton, $\phi = 0$, square raft, $S_c = 1.3$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 1.3 \, c' N_c \quad (N_c = 5.7) \\
&= 1.3 \times 30 \times 5.7 \\
&= 222.3 \;\text{kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Net ultimate bearing capacity:**
$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma' D_f = 222.3 - 54 = 168.3 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 8 — Allowable net bearing pressure:**
$$q_{a,net} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FOS} = \dfrac{168.3}{3} = 56.1 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 9 — Factor of safety (actual):**
$$FOS_{actual} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{q_{net}} = \dfrac{168.3}{146} = 1.15$$

**Step 10 — Conclusion:**
- Foundation is **NOT floating** (under-compensated).
- Applied $q_{net} = 146$ kN/m² $\;\gg\; q_{a,net} = 56.1$ kN/m².
- Actual FOS = 1.15 < 3 (required). → **UNSAFE against bearing failure.**
- **Recommendation:** Increase depth to 22.2 m OR enlarge raft to ≥ √(180000/9×6) = √3333 = 57.7 m side, OR lighten the building.

*(7 marks: 1 for sub. unit wt., 1 for Df_full, 1 for comparison, 1 for q_net, 1 for BC, 1 for FOS, 1 for conclusion)*

---

#### Question A(b) [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – July 2023]** With neat sketches, explain the **types of floating foundations**. Compare the conditions of use for each.

**Model Answer:**

A floating foundation may be classified into four types:

**1. Compensated Mat / Raft Foundation (Solid RC slab):**
A solid reinforced-concrete mat is cast at the desired depth. The weight of the soil excavated (from ground level to the base of the raft) is made approximately equal to the weight of the building plus the mat itself.
- **Use:** Multi-storey buildings on soft clay of uniform thickness where the entire footprint can be rafted.

**2. Hollow Box / Pontoon Foundation (Cellular raft):**
The raft contains **hollow cells or chambers** (either open at the top forming a basement, or closed sealed cells). Air (or light fill) inside these cells provides **buoyancy** and reduces the effective weight of the structure.
- **Use:** Where significant weight reduction is required (high water table, deep basements). E.g., large commercial complexes with underground parking.

**3. Submerged Basement Type:**
The basement is intentionally designed to be **permanently submerged below the water table**. The displaced water volume provides the buoyant force. The raft is sealed and waterproofed.
- **Use:** Marine structures, pump houses, underground metro stations in water-logged areas.

**4. Partially Compensated (Semi-Floating) Foundation:**
Compensation is intentionally kept between **70 % and 95 %**, allowing a small net pressure on the subsoil. This produces a small, controlled, uniform settlement, which may be acceptable.
- **Use:** Where the building can tolerate small, even settlements (rigid shear-wall structures, water tanks, silos).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Mat Raft | Hollow Box | Submerged Basement | Partial Float |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Weight reduction | Low | High | Very high | Moderate |
| Construction cost | Low | Moderate | High | Lowest |
| Buoyancy utilization | None | Partial | Full | Partial |
| Tolerance to settlement | Low | Low | Very low | Higher |
| Typical % compensation | 80–100% | 60–90% | 50–80% | 70–95% |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per type description + 1 mark for use + 1 mark for comparison table + 1 mark for sketch/illustration reference = 7 marks)*

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B — 14 Marks (Internal Alternative)

#### Question B(a) [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Derive the expression for the **depth of a fully floating foundation** in (i) dry soil, and (ii) submerged soil. Why is the depth requirement **lower in dry soil** for the same loading?

**Model Solution:**

**Case (i) — Dry soil above the water table:**

Let $W$ = total weight of structure, $A$ = raft area, $D_f$ = depth, $\gamma$ = dry/bulk unit weight.

Before excavation: vertical stress at depth $D_f$ is $\sigma'_0 = \gamma D_f$.

Net stress change:
$$\Delta\sigma = \dfrac{W}{A} - \gamma D_f$$

Setting $\Delta\sigma = 0$ for fully floating:
$$\dfrac{W}{A} = \gamma D_f \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{D_f = \dfrac{W}{A \gamma}}$$

**Case (ii) — Submerged soil (water table at or near GL):**

The effective unit weight of the submerged soil is $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w < \gamma$.

For the same loading condition:
$$\dfrac{W}{A} = \gamma' D_f \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{D_f = \dfrac{W}{A \gamma'}}$$

**Comparison:**

Since $\gamma' < \gamma$ (typically $\gamma' \approx 8$–10 kN/m³ vs. $\gamma \approx 16$–20 kN/m³ for dry), the **required depth $D_f$ is LARGER in submerged soil** (counter-intuitive at first glance) — *not* smaller. The student's attention is drawn to the formula:

$$\dfrac{D_{f,sub}}{D_{f,dry}} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma'} = \dfrac{18}{9} = 2$$

So submerged foundations need to be excavated **twice as deep** to reach the float condition with the same weight and area.

**Why?** Because in submerged soil, the original overburden at depth $D_f$ is *less effective* (submerged soil weighs less per m³), so a greater depth is needed to "compensate" the same structure weight.

*(7 marks: 2 for dry derivation + 2 for submerged derivation + 1 for comparison + 2 for conceptual explanation = 7 marks)*

---

#### Question B(b) [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** A **rectangular raft of 40 m × 20 m** is to be placed at a depth of **5 m** in a saturated clay deposit ($\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³, $c_u = 40$ kN/m², $\phi_u = 0°$). The water table is at the ground surface. The total column load on the raft is **160,000 kN**. Determine:
1. The **net increase in pressure** on the subsoil.
2. Whether the foundation can be classified as a **floating foundation**.
3. The **factor of safety** against bearing failure.

**Take $\gamma_w = 10$ kN/m³.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Submerged unit weight:**
$$\gamma' = 20 - 10 = 10 \;\text{kN/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Average contact pressure:**
$$A = 40 \times 20 = 800 \;\text{m}^2, \quad q_{avg} = \dfrac{160{,}000}{800} = 200 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Net pressure increase on subsoil:**
$$q_{net} = q_{avg} - \gamma' D_f = 200 - (10)(5) = 200 - 50 = 150 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Required depth for full floatation:**
$$D_{f,full} = \dfrac{W}{A \gamma'} = \dfrac{160{,}000}{800 \times 10} = 20 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**
$$D_f = 5 \;\text{m} \ll 20 \;\text{m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{NOT floating, partially compensated.}$$

**Step 6 — Ultimate bearing capacity ($\phi = 0$, rectangular, Skempton with shape factors $S_c = 1 + 0.2 B/L$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_c &= 1 + 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{20}{40} = 1 + 0.1 = 1.1 \\
q_u &= S_c \cdot c_u \cdot N_c = 1.1 \times 40 \times 5.7 = 250.8 \;\text{kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Net ultimate bearing capacity:**
$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma' D_f = 250.8 - 50 = 200.8 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 8 — Factor of safety:**
$$FOS = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{q_{net}} = \dfrac{200.8}{150} \approx 1.34$$

**Step 9 — Conclusion:**
- Foundation is **not floating**.
- Applied net pressure = 150 kN/m².
- Net ultimate BC = 200.8 kN/m².
- FOS = **1.34** (typically required ≥ 3 for normal structures, ≥ 2 for temporary or where settlements are well controlled).
- **Design is unsafe** — increase depth, enlarge raft, or reduce structural load.

*(7 marks: 1 sub γ', 1 q_avg, 1 q_net, 1 Df_full, 1 FOS, 1 BC, 1 conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use $\gamma'$ (submerged unit weight) below the water table.** A vast majority of students lose 2–3 marks by mistakenly using $\gamma_{sat}$ or $\gamma_{dry}$ in the overburden term.
> 2. **Confusing "fully floating" with "shallow raft".** Just because a raft is deep does NOT mean it is floating — the weight comparison must be explicitly done.
> 3. **Mixing up $q_{net}$ and $q_{avg}$.** $q_{avg} = W/A$ is the GROSS contact pressure. $q_{net} = W/A - \gamma D_f$ is the INCREASE in subsoil stress. This is what governs settlement.
> 4. **Neglecting shape factors** in the bearing capacity equation for square/rectangular rafts. Always apply $S_c$, $S_q$, $S_\gamma$.
> 5. **Forgetting to check BOTH bearing capacity AND settlement** — a floating foundation is designed primarily to control settlement; the BC check is a secondary safety verification.
> 6. **Units mismatch:** express $W$ in kN, $A$ in m², $\gamma$ in kN/m³, depth in m → $D_f$ in m. (1 mark lost in careless unit conversion.)

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Floating foundation** = weight of soil excavated = weight of structure (at base level). Condition: $\Delta\sigma = q_{avg} - \gamma' D_f = 0$.
- [ ] **Required depth (dry):** $D_f = W / (A \gamma)$. **Required depth (submerged):** $D_f = W / (A \gamma')$.
- [ ] **Submerged unit weight** $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ must be used everywhere below the water table.
- [ ] **Modified bearing capacity (submerged):** $q_{u(sub)} = c' N_c + \gamma' D_f (N_q - 1) + 0.5 \gamma' B N_\gamma$.
- [ ] **% Compensation** = $(\gamma' D_f A / W) \times 100$. **100 %** = fully floating.
- [ ] **Types:** Compensated Mat, Hollow Box/Pontoon, Submerged Basement, Partial/Semi-floating.
- [ ] **Adopt when:** soft clay/loose silt with very low BC; deep firm stratum; large raft area; piling uneconomical.
- [ ] **Design steps:** (i) Compute $\gamma'$, (ii) Compute $D_{f,full}$, (iii) Compare with provided $D_f$, (iv) Compute $q_{net}$, (v) Compute $q_{a,net}$ using BC + FOS, (vi) Check settlement, (vii) Decide on raft size / weight.
- [ ] **Submerged BC uses $\gamma'$**, NOT $\gamma_{sat}$ or $\gamma_w$ in the surcharge and unit-weight terms.
- [ ] **Heave problem:** If $D_f > D_{f,full}$ → over-compensated → use **tension piles / anchors** to resist uplift.
- [ ] **Net pressure $q_{net}$** (not gross $q_{avg}$) governs **settlement**.
- [ ] **Shape factors** for square/circular rafts must be applied to BC equation.
- [ ] **Real-world use:** Coastal high-rises (Mumbai, Kochi), metro stations, offshore gravity platforms, deep basements.
- [ ] **Critical exam tip:** Always state the assumption (e.g., "water table at GL" or "soil is submerged") **before** writing the formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# MODULE 4: DEEP FOUNDATIONS — PILES
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **pile** is a slender, structural member made of timber, concrete, steel, or composite material, which is driven, drilled, or otherwise installed into the ground to transmit superstructural loads to deeper, stronger soil or rock strata that possess adequate bearing capacity and acceptable settlement characteristics. Piles form a critical sub-class of **deep foundations** where the depth-to-width ratio $L/D > 10$, distinguishing them from shallow footings.

The fundamental purpose of a pile is to bypass weak, compressible surface soils (e.g., soft clays, loose sands, fill) and reach a competent bearing stratum. The loads are transferred through a combination of **shaft resistance (skin friction)** and **point resistance (end bearing)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Root System" of a Building

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a tall coconut tree standing firmly in loose beach sand. The trunk (your superstructure) does not rely on the loose surface sand; instead, its **roots (the piles)** penetrate deep into the firmer, denser soil layer beneath. Similarly, when a multi-storey building is constructed on weak coastal clay, ordinary shallow footings would sink or tilt. **Piles act as artificial roots** — they bypass the weak topsoil and grip onto or rest upon a deeper, competent stratum, transferring the building's weight to the stronger earth.
>
> A useful engineering rule of thumb: if the foundation depth $D_f > 4B$ (where $B$ is the footing width), the system transitions from a *shallow* to a *deep* foundation, and pile-type solutions become applicable.

### 1.3 When Shallow Foundations Fail — The Trigger Conditions

Pile foundations are *not* the default choice. They are employed under specific, well-defined geotechnical triggers:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Triggers Mandating Pile Use (KTU 2024 — Module 4, Unit 1):**
> 1. **Low Bearing Capacity** of surface soil (typically $q_u < 50 \text{ kN/m}^2$).
> 2. **Excessive Settlement Criteria** — when estimated settlement under a shallow footing exceeds the structural tolerance (often $25 \text{ mm}$ for isolated footings, $40 \text{ mm}$ for rafts).
> 3. **Presence of Expansive or Collapsible Soils** (e.g., black cotton soil) where seasonal moisture variation causes swell-shrink cycles.
> 4. **Scour-Prone Beds** — riverbeds, offshore locations where erosion may expose shallow footings.
> 5. **Lateral / Uplift Loads** — from wind, seismic action, hydrostatic pressure, or overturning moments in tall chimneys, transmission towers, and offshore platforms.
> 6. **Construction over Water** — bridge piers, jetties, wharves.
> 7. **Heavy Concentrated Loads** — bridge abutments, heavy industrial machinery, high-rise buildings (typically $N > 12$ storeys).

### 1.4 Standard Pile Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Diameter range**: $D = 0.25 \text{ m}$ to $2.50 \text{ m}$ (bored); smaller for driven ($0.3$ to $0.6 \text{ m}$).
- **Length range**: $L = 5 \text{ m}$ to $90 \text{ m}$+ for offshore.
- **Slenderness ratio**: $L/D$ typically between $10$ and $60$.
- **Specific weight of concrete** (used in self-weight calculations): $\gamma_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$.
- **Specific weight of steel**: $\gamma_s = 78.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$.
