# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Functional Classification of Piles (Based on Load Transfer Mechanism)

This is the single most important classification for KTU numerical problems.

### (a) End-Bearing Piles
- These piles penetrate through weak soil and **rest their tips on a hard, incompressible stratum** (rock, hard clay, dense sand).
- The load is transferred **predominantly through the tip** to the bearing stratum.
- **Analogy:** A pillar standing on a concrete slab — almost all load is delivered through the base.
- **Required condition:** $q_b \gg q_s$ (base resistance $\gg$ shaft resistance).

### (b) Friction Piles (Skin-Friction Piles)
- These piles do *not* reach a hard stratum. They develop load-carrying capacity **entirely through skin friction** along the shaft-soil interface.
- Used where the soft soil extends to a great depth and a firmer layer is uneconomical to reach.
- **Analogy:** A nail driven into wood — pull-out resistance comes from the friction along its sides, not the tip.

### (c) Combined End-Bearing and Friction Piles
- The most common real-world case. The pile derives capacity from **both** components.
- The ultimate load is:
$$Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$$
where $Q_b$ = end bearing, $Q_s$ = skin friction.

### (d) Compaction Piles
- Driven into loose granular soil to **densify** it. They are not designed to carry structural load, but to improve the engineering properties of the surrounding soil by vibration and displacement.

### (e) Tension / Uplift Piles
- Designed to resist **upward (tensile) forces** — e.g., under hydrodynamic uplift on a basement slab, or anchorage of transmission towers.
- The skin friction acts downward; the pile is in tension.

### (f) Anchor Piles
- Resist **horizontal pull-out** (e.g., anchored bulkheads, retaining walls). They work in conjunction with tie-back anchors.

### (g) Dolphin Piles / Fender Piles
- A cluster of piles driven into the seabed to protect bridge piers and wharves from ship impact.

### (h) Sheet Piles
- Interlocking sections of steel, timber, or concrete driven to form a continuous wall — used for **earth retention** and **cut-off** purposes (not strictly load-bearing in the vertical sense).

### (i) Batter Piles
- Piles installed at an **inclination** (typically $1:6$ to $1:3$ horizontal:vertical) to resist large lateral loads.

## 2.2 Classification Based on Material

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Material Classification:**

| Material Type | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|
| **Timber Piles** | Economical, easy to cut, renewable | Vulnerable to decay; max length $\approx 20$ m; below water-table best |
| **Concrete Piles (Precast)** | High capacity, durable, can be inspected | Heavy, require driving rigs, splicing difficult |
| **Concrete (Cast-in-situ / Bored)** | No vibration, customisable depth, larger diameters possible | Hidden defects, requires concrete quality control |
| **Steel Piles** | High capacity, easy to splice, can be driven hard | Corrosion in marine environments, expensive |
| **Composite Piles** | Combines benefits (e.g., concrete + steel H-section) | Joints critical |

## 2.3 Classification Based on Method of Installation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Installation Classification — KTU 2024:**

1. **Driven Piles** — Pre-formed piles (timber, precast concrete, steel) hammered into the ground. *Displacement-type.*
2. **Driven and Cast-in-Place Piles** — A steel shell is driven, then filled with concrete.
3. **Bored (Drilled) and Cast-in-Place Piles** — A borehole is augered, reinforcement placed, concrete poured. *Non-displacement.*
4. **Screw / Auger Piles** — Continuous flight auger (CFA) technique.
5. **Jacked Piles** — Pressed into the ground using hydraulic jacks. Low-vibration method ideal for urban retrofitting.

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks "Classify piles based on function" or "Differentiate between end-bearing and friction piles." Memorise the load-transfer diagrams and the $Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$ equation.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$ | Ultimate pile capacity | $Q_u$ in $\text{kN}$ |
| 2 | $Q_b = A_b \cdot q_b$ | End bearing component | $A_b = \text{area of pile tip (m}^2\text{)}$; $q_b = \text{unit end bearing (kN/m}^2\text{)}$ |
| 3 | $Q_s = \sum (A_{si} \cdot f_{si})$ | Skin friction (shaft resistance) | $A_{si} = \text{lateral surface area of layer } i$; $f_{si} = \text{unit skin friction of layer } i$ |
| 4 | $A_{si} = \pi \cdot D \cdot L_i$ | Lateral area of pile in layer $i$ | $D = \text{pile diameter (m)}$; $L_i = \text{layer thickness (m)}$ |
| 5 | $A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | Tip area (circular pile) | $D$ in metres |
| 6 | $Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{F}$ | Allowable / Safe load | $F = \text{Factor of Safety}$ ($F = 2.5$ to $3$ for driven; $F = 3$ for bored) |
| 7 | $L/D$ | Slenderness ratio (for classification) | $L/D > 10$ ⇒ pile |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Vertical pipe symbols have been replaced with `\vert` / `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This classification system is **not academic** — it is the foundational decision matrix used by:
- **Geotechnical consultants** to recommend the *type* of pile in soil investigation reports.
- **Structural engineers** to size the pile cap and reinforcement.
- **Contractors** to choose plant and equipment (e.g., rotary rig for bored, drop-hammer for driven).
- **Bridge and port engineers** for offshore monopile design (now extending to $D = 10 \text{ m}$ for wind-turbine foundations).
- **Forensic engineers** investigating pile failures (e.g., incorrect classification led to inadequate skin-friction design in the 2010 New Orleans levee post-Katrina assessments).

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Combined End-Bearing and Friction Pile Capacity

This is the **core derivation** that KTU expects in Part B numericals. We derive the ultimate load capacity of a pile embedded in layered soil.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Problem

Consider a circular pile of diameter $D$ and embedded length $L$, penetrating $n$ distinct soil layers. The pile is loaded with an axial compressive force $Q$ at its head.

**Assumptions:**
1. The pile-soil interface behaves as a Mohr-Coulomb material.
2. The load transfer is fully mobilised at ultimate conditions.
3. The base fails in general shear (Terzaghi's bearing capacity analogy).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total Ultimate Capacity (Force Equilibrium)**

The pile transfers the head load to the surrounding soil through two parallel mechanisms acting simultaneously:
- Resistance along the cylindrical pile shaft (skin friction).
- Resistance at the pile tip (end bearing).

By static equilibrium, at the point of incipient failure:
$$Q_u = Q_s + Q_b$$

**Step 2 — End-Bearing Component $Q_b$**

The end-bearing force is the unit tip stress multiplied by the tip's cross-sectional area:
$$Q_b = A_b \cdot q_b$$

For a circular pile:
$$A_b = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$$

Therefore:
$$Q_b = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot q_b$$

The unit end bearing $q_b$ is determined using:
$$q_b = c N_c^* + \sigma_v' N_q^* + 0.5 \gamma D N_\gamma^*$$

where $N_c^*, N_q^*, N_\gamma^*$ are modified bearing capacity factors that account for the deep-failure mechanism (Meyerhof, Vesic, or Coyle & Castello).

**Step 3 — Skin-Friction Component $Q_s$**

Consider an infinitesimal element of the pile shaft of length $dz$ at depth $z$. The lateral surface area is:
$$dA_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot dz$$

The frictional resistance on this element is:
$$dQ_s = f_s \cdot dA_s = f_s \cdot \pi D \cdot dz$$

Integrating over the total embedded length (or summing over discrete layers):
$$Q_s = \int_0^L f_s(z) \cdot \pi D \cdot dz$$

In discrete-layer form, for $n$ soil layers:
$$Q_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_{si} \cdot \pi D \cdot L_i$$

For a **cohesionless** layer (sand):
$$f_s = K \cdot \sigma_v' \cdot \tan \delta$$

where $K$ = coefficient of lateral earth pressure, $\sigma_v' = $ effective vertical stress at the layer mid-depth, $\delta = $ pile-soil interface friction angle.

For a **cohesive** layer (clay), using the $\alpha$-method:
$$f_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c_u}$$

where $\alpha = $ adhesion factor ($\approx 0.4$ to $1.0$, typically $0.5$).

**Step 4 — Combined Equation**

Substituting both components back:
$$Q_u = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_{si} \cdot \pi D L_i}_{\text{Skin Friction}} + \underbrace{\frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot q_b}_{\text{End Bearing}}$$

**Step 5 — Allowable Load with Factor of Safety**
$$Q_a = \frac{Q_u}{F}$$

where $F = 2.5$ for driven piles, $F = 3.0$ for bored piles (KTU 2024 recommended values per IS 2911 Part 1).

### 3.1.3 Complete Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem:**
> A circular bored pile of diameter $D = 0.5 \text{ m}$ and length $L = 12 \text{ m}$ is installed in a soil profile consisting of:
> - Layer 1: Soft clay, thickness $= 6 \text{ m}$, average undrained shear strength $\bar{c_u} = 30 \text{ kN/m}^2$, adhesion factor $\alpha = 0.6$.
> - Layer 2: Dense sand, thickness $= 6 \text{ m}$, average $N = 35$, effective unit weight $\gamma' = 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $K = 1.0$, $\delta = 30°$, $q_b = 5000 \text{ kN/m}^2$.
>
> Calculate the ultimate and allowable load. Take FOS = 3.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Skin friction in soft clay (Layer 1):**
$$f_{s1} = \alpha \cdot \bar{c_u} = 0.6 \times 30 = 18 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
$$Q_{s1} = f_{s1} \cdot \pi D L_1 = 18 \times \pi \times 0.5 \times 6 = 169.65 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Skin friction in dense sand (Layer 2):**

Using $f_{s2} = K \sigma_v' \tan \delta$, with mid-depth effective stress at $z = 9 \text{ m}$ (middle of sand layer, assuming water-table at surface for simplicity):
$$\sigma_v' = 9 \times 10 = 90 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
$$f_{s2} = 1.0 \times 90 \times \tan 30° = 90 \times 0.5774 = 51.96 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
$$Q_{s2} = 51.96 \times \pi \times 0.5 \times 6 = 489.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Total skin friction:**
$$Q_s = 169.65 + 489.7 = 659.35 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — End bearing in dense sand:**
$$Q_b = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot q_b = \frac{\pi \times 0.25}{4} \times 5000 = 981.75 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Ultimate load:**
$$Q_u = Q_s + Q_b = 659.35 + 981.75 = 1641.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Allowable load (FOS = 3):**
$$Q_a = \frac{1641.1}{3} = 547.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Answer:** $Q_u \approx 1641 \text{ kN}$, $Q_a \approx 547 \text{ kN}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pile-Type Selection Decision Chart

| Site Condition | Recommended Pile Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Soft clay over hard rock | End-bearing driven concrete pile | Tip rests on rock |
| Soft clay, no rock within reach | Friction pile (bored) | Develop shaft resistance |
| Loose sand | Bored + grouted or compaction piles | Minimise disturbance, allow densification |
| Marine / offshore | Driven steel tubular pile | Easy splicing, handles wave loads |
| Tight urban site with adjacent structures | Bored (non-displacement) | No vibration damage to neighbours |
| High lateral loads (towers, chimneys) | Battered + vertical pile group | Inclined piles resist horizontal thrust |
| Expansive (black cotton) soil | Under-reamed bored pile | Bulb provides anchorage against uplift |
| Scour-prone riverbed | Driven pile to scour-protected depth | Prevents loss of lateral support |
