# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a pile foundation. State the situations where pile foundations are preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A pile is a slender structural member (timber, concrete, steel) driven or cast into the ground to transmit loads to deeper, stronger strata, with $L/D > 10$.
- **Situations (2 marks — list any four):**
  1. Low bearing capacity of surface soil.
  2. Excessive settlement anticipated.
  3. Expansive or collapsible soils (e.g., black cotton soil).
  4. Heavy concentrated structural loads (high-rise buildings, bridges).
  5. Construction over water (offshore, bridge piers).
  6. Presence of large uplift or lateral loads.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between end-bearing piles and friction piles.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | End-Bearing Pile | Friction Pile |
|---|---|---|
| Load transfer | Through pile tip on hard stratum | Through shaft-soil friction |
| Tip resistance | $Q_b$ is dominant; $Q_b \gg Q_s$ | $Q_s$ is dominant; $Q_b \approx 0$ |
| Stratum | Must rest on rock or hard layer | No hard layer needed |
| Typical length | Short to medium | Long |
| Example | Bridge pier piles on rock | Piles in deep soft clay |

---

## PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 4]**

**(a) Classify piles based on (i) function, and (ii) method of installation. Give two examples for each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Classification by Function (4 marks):**
1. **End-bearing pile** — transmits load through tip to hard stratum (e.g., steel H-pile on rock).
2. **Friction pile** — load carried by skin friction (e.g., precast concrete pile in soft clay).
3. **Compaction pile** — used to densify loose sand (e.g., sand piles, stone columns).
4. **Tension / uplift pile** — resists uplift (e.g., piles under basement of underground tank).
5. **Anchor pile** — resists horizontal pull-out (e.g., tied-back retaining wall).
6. **Sheet pile** — earth retention (e.g., steel sheet pile cofferdam).
7. **Batter pile** — inclined to take lateral loads (e.g., piles in wharves).
8. **Dolphin pile** — berthing impact protection (e.g., jetty fenders).

**(ii) Classification by Method of Installation (3 marks):**
1. **Driven pile** — pre-formed, hammered in (e.g., precast concrete pile).
2. **Bored and cast-in-place pile** — augered then concreted (e.g., rotary bored pile).
3. **Driven and cast-in-place pile** — shell driven, then concreted (e.g., Simplex pile).
4. **Jacked pile** — hydraulic push (e.g., retrofit underpinning).
5. **Screw / CFA pile** — continuous flight auger (e.g., CFA in urban areas).

---

**(b) A concrete pile of 0.45 m diameter is driven through a 12 m thick soft clay layer ($\bar{c_u} = 40 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\alpha = 0.55$) into a medium dense sand layer of 8 m thickness. The average SPT $N$-value in sand is 22, with $\gamma' = 9 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $K = 0.9$, $\delta = 28°$. The unit end-bearing resistance is $q_b = 3500 \text{ kN/m}^2$. Compute the ultimate and safe load (FOS = 2.5). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Skin friction in soft clay:**
$$f_{s1} = \alpha \bar{c_u} = 0.55 \times 40 = 22 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
$$Q_{s1} = f_{s1} \cdot \pi D L_1 = 22 \times \pi \times 0.45 \times 12 = 373.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Skin friction in sand:**

Mid-depth of sand layer = $12 + 4 = 16 \text{ m}$ below ground.

Effective vertical stress at mid-depth (water-table at surface):
$$\sigma_v' = 16 \times 9 = 144 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
$$f_{s2} = K \sigma_v' \tan \delta = 0.9 \times 144 \times \tan 28° = 0.9 \times 144 \times 0.5317 = 68.91 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
$$Q_{s2} = 68.91 \times \pi \times 0.45 \times 8 = 779.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Total skin friction:**
$$Q_s = 373.4 + 779.2 = 1152.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — End bearing:**
$$Q_b = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot q_b = \frac{\pi \times 0.2025}{4} \times 3500 = 556.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Ultimate load:**
$$Q_u = Q_s + Q_b = 1152.6 + 556.3 = 1708.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Safe load (FOS = 2.5):**
$$Q_a = \frac{1708.9}{2.5} = 683.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_u = 1709 \text{ kN}$, $Q_a = 683.6 \text{ kN}$.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) Explain the various uses of pile foundations with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **To transfer heavy vertical loads to a deeper bearing stratum** — High-rise buildings, bridge piers, industrial structures.
2. **To resist uplift and tension forces** — Transmission towers, basement slabs below water-table, anchorages for tied-back retaining walls.
3. **To resist horizontal / lateral loads** — Wharves, jetties, offshore platforms, chimneys. *Batter piles are used here.*
4. **To reduce settlement on weak soils** — Friction piles in deep soft clay prevent excessive consolidation settlement.
5. **To provide anchorage against overturning moments** — Tall chimneys, gantry structures, cable-stayed bridges.
6. **Machine foundations** — Piles isolate vibrations from surrounding structures.
7. **To compact loose granular soils** — Compaction piles densify loose sand and increase $N$-values.
8. **Offshore construction** — Monopile foundations for wind turbines, oil platforms.
9. **Scour protection in riverbeds** — Piles driven below the maximum scour depth to maintain lateral support.
10. **As a safeguard in expansive soils** — Under-reamed piles resist the swell-shrink behaviour of black cotton soil.

**(b) A group of 9 piles (3 × 3) is arranged with centre-to-centre spacing of 3 pile diameters. Each pile is 0.5 m diameter and 15 m long, driven into clay with $\bar{c_u} = 50 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\alpha = 0.7$. Compute the group capacity. Comment on whether the group acts as a block or individual piles. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Single pile capacity:**

End bearing (assuming pile tip is in clay, $q_b \approx 9 c_u$ for undrained):
$$q_b = 9 \times 50 = 450 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
$$Q_b = \frac{\pi (0.5)^2}{4} \times 450 = 88.36 \text{ kN}$$

Skin friction:
$$f_s = 0.7 \times 50 = 35 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
$$Q_s = 35 \times \pi \times 0.5 \times 15 = 824.7 \text{ kN}$$

Single pile $Q_u = 88.36 + 824.7 = 913.06 \text{ kN}$
Single pile $Q_a = 913.06 / 3 = 304.4 \text{ kN}$

**Step 2 — Sum of individual piles:**
$$\sum Q_a = 9 \times 304.4 = 2740 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Group acting as a block (perimeter = 3 × 3D = 3 × 3 × 0.5 = 4.5 m):**
$$Q_{b,block} = 9 c_u A_{block} = 9 \times 50 \times (3D \times 3D) = 9 \times 50 \times 1.5 \times 1.5 = 1012.5 \text{ kN}$$
$$Q_{s,block} = \alpha \bar{c_u} \times \text{Perimeter} \times L = 0.7 \times 50 \times (4 \times 1.5) \times 15 = 0.7 \times 50 \times 6 \times 15 = 3150 \text{ kN}$$
$$Q_{u,block} = 1012.5 + 3150 = 4162.5 \text{ kN}$$
$$Q_{a,block} = 4162.5 / 3 = 1387.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Comment:**
The block capacity ($1387.5 \text{ kN}$) is less than the sum of individual piles ($2740 \text{ kN}$). Hence, the **group acts as a block**, and the block capacity governs the design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert area units** — $A_b$ must be in m² and $q_b$ in kN/m². A common error yields $Q_b$ that is off by a factor of 10.
> 2. **Confusing $\alpha$-method with $\beta$-method** — Use $\alpha \bar{c_u}$ for clays, $K \sigma_v' \tan \delta$ for sands. Do NOT mix them.
> 3. **Using total stress in sand** — Always use *effective* stress $\sigma_v'$ in cohesionless soils.
> 4. **Forgetting mid-depth for sand** — When computing $\sigma_v'$, use the mid-depth of the sand layer, not the pile tip depth.
> 5. **Pile group trap** — In a closely spaced pile group ($s < 3D$), the group may fail as a *block*. Always check both individual and block failure modes.
> 6. **Wrong FOS** — Driven piles use FOS = 2.5; bored piles use FOS = 3.0. Examiners specifically check this.
> 7. **Units of $N$ vs $c_u$** — $N$ is dimensionless blows/ft; $c_u$ is kN/m². They are NOT interchangeable.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Uses and Classification of Piles**

- **Definition:** A pile is a slender deep-foundation element with $L/D > 10$, transferring load to deeper strata.
- **Primary trigger for pile use:** weak surface soil OR heavy load OR uplift/lateral demand.
- **Three main classifications to memorise:**
  1. **By Function** — end-bearing, friction, combined, compaction, tension, anchor, sheet, dolphin, batter.
  2. **By Material** — timber, concrete (precast/bored), steel, composite, FRP.
  3. **By Installation** — driven, driven-and-cast, bored, CFA, jacked.
- **Master Equation:** $Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$, where $Q_b = (\pi D^2 / 4) \cdot q_b$ and $Q_s = \sum f_{si} \cdot \pi D L_i$.
- **Cohesive skin friction:** $f_s = \alpha \bar{c_u}$ (typically $\alpha = 0.4$–$1.0$).
- **Cohesionless skin friction:** $f_s = K \sigma_v' \tan \delta$.
- **Allowable load:** $Q_a = Q_u / F$, with $F = 2.5$ (driven) or $F = 3.0$ (bored).
- **Group effect:** For closely spaced piles ($s \leq 3D$), check block failure.
- **Always use effective stress in sands**, total stress in clays.
- **Mid-depth rule:** When integrating $f_s$ over a sand layer, evaluate $\sigma_v'$ at the *mid-depth* of the layer.
- **Batter piles** are inclined piles used for lateral load resistance.
- **Compaction piles** are *not* load-bearing; they densify the surrounding soil.
- **Sheet piles** are interlocking walls used for *retention*, not for carrying vertical structural loads.
- **Concrete's unit weight** (for self-weight): $\gamma_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$; **Steel:** $\gamma_s = 78.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$.
- **Bored piles** are *non-displacement*; **driven piles** are *displacement* — this affects soil-structure interaction and adjacent structures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Type and Length of Piles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection of Type and Length of Piles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pile**: A slender, columnar structural element (typically with a length-to-diameter ratio $\gt 10$) made of timber, concrete, steel, or composite materials, which is either **driven**, **drilled and cast**, or **jacked** into the ground to transfer superstructural loads to deeper, competent soil or rock strata possessing adequate bearing capacity and stiffness.

**Selection of Pile Type** is the engineering decision-making process wherein the most appropriate pile material, installation method, and geometry are chosen based on the **loading conditions**, **subsurface soil profile**, **groundwater regime**, **site accessibility**, and **economic constraints**.

**Selection of Pile Length** is the analytical process of determining the minimum embedment depth required to develop sufficient **end-bearing resistance ($Q_b$)** and **skin-friction resistance ($Q_s$)** such that the allowable load-carrying capacity ($Q_{all}$) of the pile satisfies the factor of safety requirements while accounting for **scour**, **negative skin friction**, and **soft compressible layer** considerations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Anchor in a Swimming Pool" Analogy:**
>
> Imagine you want to tie a heavy boat (the building) floating in a swimming pool (weak, soft soil) without it drifting away. The mooring rope is your **pile**. If the pool has a shallow concrete floor close by, you can use a **short, thick post** (end-bearing pile) hammered down to touch the floor. But if the pool is very deep, you must use a **very long rope** (friction pile) that grips the water along its entire length, generating resistance through friction with the pool walls.
>
> The **type** of anchor is your pile type (wood, concrete, steel), and the **length** is how deep you must embed it to safely hold the boat against storm forces. Engineers choose the anchor type based on water depth, boat weight, and seabed material—exactly as we choose pile type and length based on soil, load, and groundwater.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Constants and Metrics

- **Standard Unit Weight of Concrete**: $\gamma_c = \mathbf{24 \text{ kN/m}^3}$
- **Unit Weight of Steel**: $\gamma_s = \mathbf{78.5 \text{ kN/m}^3}$
- **Minimum Factor of Safety (F.S.) for Piles**: $\mathbf{2.5}$ (IS 2911 Part 1) for driven piles; $\mathbf{3.0}$ for bored piles
- **Typical Pile Diameter Range**: $\mathbf{300 \text{ mm}}$ to $\mathbf{1500 \text{ mm}}$
- **Typical Pile Length Range**: $\mathbf{5 \text{ m}}$ to $\mathbf{60 \text{ m}}$ (sometimes exceeding **100 m** for offshore)

## 1.4 Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 4 Learning Outcomes:**
> 1. Identify the **type of pile** to be adopted for a given field condition.
> 2. Estimate the **length of pile** required to transfer the design load to a competent stratum.
> 3. Apply code-prescribed minimum embedment, F.S., and load transfer principles as per **IS 2911** and **IS 14593**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Pile Type Selection

The selection is a multi-criteria decision. The following hierarchical logic governs the engineer's choice:

### Step 1 — Load Magnitude Classification
- **Light Loads (< 500 kN)**: Timber piles, short precast concrete piles, small-bore bored piles
- **Medium Loads (500–2000 kN)**: Precast RCC piles, driven cast-in-situ piles, H-piles
- **Heavy Loads (> 2000 kN)**: Large bored cast-in-situ piles, heavy steel pipe piles, barrettes

### Step 2 — Soil Profile Assessment
- **Soft clay over hard rock**: End-bearing bored piles (preferred) or precast driven piles with shoe
- **Loose sand over dense sand**: Friction driven precast or H-piles
- **Soft clay of large depth**: Long friction piles (timber or RCC) or under-reamed piles
- **Filled-up ground**: Driven cast-in-situ piles (Franki piles) preferred

### Step 3 — Groundwater Table (GWT) Considerations
- **High GWT** → Concrete piles preferred (steel piles corrode)
- **Fluctuating GWT** → Avoid timber (rotting); use concrete or coated steel
- **Marine/brackish water** → Use sulfate-resistant cement concrete; protective coatings on steel

### Step 4 — Installation Constraints
- **Restricted headroom** (inside existing buildings): Bored piles or jacked piles
- **Vibration-sensitive zones** (near heritage structures, hospitals): Bored piles (no driving)
- **Noisy zones** (urban): Bored or jacked piles preferred
- **Limited access for heavy rigs**: Mini-piles or hand-dug caissons

### Step 5 — Cost & Durability
- Timber: Cheapest, but durability is **20–30 years**
- Concrete: Moderate cost, durability **> 75 years**
- Steel: Higher cost, durability **25–50 years** (extendable with coatings)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are validated as per **IS 2911 (Part 1/Section 1) – 2010** and standard textbook practice (Murthy, Bowles, Das).

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Applicable Soil** |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | Ultimate Pile Capacity | $Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s}$ | kN | All soils |
| 2 | Allowable Pile Load | $Q_{all} = \dfrac{Q_{u}}{F.S.}$ | kN | All soils |
| 3 | End Bearing in Cohesionless Soil | $Q_{b} = A_{b} \cdot q'_{b} = A_{b} \cdot \sigma'_{vb} \cdot N_{q}^{*}$ | kN | Sand |
| 4 | End Bearing in Cohesive Soil | $Q_{b} = A_{b} \cdot c_{u} \cdot N_{c}^{*}$ | kN | Clay |
| 5 | Skin Friction – α Method (Clay) | $Q_{s} = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P \cdot L$ | kN | Clay |
| 6 | Skin Friction – β Method (Sand) | $Q_{s} = K \cdot \bar{\sigma}'_{v} \cdot \tan \delta \cdot P \cdot L$ | kN | Sand |
| 7 | Skin Friction – λ Method (Mixed) | $Q_{s} = \lambda \cdot \left(\bar{\sigma}'_{v} + 2 \cdot \bar{c}\right) \cdot P \cdot L$ | kN | Mixed |
| 8 | Required Length of Pile (Friction-dominated, Clay) | $L = \dfrac{Q_{all} \cdot F.S. - Q_{b}}{\alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P}$ | m | Clay |
| 9 | Required Length of Pile (Sand) | $L = \dfrac{Q_{all} \cdot F.S. - Q_{b}}{f_{s,avg} \cdot P}$ | m | Sand |
| 10 | Embedment into Rock (End Bearing) | $L_{embed} = 2D \text{ to } 3D$ | m | Rock |
| 11 | Scour-Adjusted Length (Bridges) | $L_{design} = L_{static} + L_{scour}$ | m | Riverbed |
| 12 | Negative Skin Friction Adjustment | $Q_{nf} = \beta \cdot P \cdot L_{n}$ (subtracted from capacity) | kN | Fill on clay |
| 13 | Group Efficiency (n piles) | $\eta_{g} = \dfrac{\text{Capacity of group}}{n \cdot \text{Capacity of single pile}}$ | — | All |
| 14 | Min. Spacing for Driven Piles | $S_{min} = 2.5D \text{ to } 3D$ | m | All |
| 15 | Min. Spacing for Bored Piles | $S_{min} = 2D$ | m | All |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $A_b$ = pile base area, $P$ = pile perimeter, $L$ = pile length in bearing stratum, $D$ = pile diameter, $\alpha$ = adhesion factor, $K$ = lateral earth pressure coefficient, $\delta$ = pile–soil interface friction angle, $\bar{c}$ = average cohesion, $\bar{\sigma}'_v$ = average effective vertical stress, $N_q^{*}, N_c^{*}$ = bearing capacity factors.

## 2.3 Why Each Step Matters — Real-World Utility

- **Why F.S. = 2.5 for driven and 3.0 for bored?** Driven piles have the advantage of soil compaction and pre-stressing the surrounding soil, which is absent in bored piles where stress relief occurs; hence higher F.S. is mandated.
- **Why embedment of 2D–3D in rock?** This ensures the pile does not behave as a column buckling about an unrestrained toe; the rock confines the base sufficiently to mobilize full end-bearing.
- **Why scour adjustment?** In bridge piers, floods can remove overburden, exposing the pile laterally and increasing the unsupported length; neglecting scour causes catastrophic buckling failures (e.g., the 1987 Schoharie Creek Bridge collapse, USA).

## 2.4 Comparative Pile Type Selection Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **frequently asked in KTU exams** as a 7–8 mark comparison question.

| **Criterion** | **Timber** | **Precast Concrete** | **Cast-in-Situ Bored** | **Driven Cast-in-Situ (Franki)** | **Steel H-Pile** | **Steel Pipe Pile** |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Max. Load | Low–Med | Med–High | High | High | High | Very High |
| Vibration | Moderate | High | Negligible | High | High | High |
| Noise | Moderate | High | Low | High | High | High |
| GWT Effect | Rot | Negligible | Negligible | Negligible | Corrodes | Corrodes |
| Suitable Soil | Soft clay | All except boulders | All incl. rock | Sand & soft rock | Dense strata | Any |
| Quality Control | Good | Excellent | Moderate (depends on workmanship) | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| Cost (Relative) | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 1.7 | 2.5 | 3.0 |
| Length Adjustment | Limited | Limited (spliced) | Flexible (cast to depth) | Flexible (cast to depth) | Spliced easily | Spliced easily |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Fundamental Pile Length Derivation (Clay – End Bearing + Skin Friction)

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Design Equation

> The **ultimate load capacity** of a single pile is the sum of the end-bearing resistance at its toe and the skin friction developed along its shaft. The design length is computed by equating the design load (multiplied by F.S.) to this ultimate capacity and solving for the unknown embedment length $L$.

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the fundamental ultimate capacity equation:

$$
Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Express end bearing in clay (using undrained shear strength $c_u$ and bearing capacity factor $N_c^{*}$):

$$
Q_{b} = A_{b} \cdot c_{u} \cdot N_{c}^{*}
$$

**Step 3:** Express skin friction in clay using the $\alpha$-method (where $\alpha$ is the adhesion factor depending on $c_u$):

$$
Q_{s} = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P \cdot L
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute (2) and (3) into (1):

$$
Q_{u} = A_{b} \cdot c_{u} \cdot N_{c}^{*} + \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P \cdot L
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the Factor of Safety to get the allowable capacity:

$$
Q_{all} = \dfrac{Q_{u}}{F.S.} = \dfrac{A_{b} \cdot c_{u} \cdot N_{c}^{*} + \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P \cdot L}{F.S.}
$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to express $Q_{all}$ in terms of $L$:

$$
Q_{all} \cdot F.S. = A_{b} \cdot c_{u} \cdot N_{c}^{*} + \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P \cdot L
$$

**Step 7:** Isolate the term containing $L$:

$$
\alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P \cdot L = Q_{all} \cdot F.S. - A_{b} \cdot c_{u} \cdot N_{c}^{*}
$$

**Step 8:** Solve for the required length $L$:

$$
L = \dfrac{Q_{all} \cdot F.S. - A_{b} \cdot c_{u} \cdot N_{c}^{*}}{\alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P}
$$

**Step 9:** Add scour depth (if applicable for river/marine piles) to obtain the **total design length**:

$$
L_{design} = L + L_{scour}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical conversion check:** The numerator has units of [kN], the denominator has units of [kN/m], so the resulting $L$ has units of [m] ✓.

### 3.1.3 Special Case: Pure Friction Pile (Soft Clay, No End Bearing)

When the pile is embedded in homogeneous soft clay with no firm stratum, end bearing is neglected ($Q_b = 0$):

$$
L = \dfrac{Q_{all} \cdot F.S.}{\alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P}
$$

### 3.1.4 Special Case: Pure End-Bearing Pile on Rock

When a pile rests on sound rock, skin friction may be neglected and the embedment length is governed by **structural anchorage**:

$$
L_{embed,rock} = 2D \text{ to } 3D
$$

This ensures full confinement of the pile toe, preventing punch-through or column-buckling failure.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> A precast concrete pile of **circular cross-section**, **diameter $D = 500 \text{ mm}$**, is to be driven through **soft clay** of average undrained cohesion $\bar{c} = 30 \text{ kN/m}^2$ and terminated in a **dense sand layer** of $c_u = 120 \text{ kN/m}^2$ at the base. The adhesion factor is $\alpha = 0.7$ and $N_c^{*} = 9$. The pile must carry an **allowable working load of 1000 kN**.
>
> **Determine:**
> (a) The **length of pile** required in the soft clay layer, assuming a factor of safety of **2.5**.
> (b) The **total length** of the pile if the soft clay thickness above the dense sand is **15 m** and the embedment into the dense sand should be at least **3 times the pile diameter**.
> (c) Comment on the **type of pile** most suitable for the given conditions.

### 3.2.2 Solution

#### Part (a): Length in Soft Clay

**Step 1:** Compute geometric properties.

$$
A_{b} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.5)^{2} = 0.1963 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
P = \pi \cdot D = \pi \cdot 0.5 = 1.5708 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute end bearing capacity at the pile toe (in the dense sand stratum).

$$
Q_{b} = A_{b} \cdot c_{u} \cdot N_{c}^{*} = 0.1963 \cdot 120 \cdot 9 = 212.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the pile length equation for clay.

$$
L = \dfrac{Q_{all} \cdot F.S. - Q_{b}}{\alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
L = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 2.5 - 212.0}{0.7 \cdot 30 \cdot 1.5708}
$$

$$
L = \dfrac{2500 - 212.0}{32.99} = \dfrac{2288.0}{32.99}
$$

$$
\boxed{L = 69.36 \text{ m}}
$$

#### Part (b): Total Pile Length

**Step 5:** Add the soft clay thickness (15 m) and the required embedment into dense sand (3D).

$$
L_{embed,sand} = 3D = 3 \cdot 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_{total} = L_{clay} + L_{embed,sand} = 69.36 + 1.5 = 70.86 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{total} \approx 71 \text{ m}}
$$

#### Part (c): Pile Type Selection Comment

Given the very long required length (~71 m) and the dense sand stratum at depth, the most suitable types are:

1. **Driven precast concrete piles with splicing** — practical up to 60 m; segmental splicing extends it to 80+ m.
2. **Bored cast-in-situ piles** — length can be cast in-situ to any depth; vibration-free, ideal for dense sand.
3. **Steel H-piles** — easily spliced, can be driven to great depths; costlier but faster installation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Recommendation:** **Bored cast-in-situ RCC pile** with a mechanical cleaning of the base (to ensure end bearing in the dense sand) is the optimum choice for this site.

---

## 3.3 Negative Skin Friction (Down-Drag) — Length Adjustment

When a fill is placed over a soft clay layer, the fill consolidates the clay, which settles relative to the pile. This generates **downward drag**, adding load to the pile. The net available capacity reduces:

$$
Q_{net} = Q_{u} - Q_{nf} = (Q_{b} + Q_{s}) - (\beta \cdot P \cdot L_{n})
$$

where $L_n$ is the depth of the consolidating layer. Designers must **either**:

- Coat the pile over the negative-skin-friction zone with bitumen (to eliminate drag), **or**
- Increase the pile length and base area to compensate.

---

## 3.4 Scour Depth Adjustment (Bridge & Marine Piles)

For piles in riverbeds or marine environments, the maximum expected scour depth $L_{scour}$ (computed from Lacey's formula or IS 7784) must be added to the structural length:

$$
L_{design} = L_{static} + L_{scour} + L_{fixity}
$$

where $L_{fixity}$ is the depth below the scour line required to develop lateral fixity (typically **5D to 6D** for soft clays, **3D to 4D** for sands).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Flowchart — Pile Type Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Project Requirements"] --> B["Magnitude of Working Load?"]
    B -- "Light load less than 500 kN" --> C["Soft soil profile?"]
    B -- "Medium load 500 to 2000 kN" --> D["Vibration/noise restrictions?"]
    B -- "Heavy load greater than 2000 kN" --> E["Soil profile at depth?"]
    
    C -- "Yes" --> C1["TIMBER PILE"]
    C -- "No" --> C2["SHORT PRECAST RCC"]
    
    D -- "Yes" --> D1["BORED CAST IN SITU PILE"]
    D -- "No" --> D2["PRECAST RCC or H PILE"]
    
    E -- "Soft clay over hard rock" --> E1["BORED CAST IN SITU PILE with rock socketing"]
    E -- "Loose sand over dense sand" --> E2["H PILE or driven concrete"]
    E -- "Highly variable strata" --> E3["DRIVEN CAST IN SITU FRANKI PILE"]
    
    C1 --> F["Verify F.S. and length"]
    C2 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G["DESIGN COMPLETE"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pile Load Transfer Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["SUPERSTRUCTURE LOAD Q_working"] --> B["PILE CAP"]
    B --> C["SINGLE PILE in GROUP"]
    
    C -- "Top portion" --> D1["NEGATIVE SKIN FRICTION Q_nf (if fill on clay)"]
    C -- "Middle portion" --> D2["NEUTRAL POINT: zero relative displacement"]
    C -- "Lower portion" --> D3["POSITIVE SKIN FRICTION Q_s"]
    C -- "Base / Toe" --> D4["END BEARING Q_b"]
    
    D1 --> E1["DOWNWARD drag, adds to load"]
    D3 --> E2["UPWARD resistance, supports load"]
    D4 --> E3["BEARING on firm stratum"]
    
    E1 --> F["NET CAPACITY: Q_u = Q_b + Q_s - Q_nf"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    
    F --> G{"Q_u / F.S. greater than or equal to Q_working?"}
    G -- "YES" --> H["DESIGN ACCEPTABLE"]
    G -- "NO" --> I["INCREASE LENGTH or DIAMETER"]
    I --> F
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Length Components of a Pile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PILE["PILE LENGTH COMPONENTS"] --> P1["L1: Scour depth (riverbed only)"]
    PILE --> P2["L2: Embedment above bearing stratum (skin friction zone)"]
    PILE --> P3["L3: Embedment into bearing stratum (end bearing zone = 2D to 3D)"]
    PILE --> P4["L4: Pile cap recess and projection above ground"]
    
    P1 --> P5["L_total = L1 + L2 + L3 + L4"]
    P2 --> P5
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Pile–Soil Interaction Length Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUB["SOIL PROFILE FROM TOP TO BOTTOM"] --> Z1["ZONE A: Loose/Compressible Fill (Negative skin friction, drag-down)"]
    Z1 --> Z2["ZONE B: Soft Clay (Positive skin friction develops)"]
    Z2 --> Z3["NEUTRAL PLANE (Net displacement = 0)"]
    Z3 --> Z4["ZONE C: Stiff Clay / Dense Sand (Maximum positive skin friction)"]
    Z4 --> Z5["ZONE D: Bearing Stratum - Rock/Dense Sand (End bearing Q_b)"]
    
    Z1 -.->|"Q_nf" | N1["Drag reduces net capacity"]
    Z2 -.->|"Q_s1" | N2["Frictional resistance"]
    Z4 -.->|"Q_s2" | N3["Frictional resistance"]
    Z5 -.->|"Q_b" | N4["End bearing resistance"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Model answers are written in exam-ready format with 3–4 precise points.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List any four factors governing the selection of type of pile for a given site."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Magnitude and type of working load** — light loads favour timber or precast RCC, heavy loads favour bored cast-in-situ or steel pipe piles.
2. **Subsurface soil profile and depth of firm stratum** — hard rock at shallow depth favours end-bearing bored piles; deep soft clay favours long friction piles.
3. **Groundwater table position and chemical aggressiveness** — high GWT and chemical attack favour concrete; fluctuating GWT forbids timber.
4. **Site accessibility and environmental restrictions** — limited headroom or vibration-sensitive zones restrict driven piles and favour bored or jacked piles.

*(4 factors × 1 mark = 4 marks; any 4 correct factors yield full credit)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define the term 'length of pile'. How is it decided in the case of piles resting on rock?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The length of a pile is the total embedment depth of the pile below the cut-off level, including the portion in the bearing stratum required to develop the design capacity.

**For piles resting on rock:** The length is governed by the depth of sound rock plus a **minimum embedment of 2D to 3D** (where D = pile diameter) into the rock to ensure full confinement, prevent punch-through failure, and develop the structural anchorage. The skin friction contribution in the overlying soil is added on top of this embedment length to obtain the total pile length.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry 14 marks, split typically as **Part (a) = 7 marks** (concept/theory) and **Part (b) = 7 marks** (numerical/derivation). Two full alternative questions are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 — Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the various **factors that govern the selection of pile type** for a foundation. Compare the suitability of **bored cast-in-situ piles** and **driven precast concrete piles** under six specific criteria.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **driven precast concrete pile** of **400 mm diameter** is to be installed through a **soft clay layer** (thickness 12 m, $\bar{c} = 25 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\alpha = 0.8$) and founded in a **medium dense sand** with $c_u = 90 \text{ kN/m}^2$ and $N_c^{*} = 9$. The pile must carry a **working load of 800 kN** with **F.S. = 2.5**. Determine:
   1. The **length of pile in the soft clay layer**.
   2. The **embedment into the sand** for full end-bearing.
   3. The **total length of pile**.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks):**

**Factors governing pile type selection (4 marks):**
1. **Load magnitude and type** (static vs dynamic, axial vs lateral)
2. **Soil profile** at the site (depth of firm strata, presence of boulders, cavities)
3. **Groundwater table** and water chemistry (corrosion potential)
4. **Site constraints** (headroom, vibration limits, noise ordinances, access)
5. **Durability requirements** and design life
6. **Cost and availability** of materials and equipment
7. **Adjacent structures** and risk of damage during installation

**Comparison of Bored vs Driven Precast (3 marks):**

| **Criterion** | **Bored Cast-in-Situ** | **Driven Precast RCC** |
|---|---|---|
| **Installation** | Drilling + tremie concreting | Hammering pre-cast segments |
| **Vibration/Noise** | Negligible | High |
| **Suitable Soil** | All soils including rock with socket | All except very hard strata with boulders |
| **Length Flexibility** | Cast to any depth in single pour | Splicing required for > 12 m |
| **Quality Control** | Depends on workmanship | Excellent (factory cast) |
| **Cost** | Higher | Lower for short to medium piles |
| **Ground Heave** | None | Possible (affects adjacent piles) |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Geometric Properties**

$$
A_{b} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.4)^{2} = 0.1257 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
P = \pi \cdot 0.4 = 1.2566 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: End Bearing Capacity**

$$
Q_{b} = A_{b} \cdot c_{u} \cdot N_{c}^{*} = 0.1257 \cdot 90 \cdot 9 = 101.79 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Required Length in Soft Clay**

$$
L = \dfrac{Q_{all} \cdot F.S. - Q_{b}}{\alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P}
$$

$$
L = \dfrac{800 \cdot 2.5 - 101.79}{0.8 \cdot 25 \cdot 1.2566}
$$

$$
L = \dfrac{2000 - 101.79}{25.13} = \dfrac{1898.21}{25.13}
$$

$$
L = 75.52 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Embedment in Sand (for Full End-Bearing)**

$$
L_{embed} = 3D = 3 \cdot 0.4 = 1.2 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5: Total Pile Length**

$$
L_{total} = L_{clay} + L_{embed} = 75.52 + 1.2 = 76.72 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{total} \approx 76.7 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake 1:**
> Students often forget to **subtract $Q_b$** from the numerator and end up computing an excessively large length. Always check whether end-bearing is included. For piles terminating on a hard stratum, $Q_b$ must be deducted to avoid over-estimation of $L$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake 2:**
> Some students compute $L_{clay}$ greater than the available soft clay thickness (e.g., 12 m here), which is physically impossible. The governing length is $\min(L_{clay,\text{required}}, L_{clay,\text{available}})$. If the required length exceeds the available layer, the pile must be extended into the sand for additional skin friction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **load transfer mechanism** of a single pile in a homogeneous clay deposit. Discuss the role of the **neutral plane** and **negative skin friction** in determining the effective length of the pile.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **bored cast-in-situ pile** of **600 mm diameter** is to be embedded in **soft clay** with $\bar{c} = 40 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\alpha = 0.6$, and the **pile toe is to be seated in a hard rock** at a depth of **20 m** below the ground surface. The maximum anticipated **scour depth is 2.5 m**. The pile is required to carry a **working load of 1500 kN** with **F.S. = 3.0**. The unit weight of clay is $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$. Compute:
   1. The **ultimate skin friction capacity**.
   2. The **minimum rock socket length** required.
   3. The **total pile length** including scour allowance.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks):**

**Load Transfer in Homogeneous Clay (4 marks):**
1. As the pile is loaded, the **pile head** carries the full applied load $Q$.
2. The load is transferred to the surrounding soil **progressively** along the shaft via **shear stress $\tau_s = \alpha \cdot c$** (skin friction).
3. The portion of the load remaining at the toe is resisted by **end bearing $Q_b$**.
4. The **load transfer curve** is non-linear; the upper portion of the pile mobilizes maximum skin friction while the lower portion has reduced relative displacement and therefore lower friction.

**Role of Neutral Plane and Negative Skin Friction (3 marks):**
- The **neutral plane** is the depth at which the relative displacement between the pile and soil is zero. Above it, the soil settles more than the pile, generating **negative skin friction (down-drag)** that **adds** to the pile load. Below it, the pile settles more than the soil, generating **positive skin friction** that **resists** the load.
- For effective length design, the portion of the pile subjected to negative skin friction **does not contribute** to the load capacity and must be **either coated** (e.g., bitumen) or the pile must be lengthened to compensate for the drag load.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Geometric Properties**

$$
P = \pi \cdot D = \pi \cdot 0.6 = 1.885 \text{ m}
$$

$$
A_{b} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.6)^{2} = 0.2827 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Ultimate Skin Friction Capacity**

$$
Q_{s} = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P \cdot L_{clay}
$$

The pile is in clay for 20 m (depth to rock), but scour removes 2.5 m of lateral support. The effective length for skin friction is taken as the depth of clay minus the scour zone, as the upper 2.5 m is unreliable under flood conditions:

$$
L_{eff} = 20 - 2.5 = 17.5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
Q_{s} = 0.6 \cdot 40 \cdot 1.885 \cdot 17.5 = 791.7 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{s} = 791.7 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 3: Required End Bearing for F.S. = 3.0**

$$
Q_{b,req} = Q_{all} \cdot F.S. - Q_{s} = 1500 \cdot 3.0 - 791.7 = 4500 - 791.7
$$

$$
Q_{b,req} = 3708.3 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4: Rock Socket Length**

For a rock socket, the end-bearing pressure is conservatively taken as $q_{b,rock} = 3000 \text{ kN/m}^2$ (for moderately weak rock, IS 14593). Solving:

$$
A_{b,req} = \dfrac{Q_{b,req}}{q_{b,rock}} = \dfrac{3708.3}{3000} = 1.236 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

But the pile has $A_b = 0.2827 \text{ m}^2$. Therefore, we must increase the diameter or extend into the rock. The required **socket length** to mobilize $Q_{b,req}$ via base resistance alone (using $A_b = 0.2827 \text{ m}^2$):

$$
q_{b,req} = \dfrac{Q_{b,req}}{A_{b}} = \dfrac{3708.3}{0.2827} = 13119 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

This exceeds typical rock bearing capacity. Hence, we rely on the **rock socket side resistance** (bond stress) to share the load. Using bond stress $\tau_{bond} = 700 \text{ kN/m}^2$:

$$
Q_{bond} = \tau_{bond} \cdot P \cdot L_{socket}
$$

$$
L_{socket} = \dfrac{Q_{b,req}}{\tau_{bond} \cdot P} = \dfrac{3708.3}{700 \cdot 1.885} = 2.81 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{socket} = 2.81 \text{ m} \approx 3.0 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 5: Total Pile Length**

$$
L_{total} = L_{clay} + L_{socket} + L_{scour} = 20 + 3.0 + 2.5
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{total} = 25.5 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake 3:**
> Students often forget to **include the scour depth in the total pile length**. Scour removes lateral confinement from the upper portion of the pile; the pile must extend through the scour zone, otherwise the unsupported length increases, risking **buckling failure** under axial load.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake 4:**
> For **bored piles**, the F.S. must be **3.0 (not 2.5)**, as per IS 2911. Using 2.5 will lead to under-design and loss of marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways — Must Remember for KTU Exam:**

- **Pile Type Selection is governed by**: load magnitude, soil profile, GWT, site access, cost, durability, and environmental restrictions.
- **Pile Length Selection is governed by**: required capacity $Q_{all}$, F.S. (= 2.5 driven, 3.0 bored), $Q_b$ at toe, $Q_s$ along shaft.
- **F.S. values (IS 2911)**: Driven = 2.5; Bored = 3.0; Tension piles = 3.0; Piles on rock = 3.0.
- **End-bearing in rock requires minimum embedment = 2D to 3D** of the pile diameter.
- **Negative skin friction** occurs when soil settles more than pile; it adds drag load and reduces net capacity.
- **Scour depth** must always be added to total pile length in riverbed/marine piles.
- **Soft compressible layer** at top requires either coating the pile (bitumen) or extending its length to mobilize capacity below the consolidating zone.
- **Pure friction pile equation (clay)**: $L = \dfrac{Q_{all} \cdot F.S.}{\alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P}$ (with $Q_b = 0$).
- **Pile–soil interface friction angle $\delta$** in sand is typically taken as $(2/3) \phi$ to $\phi$ for concrete piles; $0.7\phi$ to $0.9\phi$ for steel piles.
- **Adhesion factor $\alpha$** for clay piles:
  - $\alpha = 1.0$ for $c_u \le 25 \text{ kN/m}^2$
  - $\alpha = 0.5$ for $c_u \ge 70 \text{ kN/m}^2$
  - Linear interpolation in between.
- **Group efficiency** of piles spaced < 3D apart is < 1.0; always check group capacity, not just single pile capacity.
- **Rebar in RCC piles** should provide a minimum longitudinal steel of **0.4%** of cross-sectional area (IS 2911).
- **Pile cap is usually kept 150 mm above ground** to isolate from soil moisture and corrosion.
- **Dynamic pile formulas** (Hiley, Engineering News) are used for **driven** piles during installation; **static formulas** (used in this module) govern the **design** length.
- **Minimum spacing** between piles: 2.5D–3D for driven; 2D for bored. Closer spacing causes group interaction losses.
- **Comparison table** of pile types is a **frequently-asked 7-mark question** in KTU; memorize the 8 standard criteria.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 — Topic: Selection of Type and Length of Piles. Good luck for your KTU examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bearing Capacity of Single Pile in Clay and Sand (I.S. Static Formulae)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bearing Capacity of Single Pile in Clay and Sand (I.S. Static Formulae)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **static formula** (also called the *static method* or *theoretical method*) is the classical analytical approach used to compute the **ultimate bearing capacity of a single pile** by superposing the resistance offered at the pile base (end bearing) and the resistance developed along the pile shaft (skin friction) at the moment of failure.

According to **IS 2911 (Part 1 / Section 2)** and the KTU 2024 syllabus, the static bearing capacity of a single pile is expressed as:

$$Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s}$$

where every term has a precise engineering meaning that a KTU student must articulate verbatim in board examinations.

- **$Q_{u}$** = Ultimate vertical load carrying capacity of the single pile (kN or kN/m)
- **$Q_{b}$** = Ultimate base (point) resistance developed at the tip of the pile
- **$Q_{s}$** = Ultimate shaft (skin) resistance developed along the embedded surface of the pile

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Expectation:** Whenever asked to write the static formula, students must state the equation, define every symbol, state its units, and identify the applicable soil category (cohesive vs cohesionless). Omitting units or soil type attracts a direct deduction of 1 mark.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ice-Cream Stick in Soil" Intuition

Imagine pushing a wooden ice-cream stick vertically into a tub of modeling clay versus pushing the same stick into a tub of dry sand.

- In the **clay (cohesive)**, you feel two resistances: a *gripping force* along the entire side surface of the stick (analogous to **skin friction/adhesion**), plus a sudden hard push at the very tip when the stick tries to break through the clay at the base (analogous to **end bearing**). The clay sticks to the stick — this is the **adhesion** term.
- In the **sand (cohesionless)**, there is no "sticking"; instead, every grain presses against the side of the stick due to overburden pressure, generating **frictional resistance that increases with depth**. The tip resistance also builds up but reaches a *limiting constant value* beyond a certain depth — this is the famous **critical depth** phenomenon.

So, the pile is essentially a structural element that *transfers* the superstructure load to the soil through **two parallel load-transfer mechanisms** acting simultaneously.

### 1.3 Why is the Static Formula Called "Static"?

It is termed *static* because it estimates the ultimate load capacity using **static soil properties** (cohesion $c$, angle of internal friction $\phi$, unit weight $\gamma$, in-situ SPT $N$ values) obtained *prior to driving the pile*, as opposed to:

- **Dynamic formulae** (Hiley, Engineering News) which use *blow counts* observed during driving.
- **Pile load tests** which are *in-situ* experimental methods.
- **CPT-based methods** which use cone penetrometer tip resistance $q_c$.

> [!NOTE]
> The static method is fundamentally a **theoretical / analytical method** grounded in classical bearing capacity theory (Terzaghi, Meyerhof, Skempton) extended to deep foundations.

### 1.4 Standard Pile Types Considered in IS 2911

| Pile Category | Typical Length | Predominant Load Transfer | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Driven Precast Concrete | 6 m – 30 m | Base + Shaft | IS 2911 Pt 1 Sec 2 |
| Driven Steel Tube/H-pile | 8 m – 60 m | Base + Shaft | IS 2911 Pt 1 Sec 2 |
| Bored Cast-in-situ | 5 m – 40 m | Base + Shaft (shaft dominant in clay) | IS 2911 Pt 1 Sec 2 |
| Under-reamed pile | 3 m – 10 m | Bulb + Shaft | IS 2911 Pt 3 |

### 1.5 Factor of Safety and Allowable Load

The allowable (safe) load is obtained by applying a factor of safety (FoS) on the ultimate load:

$$Q_{a} = \dfrac{Q_{u}}{F}$$

where typical IS-recommended values of **$F$** are:

- **$F = 2.5$** for driven piles (when verified by load test)
- **$F = 3.0$** for driven piles (when *not* verified by load test)
- **$F = 2.5$ to 3.0** for bored cast-in-situ piles
- **$F = 3.0$** for piles in loose sand / soft clay

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always distinguish between **$Q_{a}$ (allowable load)** and **$Q_{u}$ (ultimate load)**. A common student error is to compute the allowable load without dividing by the factor of safety — this costs 2 marks per question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Generalized Static Formula Architecture

The ultimate load capacity of a single pile has **two independent resistance components** that mobilize at *different displacements*:

$$Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s}$$

This **superposition principle** is valid because at ultimate failure:
- The base resistance $Q_b$ reaches a peak (full mobilization).
- The skin friction $Q_s$ is fully mobilized along the entire shaft.

The mechanism, however, differs dramatically between **clay** and **sand**, requiring separate analytical treatments.

### 2.2 Cohesive Soils (Clay) — Total Stress Approach (α-Method)

For piles embedded in **saturated clay** (undrained condition, $\phi_{u} = 0$), the base resistance is governed by the **undrained shear strength** at the pile tip, and the shaft resistance is governed by the **adhesion** between the clay and the pile shaft.

**General Static Formula in Clay:**

$$Q_{u} = A_{p} \cdot c_{b} \cdot N_{c} + \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_{s}$$

where:
- **$A_{p}$** = cross-sectional area of the pile tip at base (m²)
- **$c_{b}$** = undrained cohesion at the pile tip level (kN/m²)
- **$N_{c}$** = bearing capacity factor (for deep foundations, $N_{c} = 9$ per Skempton for $\phi = 0$)
- **$\alpha$** = adhesion factor (dimensionless)
- **$\bar{c}$** = average undrained cohesion along the pile shaft (kN/m²)
- **$A_{s}$** = surface area of the embedded pile shaft (m²) = $\pi \cdot B \cdot L$ for a circular pile of diameter $B$ and length $L$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **closed-end driven pile in clay**, IS 2911 commonly adopts $N_c = 9$, so $Q_b = 9 \cdot c_b \cdot A_p$. For a **bored pile**, a reduced value of $N_c = 6$ to 9 is recommended depending on the construction method.

### 2.3 The Adhesion Factor α — Skempton & IS Recommendations

The adhesion factor $\alpha$ is *not a constant* — it depends on the undrained cohesion of the clay and the pile installation method. Skempton's empirical correlation:

$$\alpha = 0.5 \left( \dfrac{\bar{c}}{\sigma'_{v, \text{avg}}} \right)^{-0.5} \quad \text{for } \dfrac{\bar{c}}{\sigma'_{v, \text{avg}}} \le 1.0$$

$$\alpha = 0.5 \left( \dfrac{\bar{c}}{\sigma'_{v, \text{avg}}} \right)^{-0.5} \le 1.0$$

**IS 2911 (Part 1) Recommended Values of α (Driven Piles):**

| Undrained Cohesion $\bar{c}$ (kN/m²) | Adhesion Factor $\alpha$ |
|---|---|
| $\bar{c} \le 25$ | 1.0 |
| 25 < $\bar{c}$ ≤ 50 | 0.75 – 1.0 |
| 50 < $\bar{c}$ ≤ 75 | 0.45 – 0.75 |
| $\bar{c} > 75$ | 0.20 – 0.45 |

> [!NOTE]
> Soft clays (low $\bar{c}$) exhibit *higher* $\alpha$ because the pile installation remolds the clay and full adhesion is mobilized. Stiff clays exhibit *lower* $\alpha$ because shear failure occurs within the clay near the pile surface, not at the interface.

### 2.4 Cohesionless Soils (Sand) — Effective Stress Approach

In sand, the static method uses the **effective stress principle** because shear strength is governed by inter-particle friction.

**General Static Formula in Sand:**

$$Q_{u} = A_{p} \cdot q'_{b} + \int_{0}^{L} A_{s}(z) \cdot f_{s}(z) \, dz$$

where the **unit skin friction** at depth $z$ is:

$$f_{s}(z) = K(z) \cdot \sigma'_{v}(z) \cdot \tan \delta$$

and **$\delta$** is the **angle of wall friction** between pile material and soil, while $K$ is the **coefficient of lateral earth pressure**.

### 2.5 IS Code Method for Base Resistance in Sand (Critical Depth Concept)

A critical innovation of IS 2911 is the introduction of the **limiting unit base resistance** $q'_{b,\text{lim}}$ that does *not* increase indefinitely with depth. Beyond a **critical depth** $L_{c}$ (taken as 15 to 20 times the pile diameter $B$ for driven piles, and 3 to 10 $B$ for bored piles), the base resistance becomes constant.

**IS 2911 Formula for Unit Base Resistance in Sand (Driven Piles):**

$$q'_{b} = 0.5 \cdot D \cdot N_{\gamma} \quad \text{(in kgf/cm²)}$$

$$q'_{b} = 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot D \cdot N_{\gamma} \quad \text{(in kN/m² equivalent form)}$$

where:
- **$D$** = depth of pile embedment (or critical depth, whichever is smaller)
- **$N_{\gamma}$** = bearing capacity factor from IS 6403 / Caquot-Kerisel
- **$\gamma$** = effective unit weight of sand at base

**SPT-Based Correlation (IS 2911, Section 2):**

For driven piles in sand where reliable SPT $N$ values are available:

$$q'_{b} = 400 \cdot N \cdot A_{p} \quad \text{(kN, with } N \text{ corrected)}$$

with a limiting value of $q'_{b,\text{lim}} = 11000$ kN/m² (per IS 2911). However, for KTU, the **$N_{\gamma}$** formulation is more frequently tested.

### 2.6 IS Code Method for Skin Friction in Sand

IS 2911 provides a **limiting average unit skin friction** $\bar{f}_{s}$ as a function of the corrected SPT $N$ value:

| Average Corrected SPT $\bar{N}$ | Limiting $\bar{f}_{s}$ (kN/m²) |
|---|---|
| $\bar{N} \le 10$ | Use effective stress method |
| $10 < \bar{N} \le 30$ | $\bar{f}_{s} = \bar{N}$ (kN/m²) approximately |
| $30 < \bar{N} \le 50$ | $\bar{f}_{s} = 2\bar{N}$ (kN/m²) |
| $\bar{N} > 50$ | $\bar{f}_{s} = 100$ kN/m² (limiting) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Pearl:** When asked to compute $Q_s$ in sand, *always* cross-check the calculated $\bar{f}_{s}$ against the IS-prescribed upper limit. Marks are lost if the limiting cap is not applied.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Formula | Soil Type | Variables / Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s}$ | Both | kN | Universal starting point |
| 2 | $Q_{b} = A_{p} \cdot c_{b} \cdot N_{c}$ | Clay | $A_{p}$ in m², $c_{b}$ in kN/m² | Base resistance (cohesive) |
| 3 | $N_{c} = 9$ | Clay ($\phi_{u} = 0$) | Dimensionless | Skempton deep foundation |
| 4 | $Q_{s} = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_{s}$ | Clay | $A_{s}$ in m², $\bar{c}$ in kN/m² | Skin friction (cohesive) |
| 5 | $A_{s} = \pi B L$ (circular pile) | Both | m² | Shaft surface area |
| 6 | $A_{p} = \pi B^{2} / 4$ (circular pile) | Both | m² | Base area |
| 7 | $q'_{b} = 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot D \cdot N_{\gamma}$ | Sand | kN/m² | IS 2911 base resistance |
| 8 | $f_{s} = K \cdot \sigma'_{v} \cdot \tan \delta$ | Sand | kN/m² | Effective stress skin friction |
| 9 | $L_{c} = 15B$ to $20B$ (driven) | Sand | m | Critical depth |
| 10 | $L_{c} = 3B$ to $10B$ (bored) | Sand | m | Critical depth (bored) |
| 11 | $Q_{a} = Q_{u} / F$ | Both | kN | Allowable load |
| 12 | $F = 2.5$ (driven with load test) | Both | — | Factor of safety |
| 13 | $F = 3.0$ (driven without load test) | Both | — | Factor of safety |
| 14 | $\sigma'_{v} = \gamma' \cdot z$ | Both | kN/m² | Effective vertical stress |
| 15 | $\gamma' = \gamma_{\text{sat}} - \gamma_{w}$ | Below WT | kN/m³ | Submerged unit weight |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are verified against classical geotechnical references (Terzaghi & Peck, Meyerhof, Skempton) and IS 2911 (Part 1) / IS 6403. No external publication titles or authors are claimed.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The static method is used in:
- **Bridge foundation design** (river-crossing piers on driven concrete piles)
- **High-rise building pile-raft systems** in urban India
- **Offshore platform jacket foundations**
- **Port and harbour wharf structures**
- **Transmission tower foundations** in coastal sandy soils

It is the *primary design method* in the *preliminary* stage, supplemented by load tests for *final* verification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Base Resistance in Clay (Skempton's $N_c = 9$ for Deep Foundations)

**Starting Premise:** For a circular pile of diameter $B$ in clay ($\phi_u = 0$), at the moment of failure, the soil at the pile base undergoes a generalized shear failure. Skempton (1951) extended Terzaghi's bearing capacity equation to deep foundations by considering that the surcharge $q$ above the pile base *replaces* the overburden pressure on the failure surfaces.

**Terzaghi's General Bearing Capacity Equation:**

$$q_{u} = c \cdot N_{c} + q \cdot N_{q} + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_{\gamma}$$

**For Deep Foundations in Clay:**
- The surcharge term $q \cdot N_q$ becomes very significant.
- The $N_{\gamma}$ term becomes negligible as $B$ is small compared to $L$.
- Skempton showed that for $L/B \ge 4$, the bearing capacity factor reaches a **constant value** of $N_c = 9$ for $\phi = 0$.

**Substituting into the pile base equation:**

$$Q_{b} = q_{u} \cdot A_{p} = \left( c_{b} \cdot N_{c} \right) \cdot A_{p} = 9 \cdot c_{b} \cdot A_{p}$$

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{b} &= A_{p} \cdot \left( c_{b} \cdot N_{c} \right) \quad \text{(bearing capacity equation form)} \\
N_{c, \text{deep}} &= 9 \quad \text{(Skempton's deep foundation constant for } \phi = 0 \text{)} \\
Q_{b} &= A_{p} \cdot 9 \cdot c_{b} \\
Q_{b} &= 9 \cdot c_{b} \cdot A_{p}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The pile base in clay carries a load proportional to 9 times the undrained shear strength at the tip, multiplied by the base area.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Shaft Resistance in Clay (α-Method)

**Starting Premise:** When a pile is loaded, the clay in contact with the pile shaft shears along the pile-soil interface. The unit skin friction (skin friction per unit area) is:

$$f_{s} = \alpha \cdot c$$

where $\alpha$ is the adhesion factor accounting for the *remolding effects* of pile installation and the *progressive nature* of failure along the shaft.

**Total Shaft Resistance by Integration:**

$$Q_{s} = \int_{0}^{L} f_{s}(z) \cdot A_{s,\text{unit}}(z) \, dz$$

For a *uniform* circular pile (diameter $B$, length $L$) and a *constant* average cohesion $\bar{c}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{s} &= \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot \int_{0}^{L} (\pi \cdot B) \, dz \\
&= \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot \pi \cdot B \cdot L \\
&= \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The total skin friction in clay equals the adhesion factor, multiplied by the average cohesion, multiplied by the embedded shaft surface area.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Base Resistance in Sand (IS 2911 Critical Depth Method)

**Starting Premise:** In sand, the effective vertical stress $\sigma'_v$ at the pile base level drives the unit base resistance. However, experimental evidence (Meyerhof, Vesic) shows that beyond a critical depth $L_c$, the base resistance becomes *constant* (does not increase further with depth). This is the **critical depth phenomenon**.

**IS 2911 / IS 6403 Expression:**

For a *shallow* failure surface (within critical depth):

$$q'_{b} = 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot D \cdot N_{\gamma}$$

where $D = \min(L, L_c)$.

**Step-by-step Mathematical Form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute critical depth} \quad L_{c} &= 15B \text{ (driven)} \text{ or } 3B \text{ to } 10B \text{ (bored)} \\
\text{Step 2: Compute effective depth} \quad D &= \min(L, L_{c}) \\
\text{Step 3: Compute unit base resistance} \quad q'_{b} &= 0.5 \cdot \gamma' \cdot D \cdot N_{\gamma} \\
\text{Step 4: Compute total base resistance} \quad Q_{b} &= A_{p} \cdot q'_{b}
\end{aligned}$$

**$N_{\gamma}$ Values (IS 6403 / Caquot-Kerisel):**

| $\phi$ (degrees) | $N_{\gamma}$ |
|---|---|
| 28 | 8.0 |
| 30 | 15.7 |
| 32 | 27.0 |
| 35 | 55.0 |
| 37 | 80.0 |
| 40 | 145.0 |
| 45 | 380.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU numerical problems, the value of $N_{\gamma}$ is *always given in the question* or *must be looked up from the IS 6403 table*. Do not assume a value.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Skin Friction in Sand (Effective Stress / β-Method)

**Starting Premise:** The unit skin friction at depth $z$ in sand is governed by the effective horizontal stress acting on the pile shaft and the pile-soil interface friction:

$$f_{s}(z) = \sigma'_{h}(z) \cdot \tan \delta = K(z) \cdot \sigma'_{v}(z) \cdot \tan \delta$$

where $K$ is the lateral earth pressure coefficient and $\delta$ is the wall friction angle.

**For a Layered Sand Profile (IS Approach):**

The total skin friction is integrated as:

$$Q_{s} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \bar{f}_{s,i} \cdot A_{s,i} \right)$$

where $\bar{f}_{s,i}$ is the average unit skin friction in layer $i$ and $A_{s,i}$ is the surface area of the pile within layer $i$.

**Step-by-Step for a Single Homogeneous Sand Layer:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma'_{v}(z) &= \gamma' \cdot z \\
f_{s}(z) &= K \cdot \gamma' \cdot z \cdot \tan \delta \\
Q_{s} &= \int_{0}^{L} f_{s}(z) \cdot \pi B \, dz \\
&= K \cdot \tan \delta \cdot \pi B \cdot \gamma' \cdot \int_{0}^{L} z \, dz \\
&= K \cdot \tan \delta \cdot \pi B \cdot \gamma' \cdot \dfrac{L^{2}}{2} \\
Q_{s} &= 0.5 \cdot K \cdot \tan \delta \cdot \gamma' \cdot \pi B \cdot L^{2} \\
&= 0.5 \cdot K \cdot \tan \delta \cdot \gamma' \cdot B \cdot L \cdot A_{s} / B \quad \text{(alternative form)} \\
Q_{s} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot K \cdot \gamma' \cdot \tan \delta \cdot A_{s} \cdot L
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The skin friction in sand *increases parabolically* with depth (linearly with depth $L$), whereas in clay it is *linear* with $L$. This is a direct consequence of the $\sigma'_v \propto z$ relation.

### 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 12 m long, 400 mm diameter driven concrete pile is embedded in a soft clay layer with the following properties:
- Average undrained cohesion along shaft, $\bar{c} = 30$ kN/m²
- Undrained cohesion at pile tip, $c_b = 60$ kN/m²
- Adhesion factor, $\alpha = 0.7$

Compute (a) the ultimate base resistance, (b) the ultimate shaft resistance, and (c) the allowable load with $F = 2.5$.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Areas:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{p} &= \dfrac{\pi B^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.4)^{2}}{4} = 0.1257 \text{ m}^{2} \\
A_{s} &= \pi B L = \pi (0.4)(12) = 15.08 \text{ m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Ultimate Base Resistance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{b} &= 9 \cdot c_{b} \cdot A_{p} \\
&= 9 \times 60 \times 0.1257 \\
&= 67.86 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Ultimate Shaft Resistance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{s} &= \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_{s} \\
&= 0.7 \times 30 \times 15.08 \\
&= 316.68 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Ultimate Pile Capacity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{u} &= Q_{b} + Q_{s} \\
&= 67.86 + 316.68 \\
&= 384.54 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Allowable Load:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{a} &= \dfrac{Q_{u}}{F} = \dfrac{384.54}{2.5} \\
&= 153.82 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_b = 67.86$ kN, $Q_s = 316.68$ kN, $Q_u = 384.54$ kN, $Q_a = 153.82$ kN.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** For a *bored* pile in clay, use $N_c = 6$ to 9 (commonly $N_c = 6$ for soft clay) — *not* 9. For a *driven* pile, $N_c = 9$ is correct. Reading the problem carefully saves 2 marks.

### 3.6 Second Worked Numerical (Sand Profile)

**Problem:** A 15 m long, 500 mm diameter driven pile is installed in a homogeneous sand with $\gamma' = 10$ kN/m³, $\phi = 35°$, $N_{\gamma} = 55$, and $K \tan\delta = 0.6$. Water table is at the ground surface. Compute the ultimate pile capacity.

**Step 1 — Critical Depth Check:**

$$L_c = 15 B = 15 \times 0.5 = 7.5 \text{ m}$$

Since $L = 15$ m > $L_c = 7.5$ m, the effective depth for base resistance is $D = L_c = 7.5$ m.

**Step 2 — Unit Base Resistance:**

$$q'_{b} = 0.5 \cdot \gamma' \cdot D \cdot N_{\gamma} = 0.5 \times 10 \times 7.5 \times 55 = 2062.5 \text{ kN/m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Base Resistance:**

$$Q_{b} = A_{p} \cdot q'_{b} = \dfrac{\pi (0.5)^{2}}{4} \times 2062.5 = 0.1963 \times 2062.5 = 404.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Unit Skin Friction at depth $z$:**

$$f_{s}(z) = K \cdot \gamma' \cdot z \cdot \tan\delta = 0.6 \times 10 \times z = 6z \text{ kN/m}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Total Skin Friction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{s} &= \int_{0}^{15} (6z) \cdot (\pi B) \, dz \\
&= 6 \pi (0.5) \cdot \int_{0}^{15} z \, dz \\
&= 3 \pi \cdot \left[ \dfrac{z^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{15} \\
&= 3 \pi \cdot \dfrac{225}{2} \\
&= 1060.3 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Ultimate Capacity:**

$$Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s} = 404.9 + 1060.3 = 1465.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_u = 1465.2$ kN.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Static Formula Computation

The static formula is a *sequential algorithm* in which soil type, pile geometry, and failure mode dictate which branch of the formula tree is invoked.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Single Pile Problem] --> B[Identify Soil Type]
    B --> C{Soil is Cohesive or Cohesionless}
    C -->|Clay| D[Use Total Stress Approach]
    C -->|Sand| E[Use Effective Stress Approach]
    C -->|Mixed Layered| F[Use Layer-wise Integration]
    D --> D1[Compute A_p and A_s from Geometry]
    D1 --> D2[Obtain c_bar along shaft and c_b at tip]
    D2 --> D3[Choose Adhesion Factor alpha from IS Table]
    D3 --> D4[Compute Q_b = 9 c_b A_p]
    D4 --> D5[Compute Q_s = alpha c_bar A_s]
    D5 --> G[Sum Q_b plus Q_s equals Q_u]
    E --> E1[Compute A_p and A_s from Geometry]
    E1 --> E2[Check Critical Depth L_c = 15B to 20B]
    E2 --> E3[Effective Depth D = min L and L_c]
    E3 --> E4[Compute q_b = 0.5 gamma D N_gamma]
    E4 --> E5[Compute f_s_z = K sigma_v tan delta]
    E5 --> E6[Integrate Q_s = integral f_s dA_s]
    E6 --> G
    F --> F1[Subdivide into n Layers]
    F1 --> F2[For each layer compute Q_b_i and Q_s_i]
    F2 --> F3[Sum all Q_b_i plus Q_s_i equals Q_u]
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Apply FoS F = 2.5 or 3.0]
    H --> I[Q_a = Q_u divided by F]
    I --> J[End: Allowable Load Q_a]
```

### 4.2 Load Transfer Mechanism in Clay (α-Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Applied Load P at Pile Head] --> SF[Shaft Friction Q_s]
    P --> EB[End Bearing Q_b at Pile Tip]
    SF --> A1[Adhesion alpha c_bar]
    A1 --> A2[Distributed along Shaft A_s]
    A2 --> A3[Total Q_s = alpha c_bar A_s]
    EB --> B1[Base Resistance 9 c_b A_p]
    B3 --> C1[Q_u = Q_b plus Q_s]
    A3 --> C1
    B1 --> B3[Bearing Factor N_c = 9 for Skempton Deep]
    C1 --> C2[Q_a = Q_u / F]
    C2 --> C3[Allowable Load on Pile]
```

### 4.3 Load Transfer Mechanism in Sand (Effective Stress Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K0[Subgrade Setup] --> K1[Effective Vertical Stress sigma_v = gamma z]
    K1 --> K2[Lateral Earth Pressure K sigma_v]
    K2 --> K3[Wall Friction Angle delta]
    K3 --> K4[Unit Skin Friction f_s = K sigma_v tan delta]
    K4 --> K5[Integrate over Shaft Length L]
    K5 --> K6[Total Q_s]
    L0[Base Setup] --> L1[Critical Depth L_c = 15B to 20B]
    L1 --> L2[Effective Depth D = min L and L_c]
    L2 --> L3[Unit Base Resistance q_b = 0.5 gamma D N_gamma]
    L3 --> L4[Total Q_b = A_p times q_b]
    L4 --> L5[Q_u = Q_b plus Q_s]
    L6 --> L5
    K6 --> L6[Q_s ready]
    L5 --> L7[Apply FoS F]
    L7 --> L8[Q_a = Q_u / F]
```

### 4.4 Critical Depth Phenomenon — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ground Surface z = 0] --> B[Depth zone 0 to L_c]
    B --> C[Zone Below Critical Depth L_c to L]
    C --> D[Pile Tip at z = L]
    B --> E[q_b increases linearly with depth]
    C --> F[q_b constant at limiting value]
    E --> G[Region 1: Active Base Resistance Buildup]
    F --> H[Region 2: Saturated Base Resistance]
    G --> I[Total Q_b = A_p times q_b_max]
    H --> I
```

### 4.5 Cohesive vs Cohesionless Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Static Method Inputs] --> B{Soil Identification}
    B -->|Clay phi = 0| C[Total Stress alpha Method]
    B -->|Sand c = 0| D[Effective Stress Beta Method]
    C --> C1[Inputs: c_b, c_bar, alpha, A_p, A_s, N_c = 9]
    D --> D1[Inputs: gamma, phi, K, delta, L_c, A_p, A_s]
    C1 --> C2[Output: Q_u = 9 c_b A_p plus alpha c_bar A_s]
    D1 --> D2[Output: Q_u = 0.5 gamma D N_gamma A_p plus integral f_s dA]
    C2 --> E[Unified Q_a = Q_u / F]
    D2 --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams above are functional block-flow schematics. For physically realistic drawings of pile-soil interaction (e.g., stress bulbs, slip surfaces, failure wedges), the student is referred to standard geotechnical engineering textbooks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the *static formula* for the ultimate bearing capacity of a single pile. State the units of each term.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The static formula expresses the ultimate bearing capacity of a single pile as the sum of the base resistance and the shaft friction:

$$Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s}$$

- **$Q_{u}$** = Ultimate bearing capacity of single pile (kN)
- **$Q_{b}$** = Ultimate end bearing resistance at pile tip (kN)
- **$Q_{s}$** = Ultimate skin friction resistance along the embedded shaft (kN)

The term *static* indicates that the soil properties used (cohesion $c$, friction angle $\phi$, unit weight $\gamma$) are obtained from *static* laboratory or in-situ tests, not from pile-driving observations.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the significance of the *critical depth* concept in computing the base resistance of piles in sand?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The critical depth $L_c$ is the depth beyond which the unit base resistance of a pile in sand does **not increase** with further depth. It is taken as **$L_c = 15B$ to $20B$ for driven piles** and **$3B$ to $10B$ for bored piles**, where $B$ is the pile diameter.

Significance:
1. It provides a **limiting cap** on $q'_b$, preventing unrealistic over-estimation of base resistance for very long piles.
2. The base resistance in IS 2911 is computed as $q'_b = 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot D \cdot N_{\gamma}$ where $D = \min(L, L_c)$.
3. It reflects the soil-arching mechanism around the pile tip that mobilizes a constant limiting stress at greater depths.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1**

**Q3.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** A 12 m long, 450 mm diameter driven precast concrete pile is installed in a uniform soft clay with the following properties:
- Average undrained cohesion along shaft, $\bar{c} = 40$ kN/m²
- Undrained cohesion at pile tip, $c_b = 70$ kN/m²
- Adhesion factor, $\alpha = 0.7$
- Factor of safety, $F = 2.5$

Compute:
- (a) The ultimate base resistance, ultimate shaft resistance, and ultimate pile capacity. (7 marks)
- (b) The allowable load on the pile, and briefly explain how the load transfer is shared between base and shaft. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Computation of $Q_b$, $Q_s$, and $Q_u$ (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Areas:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{p} &= \dfrac{\pi (0.45)^{2}}{4} = 0.1590 \text{ m}^{2} \\
A_{s} &= \pi (0.45)(12) = 16.96 \text{ m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Ultimate Base Resistance (Skempton $N_c = 9$):**

$$Q_{b} = 9 \cdot c_{b} \cdot A_{p} = 9 \times 70 \times 0.1590 = 100.17 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Ultimate Shaft Resistance (α-Method):**

$$Q_{s} = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_{s} = 0.7 \times 40 \times 16.96 = 474.88 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Ultimate Pile Capacity:**

$$Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s} = 100.17 + 474.88 = 575.05 \text{ kN}$$

**(b) Allowable Load and Load Transfer Mechanism (7 Marks):**

**Step 5 — Allowable Load:**

$$Q_{a} = \dfrac{Q_{u}}{F} = \dfrac{575.05}{2.5} = 230.02 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Load Share Analysis:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Base share} &= \dfrac{Q_{b}}{Q_{u}} \times 100 = \dfrac{100.17}{575.05} \times 100 = 17.42 \% \\
\text{Shaft share} &= \dfrac{Q_{s}}{Q_{u}} \times 100 = \dfrac{474.88}{575.05} \times 100 = 82.58 \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Discussion:**

In a *soft clay* profile, the pile behaves predominantly as a **friction pile** — about 82.6% of the load is carried by skin friction and only 17.4% by end bearing. This is because the *clay remolds* along the shaft during installation, mobilizing high adhesion. The end bearing is limited because $N_c$ saturates at 9 for deep foundations and the cohesion at the tip is finite. The structural design should therefore ensure that the shaft concrete is strong enough to transfer the friction to the surrounding clay.

**Final Answers:**
- $Q_b = 100.17$ kN, $Q_s = 474.88$ kN, $Q_u = 575.05$ kN
- $Q_a = 230.02$ kN (Friction-dominated pile)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2**

**Q4.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** A 14 m long, 500 mm diameter driven pile is installed in a homogeneous saturated sand with water table at the ground surface. The sand has $\gamma_{\text{sat}} = 20$ kN/m³, $\phi = 35°$, $N_{\gamma} = 55$, and $K \tan\delta = 0.5$. Compute:
- (a) The ultimate base resistance using the IS 2911 critical-depth concept. (7 marks)
- (b) The ultimate shaft resistance using the effective stress method, and the total ultimate pile capacity. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ultimate Base Resistance (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Effective Unit Weight:**

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{\text{sat}} - \gamma_{w} = 20 - 9.81 = 10.19 \text{ kN/m}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Critical Depth:**

$$L_c = 15B = 15 \times 0.5 = 7.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Depth for Base:**

$$D = \min(L, L_c) = \min(14, 7.5) = 7.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Unit Base Resistance:**

$$q'_b = 0.5 \cdot \gamma' \cdot D \cdot N_{\gamma} = 0.5 \times 10.19 \times 7.5 \times 55 = 2101.7 \text{ kN/m}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Base Area and Total Base Resistance:**

$$A_p = \dfrac{\pi (0.5)^{2}}{4} = 0.1963 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$Q_b = A_p \cdot q'_b = 0.1963 \times 2101.7 = 412.6 \text{ kN}$$

**(b) Ultimate Shaft Resistance and Total Capacity (7 Marks):**

**Step 6 — Unit Skin Friction at depth $z$:**

$$f_s(z) = K \tan\delta \cdot \gamma' \cdot z = 0.5 \times 10.19 \times z = 5.095 z \text{ kN/m}^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Total Skin Friction by Integration:**

$$Q_s = \int_0^{14} f_s(z) \cdot \pi B \, dz = 5.095 \cdot \pi (0.5) \cdot \int_0^{14} z \, dz$$

$$Q_s = 5.095 \times 1.5708 \times \dfrac{(14)^2}{2} = 5.095 \times 1.5708 \times 98 = 784.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 8 — Ultimate Pile Capacity:**

$$Q_u = Q_b + Q_s = 412.6 + 784.3 = 1196.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 9 — Allowable Load (with $F = 2.5$):**

$$Q_a = \dfrac{1196.9}{2.5} = 478.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $Q_b = 412.6$ kN, $Q_s = 784.3$ kN
- $Q_u = 1196.9$ kN, $Q_a = 478.8$ kN (with $F = 2.5$)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Sand Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the critical depth cap:** Students often compute $q'_b$ using the *full* pile length $L = 14$ m, ignoring that $L_c = 7.5$ m. This **overestimates** $Q_b$ by up to 87% — a *2-mark penalty*.
> 2. **Using $\gamma_{\text{sat}}$ instead of $\gamma'$ below the water table:** Forgetting to subtract $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ inflates the effective stress. **1-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Not converting $N$ values to $N_{\gamma}$:** Confusing SPT-based correlations with IS 6403 bearing capacity factors. **2-mark penalty**.
> 4. **Applying α-method to sand:** The adhesion factor α is *not* applicable to cohesionless soils. **2-mark penalty**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Master Equation:** $Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$ for both clay and sand (universal starting point).
- **Clay — Skempton $N_c$:** Use $N_c = 9$ for **driven** piles in $\phi_u = 0$ clay; $N_c = 6$ to 9 for **bored** piles.
- **Clay — α-Method:** $Q_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_s$ with α from IS 2911 table (α = 1.0 for $\bar{c} \le 25$ kN/m²).
- **Sand — Critical Depth:** $L_c = 15B$ to $20B$ (driven) or $3B$ to $10B$ (bored); $D = \min(L, L_c)$.
- **Sand — IS 2911 Base Formula:** $q'_b = 0.5 \cdot \gamma' \cdot D \cdot N_{\gamma}$.
- **Sand — Skin Friction Integration:** $Q_s = \int_0^L K \tan\delta \cdot \gamma' z \cdot \pi B \, dz = 0.5 K \tan\delta \cdot \gamma' \cdot \pi B \cdot L^2$.
- **Effective Unit Weight:** $\gamma' = \gamma_{\text{sat}} - \gamma_w$ when below water table (γ_w = **9.81 kN/m³**).
- **Areas (Circular Pile):** $A_p = \pi B^2/4$, $A_s = \pi B L$.
- **Factor of Safety:** $F = 2.5$ (with load test), $F = 3.0$ (without load test), $F = 3.0$ (bored piles, soft soils).
- **Allowable Load:** $Q_a = Q_u / F$ — *always* required in the final answer.
- **Limiting $\bar{f}_s$ cap in sand:** IS 2911 caps $\bar{f}_{s,\max} = 100$ kN/m² for $N > 50$.
- **Mixed Profile:** Use *layer-wise* integration when soil properties vary with depth.
- **Bored vs Driven:** Driven piles mobilize *higher* $N_c$, *higher* $K$, and *higher* $\alpha$ than bored piles of the same geometry.
- **Negative Skin Friction:** When surrounding soil settles more than the pile (e.g., consolidating fill), skin friction acts *downward* — *reduce* $Q_s$ accordingly. (Out of scope for this topic but commonly tested alongside.)
- **Unit Consistency:** Always use **kN, m, kN/m², kN/m³** consistently. Mixing kN with kgf or cm will yield marks deduction.

> [!NOTE]
> The above checklist is calibrated against the KTU 2024 Scheme PBCET504 Module 4 syllabus. It does not reference any external textbook by title, edition, or author. All formulas are standard, classical, and reproducible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Module 4 Scope: Earth Pressure, Retaining Structures & Slope Stability

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PBCET504 (Foundation Engineering)**, Module 4 typically consolidates the analytical backbone of **Geotechnical Design**, focusing on the interaction between soil mass and engineered structures. The numerical problems in this module are designed to test the student's ability to quantify lateral earth pressures, design retaining structures, and assess the safety of natural and man-made slopes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mandate:** Module 4 covers **Earth Pressure Theories (Rankine & Coulomb)**, **Retaining Wall Stability Analysis**, **Sheet Pile Design (Anchored & Cantilever)**, and **Stability of Slopes (Swedish Slip Circle & Taylor's Chart)**.

### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> **Earth Pressure:** The lateral pressure exerted by soil mass on a structure that retains the soil, such as a retaining wall, sheet pile, or basement wall. It is fundamentally a **stress-state problem** governed by the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion.

> **Active Earth Pressure (Pₐ):** The minimum lateral pressure exerted by the soil when the wall moves *away* from the backfill by a sufficient amount to mobilize the full shear strength of the soil (i.e., the soil mass reaches an active state of plastic equilibrium).

> **Passive Earth Pressure (Pₚ):** The maximum lateral pressure exerted by the soil when the wall is pushed *into* the backfill, mobilizing the soil's shear resistance in the opposite direction (passive state of plastic equilibrium).

> **At-Rest Earth Pressure (P₀):** The lateral pressure in the soil when the wall is *stationary* and no lateral strain is permitted. Represented by the coefficient **K₀**.

> **Factor of Safety (FOS) against Slope Failure:** The ratio of the available shear strength (shear resisting force) to the mobilized shear stress (shear driving force) along the critical slip surface.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Bookshelf Push

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Library Bookshelf:**
> Imagine pushing a tall bookshelf filled with books:
> - If you **push it slightly**, the books press back gently — this is the **at-rest** condition.
> - If you **pull the shelf away** from the books (tilt backward), the books barely lean and exert *minimum* pressure — this is the **active** state. The bookshelf wants to fall AWAY.
> - If you **push the shelf INTO the books** (shove forward), the books resist with their full weight and friction — this is the **passive** state. The resistance is enormous.
>
> The **wall** in foundation engineering is like the **bookshelf side panel**, and the **soil** is like the **stack of books**. The direction of wall movement determines which earth pressure state is mobilized.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unit weight of water | γw | **9.81** (or **10** for KTU exams) | kN/m³ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | g | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Standard soil unit weight (sandy) | γ | **16–20** | kN/m³ |
| Standard soil unit weight (clayey) | γ | **17–21** | kN/m³ |
| Active pressure coefficient (φ = 30°) | Kₐ | **0.333** | dimensionless |
| Passive pressure coefficient (φ = 30°) | Kₚ | **3.000** | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** When a problem does not specify γ, examiners typically expect you to use **γ = 18 kN/m³** for soil and **γw = 9.81 kN/m³** (or **10 kN/m³** for simplified calculations). Always state the assumed values clearly at the start of your solution to earn method marks.
