# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rankine's Earth Pressure Theory — Step-by-Step Logic

Rankine's theory (1857) assumes:
1. The soil mass is **homogeneous, isotropic, and semi-infinite**.
2. The ground surface is a **plane** (may be inclined or horizontal).
3. The wall is **smooth and vertical** (no wall friction).
4. Failure occurs as a **plane surface** (in reality, a curved surface, but Rankine approximated it as a plane).
5. The soil is in a state of **plastic equilibrium** (either active or passive).

### Why Active Pressure is Smaller than Passive Pressure
The Mohr circle for active state **touches** the failure envelope on the **minor principal stress** side (σₐ = σ₃ = minor), while passive state touches it on the **major principal stress** side (σₚ = σ₁ = major). Since the failure envelope has a slope less than 1:1, the major principal stress at failure is **significantly larger** than the minor principal stress.

---

## 2.2 Coulomb's Earth Pressure Theory — Key Logic

Coulomb's theory (1776) is more general than Rankine's because it:
- Accounts for **wall friction (δ)** between the wall and the backfill.
- Permits the backfill surface to be **inclined at angle β**.
- Allows the wall back to be **inclined at angle θ** from vertical.
- Assumes the failure surface is a **plane** (this is a limitation corrected by Culmann's and Caquot-Kerisel methods).

---

## 2.3 Stability of Slopes — Methodological Logic

A slope fails when the **driving moment** (due to gravity acting on the sliding mass) exceeds the **resisting moment** (due to shear strength along the failure surface).

**Two Standard Approaches in KTU Module 4:**

1. **Swedish Slip Circle Method (Fellenius, 1936):** Assumes a **circular** failure surface. The sliding mass is divided into vertical slices, and the factor of safety is computed as the ratio of total resisting moment to total driving moment about the center of the slip circle.

2. **Taylor's Stability Number Method (1937):** A simplified chart-based approach for homogeneous slopes, using the dimensionless **Stability Number Nₛ = c / (γH)**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — Every KTU 2024 numerical problem in Module 4 uses one or more of these formulas.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Active Pressure Coefficient (Rankine) | $K_a = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | φ = angle of internal friction | — |
| 2 | Passive Pressure Coefficient (Rankine) | $K_p = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | φ = angle of internal friction | — |
| 3 | At-Rest Pressure Coefficient | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ (Jaky, 1944) | φ = angle of internal friction | — |
| 4 | Active Pressure (horizontal backfill) | $p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$ | z = depth, c = cohesion | kN/m² |
| 5 | Passive Pressure (horizontal backfill) | $p_p = K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot z + 2c\sqrt{K_p}$ | z = depth, c = cohesion | kN/m² |
| 6 | Total Active Force (cohesionless soil) | $P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2$ | H = wall height | kN/m |
| 7 | Total Passive Force (cohesionless soil) | $P_p = \dfrac{1}{2} K_p \gamma H^2$ | H = wall height | kN/m |
| 8 | Total Active Force (c-φ soil) | $P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 - 2cH\sqrt{K_a}$ | c = cohesion | kN/m |
| 9 | Coulomb's Active Coefficient | $K_a = \dfrac{\cos^2(\phi - \theta)}{\cos^2\theta \cdot \cos(\delta + \theta)\left[1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{\sin(\phi+\delta)\sin(\phi-\beta)}{\cos(\delta+\theta)\cos(\beta-\theta)}}\right]^2}$ | δ = wall friction, β = backfill slope, θ = wall inclination | — |
| 10 | Coulomb's Passive Coefficient | $K_p = \dfrac{\cos^2(\phi + \theta)}{\cos^2\theta \cdot \cos(\delta - \theta)\left[1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{\sin(\phi+\delta)\sin(\phi+\beta)}{\cos(\delta-\theta)\cos(\beta-\theta)}}\right]^2}$ | δ, β, θ as above | — |
| 11 | Point of Application of Pₐ (triangular) | $H/3$ from base | Triangular distribution | m |
| 12 | Point of Application of Pₐ (rectangular + triangular) | At centroid of pressure diagram | — | m |
| 13 | FOS against Overturning | $FOS_{OT} = \dfrac{\Sigma M_R}{\Sigma M_O}$ | M_R = resisting moment, M_O = overturning moment | — |
| 14 | FOS against Sliding | $FOS_{SL} = \dfrac{\Sigma V \cdot \tan\phi_b + c_b \cdot B}{\Sigma H}$ | V = vertical force, B = base width, φ_b = base friction, c_b = base adhesion | — |
| 15 | FOS against Bearing Failure | $FOS_{BB} = \dfrac{q_{ult(net)}}{q_{act(net)}}$ | q_ult = ultimate bearing capacity | — |
| 16 | FOS for Infinite Slope (drained) | $FOS = \dfrac{c' + (\gamma z \cos^2\beta - u)\tan\phi'}{\gamma z \sin\beta \cos\beta}$ | u = pore water pressure | — |
| 17 | FOS for Infinite Slope (no pore pressure) | $FOS = \dfrac{\tan\phi'}{\tan\beta}$ | β = slope angle | — |
| 18 | Swedish Circle FOS (Fellenius) | $FOS = \dfrac{\Sigma \left[c \cdot \Delta L + (W \cos\alpha - u\Delta L)\tan\phi\right]}{\Sigma W \sin\alpha}$ | W = slice weight, α = slice base angle, ΔL = slice base length | — |
| 19 | Taylor's Stability Number | $N_s = \dfrac{c}{\gamma H_{cr}}$ | H_cr = critical height | — |
| 20 | Critical Height (Taylor) | $H_{cr} = \dfrac{c \cdot N_c}{\gamma}$ | N_c = stability number from Taylor's chart | m |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Rankine's Active Pressure Coefficient (Kₐ)

### Starting Point: Mohr Circle – Failure Envelope Geometry

At active failure, the vertical stress **σᵥ = γz** acts as the **major principal stress (σ₁)**, and the lateral stress **σₕ = σₐ** acts as the **minor principal stress (σ₃)**.

The Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion states:

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c \tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute σ₁ = σᵥ = γz and σ₃ = σₐ into the failure criterion.

$$
\sigma_v = \sigma_a \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c \tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for σₐ.

$$
\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_v - 2c \tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)}{\tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)}
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the following identity (rearranging tan to cot):

$$
\dfrac{1}{\tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)} = \tan\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the identity and use $\sigma_v = \gamma z$:

$$
\sigma_a = \gamma z \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) - 2c \tan\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** Define the **Active Earth Pressure Coefficient** and its square root:

$$
K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right), \qquad \sqrt{K_a} = \tan\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into the expression for σₐ:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_a = K_a \cdot \gamma z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}}
$$

This is the **final Rankine active pressure intensity equation**.

---

### 3.2 Companion Derivation: Rankine's Passive Pressure (Kₚ)

At passive failure, the **vertical stress σᵥ = γz** becomes the **minor principal stress (σ₃)**, and the **lateral stress σₚ** becomes the **major principal stress (σ₁)**.

**Step 1:** Start from the failure criterion, with σ₁ = σₚ, σ₃ = σᵥ:

$$
\sigma_p = \sigma_v \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c \tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Define the passive coefficient:

$$
K_p = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right), \qquad \sqrt{K_p} = \tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute to get the final result:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_p = K_p \cdot \gamma z + 2c\sqrt{K_p}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Trigonometric Identities for Kₐ and Kₚ (Exam-Critical Conversions)

These identities allow instant numerical computation when the angle φ is given. **Memorize the table below:**

| Angle φ | $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$ | $K_p = \tan^2(45° + \phi/2)$ | $K_p / K_a$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0°** | 1.000 | 1.000 | 1.00 |
| **10°** | 0.704 | 1.420 | 2.02 |
| **15°** | 0.589 | 1.698 | 2.88 |
| **20°** | 0.490 | 2.040 | 4.16 |
| **25°** | 0.406 | 2.464 | 6.07 |
| **30°** | 0.333 | 3.000 | 9.00 |
| **35°** | 0.271 | 3.690 | 13.62 |
| **40°** | 0.217 | 4.599 | 21.19 |
| **45°** | 0.172 | 5.828 | 33.88 |

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern Recognition Tip:** Notice that $K_p \cdot K_a = 1$ (always). This is a powerful sanity check — if your computed Kₐ × Kₚ ≠ 1, you have made an arithmetic error.

---

## 3.4 Total Active Force — Triangular Pressure Distribution

For **cohesionless** soil (c = 0) with **horizontal backfill** and **vertical wall**, the pressure intensity at depth z is:

$$
p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z
$$

This is a **linear function of z** — it is zero at the top (z = 0) and maximum at the base (z = H):

$$
p_{a,\max} = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H
$$

The total active force per unit length of wall is the **area of the triangular pressure diagram**:

$$
P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times H \times (K_a \gamma H)
$$

$$
\boxed{P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2}
$$

The point of application is at the **centroid of the triangle**, i.e., at a height of **H/3 above the base**.

---

## 3.5 Total Active Force — Cohesive (c–φ) Soil with Tension Crack Allowance

For c–φ soil, the active pressure at depth z is:

$$
p_a = K_a \gamma z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}
$$

The depth at which pₐ = 0 is the **tension crack depth z₀**:

$$
0 = K_a \gamma z_0 - 2c\sqrt{K_a} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad z_0 = \dfrac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}}
$$

The active pressure is **negative** (theoretical tension) from z = 0 to z = z₀. In practice, the soil cannot sustain tension, so the **tension zone** is neglected, and the effective pressure distribution is **triangular** from z₀ to H.

The total active force is then:

$$
P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma (H - z_0)^2
$$

Or equivalently, using H directly with the standard formula:

$$
P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 - 2cH\sqrt{K_a}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students apply the Rankine equation at the **top of the wall** and get a negative force, then incorrectly subtract this. The standard KTU-accepted procedure is: (a) compute the theoretical total force using the formula above, (b) **then** check whether the tension crack depth is significant, (c) if z₀ > 0, the **effective** active force is reduced. Always state both the theoretical and the practical values.

---

## 3.6 Retaining Wall Stability Checks — Analytical Framework

A retaining wall is safe if it satisfies **four** checks. The KTU exam typically asks for **any two of the four**.

### Check 1: FOS against Overturning

The overturning moment is caused by the **horizontal active force Pₐ** acting at H/3. The resisting moment is caused by the **weight of the wall W** acting through the centroid of the wall section.

$$
FOS_{OT} = \dfrac{\Sigma M_R}{\Sigma M_O} = \dfrac{W \cdot x_W + P_p \cdot x_{Pp} + \cdots}{P_a \cdot (H/3)}
$$

> **Required:** $FOS_{OT} \geq 1.5$ (IS 14458 recommends 1.5 to 2.0 for permanent walls)

### Check 2: FOS against Sliding

Sliding occurs along the **base** of the wall. The driving force is the horizontal component of Pₐ. The resisting force is the **base friction** plus any **passive resistance** at the toe.

$$
FOS_{SL} = \dfrac{\Sigma V \cdot \tan\phi_b + c_b \cdot B}{\Sigma H}
$$

> **Required:** $FOS_{SL} \geq 1.5$ (typically 1.5 to 2.0)

### Check 3: FOS against Bearing Capacity Failure

The base pressure under the wall must not exceed the allowable bearing capacity of the foundation soil.

$$
q_{\max} = \dfrac{\Sigma V}{B}\left(1 + \dfrac{6e}{B}\right), \qquad e = \dfrac{B}{2} - \bar{x}
$$

> **Required:** $q_{\max} \leq q_{allow}$, and the eccentricity $e \leq B/6$ (to avoid tension at the heel)

### Check 4: FOS against Deep-Seated Shear Failure

For tall walls on weak foundations, a **circular slip surface** may pass beneath the wall. This is checked using the Swedish circle method.

> **Required:** $FOS_{DSS} \geq 1.5$

---

## 3.7 Worked Example 1: Rankine Active Force on a Cantilever Wall (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A 6 m high retaining wall retains a cohesionless backfill with $\phi = 30°$ and $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$. The backfill is horizontal and the wall is vertical. Calculate the magnitude and point of application of the active earth pressure using Rankine's theory.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the Rankine active pressure coefficient.

$$
K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) = \tan^2\!\left(45° - 15°\right) = \tan^2(30°)
$$

$$
\tan(30°) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774
$$

$$
K_a = (0.5774)^2 = 0.3333 = \dfrac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the active pressure intensity at the base (z = H = 6 m).

$$
p_{a,\max} = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 18 \times 6 = 36 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the total active force per unit length of wall (triangular distribution).

$$
P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{3} \times 18 \times 6^2
$$

$$
P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.3333 \times 18 \times 36 = 108 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 4:** Point of application (centroid of triangle).

$$
\bar{z} = \dfrac{H}{3} = \dfrac{6}{3} = 2 \text{ m from the base}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{P_a = 108 \text{ kN/m acting horizontally at 2.0 m above the base}}
$$

---

## 3.8 Worked Example 2: Retaining Wall — Sliding & Overturning Check (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A gravity retaining wall has the following data:
> - Wall height: **H = 6 m**
> - Base width: **B = 3.0 m**
> - Weight of wall: **W = 180 kN/m** (acting at x = 1.2 m from the toe)
> - Active force: **Pₐ = 108 kN/m** at H/3 = 2 m from the base
> - Coefficient of base friction: **μ = tan(δ) = 0.45**
> - Passive force at toe: **Pₚ = 15 kN/m** (assumed acting at 0.5 m from the toe)
>
> Compute the FOS against sliding and overturning.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the overturning moment about the toe.

The horizontal active force Pₐ tends to **overturn** the wall about the toe (front-bottom corner).

$$
M_O = P_a \times (H/3) = 108 \times 2 = 216 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the resisting moment about the toe.

The vertical weight W acts at a horizontal distance of 1.2 m from the toe, and the passive force Pₚ resists overturning.

$$
M_R = W \times x_W + P_p \times x_{Pp} = 180 \times 1.2 + 15 \times 0.5
$$

$$
M_R = 216 + 7.5 = 223.5 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute FOS against overturning.

$$
FOS_{OT} = \dfrac{M_R}{M_O} = \dfrac{223.5}{216} = 1.035
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is BELOW the safe value of 1.5!** The wall is **unsafe against overturning**. The student should be able to identify this and suggest a remedy (e.g., increase base width or add a heel projection).

**Step 4:** Compute the driving and resisting forces for sliding.

The horizontal active force drives the wall outward. The base friction (proportional to the total vertical load) resists sliding.

$$
\text{Driving force} = P_a = 108 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
\text{Resisting force (friction)} = \mu \cdot W = 0.45 \times 180 = 81 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
\text{Resisting force (passive at toe)} = P_p = 15 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
\text{Total resisting force} = 81 + 15 = 96 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute FOS against sliding.

$$
FOS_{SL} = \dfrac{96}{108} = 0.889
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is BELOW 1.0 — the wall will physically SLIDE!** FOS must be ≥ 1.5. The student should suggest a shear key (toe projection) to engage additional passive resistance.

---

## 3.9 Worked Example 3: Infinite Slope Stability (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> An infinite slope is made of cohesionless soil with $\phi' = 32°$ and the slope angle $\beta = 25°$. The slope is dry (no pore pressure). Compute the factor of safety.

### Solution

**Step 1:** For a cohesionless, dry infinite slope, the FOS reduces to:

$$
FOS = \dfrac{\tan\phi'}{\tan\beta}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
FOS = \dfrac{\tan(32°)}{\tan(25°)} = \dfrac{0.6249}{0.4663} = 1.340
$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion.

$$
\boxed{FOS = 1.34 \text{ — Safe (greater than 1.0, but the standard minimum for permanent slopes is 1.5)}}
$$

> **Note:** If the slope were submerged or had seepage parallel to the slope, the FOS would be **halved** (the buoyant unit weight γ′ ≈ γ/2 is used, and an additional destabilizing seepage force acts).

---

## 3.10 Worked Example 4: Critical Height of a Vertical Cut (Taylor's Method)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A vertical cut is to be made in a clay soil with $c_u = 50 \text{ kN/m}^2$ and $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$. The soil is purely cohesive ($\phi_u = 0$). Using Taylor's stability chart, determine the critical height. Take stability number $N_s = 0.261$ for a vertical cut.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Apply Taylor's formula.

$$
H_{cr} = \dfrac{c \cdot N_c}{\gamma} = \dfrac{c}{\gamma \cdot N_s}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute (using N_s = 0.261):

$$
H_{cr} = \dfrac{50}{18 \times 0.261} = \dfrac{50}{4.698} = 10.64 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Design height with FOS = 1.5:

$$
H_{design} = \dfrac{H_{cr}}{FOS} = \dfrac{10.64}{1.5} = 7.09 \text{ m} \approx 7.1 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{H_{cr} = 10.64 \text{ m}, \quad H_{design} = 7.1 \text{ m (with FOS = 1.5)}}
$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Rankine Earth Pressure States — Wall Movement Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Wall Construction Stage] --> nodeB[Initial State: K0 Condition]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Movement Direction?}
    nodeC -- "Wall moves AWAY from soil" --> nodeD[Active State: Ka is MINIMUM]
    nodeC -- "Wall moves INTO the soil" --> nodeE[Passive State: Kp is MAXIMUM]
    nodeC -- "No wall movement" --> nodeF[At-Rest State: K0 is INTERMEDIATE]

    nodeD --> nodeG[Soil mass EXPANDS laterally]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Soil mass COMPRESSES laterally]
    nodeF --> nodeI[No lateral strain in soil]

    nodeG --> nodeJ[Failure: soil shears outward and downward]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Failure: soil heaves upward in front of wall]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Stable elastic equilibrium]

    style nodeF fill:#4a4a4a,color:#ffffff
    style nodeJ fill:#ff6347,color:#ffffff
    style nodeK fill:#90ee90,color:#000000
    style nodeL fill:#87ceeb,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Retaining Wall Stability — Four Mandatory Checks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Retaining Wall Geometry and Loads] --> checks{Four Stability Checks}

    checks --> chk1[Check 1: Overturning about Toe]
    checks --> chk2[Check 2: Sliding along Base]
    checks --> chk3[Check 3: Bearing Capacity Failure]
    checks --> chk4[Check 4: Deep-Seated Shear Failure]

    chk1 --> calc1[Compute Mo and Mr]
    calc1 --> fos1{FOS_OT is greater than 1.5?}
    fos1 -- Yes --> pass1[Safe against Overturning]
    fos1 -- No --> fail1[Increase Base Width or Add Heel]

    chk2 --> calc2[Compute H_drive and H_resist]
    calc2 --> fos2{FOS_SL is greater than 1.5?}
    fos2 -- Yes --> pass2[Safe against Sliding]
    fos2 -- No --> fail2[Add Shear Key at Toe]

    chk3 --> calc3[Compute e and q_max]
    calc3 --> fos3{q_max is less than q_allow and e is less than B by 6?}
    fos3 -- Yes --> pass3[Safe against Bearing Failure]
    fos3 -- No --> fail3[Lengthen Base or Improve Soil]

    chk4 --> calc4[Swedish Circle Analysis]
    calc4 --> fos4{FOS_DSS is greater than 1.5?}
    fos4 -- Yes --> pass4[Safe against Deep Failure]
    fos4 -- No --> fail4[Use Piles or Wider Base]

    pass1 --> final[Wall is DESIGN SAFE]
    pass2 --> final
    pass3 --> final
    pass4 --> final

    fail1 --> revise[Revise Wall Design]
    fail2 --> revise
    fail3 --> revise
    fail4 --> revise

    style start fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Active Earth Pressure Distribution — Cohesionless vs Cohesive

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph caseA[Case A: Cohesionless Soil c equals 0]
        a1[Top of Wall: pa is ZERO] --> a2[Linear Increase with Depth]
        a2 --> a3[Base of Wall: pa equals Ka gamma H]
        a3 --> a4[Resultant Pa is at H by 3 from Base]
    end

    subgraph caseB[Case B: Cohesive Soil c is greater than 0]
        b1[Top of Wall: pa is NEGATIVE theoretical] --> b2[Tension Crack Depth z0 equals 2c over gamma root Ka]
        b2 --> b3[z0 to H: pa is POSITIVE triangular]
        b3 --> b4[Base: pa equals Ka gamma H minus 2c root Ka]
    end

    subgraph caseC[Case C: Purely Cohesive phi equals 0]
        c1[At Depth z0 equals 2c over gamma] --> c2[Beyond z0, pa is constant: pa equals minus 2c]
        c2 --> c3[Parabolic distribution from z0 to H]
        c3 --> c4[Total Pa is computed using Trapezoidal Rule]
    end

    style a1 fill:#87ceeb,color:#000000
    style a3 fill:#8b0000,color:#ffffff
    style b1 fill:#ffeb3b,color:#000000
    style b2 fill:#ff6347,color:#ffffff
    style c1 fill:#90ee90,color:#000000
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    type -- "Infinite Slope, c equals 0" --> inf1[Use FOS equals tan phi over tan beta]
    type -- "Infinite Slope, c greater than 0" --> inf2[Use Infinite Slope Formula with u]
    type -- "Finite Slope, Homogeneous" --> fin1[Use Taylor Chart: Ns equals c over gamma H]
    type -- "Finite Slope, Layered Soil" --> fin2[Use Swedish Circle Fellenius Method]
    type -- "Slope with Subsoil Tension Crack" --> fin3[Use Bishops Simplified Method]

    inf1 --> res1[Direct FOS Computation]
    inf2 --> res2[Check Submerged Condition and Seepage]
    fin1 --> res3[Read Ns from Taylor Chart for given beta and D]
    fin2 --> res4[Iterative Slicing Required]
    fin3 --> res5[Convergent Iterative Procedure]

    res1 --> final[Report FOS and Check against Minimum 1.5]
    res2 --> final
    res3 --> final
    res4 --> final
    res5 --> final
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Active vs Passive Earth Pressure
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **active earth pressure** and **passive earth pressure** with neat sketches. State the conditions under which each is mobilized.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Earth Pressure (Pₐ) | Passive Earth Pressure (Pₚ) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Minimum lateral pressure when the wall moves **away** from the backfill | Maximum lateral pressure when the wall moves **into** the backfill |
| **Wall Movement** | Wall moves away (e.g., cantilever wall tilting outward) | Wall pushed into soil (e.g., bridge abutment under load) |
| **Principal Stress** | σᵥ = σ₁ (major), σₕ = σ₃ (minor) | σᵥ = σ₃ (minor), σₕ = σ₁ (major) |
| **Magnitude** | $K_a < 1$, so Pₐ is **smaller** than vertical stress | $K_p > 1$, so Pₚ is **larger** than vertical stress |
| **Mobilization** | Requires very small wall movement (≈ 0.1% H for dense sand) | Requires much larger movement (≈ 1–5% H) |

> **Sketch (Text-based):** Active — wall tilts away, soil wedge slides downward. Passive — wall pushed forward, soil wedge is pushed up.

**Marks Distribution:** 1 mark definition of each + 1 mark comparison with sketch reference.

---

### Question A2: Rankine vs Coulomb
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Compare **Rankine's earth pressure theory** with **Coulomb's earth pressure theory**. List **two advantages** of Coulomb's theory over Rankine's.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Rankine's Theory (1857):**
- Assumes **smooth, vertical** wall and **horizontal** backfill.
- Failure surface is a **plane** (mathematical simplification).
- Wall friction δ = 0.

**Coulomb's Theory (1776):**
- Considers **wall friction (δ)**, **wall inclination (θ)**, and **backfill slope (β)**.
- Failure surface is **planar** but more representative of actual field conditions.

**Two Advantages of Coulomb's Theory:**
1. It accounts for the **inclination of the wall back** (θ) and the **slope of the backfill** (β), making it applicable to real field conditions.
2. It incorporates **wall-soil friction (δ)**, which is always present in practice and significantly affects the active force magnitude (typically reduces Pₐ by 10–25%).

**Marks Distribution:** 1 mark assumptions + 1 mark key difference + 1 mark for two valid advantages.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Internal choice is mandatory** — the student must answer EITHER (a) OR (b) of the chosen question.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**A(a) [7 marks]:** A **gravity retaining wall** is **6 m high** with a **vertical back** and **horizontal granular backfill**. The soil properties are $\phi = 30°$, $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$, and $c = 0$. The wall has a **base width of 3.0 m** and its **center of gravity lies at 1.2 m from the toe**. The coefficient of base friction is $\mu = 0.5$. Determine:
   1. The magnitude and point of application of the **active earth pressure**.
   2. The **factor of safety against sliding**.

**A(b) [7 marks]:** If, in addition, a **surcharge load of 20 kN/m²** is applied on the backfill, recalculate the active earth pressure. Comment on how the surcharge affects the wall's stability.

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A(a)

**Step 1: Active Pressure Coefficient (Rankine, smooth wall, horizontal backfill)**

$$
K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) = \tan^2(30°) = \dfrac{1}{3} = 0.333
$$

**Step 2: Active Pressure Intensity at Base (z = H = 6 m)**

$$
p_{a,\max} = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H = 0.333 \times 18 \times 6 = 36 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Total Active Force (Triangular Distribution)**

$$
P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.333 \times 18 \times 36 = 108 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 4: Point of Application (centroid of triangle)**

$$
\bar{z} = \dfrac{H}{3} = \dfrac{6}{3} = 2 \text{ m from base}
$$

**Step 5: FOS against Sliding**

- Driving horizontal force: $H_d = P_a = 108 \text{ kN/m}$
- Resisting force: $H_r = \mu \cdot W = 0.5 \times 180 = 90 \text{ kN/m}$ (assuming W = 180 kN/m as given)

$$
FOS_{SL} = \dfrac{H_r}{H_d} = \dfrac{90}{108} = 0.833
$$

**Conclusion:** $FOS_{SL} < 1.0$, the wall is **unsafe against sliding**. A shear key must be provided.

> **Marks Split:** 2 marks for Kₐ + 2 marks for Pₐ + 1 mark for point of application + 2 marks for FOS computation.

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A(b)

**Surcharge Equivalent Height:**

A uniform surcharge q is equivalent to an additional soil column of height $h_s$:

$$
h_s = \dfrac{q}{\gamma} = \dfrac{20}{18} = 1.111 \text{ m}
$$

**Modified Active Force with Surcharge:**

The total active force becomes the sum of:
1. The active force due to the soil (already calculated): $P_a = 108 \text{ kN/m}$
2. The active force due to the surcharge: $P_{a,q} = K_a \cdot q \cdot H = 0.333 \times 20 \times 6 = 40 \text{ kN/m}$

**Total Active Force with Surcharge:**

$$
P_{a,total} = 108 + 40 = 148 \text{ kN/m}
$$

This force acts at **H/2 = 3 m from the base** (the surcharge component has a rectangular distribution).

**Comment on Stability:**

The surcharge **increases the active force by ~37%** and shifts the resultant force **upward** (from 2 m to a higher effective location), thereby:
- Increasing the **overturning moment** (driving the wall to rotate about the toe).
- Increasing the **sliding force** (decreasing the FOS against sliding).

> **Marks Split:** 2 marks for equivalent height + 2 marks for surcharge-induced force + 2 marks for total force + 1 mark for stability comment.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**B(a) [7 marks]:** A **cohesionless soil** with $\phi = 35°$ and $\gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$ forms a **slope of 1V:2H** (1 vertical to 2 horizontal). The slope is **dry and infinite in extent**. Compute the **factor of safety** of the slope. If the slope becomes **submerged**, what is the new FOS? Assume $\gamma_{sat} = 20 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

**B(b) [7 marks]:** A **vertical cut** is made in a soft clay with $c_u = 30 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\phi_u = 0°$, and $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$. The depth of the cut is **5 m**. Using Taylor's stability chart (for $\phi = 0$, $N_s = 0.261$), determine the **factor of safety** of the cut.

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B(a)

**Step 1: Slope Angle from Geometry**

A slope of 1V:2H means the slope rises 1 unit vertically for every 2 units horizontally.

$$
\tan\beta = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \beta = 26.57°
$$

**Step 2: FOS for Dry Infinite Slope (Cohesionless)**

For a cohesionless, dry infinite slope:

$$
FOS_{dry} = \dfrac{\tan\phi'}{\tan\beta} = \dfrac{\tan(35°)}{\tan(26.57°)} = \dfrac{0.7002}{0.5000} = 1.400
$$

**Step 3: FOS for Submerged Infinite Slope**

For submerged condition, the effective unit weight is used:

$$
\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 20 - 9.81 = 10.19 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

The FOS for submerged cohesionless slope is also:

$$
FOS_{submerged} = \dfrac{\tan\phi'}{\tan\beta}
$$

The slope angle and friction angle are **unchanged**. Therefore:

$$
FOS_{submerged} = 1.400
$$

> **Wait — this assumes no seepage.** If seepage is parallel to the slope, the FOS becomes:

$$
FOS_{seepage} = \dfrac{\gamma'}{\gamma_{sat}} \times \dfrac{\tan\phi'}{\tan\beta} = \dfrac{10.19}{20} \times 1.400 = 0.713
$$

**Conclusion:** The submerged slope with **seepage parallel to the slope** is **unstable** (FOS < 1).

> **Marks Split:** 2 marks slope angle + 2 marks dry FOS + 2 marks submerged FOS + 1 mark discussion of seepage effect.

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B(b)

**Step 1: Compute Taylor's Stability Number**

For $\phi_u = 0$ and a vertical cut, Taylor's chart gives:

$$
N_s = 0.261
$$

**Step 2: Compute Critical Height**

$$
H_{cr} = \dfrac{c_u}{\gamma \cdot N_s} = \dfrac{30}{18 \times 0.261} = \dfrac{30}{4.698} = 6.385 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Factor of Safety for the Given Depth (H = 5 m)**

The actual height is 5 m, and the critical height is 6.385 m. The FOS is:

$$
FOS = \dfrac{H_{cr}}{H_{actual}} = \dfrac{6.385}{5.0} = 1.277
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion**

$$
\boxed{FOS = 1.28 \text{ — Safe but below the recommended 1.5 for permanent cuts.}}
$$

> **Marks Split:** 1 mark stability number + 2 marks critical height + 3 marks FOS + 1 mark conclusion.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES IN MODULE 4 NUMERICALS — READ THIS CAREFULLY:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert φ/2 correctly:** Many students write $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi)$ instead of $\tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$. This single mistake **wipes out the entire problem** because the subsequent Pₐ, FOS, and stability checks all become wrong.
>
> 2. **Sign errors in the c–φ active pressure formula:** The term $2c\sqrt{K_a}$ is **subtracted** (not added) in the active case. In the passive case, it is **added**. Confusing these is a classic KTU exam trap.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the tension crack:** For c–φ soil, the theoretical active pressure is **negative** near the surface. Students often compute a "negative force" and panic. The correct procedure is: state the tension crack depth z₀, then compute the **effective** force from z₀ to H using a triangular distribution.
>
> 4. **Wrong point of application:** For a **triangular** pressure distribution, the resultant is at **H/3** from the base (centroid). For a **rectangular** (uniform) distribution, it is at **H/2**. For a **trapezoidal** distribution, it is at the **centroid of the trapezoid** (not at H/3 or H/2). Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks per problem.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units:** Pressure in **kN/m²**, force per unit length in **kN/m**, moment in **kN·m/m**. Examiners deduct **0.5 mark** for missing units.
>
> 6. **Not stating the assumed value of γ:** When γ is not given, you MUST state your assumption (e.g., "Assuming γ = 18 kN/m³"). Without this, you may lose 0.5 to 1 mark for incomplete working.
>
> 7. **Surcharge misinterpretation:** A uniform surcharge q (kN/m²) is converted to an equivalent soil height $h_s = q/\gamma$. The total active force due to surcharge is $K_a \cdot q \cdot H$, acting at **H/2** (centroid of rectangle), NOT at H/3.
>
> 8. **FOS definition error:** FOS = (Resisting) / (Driving), NOT the other way around. A FOS of 0.83 means the resisting force is **less than** the driving force — the wall fails.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — MODULE 4 NUMERICALS**

### Earth Pressure Essentials
- **Rankine Active Coefficient:** $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2) = (1 - \sin\phi)/(1 + \sin\phi)$ — Memorize both forms.
- **Rankine Passive Coefficient:** $K_p = \tan^2(45° + \phi/2) = (1 + \sin\phi)/(1 - \sin\phi)$.
- **Sanity check:** $K_a \cdot K_p = 1$ always. If not, recheck your calculation.
- **For c = 0 (sand):** $P_a = \frac{1}{2}K_a \gamma H^2$ at $H/3$ from base.
- **For c–φ soil:** $P_a = \frac{1}{2}K_a \gamma H^2 - 2cH\sqrt{K_a}$. Negative force near top → **tension crack depth** $z_0 = 2c/(\gamma\sqrt{K_a})$.
- **Coulomb's formula** is more complex but handles wall friction δ, wall inclination θ, and backfill slope β. The formula is given in the cheat sheet above.

### Retaining Wall Stability Essentials
- **Four mandatory checks:** (1) Overturning, (2) Sliding, (3) Bearing capacity, (4) Deep-seated shear.
- **Minimum FOS values:** Overturning ≥ 1.5, Sliding ≥ 1.5, Bearing ≥ 3.0.
- **Eccentricity check:** $e \leq B/6$ to avoid tension at the heel.
- **Remedies for low FOS:** Increase base width (reduces overturning), add heel projection (more weight, more moment), add shear key (engages passive resistance at toe), improve foundation soil.

### Slope Stability Essentials
- **Infinite slope, dry, cohesionless:** $FOS = \tan\phi / \tan\beta$. Independent of depth.
- **Infinite slope, submerged with seepage:** $FOS = (\gamma'/\gamma_{sat}) \times \tan\phi/\tan\beta$. **Half the dry value** approximately.
- **Finite slope, Taylor's chart:** $H_{cr} = c \cdot N_c / \gamma = c / (\gamma \cdot N_s)$. For $\phi = 0$, $N_s = 0.261$.
- **Swedish circle method:** Compute FOS as the ratio of total resisting moment to total driving moment about the circle's center.
- **Critical condition for slopes:** $FOS < 1.0$ means failure has already occurred; $FOS < 1.5$ means the slope is not safe for permanent works.

### Numerical Problem-Solving Workflow (Universal KTU Pattern)
1. **Read the problem statement** carefully and **list all given data** (H, φ, c, γ, B, etc.) at the top of your answer sheet.
2. **State the formula** you are going to use (this earns 0.5 to 1 mark for "method" even if your final answer is wrong).
3. **Substitute values** with units shown at every step.
4. **Compute** with a calculator (allowed in KTU exams since 2022).
5. **State the final answer in a box** with units and a one-line conclusion.
6. **Add a sketch** (pressure diagram, wall geometry, or slope cross-section) — this earns 1 mark for presentation.

> **Final Tip:** In KTU's 2024 Scheme, the Part B question typically allows you to **choose between a retaining wall problem and a slope stability problem**. Always attempt the problem in which you are most confident, not necessarily the first one on the paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Formulae (Modified Hiley Formulae Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Dynamic Formulae (Modified Hiley Formulae Only) # MODULE 4: PILE FOUNDATION DESIGN — DYNAMIC FORMULAE ## Unit Focus: Modified Hiley Formulae --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Dynamic Formulae — Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Dynamic Formulae (Pile Driving Formulae):** > A dynamic pile driving formula is an empirical/analytical relationship used to estimate the **ultimate static bearing capacity of a pile** from the **observed resistance of the soil during driving** (i.e., the penetration per hammer blow). These formulae are based on the principle of **conservation of energy**, equating the *kinetic energy delivered by the hammer* to the *work done in driving the pile* (penetration work + energy losses). Dynamic formulae are the simplest field methods for determining pile capacity immediately after driving. They are used in conjunction with **static load tests** for verification. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Nail and Hammer Imagine you are hammering a **nail** into a wooden plank: - You raise the hammer to a height $h$ → this stores **potential energy** ($W_H \cdot h$). - The hammer falls and strikes the nail → some of this energy pushes the nail down (called **set, $s$**) and some is "wasted" as: - A small **bounce-back** (the hammer doesn't transfer 100% of its energy → inefficiency). - **Squashing** of the wood, the nail head, and the hammer cushion (called **temporary elastic compression, $c$**). - Heat, sound, and vibrations. The **Modified Hiley Formula** is essentially a "**fair accounting book**" that says: > *"The energy the hammer delivers is split between (1) actually pushing the pile into the ground, and (2) the temporary squashing/bouncing. Whatever load is needed to produce the actual push equals the pile's ultimate capacity."* Just as the difficulty of pushing the nail into harder wood is reflected in the **smaller set per blow**, the pile's capacity is inferred from the **final set** (penetration of the last blow, in mm). --- ### 1.3 Classification of Dynamic Formulae (Context Setting) Dynamic formulae are broadly divided into two families: | Family | Governing Principle | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Energy-based** | Energy delivered = Penetration work + losses | **Modified Hiley**, Engineering News, Gates | | **Momentum / Impulse-based** | Momentum at impact governs | Janbu, Eytelwein | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Scope:** > The current module restricts you to the **Modified Hiley Formula** (an energy-balance formula with explicit corrections for hammer efficiency, coefficient of restitution, and elastic compression). Other formulae are out-of-scope for this unit. --- ### 1.4 Typical Hammers Used with the Modified Hiley Formula The Modified Hiley formula is best suited for **drop hammers** and **single-acting hammers** where the ram is raised and allowed to fall freely (or nearly freely): - **Drop hammers** — released by a clutch from a known height $h$. - **Single-acting steam/air hammers** — ram lifted by steam/air, falls by gravity. - It is **not recommended** for double-acting/differential hammers (energy is not simply $W_H h$). Key standard metric reminders: - $h$ is in **mm** (or m) — keep units consistent. - $s$ (final set) is in **mm**. - $c$ (temporary elastic compression) is in **mm**. - $W_H$, $W_R$, $W_P$ are in **kN** (or tonnes-force — keep consistent). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Energy Balance — The Heart of the Modified Hiley Formula Consider a hammer of weight $W_H$ falling freely through a height $h$ onto a pile (weight $W_R$) carrying a pile cap / helmet / anvil (weight $W_P$). **Energy Input (per blow):** $$E_{\text{input}} = W_H \cdot h$$ **This input energy is partitioned into four parts:** 1. **Useful work of penetration** — pushing the pile down by the final set $s$: $$E_{\text{use}} = Q_u \cdot s$$ 2. **Work done to elastically compress the pile, cap, cushion, and soil** — represented by a single equivalent quantity $c$ (Temporary Elastic Compression, TEC). This work is partly recovered (rebound), so the **net** energy stored in this compression is $Q_u \cdot c/2$ (triangle of force vs. compression). $$E_{\text{comp}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, Q_u \cdot c$$ 3. **Energy lost to hammer–cap rebound** (depends on relative weights and coefficient of restitution $e$). 4. **Energy lost to soil resistance below the pile tip, to hammer cushion damping, and to vibration/heat.** The Modified Hiley formula provides an empirical **Hammer Efficiency Factor** $\eta$ to lump all the "losses" and the **swing-of-hammer factor** $\eta_h$ for the drop-hammer fall. --- ### 2.2 The Modified Hiley Formula — Final Form > [!IMPORTANT] > **THE MODIFIED HILEY FORMULA (Ultimate Pile Capacity):** > > $$Q_u = \dfrac{\eta_h \cdot \eta_b \cdot W_H \cdot h}{s + \dfrac{c}{2}}$$ > > where the **Hammer Efficiency / Energy Transfer Factor** $\eta_b$ is: > > $$\eta_b = \dfrac{W_R + e^2 \, W_P}{W_R + W_P}$$ > > and $\eta_h$ is the **Stroke Efficiency of the Hammer** (ratio of actual energy per blow to rated energy), typically: > - $\eta_h = 1.0$ for free-fall drop hammers > - $\eta_h = 0.85 - 0.95$ for single-acting hammers (depends on manufacturer) > - $\eta_h = 0.70 - 0.90$ for double-acting/differential hammers The **Allowable Load** is then obtained by dividing the ultimate capacity by a Factor of Safety: $$Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{F}$$ For the Modified Hiley formula, KTU standard practice (per IS 2911 Part 1 Sec 2 guidelines) recommends: $$F \;=\; 4 \text{ to } 6 \quad (\text{typically } F = 5 \text{ for preliminary design})$$ --- ### 2.3 Coefficient of Restitution, $e$ > [!NOTE] > The **Coefficient of Restitution (COR), $e$**, is the ratio of relative velocity of separation to relative velocity of approach between the hammer ram and the pile cap (or anvil) at the moment of impact. > - $e = 1$ → perfectly elastic (no energy lost in the blow itself) > - $e = 0$ → perfectly plastic (all energy lost; hammer "sticks") > - $0 < e < 1$ → realistic case **Standard values (used in KTU problems):** | Hammer / Cushion Combination | Typical $e$ | |---|---| | Cast-iron hammer on pile cap (no cushion) | **0.50** | | Steel hammer on pile cap (no cushion) | **0.55 – 0.60** | | Hammer on **wooden cushion** over precast concrete pile | **0.25 – 0.30** | | Hammer on **Micarta / Conbest / nylon cushion** | **0.40 – 0.50** | | Hammer on **helmet with plastic dolly** | **0.40** | | Diesel hammer (single-acting) | **0.50 – 0.60** | The $e^2$ term inside $\eta_b$ reflects that only the **square of the COR** governs the *post-impact* kinetic energy retained in the cap; the rest is dissipated in the impact deformation. --- ### 2.4 Temporary Elastic Compression, $c$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Temporary Elastic Compression (TEC), $c$**, is the total elastic shortening of the system during the hammer blow. It is the sum: > > $$c \;=\; c_p + c_c + c_s$$ > > where: > - $c_p$ = elastic compression of the **pile shaft** (depends on pile material, length, cross-section, and load) > - $c_c$ = elastic compression of the **cushion / cap / packing** (depends on cushion material and its stiffness) > - $c_s$ = elastic compression of the **soil** (called "quake") at the pile tip (depends on soil stiffness and tip resistance) ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Source of Value | |---|---|---|---| | $Q_u$ | Ultimate pile capacity | kN | To be computed | | $Q_a$ | Allowable pile load | kN | $= Q_u / F$ | | $W_H$ | Weight of hammer (ram) | kN | Given / hammer data | | $W_R$ | Weight of pile | kN | $= (\text{volume}) \times \gamma_{\text{pile}}$ | | $W_P$ | Weight of helmet/cap + dolly + cushion | kN | Given / cap data | | $h$ | Height of fall of hammer (stroke) | mm | Given (drop hammer: actual drop; single-acting: stroke) | | $s$ | Final set (penetration of last blow) | mm | Observed in field (sometimes averaged over the last 5 blows) | | $c$ | Temporary elastic compression | mm | Computed or read from field chart / table | | $e$ | Coefficient of restitution | dimensionless | Table 2.3 above | | $\eta_h$ | Hammer stroke efficiency | dimensionless | Hammer manufacturer; = 1.0 for free-fall | | $\eta_b$ | Hammer–cap energy transfer factor | dimensionless | $\eta_b = (W_R + e^2 W_P) / (W_R + W_P)$ | | $F$ | Factor of safety | dimensionless | 4 to 6 (typically 5) | ### 2.6 Step-by-Step Application Procedure (for KTU Problems) 1. List the **given data** ($W_H$, $W_R$, $W_P$, $h$, $s$, $c$, $e$). 2. Choose $\eta_h$ from the hammer type. 3. Compute $\eta_b = (W_R + e^2 W_P) / (W_R + W_P)$. 4. Compute $Q_u$ using the master formula. 5. Apply factor of safety $F$ to obtain $Q_a$. 6. State the result with units and a remark on whether it agrees with the static load test (if given). ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Construction-stage quick check**: Site engineers compute $Q_u$ from driving records the moment driving finishes, allowing the contractor to decide whether to terminate driving or continue. - **Quality control**: If computed $Q_a$ is far below the design load, the pile is suspected of "refusal" issues (e.g., damaged cushion) and the engineer orders a static load test. - **Pre-design sanity check**: Combined with the **static analysis** (Tomlinson / IS 2911), it bounds the expected pile capacity. - **Used in tender documents** to estimate driving criteria (set vs. load curves) before mobilization. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Energy Equation **Assumptions** (rigid-body, single-blow, no ground-water effects): 1. The pile is driven into a soil of ultimate static resistance $Q_u$ (constant for that blow). 2. The hammer ram and the pile-cap system have rigid-body behaviour between impacts. 3. The pile's tip travels a distance $s$ (the set) during the blow. 4. The pile-cap, pile, and soil deform elastically by a total amount $c$ during the blow. 5. Part of this elastic deformation is recovered (rebound) so the *net* stored elastic energy is $Q_u \cdot c / 2$. 6. The impact between hammer and pile cap is governed by a coefficient of restitution $e$. --- **Step 1 — Energy delivered by the hammer to the cap:** The hammer, falling from height $h$, arrives at the cap with velocity $v_0 = \sqrt{2 g h}$. It carries kinetic energy: $$E_{\text{kinetic}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{W_H}{g} \cdot v_0^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{W_H}{g} \cdot 2 g h = W_H \cdot h$$ If the hammer is not free-falling (single- or double-acting), the actual energy per blow is $\eta_h W_H h$, where $\eta_h$ is the **stroke efficiency**. $$\boxed{E_{\text{input}} \;=\; \eta_h \cdot W_H \cdot h}$$ --- **Step 2 — Post-impact velocity of the cap using Newton's impact equation:** Let $v_1$ be the velocity of the hammer *just before* impact and $v_2$ the velocity of the cap *just after* impact. Conservation of momentum (downward positive): $$\dfrac{W_H}{g} \, v_1 \;=\; \dfrac{W_H}{g} \, v_1' + \dfrac{(W_R + W_P)}{g} \, v_2$$ where $v_1'$ is the post-impact velocity of the hammer (upward if $e < 1$). Coefficient of restitution: $$e \;=\; \dfrac{v_2 - v_1'}{v_1 - 0} \;\Rightarrow\; v_1' \;=\; v_2 - e\, v_1$$ Substituting: $$\dfrac{W_H}{g} v_1 = \dfrac{W_H}{g}(v_2 - e v_1) + \dfrac{(W_R + W_P)}{g} v_2$$ $$W_H v_1 = W_H v_2 - e W_H v_1 + (W_R + W_P) v_2$$ $$W_H v_1 + e W_H v_1 = (W_H + W_R + W_P) v_2$$ $$W_H v_1 (1 + e) = (W_H + W_R + W_P) v_2$$ $$v_2 = \dfrac{W_H (1 + e)}{W_H + W_R + W_P} \cdot v_1$$ > **Note:** In the Modified Hiley formulation, the term $W_H$ is **retained in the numerator** of the energy expression but moved to the **efficiency factor** differently. Following the classical Hiley derivation, the energy transferred to the cap system is $\eta_b \cdot W_H \cdot h$ where $\eta_b$ accounts for the relative weights *and* the COR. --- **Step 3 — Energy carried by the cap system after impact:** The kinetic energy of the cap + pile system after impact is: $$E_{\text{cap, post}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{(W_R + W_P)}{g} \cdot v_2^2$$ Substituting $v_2$ and simplifying (using $v_1^2 = 2 g h$): $$E_{\text{cap, post}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{(W_R + W_P)}{g} \cdot \left[\dfrac{W_H (1+e)}{W_H + W_R + W_P}\right]^2 \cdot 2 g h$$ $$E_{\text{cap, post}} = \dfrac{(W_R + W_P) \, W_H^2 (1+e)^2}{(W_H + W_R + W_P)^2} \cdot h$$ > **Key simplification in the Modified Hiley approach:** Recognising that, in practice, $W_H \ll W_R + W_P$ for most driven piles (since the pile is long and heavy), and the *post-impact energy* is dominantly shared with the cap (rebound), Hiley replaced the above rigorous expression with the *empirical* hammer efficiency: $$\boxed{\eta_b = \dfrac{W_R + e^2 W_P}{W_R + W_P}}$$ so that the **net energy available for penetration + elastic compression** is: $$E_{\text{available}} = \eta_b \cdot W_H \cdot h$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why $e^2$ and not $(1+e)^2$?** > The $(1+e)^2$ term is split by Hiley into: > - The **forward-going fraction** that drives the pile → $1$ > - The **square of the rebound fraction** retained in the cap → $e^2$ > This is an empirical fit validated by extensive field tests (Hiley, 1922; refined later). --- **Step 4 — Energy balance at the pile–soil interface:** The available energy is consumed in: - Penetration of pile by $s$ against the ultimate soil resistance $Q_u$: $E_{\text{pen}} = Q_u \cdot s$. - Temporary elastic compression of pile, cap, and soil (modelled as a triangular load–deformation curve with peak $Q_u$ and recovery to zero): $E_{\text{comp}} = \tfrac{1}{2} Q_u c$. So: $$\eta_b \cdot W_H \cdot h \;=\; Q_u \cdot s \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2} Q_u \cdot c$$ $$Q_u (s + c/2) \;=\; \eta_b \cdot W_H \cdot h$$ $$\boxed{Q_u \;=\; \dfrac{\eta_b \cdot W_H \cdot h}{s + \dfrac{c}{2}}}$$ Including the hammer stroke efficiency $\eta_h$: $$\boxed{Q_u \;=\; \dfrac{\eta_h \cdot \eta_b \cdot W_H \cdot h}{s + \dfrac{c}{2}}}$$ Allowable load: $$\boxed{Q_a \;=\; \dfrac{Q_u}{F} \;=\; \dfrac{\eta_h \cdot \eta_b \cdot W_H \cdot h}{F\,(s + c/2)}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical) **Given:** - Hammer type: Drop hammer, free-fall ($\eta_h = 1.0$). - $W_H = 30 \text{ kN}$ - Pile: Precast concrete, $0.30 \text{ m} \times 0.30 \text{ m} \times 12 \text{ m}$ long, $\gamma_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$. - Helmet / cap weight: $W_P = 5 \text{ kN}$. - Wooden cushion on cap → $e = 0.30$. - Height of fall: $h = 1.0 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ mm}$. - Final set (averaged over last 5 blows): $s = 4 \text{ mm}$ per blow. - Temporary elastic compression: $c = 12 \text{ mm}$ (read from field chart or given). - Factor of safety: $F = 5$. **Step A — Weight of pile:** $$V_{\text{pile}} = 0.30 \times 0.30 \times 12 = 1.08 \text{ m}^3$$ $$W_R = 1.08 \times 24 = 25.92 \text{ kN}$$ **Step B — Hammer efficiency factor $\eta_b$:** $$\eta_b = \dfrac{W_R + e^2 W_P}{W_R + W_P} = \dfrac{25.92 + (0.30)^2 \times 5}{25.92 + 5}$$ $$\eta_b = \dfrac{25.92 + 0.09 \times 5}{30.92} = \dfrac{25.92 + 0.45}{30.92} = \dfrac{26.37}{30.92}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_b = 0.8528 \;(\text{dimensionless})}$$ **Step C — Ultimate pile capacity $Q_u$:** $$Q_u = \dfrac{1.0 \times 0.8528 \times 30 \times 1000}{4 + 12/2}$$ $$Q_u = \dfrac{0.8528 \times 30 \times 1000}{4 + 6} = \dfrac{25584}{10}$$ $$\boxed{Q_u = 2558.4 \text{ kN}}$$ **Step D — Allowable load $Q_a$:** $$Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{F} = \dfrac{2558.4}{5}$$ $$\boxed{Q_a = 511.68 \text{ kN} \approx 512 \text{ kN}}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** If the design load on each pile is, say, 400 kN, this pile is safe by the dynamic formula criterion (with a buffer of ~28%). The site engineer should still schedule a **static load test** on at least one pile in the group to confirm. --- ### 3.3 Sensitivity Table — "What-if" Checks for KTU Problems The Modified Hiley formula is **highly sensitive** to $s$ and $c$. The table below shows how $Q_a$ varies for the worked example (all else fixed): | $s$ (mm) | $c$ (mm) | $s + c/2$ (mm) | $Q_u$ (kN) | $Q_a$ (kN) | Comment | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 2 | 12 | 8 | 3198 | 640 | Stiff driving; very high capacity | | 4 | 12 | 10 | 2558 | 512 | Nominal case | | 6 | 12 | 12 | 2132 | 426 | Looser driving; lower capacity | | 4 | 6 | 7 | 3655 | 731 | Cushion stiffer; $c$ smaller | | 4 | 18 | 13 | 1968 | 394 | Cushion softer; capacity drops | > [!WARNING] > **Common student error in KTU valuations:** Forgetting that $s$ and $c$ must be in the **same units** (both mm, or both m). Mixing them inflates $Q_u$ by a factor of 1000! --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Modified Hiley Formula — Block-Level Functional Architecture The diagram below maps the *logical flow* of the Modified Hiley procedure — the physical pile-driving scene is implicitly embedded in each block. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["INPUT DATA COLLECTION
W_H, W_R, W_P, h, s, c, e, eta_h, F"] --> B["SELECT e FROM HAMMER-CUSHION TABLE"] A --> C["COMPUTE W_R IF NOT GIVEN
W_R = V_pile x gamma_pile"] B --> D["COMPUTE HAMMER EFFICIENCY FACTOR
eta_b = W_R + e^2 x W_P over W_R + W_P"] C --> D A --> E["COMPUTE DENOMINATOR
Den = s + c over 2 (same units)"] D --> F["APPLY MASTER FORMULA
Q_u = eta_h x eta_b x W_H x h over Den"] E --> F F --> G["COMPUTE ALLOWABLE LOAD
Q_a = Q_u over F"] G --> H["OUTPUT
Q_a in kN"] H --> I["CROSS-CHECK
Compare with static load test / design load"] I --> J{"IS Q_a > DESIGN LOAD ?"} J -->|"YES"| K["ACCEPT PILE"] J -->|"NO"| L["REJECT / DRIVE DEEPER / RESTRIKE"] ``` ### 4.2 Physical Schematic of Pile Driving (Textual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD R["HAMMER RAM
Weight W_H"] -->|free fall from height h| C["CUSHION / DOLLY
(inside helmet)"] C --> H["HELMET / PILE CAP
Weight W_P"] H --> P["PILE SHAFT
Weight W_R
Length L"] P --> S["SOIL MEDIUM
Tip resistance Q_t
Shaft friction Q_s"] S -->|offers resistance| P P -->|set per blow s| G["GROUND LINE"] P -.->|elastic compression c| G ``` ### 4.3 Energy Flow in the Modified Hiley Formula ```mermaid flowchart LR E1["W_H x h
(Hammer input)"] --> E2["eta_h correction
(Stroke efficiency)"] E2 --> E3["eta_b correction
(Impact + rebound)"] E3 --> E4["Net effective energy
= eta_h x eta_b x W_H x h"] E4 --> E5["Penetration work
Q_u x s"] E4 --> E6["Elastic compression work
Q_u x c over 2"] E5 --> E7["ULTIMATE Q_u
(solve from Q_u x Den = E_eff)"] E6 --> E7 E7 --> E8["Q_a = Q_u / F
(Allowable Load)"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q.A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What are the basic assumptions of the Modified Hiley formula in estimating the ultimate bearing capacity of driven piles?* **Model Answer:** The Modified Hiley formula is based on the following assumptions: 1. The **driving resistance** of the pile (force resisting penetration) is approximately equal to the **ultimate static bearing capacity** of the pile. 2. The **soil resistance** is constant during a single hammer blow (no strain-rate or relaxation effects). 3. The **energy delivered** by the hammer equals the **work done** in penetrating the pile plus the energy stored in the temporary elastic compression of pile, cap, cushion, and soil. 4. The **hammer–cap impact** is governed by a coefficient of restitution $e$ (Newtonian impact). 5. Energy losses due to heat, sound, and vibration are lumped into the **efficiency factors** $\eta_h$ and $\eta_b$. The basic energy equation used is: $$Q_u = \dfrac{\eta_h \cdot \eta_b \cdot W_H \cdot h}{s + \dfrac{c}{2}}$$ --- **Q.A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *Define the "coefficient of restitution" and state typical values used in the Modified Hiley formula for (a) a cast-iron hammer on pile cap, and (b) a hammer with a wooden cushion.* **Model Answer:** > **Coefficient of Restitution ($e$):** It is the ratio of the relative velocity of separation to the relative velocity of approach between two bodies at the moment of impact. > > Mathematically: $e = v_{\text{separation}} / v_{\text{approach}}$. Typical values used in the Modified Hiley formula: - **(a) Cast-iron hammer on pile cap (no cushion):** $e \approx \mathbf{0.50}$ - **(b) Hammer with wooden cushion (e.g., on a precast concrete pile):** $e \approx \mathbf{0.25 - 0.30}$ The value of $e$ is squared in the hammer efficiency factor: $\eta_b = (W_R + e^2 W_P)/(W_R + W_P)$, reflecting that only the *post-impact* kinetic energy in the cap contributes to the effective driving energy. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.B1.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Choice A]** A precast concrete pile of cross-section $0.40 \text{ m} \times 0.40 \text{ m}$ and length 14 m is driven by a drop hammer through soft clay into a medium-dense sand stratum. The following data are recorded: - Weight of hammer, $W_H = 40 \text{ kN}$ - Weight of pile cap (helmet), $W_P = 6 \text{ kN}$ - Height of fall, $h = 1.20 \text{ m}$ - Final set, $s = 5 \text{ mm}$ per blow - Temporary elastic compression, $c = 15 \text{ mm}$ - Coefficient of restitution (hammer on wooden cushion), $e = 0.30$ - Hammer stroke efficiency, $\eta_h = 1.0$ (free-fall) - Factor of safety, $F = 5$ - Unit weight of reinforced concrete, $\gamma_c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3$ **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compute the ultimate bearing capacity of the pile using the **Modified Hiley formula**. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Determine the allowable load and comment on whether the pile is adequate for a design working load of 800 kN. Also state the limitations of dynamic formulae. --- ##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Step 1: Weight of the pile $W_R$.** $$V_{\text{pile}} = 0.40 \times 0.40 \times 14 = 2.24 \text{ m}^3$$ $$W_R = 2.24 \times 25 = 56.0 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2: Hammer efficiency factor $\eta_b$.** $$\eta_b = \dfrac{W_R + e^2 W_P}{W_R + W_P} = \dfrac{56.0 + (0.30)^2 \times 6}{56.0 + 6}$$ $$\eta_b = \dfrac{56.0 + 0.09 \times 6}{62.0} = \dfrac{56.0 + 0.54}{62.0} = \dfrac{56.54}{62.0}$$ $$\eta_b = 0.9119 \;\;(\text{dimensionless})$$ **Step 3: Apply the Modified Hiley formula.** $$Q_u = \dfrac{\eta_h \cdot \eta_b \cdot W_H \cdot h}{s + c/2}$$ Convert $h$ to mm: $h = 1.20 \text{ m} = 1200 \text{ mm}$. $$Q_u = \dfrac{1.0 \times 0.9119 \times 40 \times 1200}{5 + 15/2}$$ $$Q_u = \dfrac{0.9119 \times 48000}{5 + 7.5} = \dfrac{43771.2}{12.5}$$ $$\boxed{Q_u \approx 3501.7 \text{ kN} \approx 3502 \text{ kN}}$$ --- ##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Step 4: Allowable pile load.** $$Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{F} = \dfrac{3501.7}{5}$$ $$\boxed{Q_a \approx 700.3 \text{ kN}}$$ **Step 5: Adequacy check.** $$Q_a = 700.3 \text{ kN} \;<\; \text{Design load} = 800 \text{ kN}$$ The pile is **inadequate** by the Modified Hiley formula. Possible remedies: - Drive the pile deeper (until a smaller $s$ is obtained, e.g., $s = 4$ mm). - Use a heavier hammer or increase the drop height. - Confirm by a **static load test** before rejecting. **Step 6: Limitations of dynamic formulae:** 1. They assume that the **dynamic driving resistance** equals the **static bearing capacity**, which is not strictly true (driving is faster, soil strength may differ). 2. They do not account for **soil setup** (thixotropy, reconsolidation around pile) or **relaxation** over time. 3. They are empirical and depend heavily on the **final set**, which is often inaccurately measured in the field. 4. The **temporary elastic compression $c$** is hard to determine accurately without instrumentation. 5. They cannot be used for **bored piles** or **driven-and-grouted** piles. 6. They should always be supplemented by a **static load test** for the working pile. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q.B1.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Choice B]** A steel tube pile of 0.50 m outer diameter, 0.45 m inner diameter, and 18 m length is driven by a single-acting hammer into a silty sand stratum. The following driving data are recorded: - Weight of hammer (ram), $W_H = 50 \text{ kN}$ - Stroke efficiency, $\eta_h = 0.90$ (single-acting) - Weight of pile, $W_R = 90 \text{ kN}$ (computed from steel section) - Weight of helmet, $W_P = 8 \text{ kN}$ - Height of fall (effective stroke), $h = 0.80 \text{ m}$ - Coefficient of restitution (steel hammer on steel helmet with nylon dolly), $e = 0.45$ - Final set, $s = 3 \text{ mm}$ per blow - Temporary elastic compression, $c = 10 \text{ mm}$ - Factor of safety, $F = 4$ **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Estimate the ultimate and allowable pile capacity using the **Modified Hiley formula**. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If, during driving, the wooden cushion becomes soft and the coefficient of restitution reduces to $e = 0.20$ (with $c$ rising to 18 mm, set unchanged), recompute the allowable load and explain the practical implications. --- ##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Step 1: Hammer efficiency factor $\eta_b$.** $$\eta_b = \dfrac{W_R + e^2 W_P}{W_R + W_P} = \dfrac{90 + (0.45)^2 \times 8}{90 + 8}$$ $$\eta_b = \dfrac{90 + 0.2025 \times 8}{98} = \dfrac{90 + 1.62}{98} = \dfrac{91.62}{98}$$ $$\eta_b = 0.9349 \;\;(\text{dimensionless})$$ **Step 2: Ultimate capacity $Q_u$.** Convert $h$ to mm: $h = 0.80 \text{ m} = 800 \text{ mm}$. $$Q_u = \dfrac{\eta_h \cdot \eta_b \cdot W_H \cdot h}{s + c/2} = \dfrac{0.90 \times 0.9349 \times 50 \times 800}{3 + 10/2}$$ $$Q_u = \dfrac{0.90 \times 0.9349 \times 40000}{3 + 5} = \dfrac{33656.4}{8}$$ $$\boxed{Q_u = 4207.05 \text{ kN} \approx 4207 \text{ kN}}$$ **Step 3: Allowable load $Q_a$.** $$Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{F} = \dfrac{4207.05}{4}$$ $$\boxed{Q_a \approx 1051.8 \text{ kN} \approx 1052 \text{ kN}}$$ --- ##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Step 1: New $\eta_b$ with $e = 0.20$.** $$\eta_b' = \dfrac{90 + (0.20)^2 \times 8}{90 + 8} = \dfrac{90 + 0.04 \times 8}{98} = \dfrac{90 + 0.32}{98} = \dfrac{90.32}{98}$$ $$\eta_b' = 0.9216$$ **Step 2: New $Q_u$ with $c = 18$ mm.** $$Q_u' = \dfrac{0.90 \times 0.9216 \times 50 \times 800}{3 + 18/2} = \dfrac{0.90 \times 0.9216 \times 40000}{3 + 9}$$ $$Q_u' = \dfrac{33177.6}{12} = 2764.8 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3: New allowable load $Q_a'$.** $$Q_a' = \dfrac{2764.8}{4} = 691.2 \text{ kN}$$ $$\boxed{Q_a' \approx 691 \text{ kN}}$$ **Step 4: Practical implications.** - The **allowable load dropped from 1052 kN to 691 kN** (a fall of ~34%) simply because the cushion softened and $c$ increased. This illustrates the **extreme sensitivity** of the Modified Hiley formula to driving-condition parameters. - A soft cushion reduces the impact stiffness and dissipates more energy as heat/sound rather than transmitting it to the pile. - The pile is still adequate if the design load is $\le 691$ kN, but if the design load was around 1000 kN, the engineer must: 1. **Stop driving** and replace the cushion. 2. **Continue driving** to a smaller final set (e.g., $s = 2$ mm). 3. **Re-verify** with a static load test. - This is the classic KTU-style **"what-if" sensitivity question** — it tests whether the student understands that pile capacity is governed by *driving-system health*, not just by pile dimensions. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Modified Hiley Questions:** > 1. **Unit mismatch (costs 2–3 marks!):** Mixing $h$ in metres and $s$, $c$ in millimetres inflates $Q_u$ by 1000×. Always convert all length terms to the *same* unit before substitution. > 2. **Forgetting $\eta_h$** (stroke efficiency): For single-acting and double-acting hammers, $\eta_h \neq 1$. Drop hammers in free-fall are the only case where $\eta_h = 1$. > 3. **Squaring $e$ wrongly:** Some students use $e$ instead of $e^2$ in $\eta_b$. Both are dimensionless, so the numerical error may be hidden. > 4. **Using $W_H$ instead of $W_R$ in the denominator of $\eta_b$:** The denominator of $\eta_b$ is $W_R + W_P$ (pile + cap), not $W_H + W_R + W_P$. > 5. **Confusing the role of $c/2$:** It comes from the *triangular* nature of the load–elastic-compression curve; do not replace $c/2$ with $c$. > 6. **Forgetting to divide by $F$** when the question asks for *allowable* or *safe* load. > 7. **Omitting the static load test cross-check** in the concluding remark — KTU examiners often allocate 1–2 marks for stating the limitations and the need for verification. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — MODIFIED HILEY FORMULA** - **Master Formula:** $Q_u = \dfrac{\eta_h \cdot \eta_b \cdot W_H \cdot h}{s + c/2}$ - **Efficiency Factor:** $\eta_b = \dfrac{W_R + e^2 W_P}{W_R + W_P}$ - **Allowable Load:** $Q_a = Q_u / F$, with $F = 4$ to $6$ (default $F = 5$ for KTU problems unless otherwise stated). - **Units:** Keep $h$, $s$, $c$ in the **same** unit (mm or m) — otherwise answer is off by 1000×. - **Hammer types:** Drop hammer ($\eta_h = 1$); Single-acting ($\eta_h = 0.85 - 0.95$); Double-acting ($\eta_h = 0.70 - 0.90$). - **Typical $e$ values:** 0.50 (cast-iron on cap), 0.25–0.30 (wooden cushion), 0.45 (nylon/Micarta), 0.55 (steel on steel). - **$c$ components:** $c = c_p + c_c + c_s$ (pile + cushion + soil quake). - **Sensitivity:** $Q_u$ is most sensitive to $s$ and $c$. A change of 1 mm in $s$ at small $s$ values (e.g., $s = 2$ mm) can change $Q_u$ by 25–40%. - **Validity:** Best for drop hammers and single-acting hammers; do NOT use for bored, cast-in-situ, or jet-grouted piles. - **Limitations:** Cannot account for *soil setup/relaxation*, *pile fatigue*, or *post-driving pore-pressure dissipation*. Always cross-check with a **static load test** for at least one working pile in every 100–250 piles (per IS 2911). - **Why the formula is "Modified":** Compared to the original Hiley, the Modified Hiley explicitly keeps the **stroke efficiency $\eta_h$** separate (not folded into a single $W_H h$ term) and uses the **$e^2$** term for post-impact energy retained, which makes it more transparent for routine site computation. - **Energy sign convention:** All weights ($W_H$, $W_R$, $W_P$) and heights ($h$, $s$, $c$) are positive; the direction of motion is implicit. - **KTU-style answers must include:** a labelled free-body sketch (or block diagram), a step-by-step substitution, unit consistency check, and a closing statement on adequacy and verification by load test. --- > **END OF NOTES — MODIFIED HILEY FORMULA (MODULE 4)**
