<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define group action in pile foundations. Under what soil conditions is the block failure mode critical?**

**Model Answer:**
Group action refers to the mutual interaction of stress zones of closely spaced piles connected by a pile cap, leading to a combined load-transfer mechanism. When spacing is less than 3 pile diameters, the soil enclosed by the perimeter piles acts as a monolithic block. **Block failure is critical in soft, saturated clays** where the undrained shear strength of the soil between piles is lower than the sum of individual pile skin frictions.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Converse–Labarre formula for group efficiency. What are its limitations?**

**Model Answer:**
The Converse–Labarre formula is:

$$\eta_g = 1 - \dfrac{\theta}{90} \cdot \dfrac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{m n}$$

where $\theta = \tan^{-1}(D/s)$ in degrees, $m$ and $n$ are the number of rows and columns respectively.
**Limitations:**
- Empirical; valid only for **driven piles in clay**.
- Does not account for **pile cap rigidity**.
- Assumes **uniform soil profile**.
- Inaccurate for very large groups ($n > 25$) or **bored piles**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]

**A pile group of 16 piles arranged in a 4 × 4 pattern is proposed in a soft clay deposit.**
- Pile diameter: $D = 0.5$ m
- Pile length: $L = 15$ m
- Centre-to-centre spacing: $s = 1.5$ m
- Undrained cohesion: $c = 50$ kN/m²
- Adhesion factor: $\alpha = 0.9$
- Single pile capacity: $Q_s = 700$ kN
- Take $N_c = 9$

**Compute:**
- **(a)** Group efficiency using the Converse–Labarre formula.
- **(b)** Block failure capacity and the design capacity of the group, applying a factor of safety of 2.5.

#### 🔹 Part (a) — Group Efficiency (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute the overlap angle θ**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{s}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.5}{1.5}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.3333) = 18.435^\circ$$

**Step 2: Bracket term with m = 4, n = 4**

$$\dfrac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{m n} = \dfrac{(3)(4) + (3)(4)}{4 \cdot 4} = \dfrac{24}{16} = 1.5$$

**Step 3: Apply Converse–Labarre formula**

$$\eta_g = 1 - \dfrac{18.435}{90} \times 1.5 = 1 - 0.2048 \times 1.5 = 1 - 0.3072 = 0.6928$$

$$\boxed{\eta_g \approx 69.28\ \%}$$

#### 🔹 Part (b) — Block Failure & Design Capacity (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Block dimensions**

$$B_g = (n-1)s + D = (3)(1.5) + 0.5 = 5.0\ \text{m}$$
$$L_g = (m-1)s + D = 5.0\ \text{m}$$
$$A_b = 5.0 \times 5.0 = 25.0\ \text{m}^2$$
$$P_b = 2(5.0 + 5.0) = 20.0\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2: End-bearing contribution**

$$Q_b = c \cdot N_c \cdot A_b = 50 \times 9 \times 25.0 = 11{,}250\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 3: Skin-friction contribution**

$$Q_{s,\text{block}} = \alpha \cdot c \cdot P_b \cdot L = 0.9 \times 50 \times 20.0 \times 15 = 13{,}500\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 4: Total block capacity**

$$Q_{g,\text{block}} = 11{,}250 + 13{,}500 = 24{,}750\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 5: Individual-pile-sum capacity**

$$Q_{g,\text{ind}} = n \cdot Q_s = 16 \times 700 = 11{,}200\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 6: Design capacity (lesser governs)**

$$Q_g = \min(11{,}200,\ 24{,}750) = 11{,}200\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 7: Allowable load with FS = 2.5**

$$Q_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{11{,}200}{2.5} = 4{,}480\ \text{kN}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{allow}} = 4480\ \text{kN}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Conclusion:** Individual pile sum governs. Block failure is **not critical** in this case due to the relatively wide spacing ($s = 3D$).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]

**A 3 × 3 pile group is to be installed in a clay stratum.** The following data are given:
- Pile diameter: $D = 0.45$ m
- Pile length: $L = 10$ m
- Centre-to-centre spacing: $s = 1.0$ m
- Undrained cohesion: $c = 40$ kN/m²
- Single pile capacity: $Q_s = 500$ kN
- $N_c = 9$, $\alpha = 0.8$

**Compute:**
- **(a)** Group efficiency by Converse–Labarre and the ultimate group capacity.
- **(b)** Settlement of the group at a depth of 5 m below the pile tip, using the 2V:1H method, if a uniformly distributed load of 3000 kN is applied on the equivalent raft.

Assume the underlying compressible clay has compression index $C_c = 0.25$, initial void ratio $e_0 = 0.9$, and thickness $H = 5$ m.

#### 🔹 Part (a) — Group Efficiency (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Overlap angle**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.45}{1.0}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.45) = 24.23^\circ$$

**Step 2: Bracket term with m = 3, n = 3**

$$\dfrac{(2)(3) + (2)(3)}{9} = \dfrac{12}{9} = 1.333$$

**Step 3: Efficiency**

$$\eta_g = 1 - \dfrac{24.23}{90} \times 1.333 = 1 - 0.2692 \times 1.333 = 1 - 0.3589 = 0.6411$$

$$\boxed{\eta_g \approx 64.11\ \%}$$

**Step 4: Ultimate group capacity**

$$Q_g = \eta_g \cdot n \cdot Q_s = 0.6411 \times 9 \times 500 = 2885\ \text{kN}$$

#### 🔹 Part (b) — Settlement at 5 m Depth (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Equivalent raft dimensions at 5 m below pile tip**

$$B_{\text{eq}} = B_g + z = [(3-1)(1.0) + 0.45] + 5 = 2.45 + 5 = 7.45\ \text{m}$$
$$L_{\text{eq}} = L_g + z = 2.45 + 5 = 7.45\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2: Stress increase at depth**

$$\Delta \sigma_z = \dfrac{3000}{7.45 \times 7.45} = \dfrac{3000}{55.50} = 54.05\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Consolidation settlement (clay layer thickness H = 5 m)**

$$S_c = \dfrac{C_c}{1 + e_0} \cdot H \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_0' + \Delta \sigma_z}{\sigma_0'}\right)$$

Assuming $\sigma_0' = 100$ kN/m² (effective overburden at mid-layer):

$$S_c = \dfrac{0.25}{1.9} \times 5000 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100 + 54.05}{100}\right)$$

$$S_c = 0.1316 \times 5000 \times \log_{10}(1.5405) = 657.9 \times 0.1877 = 123.5\ \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{S_c \approx 123.5\ \text{mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Conclusion:** Such a high settlement is unacceptable for normal structures (limit: 65 mm for raft, 25–40 mm for isolated footings). Hence, the pile group must be redesigned with **closer spacing** or **larger diameter**, or **pre-consolidation** must be used.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Converse–Labarre formula — wrong bracket:** Some students write $\dfrac{(m-1) + (n-1)}{mn}$ instead of $\dfrac{(m-1)n + (n-1)m}{mn}$. This causes **2–3 mark deductions**.
> 2. **θ in degrees vs radians:** The formula uses **degrees** ($\theta / 90$). Forgetting to convert $\tan^{-1}$ output to degrees gives a 30–50% error.
> 3. **Block failure perimeter — missing the +D term:** The block width is $(n-1)s + D$, **not** $(n-1)s$. The +D is the diameter of the corner piles.
> 4. **Forgetting to take the lesser of $n \cdot Q_s$ and $Q_{g,\text{block}}$.** Always present both and state which governs.
> 5. **Neglecting the 2V:1H method's unit consistency:** Stress $\Delta\sigma_z$ is in kN/m² only if the load is in kN and dimensions in metres.
> 6. **In sketches, failing to label the pile cap, pile length L, spacing s, and diameter D** — KTU examiners reward 0.5–1 mark for clean, labelled diagrams.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Group action** = stress-zone interaction between adjacent piles in a pile group.
- ✅ **Minimum spacing** per IS 2911: **2.5 D to 3 D** (3 D is standard).
- ✅ **Group efficiency $\eta_g$** = $Q_g / (n \cdot Q_s)$, usually **less than 1** for clays and **greater than 1** sometimes for sands.
- ✅ **Converse–Labarre formula:** valid for **driven piles in clay**; uses $\theta$ in degrees.
- ✅ **Block failure** is critical in **soft clays with closely spaced piles** ($s < 3D$).
- ✅ **Block capacity in clay:** $Q_{g,\text{block}} = c \cdot N_c \cdot A_b + \alpha \cdot c \cdot P_b \cdot L$.
- ✅ **Design capacity** = **lesser of** individual pile sum and block capacity, divided by **FS = 2.5 to 3.0**.
- ✅ **Settlement estimation:** use **2V:1H** method, with equivalent raft at 2/3 pile length (or pile tip).
- ✅ **Settlement ratio $R_s$** for pile groups in clay ≈ **3 to 8** typically.
- ✅ For **cohesionless soils**, group efficiency can exceed 100% due to **soil compaction** during driving.
- ✅ Always **draw a labelled figure** in the KTU exam — it earns easy partial credit.
- ✅ **Practical tip:** Most KTU board questions give 7 marks each for (a) efficiency + (b) block capacity / settlement. Practise both parts separately.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Group Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Group Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In deep foundation engineering, a **pile group** is a cluster of piles connected by a rigid pile cap, designed to transfer heavy superstructure loads to deeper, more competent soil or rock strata. When piles function collectively rather than as isolated units, their individual load-carrying capacities are modified due to **pile-soil-pile interaction phenomena**. The parameter that quantifies this collective behavior is termed **Group Efficiency (η_g)**.

Formally, **Group Efficiency (η_g)** is defined as the ratio of the **actual ultimate load-carrying capacity of a pile group** to the **theoretical sum of the ultimate load capacities of all individual piles acting in isolation**, expressed mathematically as:

$$\eta_g = \frac{Q_{g(\text{ult})}}{n \cdot Q_{p(\text{ult})}}$$

where:
- $Q_{g(\text{ult})}$ = Ultimate load capacity of the pile group (kN)
- $Q_{p(\text{ult})}$ = Ultimate load capacity of a single pile acting independently (kN)
- $n$ = Total number of piles in the group

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Group efficiency is a critical design parameter in Module 4 (Pile Foundations). Students must master both the **Converse-Labarre equation** (for clayey soils) and the **field observation trend** (for sandy soils), along with the **block failure concept** under IS 2911 (Part 1) guidelines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a classroom of students each solving a problem independently in separate rooms. Each student has access to fresh resources (books, internet, energy) and can solve problems at full personal capacity. This represents **isolated single-pile behavior** — no interference.

Now imagine the same students crammed into a small study hall solving the same problem collectively. They start jostling for resources, stepping on each other's workspace, their thinking overlaps, and the combined output is *less* than what each could produce alone. This is **pile group behavior in clay**.

Conversely, if the students are placed in a *compacted* sand pit, the surrounding grains get densified and the group collectively behaves like a stronger composite mass — the team output *exceeds* the sum of individual outputs. This is **pile group behavior in sand**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** In **clay (cohesive soil)**, η_g is typically **less than 1** because stress zones overlap and cause interference shear failure. In **sand (cohesionless soil)**, η_g is often **greater than 1** because driving piles compacts the surrounding sand, increasing its density and shear strength.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Minimum pile spacing (s):** **2.5d** to **3.0d** as per IS 2911 (Part 1) — Section 5.4
- **Critical spacing:** **3.0d** (beyond this, piles behave independently)
- **Typical η_g in clay:** **0.7 to 1.0**
- **Typical η_g in sand:** **1.0 to 2.0**

where **d** = diameter (or width) of the pile.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Group Efficiency Deviates from Unity

The deviation of η_g from 1.0 arises from three fundamental geotechnical mechanisms:

1. **Stress Zone Overlap:** The zone of influence (pressure bulb) around each pile extends roughly **3d to 4d** laterally. When piles are spaced less than this critical distance, the bulbs merge, creating **stress superposition** that reduces effective bearing capacity.
2. **Soil Displacement & Remolding:** Pile driving remolds and displaces surrounding soil. In clay, this causes loss of shear strength (sensitivity effect); in sand, it causes **densification**.
3. **Block Shear Failure:** Closely spaced piles may fail as a single monolithic block (block failure mode) rather than as individual piles, governed by the **Terzaghi bearing capacity** of the imaginary block enclosing all piles plus the **perimeter skin friction** along the block's outer boundary.

## 2.2 Factors Governing Group Efficiency

| S.No | Governing Factor | Influence on η_g |
|------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Pile spacing (s)** | Higher s → η_g approaches 1.0 |
| 2 | **Soil type (clay vs sand)** | Clay → η_g < 1; Sand → η_g > 1 |
| 3 | **Number of piles (n)** | More piles → greater stress interaction → lower η_g |
| 4 | **Pile installation method** | Driven > bored (due to compaction) in sand |
| 5 | **Pile length-to-diameter ratio (L/d)** | Longer piles show greater interaction |
| 6 | **Arrangement geometry** | Square < rectangular (with rows aligned to load) |
| 7 | **Soil sensitivity (clay)** | High sensitivity → very low η_g |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU FORMULAS** — Memorize the following table for Module 4 examination:

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Applicability |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Group Efficiency (General) | $\eta_g = \dfrac{Q_{g(\text{ult})}}{n \cdot Q_{p(\text{ult})}}$ | All soil types |
| 2 | Converse-Labarre Formula | $\eta_g = 1 - \theta \cdot \dfrac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{90 \cdot m \cdot n}$ | Cohesive soils (clay) |
| 3 | Angle in Converse-Labarre | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{s}\right)$ in degrees | All piles |
| 4 | Block Failure — End Bearing | $Q_{b(\text{block})} = A_b \cdot c \cdot N_c^* + A_b \cdot q' \cdot (N_q^* - 1)$ | Block check |
| 5 | Block Failure — Skin Friction | $Q_{s(\text{block})} = 2(B+L) \cdot L \cdot c_a$ | Block perimeter |
| 6 | Minimum Pile Spacing (IS 2911) | $s_{\min} = 2.5d$ to $3.0d$ | IS Code requirement |
| 7 | Critical Spacing | $s_{\text{crit}} = 3.0d$ | Independence threshold |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In the table above, m = number of rows, n = number of columns, d = pile diameter, s = pile center-to-center spacing, c = undrained cohesion, N_c*, N_q* = bearing capacity factors for block geometry, c_a = adhesion along block perimeter, A_b = block base area, B,L = block width and length, q' = effective surcharge at pile tip.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge piers and abutments:** Designed on multi-pile groups where group efficiency dictates pile count and cap dimensions.
- **High-rise building foundations:** Mat-pile systems rely on group behavior; under-prediction causes excessive settlement, over-prediction causes cap failure.
- **Offshore wind turbine jackets:** Group efficiency governs the lateral and axial capacity of clustered monopiles.
- **Industrial tanks and silos:** Heavy concentrated loads are transferred via pile groups; efficiency dictates whether **block failure check** governs design.

## 2.5 Failure Modes of Pile Groups

A pile group may fail by either of two mechanisms, and the design must check **both**:

1. **Individual Pile Failure (n-pile failure):** Each pile fails independently, governed by single-pile capacity. Group capacity = n × Q_p(ult).
2. **Block Failure:** The entire group behaves as a monolithic block. The group punches into the soil as a unit. Group capacity = bearing capacity of the block + skin friction on the block's outer perimeter.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Many students forget to check the **block failure mode** and lose marks. For pile groups with spacing ≤ 3d, **block failure often governs** and yields a *lower* capacity than n-pile failure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Converse-Labarre Formula

The Converse-Labarre formula is an **empirical reduction factor** that accounts for the average loss of bearing capacity due to the overlapping of individual pile stress zones in a rectangular pile group of **m rows × n columns**.

### Step 1: Concept of Overlap Angle

For two adjacent piles of diameter **d** spaced at center-to-center distance **s**, the angle subtended at the base by the effective influence zone of one pile onto its neighbor is:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d}{s}\right) \quad \text{[in degrees]}$$

This represents the angular measure (out of a total 360° around each pile) over which the neighbor's stress zone interferes.

### Step 2: Counting Interference Events

In a group of **m × n** piles:
- Number of pile pairs **within a single row** = (n − 1)
- Number of pile pairs **within a single column** = (m − 1)
- Total pairs within rows (across all rows) = m(n − 1)
- Total pairs within columns (across all columns) = n(m − 1)
- **Total interference events** = m(n − 1) + n(m − 1)

### Step 3: Normalizing the Interference

The **average loss of capacity per pile** (as a fraction) is the ratio of the total interference angle summed over the group to the total angular freedom available:

$$\text{Fractional Loss} = \theta \cdot \frac{m(n-1) + n(m-1)}{90 \cdot m \cdot n}$$

The denominator **90·m·n** emerges because each pile has 360° of angular influence; accounting for symmetry (4 quadrants), each pile effectively contributes 90°/pile, and summed over m·n piles gives 90·m·n.

### Step 4: Assembling the Efficiency

Efficiency = 1 − (Fractional Loss):

$$\eta_g = 1 - \theta \cdot \frac{m(n-1) + n(m-1)}{90 \cdot m \cdot n}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_g = 1 - \theta \cdot \frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{90 \cdot m \cdot n}}$$

This is the **Converse-Labarre equation** universally applied for clayey soils in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A pile group consists of **3 rows × 4 columns** (m = 3, n = 4) of **600 mm diameter** bored piles spaced at **1.5 m center-to-center** in a soft clay deposit. The undrained cohesion of clay is **c = 40 kN/m²** and the average adhesion factor is **α = 0.6**. Length of each pile = **15 m**. Determine the group efficiency using the Converse-Labarre formula and the safe load capacity of the group.

### Step A: Compute the Overlap Angle θ

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d}{s}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.6}{1.5}\right)$$

Computing the ratio:

$$\frac{d}{s} = \frac{0.6}{1.5} = 0.4$$

Therefore:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.4) = 21.80^{\circ}$$

### Step B: Compute the Geometric Factor

$$(n-1)m + (m-1)n = (4-1)(3) + (3-1)(4) = (3)(3) + (2)(4) = 9 + 8 = 17$$

$$m \cdot n = 3 \times 4 = 12$$

$$90 \cdot m \cdot n = 90 \times 12 = 1080$$

### Step C: Compute Group Efficiency

$$\eta_g = 1 - \theta \cdot \frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{90 \cdot m \cdot n}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$\eta_g = 1 - 21.80 \times \frac{17}{1080}$$

Computing the fraction:

$$\frac{17}{1080} = 0.015741$$

Computing the product:

$$21.80 \times 0.015741 = 0.3432$$

Subtracting from unity:

$$\eta_g = 1 - 0.3432 = 0.6568 \approx 0.66$$

### Step D: Compute Single Pile Ultimate Capacity

For a bored pile in clay, ultimate capacity:

$$Q_{p(\text{ult})} = Q_b + Q_s = c \cdot A_b \cdot N_c + \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_s$$

Using $N_c = 9$ (standard for bored piles in clay, per Skempton):

Base area:

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.6)^2 = 0.2827 \text{ m}^2$$

Base resistance:

$$Q_b = c \cdot A_b \cdot N_c = 40 \times 0.2827 \times 9 = 101.77 \text{ kN}$$

Surface area of pile shaft:

$$A_s = \pi \cdot d \cdot L = \pi \times 0.6 \times 15 = 28.274 \text{ m}^2$$

Skin friction:

$$Q_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_s = 0.6 \times 40 \times 28.274 = 678.59 \text{ kN}$$

Total single pile capacity:

$$Q_{p(\text{ult})} = 101.77 + 678.59 = 780.36 \text{ kN}$$

### Step E: Compute Group Capacity and Safe Load

Total number of piles:

$$n_{\text{piles}} = m \times n = 3 \times 4 = 12$$

Theoretical sum of individual capacities:

$$12 \times Q_{p(\text{ult})} = 12 \times 780.36 = 9364.32 \text{ kN}$$

Group ultimate capacity:

$$Q_{g(\text{ult})} = \eta_g \times (n \times Q_{p(\text{ult})}) = 0.66 \times 9364.32 = 6180.45 \text{ kN}$$

Applying Factor of Safety **F = 2.5** (standard for pile groups):

$$Q_{g(\text{safe})} = \frac{6180.45}{2.5} = 2472.18 \text{ kN}$$

### Step F: Check Block Failure

Block dimensions:
- Width $B = (n-1)s + d = (4-1)(1.5) + 0.6 = 5.1$ m
- Length $L = (m-1)s + d = (3-1)(1.5) + 0.6 = 3.6$ m

Block base area:

$$A_{b(\text{block})} = B \times L = 5.1 \times 3.6 = 18.36 \text{ m}^2$$

For block failure (cohesive soil, L/B ratio correction may apply, but conservatively use $N_c = 9$):

$$Q_{b(\text{block})} = c \cdot A_b \cdot N_c = 40 \times 18.36 \times 9 = 6609.6 \text{ kN}$$

Block perimeter skin friction:

$$Q_{s(\text{block})} = 2(B+L) \cdot L \cdot c_a = 2(5.1 + 3.6) \times 15 \times (0.6 \times 40)$$

Computing step by step:

$$2(B+L) = 2(5.1 + 3.6) = 2 \times 8.7 = 17.4 \text{ m}$$

$$c_a = \alpha \cdot c = 0.6 \times 40 = 24 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$Q_{s(\text{block})} = 17.4 \times 15 \times 24 = 6264.0 \text{ kN}$$

Total block capacity:

$$Q_{\text{block(ult)}} = 6609.6 + 6264.0 = 12873.6 \text{ kN}$$

### Step G: Governing Capacity

| Mode | Ultimate Capacity (kN) | Safe Capacity (kN) |
|------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Single-pile sum (with η_g = 0.66) | 6180.45 | 2472.18 |
| Block failure | 12873.6 | 5149.4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The **single-pile group efficiency check** governs (6180.45 kN < 12873.6 kN). The safe group capacity is **2472.18 kN**. Students should clearly state which mode governs and apply the lower value.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Group Efficiency Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pile Group Design] --> B{Determine Spacing s}
    B --> C[Compute n-pile Capacity]
    B --> D[Compute Block Capacity]
    C --> E[Apply Converse-Labarre Formula]
    D --> F[Terzaghi Block Bearing + Perimeter Skin Friction]
    E --> G[Compare Both Capacities]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Which is Lower?}
    H --> I[Governing Ultimate Capacity]
    I --> J[Apply Factor of Safety F=2.5]
    J --> K[Safe Group Capacity Qg safe]
    K --> L[End: Final Design Output]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Pile Group Layout (3x4)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ROW1 [Row 1]
        P11[Pile 1,1] --- P12[Pile 1,2] --- P13[Pile 1,3] --- P14[Pile 1,4]
    end
    subgraph ROW2 [Row 2]
        P21[Pile 2,1] --- P22[Pile 2,2] --- P23[Pile 2,3] --- P24[Pile 2,4]
    end
    subgraph ROW3 [Row 3]
        P31[Pile 3,1] --- P32[Pile 3,2] --- P33[Pile 3,3] --- P34[Pile 3,4]
    end
    P11 --- P21
    P12 --- P22
    P13 --- P23
    P14 --- P24
    P21 --- P31
    P22 --- P32
    P23 --- P33
    P24 --- P34
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Overlap Angle Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Pile 1 Center] --> B1[Stress Zone Radius 3d]
    A2[Pile 2 Center] --> B2[Stress Zone Radius 3d]
    A1 --> A2
    A1 --- A2
    A1 --- C1[diameter d]
    A1 --- C2[Spacing s]
    C2 --> D[Overlap Angle theta equals arctan d by s]
    D --> E[Reduction in Group Efficiency]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Architecture: Group Efficiency Calculation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP[Inputs: d, s, m, n, c, alpha, L] --> A1[Compute theta]
    INP --> A2[Compute single pile Qp ult]
    INP --> A3[Compute block dimensions B and L]
    A1 --> ETA[Converse Labarre Formula]
    A2 --> SUM[n times Qp ult]
    ETA --> CAP1[Eta times n times Qp ult]
    SUM --> CAP1
    A3 --> BLK[Block failure check]
    BLK --> CAP2[Qb block plus Qs block]
    CAP1 --> COMP[Minimum of two capacities]
    CAP2 --> COMP
    COMP --> SAFE[Divide by F equals 2.5]
    SAFE --> OUT[Qg safe output]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Failure Mode Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pile Group Subjected to Load] --> B{Spacing s less than 3d}
    B --> C[Yes: Check Block Failure]
    B --> D[No: Individual Pile Failure Dominates]
    C --> E{Block Capacity less than n-pile capacity}
    D --> F[Governing: n-pile with Efficiency]
    E --> G[Yes: Block Governs]
    E --> H[No: n-pile with Efficiency Governs]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define group efficiency of a pile foundation. State the Converse-Labarre formula. [3 Marks]
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Group efficiency (η_g) is defined as the ratio of the ultimate load-carrying capacity of a pile group to the theoretical sum of the ultimate capacities of all piles acting as isolated units:

$$\eta_g = \frac{Q_{g(\text{ult})}}{n \cdot Q_{p(\text{ult})}}$$

The Converse-Labarre formula (applicable for clayey/cohesive soils) is:

$$\eta_g = 1 - \theta \cdot \frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{90 \cdot m \cdot n}$$

where θ = tan⁻¹(d/s) in degrees, d is pile diameter, s is pile spacing, m is number of rows, and n is number of columns.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between individual pile failure and block failure modes in pile groups. [3 Marks]
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Individual Pile Failure | Block Failure |
|--------|------------------------|---------------|
| Mechanism | Each pile fails independently | Entire group acts as a monolithic block |
| Capacity Formula | $Q_g = \eta_g \cdot n \cdot Q_{p(\text{ult})}$ | $Q_g = Q_{b(\text{block})} + Q_{s(\text{block})}$ |
| Governing Spacing | s > 3d | s ≤ 3d |
| Governing Soil | Variable | Often clayey deposits |
| Critical Check | Sum of individual capacities | Terzaghi bearing + perimeter skin friction |

The design must adopt the **lower** of the two capacities as the governing ultimate capacity.

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**A pile group consists of 4 piles of 500 mm diameter, arranged in a square pattern, spaced at 1.25 m center-to-center. The piles are 12 m long, embedded in clay with undrained cohesion c = 50 kN/m² and adhesion factor α = 0.5. Neglect the end bearing component. Compute: [14 Marks]**
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) The group efficiency using the Converse-Labarre formula. (7 Marks)**

**(b) The safe load capacity of the group with a factor of safety of 3. Also check the block failure capacity. (7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Question A (a):

**Given:** d = 0.5 m, s = 1.25 m, m = 2, n = 2, c = 50 kN/m², α = 0.5, L = 12 m, F = 3.

**Step 1: Compute overlap angle θ**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d}{s}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{1.25}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.4) = 21.80^{\circ}$$

**Step 2: Compute geometric factor**

$$(n-1)m + (m-1)n = (2-1)(2) + (2-1)(2) = 2 + 2 = 4$$

$$90 \cdot m \cdot n = 90 \times 2 \times 2 = 360$$

**Step 3: Apply Converse-Labarre**

$$\eta_g = 1 - 21.80 \times \frac{4}{360} = 1 - 0.2422 = 0.7578 \approx 0.76$$

#### Solution to Question A (b):

**Step 1: Single pile ultimate capacity (skin friction only, end bearing neglected)**

$$A_s = \pi \cdot d \cdot L = \pi \times 0.5 \times 12 = 18.85 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_{p(\text{ult})} = \alpha \cdot c \cdot A_s = 0.5 \times 50 \times 18.85 = 471.24 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Group ultimate capacity (n-pile check)**

$$n \times Q_{p(\text{ult})} = 4 \times 471.24 = 1884.96 \text{ kN}$$

$$Q_{g(\text{ult})} = \eta_g \times 1884.96 = 0.76 \times 1884.96 = 1432.57 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Safe group capacity (n-pile)**

$$Q_{g(\text{safe})} = \frac{1432.57}{3} = 477.52 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Block failure check**

Block dimensions:
- $B = (n-1)s + d = (2-1)(1.25) + 0.5 = 1.75$ m
- $L_b = (m-1)s + d = (2-1)(1.25) + 0.5 = 1.75$ m

Block base area:

$$A_{b(\text{block})} = 1.75 \times 1.75 = 3.0625 \text{ m}^2$$

End bearing (using $N_c = 9$ for square block):

$$Q_{b(\text{block})} = c \cdot N_c \cdot A_b = 50 \times 9 \times 3.0625 = 1378.13 \text{ kN}$$

Perimeter skin friction:

$$Q_{s(\text{block})} = 2(B+L_b) \cdot L \cdot c_a = 2(1.75+1.75) \times 12 \times (0.5 \times 50)$$

$$= 2 \times 3.5 \times 12 \times 25 = 2100 \text{ kN}$$

Total block capacity:

$$Q_{\text{block(ult)}} = 1378.13 + 2100 = 3478.13 \text{ kN}$$

Block safe capacity:

$$Q_{\text{block(safe)}} = \frac{3478.13}{3} = 1159.38 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Governing capacity**

| Mode | Safe Capacity (kN) |
|------|--------------------|
| n-pile with efficiency | 477.52 |
| Block failure | 1159.38 |

**Conclusion:** The **n-pile group efficiency mode governs** with safe capacity = **477.52 kN**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**A pile group of 9 piles (3 × 3 arrangement) with diameter 450 mm and spacing 1.2 m is driven 14 m into a clay layer with c = 60 kN/m² and α = 0.65. The unit weight of soil is 18 kN/m³. Determine: [14 Marks]**
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) The group efficiency and ultimate group capacity. (7 Marks)**

**(b) The safe load per pile and settlement ratio (assume elastic modulus E_s = 8000 kN/m² for clay). (7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Question B (a):

**Given:** d = 0.45 m, s = 1.2 m, m = 3, n = 3, c = 60 kN/m², α = 0.65, L = 14 m.

**Step 1: Overlap angle**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.45}{1.2}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.375) = 20.56^{\circ}$$

**Step 2: Geometric factor**

$$(n-1)m + (m-1)n = (3-1)(3) + (3-1)(3) = 6 + 6 = 12$$

$$90 \cdot m \cdot n = 90 \times 9 = 810$$

**Step 3: Group efficiency**

$$\eta_g = 1 - 20.56 \times \frac{12}{810} = 1 - 0.3046 = 0.6954 \approx 0.70$$

**Step 4: Single pile capacity**

Base area:

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.45)^2 = 0.1590 \text{ m}^2$$

Shaft area:

$$A_s = \pi \times 0.45 \times 14 = 19.79 \text{ m}^2$$

Base resistance:

$$Q_b = c \cdot A_b \cdot N_c = 60 \times 0.1590 \times 9 = 85.86 \text{ kN}$$

Skin friction:

$$Q_s = \alpha \cdot c \cdot A_s = 0.65 \times 60 \times 19.79 = 771.81 \text{ kN}$$

Single pile capacity:

$$Q_{p(\text{ult})} = 85.86 + 771.81 = 857.67 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Group ultimate capacity**

$$Q_{g(\text{ult})} = \eta_g \times n \times Q_{p(\text{ult})} = 0.70 \times 9 \times 857.67 = 5403.3 \text{ kN}$$

#### Solution to Question B (b):

**Step 1: Safe group capacity (F = 2.5)**

$$Q_{g(\text{safe})} = \frac{5403.3}{2.5} = 2161.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Safe load per pile**

$$Q_{\text{per pile}} = \frac{2161.3}{9} = 240.15 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Settlement ratio R_s**

For pile groups in clay, the settlement ratio is empirically:

$$R_s = \frac{(4B + 9)}{B + 12} \cdot \frac{s}{d}$$

Wait, a more standard formula (Skempton et al.) is:

$$R_s = \frac{\text{Settlement of group}}{\text{Settlement of single pile at same load per pile}}$$

Using simplified Vesic's formula:

$$R_s = \sqrt{\frac{B_g}{d}}$$

where $B_g$ = width of pile group = $(n-1)s + d = (3-1)(1.2) + 0.45 = 2.85$ m.

$$R_s = \sqrt{\frac{2.85}{0.45}} = \sqrt{6.33} = 2.517$$

**Step 4: Settlement calculation**

Using rigid footing on equivalent raft at depth $L/3 = 14/3 \approx 4.67$ m below ground (approximate):

Net stress increment on equivalent raft:

$$\Delta q = \frac{Q_{g(\text{safe})}}{B_g \times L_g} = \frac{2161.3}{2.85 \times 2.85} = 266.07 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Influence depth (2B method): $H = 2 \times 2.85 = 5.7$ m.

Settlement by 1D consolidation (approximate):

$$S_g = \frac{\Delta q \cdot H}{E_s} = \frac{266.07 \times 5.7}{8000} = 0.190 \text{ m} = 190 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** Safe load per pile = 240.15 kN; Settlement ratio R_s ≈ 2.52; Estimated group settlement ≈ 190 mm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert θ to degrees** — Students commonly leave θ in radians, producing drastically wrong η_g values.
> 2. **Misidentifying m and n** — Convention: m = number of rows, n = number of columns. Reversing these changes the formula.
> 3. **Omitting block failure check** — A complete solution must compare the n-pile efficiency mode with block failure mode and adopt the lower.
> 4. **Using F = 2.5 vs F = 3** — For pile groups in clay with dead + live loads, **F = 2.5** is typical; with seismic/wind, **F = 2.0** may apply. Always state the F used.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — Maintain d and s in the **same units** (both in meters or both in mm) when computing θ.
> 6. **Overestimating sand efficiency** — η_g > 1 in sand is empirical (often 1.0–1.5); do not extrapolate beyond realistic field data.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Group Efficiency**

- [x] **Group Efficiency (η_g)** = ratio of group capacity to n × single pile capacity.
- [x] **In clay:** η_g < 1 (typically 0.7–1.0); use **Converse-Labarre formula**.
- [x] **In sand:** η_g > 1 (typically 1.0–2.0) due to compaction effect; use field test data.
- [x] **Converse-Labarre:** $\eta_g = 1 - \theta \cdot \dfrac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{90mn}$ with $\theta = \tan^{-1}(d/s)$ in degrees.
- [x] **Minimum pile spacing (IS 2911):** 2.5d (friction piles in compressible strata), 2.0d (end-bearing on rock), 3.0d (loose sand/soft clay).
- [x] **Block failure check is mandatory** when spacing < 3d; it involves Terzaghi bearing + perimeter adhesion.
- [x] **Group settlement** exceeds single pile settlement by factor $R_s = \sqrt{B_g/d}$ (Vesic's approximation).
- [x] **Design capacity** = minimum of (n-pile with efficiency) and (block failure) capacities, divided by F.
- [x] **Standard factor of safety** for pile groups: 2.5 (working loads), 2.0 (seismic), 3.0 (highly sensitive structures).
- [x] **Pile arrangement:** Square < rectangular (with long side parallel to load direction) for higher efficiency.
- [x] **Driving piles in sand increases η_g**; boring/drilling in clay increases η_g (less remolding).
- [x] **Code reference:** IS 2911 (Part 1) - 2010 (latest revision) governs pile group design in India.
- [x] **Always check both** the individual pile failure mode and the block failure mode.
- [x] **Standard convention:** m = rows (along length), n = columns (along width).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity of Pile Groups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET504 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capacity of Pile Groups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Pile Group** is a system of two or more piles connected at the top by a structurally rigid **pile cap**, working in unison to transfer heavy superstructure loads to deeper, stronger soil strata when a single pile is structurally or geotechnically inadequate. The collective vertical load-bearing capacity, settlement behavior, and failure mechanism of such a clustered arrangement are evaluated as an **integrated system**, not as the arithmetic sum of isolated single piles.

The **Capacity of a Pile Group (Q_g)** is defined as the minimum of:
1. The **sum of individual pile capacities** (n × Q_s) — represents individual pile failure.
2. The **block capacity (Q_b)** — represents the group acting as a single monolithic block failing in shear along its perimeter.

Mathematically:
$$Q_g = \min(n \cdot Q_s, \, Q_{block})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A pile group may fail as a *block* even when individual piles are safe. This dichotomy between individual and block failure is the single most examined concept in this module.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bunch of Pencils"

Imagine trying to push a single pencil into sand — it slides in easily and the sand flows around it. But now bundle 9 pencils together tightly (a 3×3 array) and push them in as one rigid unit. The bundle behaves like a single thick "log": the sand cannot flow *between* the pencils, and the entire bundle must be pushed as one. The resistance is governed by the **shear strength along the outer perimeter of the bundle**, not by the friction of each pencil's shaft.

This is the essence of **block failure** in pile groups. When piles are spaced too closely, the soil between them gets "trapped" inside the group, and the group fails collectively. Engineers therefore design with sufficient spacing (typically 2.5D to 3D) to ensure individual pile behavior dominates.

### 1.3 Key Metrics in Pile Group Design

- **Pile Spacing (s):** Center-to-center distance; minimum **2D**, optimum **3D**, maximum **6D** (where D = pile diameter).
- **Group Efficiency (η_g):** Ratio of actual group capacity to the sum of individual capacities.
- **Settlement Ratio (R_s):** Ratio of group settlement to single pile settlement at equivalent working load — typically 2 to 10.
- **Diameter of Pile (D):** Standard metric, typically **0.3 m to 1.5 m** for bored piles, **0.2 m to 0.6 m** for driven piles.

> [!NOTE]
> The **block failure** check is mandatory in **clayey soils** and is often critical. In **sandy soils**, individual pile failure usually governs because soil grains can flow freely between closely-spaced piles.

### 1.4 Why Pile Groups Are Used

1. **High Load Capacity:** A single pile rarely exceeds 2000–3000 kN; groups can carry 10,000+ kN.
2. **Settlement Control:** Distributing load over a wider footprint reduces differential settlement.
3. **Seismic Stability:** Multiple piles resist lateral and uplift forces more effectively.
4. **Practical Constructability:** Pile caps provide a flat surface for column/footing connections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Modes of Failure of a Pile Group

**Mode 1 — Individual Pile Failure (Pile-to-Soil Shear):**
Each pile slips relative to the surrounding soil. The group capacity is simply the sum of n individual capacities:
$$Q_g = n \cdot Q_s$$
This is the **upper bound** for sandy soils with adequate spacing (s ≥ 3D).

**Mode 2 — Block Failure (Block-to-Soil Shear):**
The cap, piles, and trapped soil move as a single unit. The group fails by shear along the perimeter of an equivalent block having plan dimensions (B_g × L_g).
$$Q_{block} = q_b \cdot A_g + \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P_g \cdot L$$
This is **critical in clays** and governs the design.

### 2.2 Group Efficiency (η_g)

The **efficiency of a pile group** quantifies the loss (or gain) in capacity due to pile-soil-pile interaction:
$$\eta_g = \frac{Q_g}{n \cdot Q_s}$$
For design purposes:
$$Q_{design} = \eta_g \cdot n \cdot Q_s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Efficiency is **always less than 1.0** in practice. Typical values: 0.7 to 0.9 for driven piles in sand; 0.4 to 0.8 for bored piles in clay.

### 2.3 Converse–Labarre Formula (Most Commonly Tested in KTU)

$$\eta_g = 1 - \frac{\theta}{90} \cdot \left[ \frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{mn} \right]$$
where:
- $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{s}\right)$ — expressed in **degrees**
- $D$ = pile diameter
- $s$ = center-to-center pile spacing
- $m$ = number of rows
- $n$ = number of piles per row
- $mn$ = total number of piles

### 2.4 Feld's Rule (Quick Approximation for Sand)

$$\eta_g = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{D \cdot [m(n-1) + n(m-1)]}{s \cdot mn}}$$
Used for rough estimates; less accurate than Converse–Labarre.

### 2.5 Block Capacity Formulae

**For Cohesive Soil (Clay):**
$$Q_{block} = c_u \cdot N_c \cdot A_g + \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot P_g \cdot L$$
With $N_c = 9$ (Skempton's value for deep foundations) and:
- $A_g = B_g \cdot L_g$ (plan area of pile group)
- $P_g = 2(B_g + L_g)$ (perimeter of group)
- $B_g = (n-1)s + D$ ; $L_g = (m-1)s + D$

**For Cohesionless Soil (Sand):**
$$Q_{block} = q_b \cdot A_g + \bar{\sigma}_v \cdot K_s \cdot \tan\delta \cdot P_g \cdot L$$
where $\bar{\sigma}_v$ is the average effective vertical stress, $K_s$ is the lateral earth pressure coefficient, and $\delta$ is the pile-soil interface friction angle.

### 2.6 Settlement of Pile Groups (2V:1H Method)

A pile group of length L_b is idealized as an **equivalent raft** located at a depth:
$$z_{eq} = \dfrac{2L_b}{3} \text{ (from pile cap)}$$
or equivalently, at a depth $\dfrac{L_b}{3}$ above the pile tip.

The equivalent raft has plan dimensions $(B_g) \times (L_g)$. The stress increase at depth $z$ below the equivalent raft is:
$$\Delta\sigma_z = \dfrac{Q_g - W_g}{(B_g + z)(L_g + z)}$$
The settlement is then computed using 1D consolidation or elastic theory:
$$\Delta s = \sum \dfrac{C_c}{1+e_0} \cdot H \cdot \log\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_0' + \Delta\sigma}{\sigma_0'}\right)$$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Applicable Soil | Units |
|----------|---------|----------------|-------|
| Group capacity | $Q_g = \min(nQ_s, Q_{block})$ | All | kN |
| Converse–Labarre efficiency | $\eta_g = 1 - \dfrac{\theta}{90}\!\left[\dfrac{(n-1)m+(m-1)n}{mn}\right]$ | Sand/Silt | dimensionless |
| $\theta$ in Converse–Labarre | $\theta = \tan^{-1}(D/s)$ | — | degrees |
| Block capacity (clay) | $Q_{block} = c_u N_c A_g + \alpha \bar{c} P_g L$ | Clay | kN |
| Block base resistance (clay) | $c_u N_c A_g$ with $N_c = 9$ | Clay | kN |
| Block side resistance (clay) | $\alpha \bar{c} P_g L$ | Clay | kN |
| Group plan width | $B_g = (n-1)s + D$ | All | m |
| Group plan length | $L_g = (m-1)s + D$ | All | m |
| Group perimeter | $P_g = 2(B_g + L_g)$ | All | m |
| Stress increase (2:1) | $\Delta\sigma = (Q_g - W_g)/[(B_g+z)(L_g+z)]$ | All | kN/m² |
| Equivalent raft depth | $z_{eq} = 2L_b/3$ from cap | All | m |
| Settlement ratio $R_s$ | $R_s = s_g/s_p$ (typ. 2–10) | All | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **2V:1H method** is the most frequently tested approach for settlement in KTU 2024 papers. Memorize the formula $\Delta\sigma = Q/[(B+z)(L+z)]$ — the divisor dimensions expand at a 1:2 (vertical:horizontal) ratio, hence the name.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Converse–Labarre Efficiency Formula

The Converse–Labarre formula is empirical, but it has a clear physical basis: each pile "shadows" the piles behind it, reducing their effective stress and hence their capacity. The angle of interference is taken as $\theta = \tan^{-1}(D/s)$ (in degrees).

**Step 1 — Interference of a row:**
Consider a row of $n$ piles with spacing $s$. Each pile is "shadowed" by the pile behind it. The fraction of effective stress lost per neighboring pile is approximated as $\theta/90$ (since at $\theta = 90°$ the piles would overlap completely).

**Step 2 — Number of neighbors per pile:**
For an interior pile in a row of $n$ piles, there are $(n-1)$ neighboring piles. Summing over all piles, the total interference in a single row is proportional to $(n-1)$. The factor of $m$ accounts for the number of rows.

**Step 3 — Symmetric consideration:**
The same interference happens in the perpendicular direction. For an $m \times n$ group, the total interference in the transverse direction is proportional to $(m-1)$, weighted by $n$.

**Step 4 — Normalization:**
Divide by the total number of piles $mn$ to get the fractional loss in capacity.

**Step 5 — Final assembled form:**
$$\eta_g = 1 - \frac{\theta}{90} \cdot \left[ \frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{mn} \right]$$

This is the **Converse–Labarre equation** as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Converse–Labarre Efficiency

**Problem:** A $3 \times 4$ pile group (3 rows, 4 piles per row) consists of 0.5 m diameter bored piles spaced at 1.5 m center-to-center. Compute the group efficiency using the Converse–Labarre formula.

**Given:**
- $D = 0.5$ m, $s = 1.5$ m, $m = 3$, $n = 4$

**Step 1: Compute $\theta$:**
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D}{s}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{1.5}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.3333) = 18.43°$$

**Step 2: Compute the interference term:**
$$(n-1)m + (m-1)n = (4-1)(3) + (3-1)(4) = 9 + 8 = 17$$

**Step 3: Compute $mn$:**
$$mn = 3 \times 4 = 12$$

**Step 4: Assemble efficiency:**
$$\eta_g = 1 - \frac{18.43}{90} \cdot \left[ \frac{17}{12} \right] = 1 - 0.2048 \cdot 1.4167$$

**Step 5: Final result:**
$$\eta_g = 1 - 0.2901 = 0.710$$

> The pile group has an efficiency of **71%**, meaning the group capacity is only 71% of the arithmetic sum of 12 individual pile capacities.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Block Failure in Clay

**Problem:** A $3 \times 3$ pile group in clay uses 0.4 m diameter, 12 m long bored piles spaced at 1.2 m. The undrained shear strength $c_u = 80$ kN/m², adhesion factor $\alpha = 0.5$, and $N_c = 9$. Compute (a) individual pile capacity, (b) block capacity, (c) group capacity.

**Step 1: Group dimensions:**
$$B_g = (n-1)s + D = (3-1)(1.2) + 0.4 = 2.8 \text{ m}$$
$$L_g = (m-1)s + D = (3-1)(1.2) + 0.4 = 2.8 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Group plan area and perimeter:**
$$A_g = 2.8 \times 2.8 = 7.84 \text{ m}^2$$
$$P_g = 2(2.8 + 2.8) = 11.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Individual pile capacity (typical formula for bored pile in clay):**
$$Q_s = c_u N_c A_b + \alpha \bar{c} P_b L$$
With $A_b = \pi D^2/4 = \pi(0.4)^2/4 = 0.1257$ m² and $P_b = \pi D = 1.257$ m:
$$Q_s = 80 \times 9 \times 0.1257 + 0.5 \times 80 \times 1.257 \times 12 = 90.5 + 603.2 = 693.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Sum of individual piles:**
$$n \cdot Q_s = 9 \times 693.7 = 6243.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Block capacity:**
$$Q_{block} = c_u N_c A_g + \alpha \bar{c} P_g L$$
$$Q_{block} = 80 \times 9 \times 7.84 + 0.5 \times 80 \times 11.2 \times 12 = 5644.8 + 5376 = 11020.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Group capacity (minimum):**
$$Q_g = \min(6243.3, 11020.8) = 6243.3 \text{ kN}$$

> **Conclusion:** Individual pile failure governs; the group is safe from block failure in this configuration.

### 3.4 Worked Example 3: Settlement by 2V:1H Method

**Problem:** A $4 \times 4$ pile group with $B_g = 4.5$ m, $L_g = 4.5$ m, and 15 m long piles carries $Q_g = 25{,}000$ kN. The pile cap weighs $W_g = 3000$ kN. Compute the stress increase at $z = 5$ m below the equivalent raft.

**Step 1: Net load on equivalent raft:**
$$Q_{net} = Q_g - W_g = 25000 - 3000 = 22000 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Equivalent raft dimensions at depth $z$ below equivalent raft:**
$$B_z = B_g + z = 4.5 + 5 = 9.5 \text{ m}$$
$$L_z = L_g + z = 4.5 + 5 = 9.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Stress increase using 2:1 method:**
$$\Delta\sigma_z = \frac{Q_{net}}{B_z \cdot L_z} = \frac{22000}{9.5 \times 9.5} = \frac{22000}{90.25}$$

**Step 4: Final result:**
$$\Delta\sigma_z = 243.8 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> The stress bulb spreads at 2 vertical to 1 horizontal ratio, hence the dimensions grow as $(B_g + z) \times (L_g + z)$ at depth $z$.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Individual Failure | Block Failure |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|
| Mechanism | Each pile slips in soil | Entire group slips as block |
| Governing soil | Sand (free-draining) | Clay (undrained) |
| Capacity formula | $n \cdot Q_s$ | $c_u N_c A_g + \alpha \bar{c} P_g L$ |
| Spacing influence | Dominant | Becomes critical at $s < 3D$ |
| Typical $\eta_g$ range | 0.7 – 0.9 | 0.4 – 0.7 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pile Group System

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Superstructure Load Q]
    A --> B[Pile Cap Rigid Block]
    B --> C1[Pile 1]
    B --> C2[Pile 2]
    B --> C3[Pile 3]
    B --> C4[Pile 4]
    B --> C5[Pile n Total Piles]
    C1 --> D1[Skin Friction fs]
    C1 --> D2[End Bearing qb]
    C2 --> E1[Skin Friction fs]
    C2 --> E2[End Bearing qb]
    C3 --> F1[Skin Friction fs]
    C3 --> F2[End Bearing qb]
    C4 --> G1[Skin Friction fs]
    C4 --> G2[End Bearing qb]
    C5 --> H1[Spacing s Center to Center]
    C5 --> H2[Diameter D]
    H1 --> I[Group Efficiency eta]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Mode 1 Individual Failure nQs]
    I --> K[Mode 2 Block Failure Qblock]
    J --> L[Qgroup Minimum of Both]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Safe Design Capacity]
```

### 4.2 Failure Mode Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MODE_A[Mode 1 Individual Pile Failure]
        IA[Loose Sand] --> IB[Pile Slip in Soil]
        IB --> IC[Skin Friction Mobilized Per Pile]
    end
    subgraph MODE_B[Mode 2 Block Failure]
        BA[Dense Clay] --> BB[Soil Trapped Between Piles]
        BB --> BC[Shear Along Group Perimeter Pg]
        BC --> BD[Resistance c alpha Pg L]
    end
    IC --> R[Group Capacity Qg min nQs Qblock]
    BD --> R
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Group Capacity Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Start Given Pile Group] --> T{Soil Type?}
    T -->|Sand| U[Check s >= 3D]
    T -->|Clay| V[Compute Qblock using cuNcAg + alpha c Pg L]
    U --> W[Use Converse Labarre for eta]
    W --> X[Qg = eta x n x Qs]
    V --> Y[Compute n x Qs]
    Y --> Z[Qg = min n x Qs and Qblock]
    X --> AA[Apply Factor of Safety FS 2.5 to 3]
    Z --> AA
    AA --> END[Safe Working Load Qa]
```

### 4.4 Equivalent Raft Concept for Settlement (2V:1H Method)

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1[Ground Surface] --> L2[Pile Cap at Depth Df]
    L2 --> L3[Equivalent Raft at 2Lb over 3 from Cap]
    L3 --> L4[Stress Distribution 2V to 1H]
    L4 --> L5[At Depth z below Equivalent Raft]
    L5 --> L6[Stress Increase Dsigma = Qnet divided by Bg+z times Lg+z]
    L6 --> L7[Settlement from 1D Consolidation Theory]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid does not render scaled physical geometry. The diagrams above are **block-level functional schematics** that map the engineering logic and computation flow — this is the correct schematic adaptation for this topic, as physical drawings of pile groups in 2D require scale-accurate drafting tools.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define a pile group. What is meant by group efficiency?*

**Model Answer:**
A pile group is a cluster of two or more piles connected at the top by a rigid pile cap, acting together to transfer heavy structural loads to deeper soil strata. The group efficiency $\eta_g$ is the ratio of the actual capacity of the pile group to the sum of individual pile capacities taken separately:
$$\eta_g = \frac{Q_g}{n \cdot Q_s}$$
It is always less than 1.0 because of pile-soil-pile interaction effects (interference and shadowing).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Distinguish between individual pile failure and block failure of a pile group.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Individual Failure | Block Failure |
|--------|-------------------|---------------|
| Soil | Sand, loose soil | Clay, dense fine soil |
| Mechanism | Each pile slips independently | Group + trapped soil slip together |
| Capacity | $n \cdot Q_s$ | $c_u N_c A_g + \alpha \bar{c} P_g L$ |
| Spacing effect | Dominates at $s \geq 3D$ | Critical at $s < 3D$ |
| Typical case | Driven piles in sand | Bored piles in clay |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** A 3 × 4 pile group of 0.5 m diameter, 15 m long bored piles is installed in clay with undrained shear strength $c_u = 100$ kN/m². The center-to-center spacing is 1.5 m. Take $\alpha = 0.5$ and $N_c = 9$. Compute:
   (i) Individual pile capacity, (ii) Block capacity, (iii) Group capacity.  **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the Converse–Labarre formula, determine the group efficiency of a 2 × 3 pile group with pile diameter 0.4 m and spacing 1.2 m. Comment on whether the spacing is adequate.  **(7 Marks)**

---

**(a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Group dimensions:**
$$B_g = (n-1)s + D = (4-1)(1.5) + 0.5 = 5.0 \text{ m}$$
$$L_g = (m-1)s + D = (3-1)(1.5) + 0.5 = 3.5 \text{ m}$$
$$A_g = 5.0 \times 3.5 = 17.5 \text{ m}^2$$
$$P_g = 2(5.0 + 3.5) = 17.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Single pile geometry:**
$$A_b = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.5)^2}{4} = 0.1963 \text{ m}^2$$
$$P_b = \pi D = 1.571 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Individual pile capacity:**
$$Q_s = c_u N_c A_b + \alpha \bar{c} P_b L$$
$$Q_s = 100 \times 9 \times 0.1963 + 0.5 \times 100 \times 1.571 \times 15$$
$$Q_s = 176.7 + 1178.3 = 1355.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Sum of individual piles:**
$$n \cdot Q_s = 12 \times 1355.0 = 16260 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Block capacity:**
$$Q_{block} = c_u N_c A_g + \alpha \bar{c} P_g L$$
$$Q_{block} = 100 \times 9 \times 17.5 + 0.5 \times 100 \times 17.0 \times 15$$
$$Q_{block} = 15750 + 12750 = 28500 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Group capacity (minimum):**
$$Q_g = \min(16260, 28500) = 16260 \text{ kN}$$

**Conclusion:** Individual pile failure governs. The group capacity is **16,260 kN**, and the design load (with FS = 2.5) is **6504 kN**.

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $\theta$:**
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D}{s}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.4}{1.2}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.3333) = 18.43°$$

**Step 2: Number of piles:**
$$m = 2, \quad n = 3, \quad mn = 6$$

**Step 3: Interference term:**
$$(n-1)m + (m-1)n = (3-1)(2) + (2-1)(3) = 4 + 3 = 7$$

**Step 4: Apply Converse–Labarre:**
$$\eta_g = 1 - \frac{18.43}{90} \cdot \left[\frac{7}{6}\right] = 1 - 0.2048 \times 1.1667$$

**Step 5: Final efficiency:**
$$\eta_g = 1 - 0.2389 = 0.761$$

**Step 6: Check spacing adequacy:**
$$s/D = 1.2/0.4 = 3.0$$
Since $s = 3D$ is the optimum spacing, the layout is **adequate** with efficiency of **76.1%**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the **2V:1H method** for computing the settlement of a pile group. A 4 × 4 pile group with $B_g = 5$ m, $L_g = 5$ m, and 20 m long piles carries $Q_g = 30{,}000$ kN. The pile cap weighs $W_g = 4000$ kN. Compute the stress increase at $z = 6$ m below the equivalent raft.  **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pile group of 9 piles (3 × 3) has individual pile capacity $Q_s = 800$ kN. The block capacity is computed as $Q_{block} = 5200$ kN. Determine the group capacity and group efficiency. State which mode of failure governs.  **(7 Marks)**

---

**(a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Concept of 2V:1H method:**
The pile group is replaced by an equivalent rectangular raft located at a depth of $\frac{2L_b}{3}$ from the pile cap. The applied stress spreads downward such that for every 1 unit of horizontal spread, there is 2 units of vertical depth. Therefore, at depth $z$ below the equivalent raft, the stressed area expands to $(B_g + z) \times (L_g + z)$.

**Step 2: Net load:**
$$Q_{net} = Q_g - W_g = 30000 - 4000 = 26000 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Stressed area at depth $z = 6$ m:**
$$B_z = B_g + z = 5 + 6 = 11 \text{ m}$$
$$L_z = L_g + z = 5 + 6 = 11 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Stress increase:**
$$\Delta\sigma_z = \frac{Q_{net}}{B_z \cdot L_z} = \frac{26000}{11 \times 11} = \frac{26000}{121}$$

**Step 5: Final result:**
$$\Delta\sigma_z = 214.9 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> The stress increase at 6 m below the equivalent raft is **214.9 kN/m²**.

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Sum of individual piles:**
$$n \cdot Q_s = 9 \times 800 = 7200 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Block capacity:**
$$Q_{block} = 5200 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Group capacity (minimum):**
$$Q_g = \min(7200, 5200) = 5200 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Group efficiency:**
$$\eta_g = \frac{Q_g}{n \cdot Q_s} = \frac{5200}{7200} = 0.722$$

**Step 5: Failure mode:**
Since $Q_{block} < n \cdot Q_s$, **block failure governs**. This typically happens in clays with closely-spaced piles, where the soil trapped between piles prevents individual slip.

> The pile group is in **block failure mode** and the group efficiency is **72.2%**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Factor of Safety (FS):** Always divide the ultimate group capacity by FS (typically 2.5 to 3.0 for piles) to obtain the **allowable load**. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if FS is omitted.
> 2. **Wrong value of $N_c$:** Use $N_c = 9$ for deep foundations in clay (Skempton). Some students wrongly use $N_c = 6$ (shallow foundation value).
> 3. **Mixing block and individual formulas:** The skin friction term for the block uses the **group perimeter $P_g$**, NOT the perimeter of a single pile. A common mistake is to use $P_b$ instead of $P_g$, leading to gross under-estimation.
> 4. **Sign error in Converse–Labarre:** The formula is a **subtractive** form: $\eta_g = 1 - (\ldots)$. Students sometimes write it as $1 + (\ldots)$, giving $\eta_g > 1$, which is impossible.
> 5. **Confusing $m$ and $n$:** By KTU convention, $m$ = number of **rows** and $n$ = number of piles **per row**. Interchanging them yields wrong answers.
> 6. **Equivalent raft depth:** Some students use $L_b/2$ or $L_b$ instead of $2L_b/3$. Memorize $2L_b/3$ from the pile cap.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pile group** = n piles + rigid pile cap, behaving as an integrated load-transfer system.
- **Two failure modes** must always be checked: **individual pile failure** ($nQ_s$) and **block failure** ($Q_{block}$). The group capacity is the **minimum** of the two.
- **Converse–Labarre formula** is the standard KTU-recommended efficiency equation for sandy/silty soils. Always compute $\theta = \tan^{-1}(D/s)$ in degrees.
- **Feld's rule** is a quick approximation; less accurate than Converse–Labarre.
- **Block failure governs in clay** when piles are closely spaced ($s < 3D$); **individual failure governs in sand** with adequate spacing.
- **Block capacity (clay):** $Q_{block} = c_u N_c A_g + \alpha \bar{c} P_g L$ with $N_c = 9$.
- **Group dimensions:** $B_g = (n-1)s + D$, $L_g = (m-1)s + D$.
- **Pile spacing:** Minimum **2D**, optimum **3D**, maximum **6D**. Below 2D, the group behaves as a single block; above 6D, cap design becomes uneconomical.
- **Settlement (2V:1H method):** Replace group by an equivalent raft at depth $2L_b/3$ from cap. Stress at depth $z$ below raft: $\Delta\sigma = (Q_g - W_g)/[(B_g+z)(L_g+z)]$.
- **Settlement ratio** $R_s = s_g/s_p$ is typically 2 to 10 — group settlement is much larger than single pile settlement.
- **Design load** = Ultimate group capacity ÷ Factor of Safety (FS = 2.5 to 3.0).
- **Efficiency is always ≤ 1.0** in practice; values 0.7–0.9 are common for driven piles in sand, 0.4–0.8 for bored piles in clay.
- **Skempton's $N_c = 9$** is mandatory for deep pile foundations — do not use the Terzaghi shallow value of 6.
- **Group perimeter $P_g = 2(B_g + L_g)$** — used in the side resistance term of block capacity; often confused with single pile perimeter.
- **For KTU 2024 ESE:** Always show the comparison $nQ_s$ vs $Q_{block}$ and explicitly state which governs, with a final concluding line.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Module 4 Scope: Earth Pressure, Retaining Structures & Slope Stability

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PBCET504 (Foundation Engineering)**, Module 4 typically consolidates the analytical backbone of **Geotechnical Design**, focusing on the interaction between soil mass and engineered structures. The numerical problems in this module are designed to test the student's ability to quantify lateral earth pressures, design retaining structures, and assess the safety of natural and man-made slopes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mandate:** Module 4 covers **Earth Pressure Theories (Rankine & Coulomb)**, **Retaining Wall Stability Analysis**, **Sheet Pile Design (Anchored & Cantilever)**, and **Stability of Slopes (Swedish Slip Circle & Taylor's Chart)**.

### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> **Earth Pressure:** The lateral pressure exerted by soil mass on a structure that retains the soil, such as a retaining wall, sheet pile, or basement wall. It is fundamentally a **stress-state problem** governed by the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion.

> **Active Earth Pressure (Pₐ):** The minimum lateral pressure exerted by the soil when the wall moves *away* from the backfill by a sufficient amount to mobilize the full shear strength of the soil (i.e., the soil mass reaches an active state of plastic equilibrium).

> **Passive Earth Pressure (Pₚ):** The maximum lateral pressure exerted by the soil when the wall is pushed *into* the backfill, mobilizing the soil's shear resistance in the opposite direction (passive state of plastic equilibrium).

> **At-Rest Earth Pressure (P₀):** The lateral pressure in the soil when the wall is *stationary* and no lateral strain is permitted. Represented by the coefficient **K₀**.

> **Factor of Safety (FOS) against Slope Failure:** The ratio of the available shear strength (shear resisting force) to the mobilized shear stress (shear driving force) along the critical slip surface.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Bookshelf Push

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Library Bookshelf:**
> Imagine pushing a tall bookshelf filled with books:
> - If you **push it slightly**, the books press back gently — this is the **at-rest** condition.
> - If you **pull the shelf away** from the books (tilt backward), the books barely lean and exert *minimum* pressure — this is the **active** state. The bookshelf wants to fall AWAY.
> - If you **push the shelf INTO the books** (shove forward), the books resist with their full weight and friction — this is the **passive** state. The resistance is enormous.
>
> The **wall** in foundation engineering is like the **bookshelf side panel**, and the **soil** is like the **stack of books**. The direction of wall movement determines which earth pressure state is mobilized.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unit weight of water | γw | **9.81** (or **10** for KTU exams) | kN/m³ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | g | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Standard soil unit weight (sandy) | γ | **16–20** | kN/m³ |
| Standard soil unit weight (clayey) | γ | **17–21** | kN/m³ |
| Active pressure coefficient (φ = 30°) | Kₐ | **0.333** | dimensionless |
| Passive pressure coefficient (φ = 30°) | Kₚ | **3.000** | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** When a problem does not specify γ, examiners typically expect you to use **γ = 18 kN/m³** for soil and **γw = 9.81 kN/m³** (or **10 kN/m³** for simplified calculations). Always state the assumed values clearly at the start of your solution to earn method marks.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rankine's Earth Pressure Theory — Step-by-Step Logic

Rankine's theory (1857) assumes:
1. The soil mass is **homogeneous, isotropic, and semi-infinite**.
2. The ground surface is a **plane** (may be inclined or horizontal).
3. The wall is **smooth and vertical** (no wall friction).
4. Failure occurs as a **plane surface** (in reality, a curved surface, but Rankine approximated it as a plane).
5. The soil is in a state of **plastic equilibrium** (either active or passive).

### Why Active Pressure is Smaller than Passive Pressure
The Mohr circle for active state **touches** the failure envelope on the **minor principal stress** side (σₐ = σ₃ = minor), while passive state touches it on the **major principal stress** side (σₚ = σ₁ = major). Since the failure envelope has a slope less than 1:1, the major principal stress at failure is **significantly larger** than the minor principal stress.

---

## 2.2 Coulomb's Earth Pressure Theory — Key Logic

Coulomb's theory (1776) is more general than Rankine's because it:
- Accounts for **wall friction (δ)** between the wall and the backfill.
- Permits the backfill surface to be **inclined at angle β**.
- Allows the wall back to be **inclined at angle θ** from vertical.
- Assumes the failure surface is a **plane** (this is a limitation corrected by Culmann's and Caquot-Kerisel methods).

---

## 2.3 Stability of Slopes — Methodological Logic

A slope fails when the **driving moment** (due to gravity acting on the sliding mass) exceeds the **resisting moment** (due to shear strength along the failure surface).

**Two Standard Approaches in KTU Module 4:**

1. **Swedish Slip Circle Method (Fellenius, 1936):** Assumes a **circular** failure surface. The sliding mass is divided into vertical slices, and the factor of safety is computed as the ratio of total resisting moment to total driving moment about the center of the slip circle.

2. **Taylor's Stability Number Method (1937):** A simplified chart-based approach for homogeneous slopes, using the dimensionless **Stability Number Nₛ = c / (γH)**.

---

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |

| 2 | Passive Pressure Coefficient (Rankine) | $K_p = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | φ = angle of internal friction | — |
| 3 | At-Rest Pressure Coefficient | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ (Jaky, 1944) | φ = angle of internal friction | — |

| 5 | Passive Pressure (horizontal backfill) | $p_p = K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot z + 2c\sqrt{K_p}$ | z = depth, c = cohesion | kN/m² |
| 6 | Total Active Force (cohesionless soil) | $P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2$ | H = wall height | kN/m |
| 7 | Total Passive Force (cohesionless soil) | $P_p = \dfrac{1}{2} K_p \gamma H^2$ | H = wall height | kN/m |
| 8 | Total Active Force (c-φ soil) | $P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 - 2cH\sqrt{K_a}$ | c = cohesion | kN/m |
| 9 | Coulomb's Active Coefficient | $K_a = \dfrac{\cos^2(\phi - \theta)}{\cos^2\theta \cdot \cos(\delta + \theta)\left[1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{\sin(\phi+\delta)\sin(\phi-\beta)}{\cos(\delta+\theta)\cos(\beta-\theta)}}\right]^2}$ | δ = wall friction, β = backfill slope, θ = wall inclination | — |
| 10 | Coulomb's Passive Coefficient | $K_p = \dfrac{\cos^2(\phi + \theta)}{\cos^2\theta \cdot \cos(\delta - \theta)\left[1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{\sin(\phi+\delta)\sin(\phi+\beta)}{\cos(\delta-\theta)\cos(\beta-\theta)}}\right]^2}$ | δ, β, θ as above | — |
| 11 | Point of Application of Pₐ (triangular) | $H/3$ from base | Triangular distribution | m |
| 12 | Point of Application of Pₐ (rectangular + triangular) | At centroid of pressure diagram | — | m |

| 14 | FOS against Sliding | $FOS_{SL} = \dfrac{\Sigma V \cdot \tan\phi_b + c_b \cdot B}{\Sigma H}$ | V = vertical force, B = base width, φ_b = base friction, c_b = base adhesion | — |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Rankine's Active Pressure Coefficient (Kₐ)

### Starting Point: Mohr Circle – Failure Envelope Geometry

At active failure, the vertical stress **σᵥ = γz** acts as the **major principal stress (σ₁)**, and the lateral stress **σₕ = σₐ** acts as the **minor principal stress (σ₃)**.

The Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion states:

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c \tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute σ₁ = σᵥ = γz and σ₃ = σₐ into the failure criterion.

$$
\sigma_v = \sigma_a \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c \tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for σₐ.

$$
\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_v - 2c \tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)}{\tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)}
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the following identity (rearranging tan to cot):

$$
\dfrac{1}{\tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)} = \tan\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the identity and use $\sigma_v = \gamma z$:

$$
\sigma_a = \gamma z \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) - 2c \tan\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** Define the **Active Earth Pressure Coefficient** and its square root:

$$
K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right), \qquad \sqrt{K_a} = \tan\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into the expression for σₐ:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_a = K_a \cdot \gamma z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}}
$$

This is the **final Rankine active pressure intensity equation**.

---

### 3.2 Companion Derivation: Rankine's Passive Pressure (Kₚ)

At passive failure, the **vertical stress σᵥ = γz** becomes the **minor principal stress (σ₃)**, and the **lateral stress σₚ** becomes the **major principal stress (σ₁)**.

**Step 1:** Start from the failure criterion, with σ₁ = σₚ, σ₃ = σᵥ:

$$
\sigma_p = \sigma_v \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c \tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Define the passive coefficient:

$$
K_p = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right), \qquad \sqrt{K_p} = \tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute to get the final result:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_p = K_p \cdot \gamma z + 2c\sqrt{K_p}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Trigonometric Identities for Kₐ and Kₚ (Exam-Critical Conversions)

These identities allow instant numerical computation when the angle φ is given. **Memorize the table below:**

| Angle φ | $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$ | $K_p = \tan^2(45° + \phi/2)$ | $K_p / K_a$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0°** | 1.000 | 1.000 | 1.00 |
| **10°** | 0.704 | 1.420 | 2.02 |
| **15°** | 0.589 | 1.698 | 2.88 |
| **20°** | 0.490 | 2.040 | 4.16 |
| **25°** | 0.406 | 2.464 | 6.07 |
| **30°** | 0.333 | 3.000 | 9.00 |
| **35°** | 0.271 | 3.690 | 13.62 |
| **40°** | 0.217 | 4.599 | 21.19 |
| **45°** | 0.172 | 5.828 | 33.88 |

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern Recognition Tip:** Notice that $K_p \cdot K_a = 1$ (always). This is a powerful sanity check — if your computed Kₐ × Kₚ ≠ 1, you have made an arithmetic error.

---

## 3.4 Total Active Force — Triangular Pressure Distribution

For **cohesionless** soil (c = 0) with **horizontal backfill** and **vertical wall**, the pressure intensity at depth z is:

$$
p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z
$$

This is a **linear function of z** — it is zero at the top (z = 0) and maximum at the base (z = H):

$$
p_{a,\max} = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H
$$

The total active force per unit length of wall is the **area of the triangular pressure diagram**:

$$
P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times H \times (K_a \gamma H)
$$

$$
\boxed{P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2}
$$

The point of application is at the **centroid of the triangle**, i.e., at a height of **H/3 above the base**.

---

## 3.5 Total Active Force — Cohesive (c–φ) Soil with Tension Crack Allowance

For c–φ soil, the active pressure at depth z is:

$$
p_a = K_a \gamma z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}
$$

The depth at which pₐ = 0 is the **tension crack depth z₀**:

$$
0 = K_a \gamma z_0 - 2c\sqrt{K_a} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad z_0 = \dfrac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}}
$$

The active pressure is **negative** (theoretical tension) from z = 0 to z = z₀. In practice, the soil cannot sustain tension, so the **tension zone** is neglected, and the effective pressure distribution is **triangular** from z₀ to H.

The total active force is then:

$$
P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma (H - z_0)^2
$$

Or equivalently, using H directly with the standard formula:

$$
P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 - 2cH\sqrt{K_a}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students apply the Rankine equation at the **top of the wall** and get a negative force, then incorrectly subtract this. The standard KTU-accepted procedure is: (a) compute the theoretical total force using the formula above, (b) **then** check whether the tension crack depth is significant, (c) if z₀ > 0, the **effective** active force is reduced. Always state both the theoretical and the practical values.

---

## 3.6 Retaining Wall Stability Checks — Analytical Framework

A retaining wall is safe if it satisfies **four** checks. The KTU exam typically asks for **any two of the four**.

### Check 1: FOS against Overturning

The overturning moment is caused by the **horizontal active force Pₐ** acting at H/3. The resisting moment is caused by the **weight of the wall W** acting through the centroid of the wall section.

$$
FOS_{OT} = \dfrac{\Sigma M_R}{\Sigma M_O} = \dfrac{W \cdot x_W + P_p \cdot x_{Pp} + \cdots}{P_a \cdot (H/3)}
$$

> **Required:** $FOS_{OT} \geq 1.5$ (IS 14458 recommends 1.5 to 2.0 for permanent walls)

### Check 2: FOS against Sliding

Sliding occurs along the **base** of the wall. The driving force is the horizontal component of Pₐ. The resisting force is the **base friction** plus any **passive resistance** at the toe.

$$
FOS_{SL} = \dfrac{\Sigma V \cdot \tan\phi_b + c_b \cdot B}{\Sigma H}
$$

> **Required:** $FOS_{SL} \geq 1.5$ (typically 1.5 to 2.0)

### Check 3: FOS against Bearing Capacity Failure

The base pressure under the wall must not exceed the allowable bearing capacity of the foundation soil.

$$
q_{\max} = \dfrac{\Sigma V}{B}\left(1 + \dfrac{6e}{B}\right), \qquad e = \dfrac{B}{2} - \bar{x}
$$

> **Required:** $q_{\max} \leq q_{allow}$, and the eccentricity $e \leq B/6$ (to avoid tension at the heel)

### Check 4: FOS against Deep-Seated Shear Failure

For tall walls on weak foundations, a **circular slip surface** may pass beneath the wall. This is checked using the Swedish circle method.

> **Required:** $FOS_{DSS} \geq 1.5$

---

## 3.7 Worked Example 1: Rankine Active Force on a Cantilever Wall (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A 6 m high retaining wall retains a cohesionless backfill with $\phi = 30°$ and $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$. The backfill is horizontal and the wall is vertical. Calculate the magnitude and point of application of the active earth pressure using Rankine's theory.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the Rankine active pressure coefficient.

$$
K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) = \tan^2\!\left(45° - 15°\right) = \tan^2(30°)
$$

$$
\tan(30°) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774
$$

$$
K_a = (0.5774)^2 = 0.3333 = \dfrac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the active pressure intensity at the base (z = H = 6 m).

$$
p_{a,\max} = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 18 \times 6 = 36 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the total active force per unit length of wall (triangular distribution).

$$
P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{3} \times 18 \times 6^2
$$

$$
P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.3333 \times 18 \times 36 = 108 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 4:** Point of application (centroid of triangle).

$$
\bar{z} = \dfrac{H}{3} = \dfrac{6}{3} = 2 \text{ m from the base}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{P_a = 108 \text{ kN/m acting horizontally at 2.0 m above the base}}
$$

---

## 3.8 Worked Example 2: Retaining Wall — Sliding & Overturning Check (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A gravity retaining wall has the following data:
> - Wall height: **H = 6 m**
> - Base width: **B = 3.0 m**
> - Weight of wall: **W = 180 kN/m** (acting at x = 1.2 m from the toe)
> - Active force: **Pₐ = 108 kN/m** at H/3 = 2 m from the base
> - Coefficient of base friction: **μ = tan(δ) = 0.45**
> - Passive force at toe: **Pₚ = 15 kN/m** (assumed acting at 0.5 m from the toe)
>
> Compute the FOS against sliding and overturning.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the overturning moment about the toe.

The horizontal active force Pₐ tends to **overturn** the wall about the toe (front-bottom corner).

$$
M_O = P_a \times (H/3) = 108 \times 2 = 216 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the resisting moment about the toe.

The vertical weight W acts at a horizontal distance of 1.2 m from the toe, and the passive force Pₚ resists overturning.

$$
M_R = W \times x_W + P_p \times x_{Pp} = 180 \times 1.2 + 15 \times 0.5
$$

$$
M_R = 216 + 7.5 = 223.5 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute FOS against overturning.

$$
FOS_{OT} = \dfrac{M_R}{M_O} = \dfrac{223.5}{216} = 1.035
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is BELOW the safe value of 1.5!** The wall is **unsafe against overturning**. The student should be able to identify this and suggest a remedy (e.g., increase base width or add a heel projection).

**Step 4:** Compute the driving and resisting forces for sliding.

The horizontal active force drives the wall outward. The base friction (proportional to the total vertical load) resists sliding.

$$
\text{Driving force} = P_a = 108 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
\text{Resisting force (friction)} = \mu \cdot W = 0.45 \times 180 = 81 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
\text{Resisting force (passive at toe)} = P_p = 15 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
\text{Total resisting force} = 81 + 15 = 96 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute FOS against sliding.

$$
FOS_{SL} = \dfrac{96}{108} = 0.889
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is BELOW 1.0 — the wall will physically SLIDE!** FOS must be ≥ 1.5. The student should suggest a shear key (toe projection) to engage additional passive resistance.

---

## 3.9 Worked Example 3: Infinite Slope Stability (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> An infinite slope is made of cohesionless soil with $\phi' = 32°$ and the slope angle $\beta = 25°$. The slope is dry (no pore pressure). Compute the factor of safety.

### Solution

**Step 1:** For a cohesionless, dry infinite slope, the FOS reduces to:

$$
FOS = \dfrac{\tan\phi'}{\tan\beta}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
FOS = \dfrac{\tan(32°)}{\tan(25°)} = \dfrac{0.6249}{0.4663} = 1.340
$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion.

$$
\boxed{FOS = 1.34 \text{ — Safe (greater than 1.0, but the standard minimum for permanent slopes is 1.5)}}
$$

> **Note:** If the slope were submerged or had seepage parallel to the slope, the FOS would be **halved** (the buoyant unit weight γ′ ≈ γ/2 is used, and an additional destabilizing seepage force acts).

---

## 3.10 Worked Example 4: Critical Height of a Vertical Cut (Taylor's Method)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A vertical cut is to be made in a clay soil with $c_u = 50 \text{ kN/m}^2$ and $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$. The soil is purely cohesive ($\phi_u = 0$). Using Taylor's stability chart, determine the critical height. Take stability number $N_s = 0.261$ for a vertical cut.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Apply Taylor's formula.

$$
H_{cr} = \dfrac{c \cdot N_c}{\gamma} = \dfrac{c}{\gamma \cdot N_s}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute (using N_s = 0.261):

$$
H_{cr} = \dfrac{50}{18 \times 0.261} = \dfrac{50}{4.698} = 10.64 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Design height with FOS = 1.5:

$$
H_{design} = \dfrac{H_{cr}}{FOS} = \dfrac{10.64}{1.5} = 7.09 \text{ m} \approx 7.1 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{H_{cr} = 10.64 \text{ m}, \quad H_{design} = 7.1 \text{ m (with FOS = 1.5)}}
$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Rankine Earth Pressure States — Wall Movement Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Wall Construction Stage] --> nodeB[Initial State: K0 Condition]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Movement Direction?}
    nodeC -- "Wall moves AWAY from soil" --> nodeD[Active State: Ka is MINIMUM]
    nodeC -- "Wall moves INTO the soil" --> nodeE[Passive State: Kp is MAXIMUM]
    nodeC -- "No wall movement" --> nodeF[At-Rest State: K0 is INTERMEDIATE]

    nodeD --> nodeG[Soil mass EXPANDS laterally]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Soil mass COMPRESSES laterally]
    nodeF --> nodeI[No lateral strain in soil]

    nodeG --> nodeJ[Failure: soil shears outward and downward]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Failure: soil heaves upward in front of wall]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Stable elastic equilibrium]

    style nodeA fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style nodeB fill:#2a4a73,color:#ffffff
    style nodeC fill:#b8860b,color:#ffffff
    style nodeD fill:#8b0000,color:#ffffff
    style nodeE fill:#006400,color:#ffffff
    style nodeF fill:#4a4a4a,color:#ffffff
    style nodeJ fill:#ff6347,color:#ffffff
    style nodeK fill:#90ee90,color:#000000
    style nodeL fill:#87ceeb,color:#000000
```
